## CONCEPTMAP

- Used for measuring $\mathrm{H}^{+}$ concentration in a solution.
- Neutral solution $\mathrm{pH}=7$.
- Acidic solution = pH less than 7.
- Basic solution pH greater than 7 .
- Sour in taste
- Changes blue litmus to red
- Acid + metal → salt + $\mathrm{H}_{2} \uparrow$
- Metal carbonate/metal hydrogen carbonate + acid → salt $+\mathrm{CO}_{2}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$
- Base + acid → salt $+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$
- Metal oxide + acid → salt + $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$
- Conduct electricity due to presence of $\mathrm{H}^{+}$ion
- Hydrogen ion of acids cannot exist alone, they exist after combining with $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ molecules.

[^0]- Bitter in taste
- Change red litmus to blue
- Non-metallic oxides + Base → salt $+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$
- Gives $\mathrm{OH}^{-}$ion in aqueous solution.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-050.jpg?height=132&width=33&top_left_y=1377&top_left_x=385)
- Salts can be acidic, basic or neutral.

Same common salts :

- NaOH : Formed by chlor-alkali process used in soaps, detergents paper making etc.
- $\mathrm{CaOCl}_{2}: \mathrm{Ca}(\mathrm{OH})_{2}+\mathrm{Cl}_{2} \rightarrow \mathrm{CaOCl}_{2}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$
- Used for bleaching cotton and lines, as oxidising agent.
- $\mathrm{NaHCO}_{3}: \mathrm{NaCl}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}+\mathrm{CO}_{2}+\mathrm{NH}_{3} \rightarrow$
- Used as antaacid, in soda acid fire extinguishers etc.
- $\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{CO}_{3}$ : Used in glass, soap and paper industry for removing permanent hardness of water.

$$
\mathrm{NH}_{4} \mathrm{Cl}+\mathrm{NaHCO}_{3}
$$

## ACIDS

The word "acid" comes from the Latin word acidus, meaning "sour" or "tart," since aqueous solutions of acids have a sour or tart taste. Lemon and orange taste sour because they contain citric acid and ascorbic acid (vitamin C). Another common acid is vinegar, which is the sour liquid produced when apple cider, grape juice, or other plant juices ferment beyond the formation of alcohol. Vinegar is a 5 percent aqueous solution of acetic acid.
Liebig defined acid as a compound containing one or more replaceable hydrogen(s). Acid dissociate in water and produce hydrogen ion $\left(\mathrm{H}^{+}\right)$e.g. HCl is an acid and on dissolving in water it gives $\mathrm{H}^{+}$and $\mathrm{Cl}^{-}$ions.

$$
\mathrm{HCl}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \longrightarrow \mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{O}^{+}+\mathrm{Cl}^{-}
$$

Hydrogen ions cannot exist alone but they exist after combining with water molecules. Thus hydrogen ion must always be shown as $\mathrm{H}^{+}(\mathrm{aq})$ or hydronium ion $\left(\mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{O}^{+}\right)$.

$$
\mathrm{H}^{+}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \longrightarrow \mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{O}^{+}
$$

On the basis of sources acids can be classified into two groups. These are
(a) Organic acid: Acid obtained from plants material and animals is called organic acid. e.g citric acid (oranges \& lemons), acetic acid (vinegar), oleic acid (olive oil) etc.
(b) Mineral acid: Acid obtained from minerals is called mineral or inorganic acid. They do not contain carbon. e.g. $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}, \mathrm{HCl}, \mathrm{HNO}_{3}$ etc.

## Physical Properties of Acids

(a) Taste : Acids are generally sour in taste.
(b) Effect on indicator : Acid changes the colour of indicators as given in the table.

| Indicator | Change of colour |  |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Litmus | From | To |
| Methyl orange | Blue | Red |
| Phenolphthalein | Orange | Red |

(c) Electrical Conductivity : Acids conduct electricity in aqueous solution, since they contain dissolved ions. For example, soluble acids like hydrochloric acid and sulphuric acid are used as electrolytes.
(d) Corrosive action : Strong acids are corrosive in nature. They corrode metals and release hydrogen gas. They can also burn skin. Know!

Acids are generally not corrosive except mineral acids $\left(\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}, \mathrm{HNO}{ }_{3}, \mathrm{HCl}\right.$ etc) and most of them are solids like organic acids (oxalic acid, tartaric acid, ascorbic acid, citric acid etc.)

## Chemical Properties of Acids

(a) Reaction on metals :
(i) Metals which lie above hydrogen in the metal activity series (already studied in chapter 1) generally react with dilute acids to form their respective salt and hydrogen.

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \text { Metal + Acid } \longrightarrow \text { Salt + Hydrogen } \\
& \begin{aligned}
\mathrm{Mg}(\mathrm{~s}) & +\underset{\text { Magnesi }}{2 \mathrm{HCl}(\mathrm{aq})}
\end{aligned} \longrightarrow \begin{array}{c}
\mathrm{MgCl}_{2}(\mathrm{aq}) \\
\text { Magnesium }
\end{array}+\underset{\text { Hydrochloric acid }}{\mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \uparrow} \\
& \begin{array}{c}
\text { Magnesium chloride } \\
\text { Hydrogen }(\mathrm{s})+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}(\mathrm{aq})
\end{array} \longrightarrow \begin{array}{c}
\mathrm{FeSO}_{4}(\mathrm{aq})++\underset{\text { Iron }(\text { II }) \text { sulphate }}{\mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \uparrow} \\
\text { Iron }
\end{array} \text { Sulpdrogen }
\end{aligned}
$$

(ii) Highly active metals like potassium, sodium and calcium also react in similar manner, but they tend to explode on combining with acids.

$$
\underset{\text { Sodium }}{2 \mathrm{Na}(\mathrm{~s})}+\underset{\text { Dilute hydrochloric acid }}{2 \mathrm{HCl}(\mathrm{aq})} \longrightarrow \underset{\text { Sodium chloride }}{2 \mathrm{NaCl}(\mathrm{aq})}+\underset{\text { Hydrogen }}{\mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \uparrow}
$$

Nitric acid (of various concentrations) usually exhibits oxidizing property, rather than acidic property. Metals such as magnesium combine with extremely dilute (1\%) nitric acid to liberate hydrogen.

$$
\underset{\text { Magnesium }}{\mathrm{Mg}(\mathrm{~s})}+\underset{\text { Nitric acid }}{2 \mathrm{HNO}_{3}(\mathrm{aq})} \longrightarrow \underset{\text { Magnesium nitrate }}{\mathrm{Mg}\left(\mathrm{NO}_{3}\right)_{2}(\mathrm{aq})+}+\underset{\text { Hydrogen }}{\mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \uparrow}
$$

Carbonic acid being very weak acid does not react with any metal at all.

## ILLUSTRATION : 1

Which gas is usually liberated when an acid reacts with a metal? Illustrate with an example. How will you test for the presence of this gas ?

## SOLUTION :

The gas liberated is hydrogen.

$$
\mathrm{Zn}(\mathrm{~s})+2 \mathrm{HCl}(\mathrm{aq}) \longrightarrow \mathrm{ZnCl}_{2}(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \uparrow
$$

Take a burning splinter near the gas. If it burns explosively with a 'pop' sound, the gas is hydrogen.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-052.jpg?height=136&width=419&top_left_y=685&top_left_x=196)

Ask your teacher to demonstrate lab preparation of hydrogen in your school lab. Set up the experimental set as shown in following figure.
Granulated zinc is taken in a Woulfe's bottle. Dilute sulphuric acid is poured dropwise through a thistle funnel on granulated zinc pieces. The gas evolved is collected over water by the downward displacement of water.
In the same experiment if dil $\mathrm{NHO}_{3}$ or dil HCl is used instead of dil $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}$ What will happen? do you find the same observation?

## Precautionary measures

- Make sure your experimental apparatus is set up properly.
- Sometimes the bubbles of hydrogen formed stick to the surface of zinc metal and this prevents further reaction of the acid on the metal. This can be prevented by adding a few crystals of copper sulphate to the reaction mixture.

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-052.jpg?height=469&width=753&top_left_y=927&top_left_x=1041)
Laboratory preparation of dihydrogen

(b) Reaction with basic oxides

Basic oxides get neutralized when they react with acids.

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \\
& \underset{\text { Sodium oxide }}{\mathrm{Na} 2 \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{~s})}+\underset{\text { Hydrochloric acid }}{2 \mathrm{HCl}(\mathrm{aq})} \longrightarrow \underset{\text { Sodium chloride }}{2 \mathrm{NaCl}(\mathrm{aq})}+\underset{\text { Water }}{\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l})} \\
& \underset{\text { Lead oxide }}{\mathrm{PbO}(\mathrm{~s})}+\underset{\mathrm{Nitric}_{2} \text { acid }}{2 \mathrm{HNO}_{3}(\mathrm{aq})} \longrightarrow \underset{\text { Lead nitrate }}{\mathrm{Pb}\left(\mathrm{NO}_{3}\right)_{2}(\mathrm{aq})+\underset{\text { Water }}{\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l})}} \\
& \underset{\text { Copper oxide }}{\mathrm{CuO}(\mathrm{~s})}+\underset{\text { Sulphuric acid }}{\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}(\mathrm{aq})} \longrightarrow \underset{\text { Copper sulphate }}{\mathrm{CuSO}_{4}(\mathrm{aq})+\underset{\text { Water }}{\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l})}}
\end{aligned}
$$

(c) Reaction with basic hydroxides

Acids undergo neutralization reaction with basic hydroxides to form salt and water.

\[

\]

(d) Reaction with carbonates and bicarbonates (hydrogen carbonates)

Acids react with carbonates and bicarbonates to form their respective salt, water and carbon dioxide.
Carbonate/Bicarbonate + Acid ⟶ Salt + Water + Carbon dioxide

$$
\underset{\text { Sodium carbonate }}{\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{CO}_{3}(\mathrm{aq})}+\underset{\text { Hydrochloric acid }}{2 \mathrm{HCl}(\mathrm{aq})} \longrightarrow \underset{\text { Sodium chloride }}{2 \mathrm{NaCl}(\mathrm{aq})}+\underset{\text { Water }}{\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l})}+\underset{\text { Carbon dioxide }}{\mathrm{CO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \uparrow}
$$

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-052.jpg?height=86&width=102&top_left_y=2558&top_left_x=1820)

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \underset{\text { Zinc carbonate }}{\mathrm{ZnCO}_{3}(\mathrm{~s})}+\underset{\text { Sulphuric acid }}{\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}(\mathrm{aq})} \longrightarrow \underset{\text { Zinc sulphate }}{\mathrm{ZnSO}_{4}(\mathrm{aq})}+\underset{\text { Water }}{\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l})}+\underset{\text { Carbon dioxide }}{\mathrm{CO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \uparrow} \\
& \underset{\text { Sod. hydrogen carbonate }}{2 \mathrm{NaHCO}_{3}(\mathrm{aq})}+\underset{\text { Sulphuric acid }}{\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}(\mathrm{l})} \longrightarrow \underset{\text { Sodium sulphate }}{\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}(\mathrm{aq})}+\underset{\text { Water }}{2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l})}+\underset{\text { Carbon dioxide }}{2 \mathrm{CO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \uparrow}
\end{aligned}
$$

## ILLUSTRATION : 2

Metal compound A reacts with dilute hydrochloric acid to produce effervescence. The gas evolved extinguishes a burning candle. Write a balanced chemical equation for the reaction, if one of the compounds formed is calcium chloride.

## SOLUTION :

The compound is calcium carbonate. The gas evolved is carbon dioxide which extinguishes a burning candle.

$$
\underset{\text { Calcium carbonate }}{\mathrm{CaCO}_{3}(\mathrm{~s})}+2 \mathrm{HCl}(\mathrm{dil}) \longrightarrow \mathrm{CaCl}_{2}(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l})+\mathrm{CO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})
$$

## (e) Reaction with sulphites and bisulphites (hydrogen sulphites)

Acids react with sulphites and bisulphites to form their respective salt, water and sulphur dioxide.

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \text { Sulphite/Bisulphite }+ \text { Acid } \longrightarrow \text { Salt }+ \text { Water + Sulphur dioxide } \\
& \underset{\text { Sodium sulphite }}{\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{3}(\mathrm{aq})+} \underset{\text { Nitric acid }}{2 \mathrm{HNO}_{3}(\mathrm{aq})} \longrightarrow \underset{\text { Sodium nitrate }}{2 \mathrm{NaNO}_{3}(\mathrm{aq})+\underset{\text { Water }}{\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l})}+\underset{\text { Sulphur dioxide }}{\mathrm{SO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \uparrow}} \\
& \underset{\text { Zinc sulphite }}{\mathrm{ZnSO}_{3}(\mathrm{~s})+} \underset{\text { Nitric acid }}{2 \mathrm{HNO}_{3}(\mathrm{aq})} \longrightarrow \underset{\text { Zinc nitrate }}{\mathrm{Zn}\left(\mathrm{NO}_{3}\right)_{2}(\mathrm{aq})+\underset{\text { Water }}{\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l})}+\underset{\text { Sulphur dioxide }}{\mathrm{SO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \uparrow}} \\
& \underset{\substack{\text { Potassium hydrogen } \\
\text { sulphite }}}{\mathrm{KHSO}_{3}(\mathrm{aq})}+\underset{\text { Hydrochloric acid }}{\mathrm{HCl}(\mathrm{aq})} \longrightarrow \underset{\text { Potassium chloride }}{\mathrm{KCl}(\mathrm{aq})}+\underset{\text { Water }}{\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l})+}+\underset{\text { Sulphur dioxide }}{\mathrm{SO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \uparrow}
\end{aligned}
$$

Time to

## Check Your Knowledge

- What will happen if lead sulphite is taken in a test tube and few $\mathbf{m L}$ of aqueous nitric acid is added to it. After that litmus paper is placed at the mouth of test tube?


## SOLUTION

Litmus paper will turn red as it is exposed to evolved gas sulphur dioxide which is acidic in nature. Reaction occur is following

$$
\underset{\text { Lead sulphite }}{\mathrm{PbSO}_{3}(\mathrm{~s})}+\underset{\text { Nitric acid }}{2 \mathrm{HNO}_{3}(\mathrm{aq})} \longrightarrow \underset{\text { Lead nitrate }}{\mathrm{Pb}\left(\mathrm{NO}_{3}\right)_{2}(\mathrm{aq})}+\underset{\text { Water }}{\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l})}+\underset{\substack{\text { Sulphurdioxide } \\ \text { (acidic) }}}{\mathrm{SO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \uparrow}
$$

## (f) Reaction with metal sulphides

Acids react with metal sulphides to form their respective salt and hydrogen sulphide.
Metals sulphide + Acid ⟶ Salt + Hydrogen sulphide
$\underset{\text { Magnesium sulphide }}{\mathrm{MgS}(\mathrm{s})}+\underset{\text { Hydrochloric acid }}{2 \mathrm{HCl}(\mathrm{aq})} \longrightarrow \underset{\text { Magnesium chloride }}{\mathrm{MgCl}_{2}(\mathrm{aq})}+\underset{\text { Hydrogen sulphide }}{\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{~S}(\mathrm{~g})}$
$\underset{\text { Iron (II) sulphide }}{\mathrm{FeS}(\mathrm{s})}+\underset{\text { Sulphuric acid }}{\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}(\mathrm{aq})} \longrightarrow \underset{\text { Iron (II) sulphate }}{\mathrm{FeSO}_{4}(\mathrm{aq})}+\underset{\text { Hydrogen sulphide }}{\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{~S}(\mathrm{~g})}$

## CASE STUDY-1 : Get Closer to Acids

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-054.jpg?height=976&width=511&top_left_y=1350&top_left_x=375)
An acid is a substance, whose aqueous solution releases hydrogen ion.

CASE I : Are all compounds containing hydrogen acids?
When an acid is dissolved in water, then in solution it produces hydrogen ion $\left(\mathrm{H}^{+}\right)$. But compounds like glucose and alcohol do not dissociate in water to produce hydrogen ion. Therefore, even though they have hydrogen in their formula they are not acids.

## Think Out of the Box

Q. 1 You are given $\mathrm{HNO}_{3}$ solution and glucose solution which one do you think will show electrical conductivity and why?
Q. 2 Among the given substances, find whose property can be determined by universal indicator.
(i) $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOH}(\mathrm{aq})$
(ii) $\quad \mathrm{HCl}(\mathrm{g})$
(iii) $\quad \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}(\mathrm{~s})$
(iv) $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}(\mathrm{aq})$
(v) $\quad \mathrm{HNO}_{3}$ (aq)
(vi) $\quad \mathrm{HCl}(\mathrm{aq})$
(vii) $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{CO}_{3}(\mathrm{aq})$
Q. 3 If you have to store sulphuric acid $\left(\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}\right)$ which container will you prefer metallic or ceramic, and why?

CASE II : To determine whether acid will show their acidic behaviourcas in absence of water.

Take sodium chloride in a boiling tube and add some concentrated sulphuric acid to it. Following reaction will take place,

$$
2 \mathrm{NaCl}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}+2 \mathrm{HCl}
$$

The gaseous hydrochloric acid formed, starts coming out of the tube.

Now, if we will place 'dry' blue litmus paper near the mouth of the tube it does not show any change, but if we place 'moist' blue litmus paper it turns red. This is because acids produces $\mathrm{H}^{+}$ion only in presence of water, hence in absence of water they do not show acidic behaviour.

CASE III : While using an acid in lab, we notice a burned hole in our lab coat.

Acids are corrosive in nature. While handling an acid, if it falls accidently on skin, clothes or wood it causes severe burns on the skin, it cuts hole in clothes and burns the wood producing black spots.
CONNECTING TOPIC CONNECTING TOPIC CONNECTING TOPIC CONNECTING TOPIC CONNECTING TOPIC CONNECTING TOPIC

## CONNECTING TOPIC

## CLASSIFICATION OF ACIDS

## (1) Classification Based on the Presence of Oxygen

(i) Oxy-acids

Oxy-acids are those, which contain oxygen in their composition.
Examples : Sulphuric acid $\left(\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}\right)$, Oxalic acid $\left(\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}\right)$, Nitric acid $\left(\mathrm{HNO}_{3}\right)$, Acetic acid $\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOH}\right)$.
(ii) Hydracids

Acids which contain hydrogen together with other element and not any oxygen in their composition, are called hydracids. Examples: Hydrochloric and (HCl), Hydroiodic acid (HI), Hydrobromic acid (HBr).

Time to
Check Your Knowledge

## $\mathrm{HClO}_{4}$ is a hydracid or oxyacid?

## SOLUTION

It is an oxy-acid as it contains oxygen in its composition.

## (2) Classification Based on the Strength of the Acid

Almost all the acids dissociate in water and produce hydrogen ion to a lesser or greater extent. The strength of an acid depends on the concentration of the hydrogen ions present in a solution. Greater the tendency to loose hydrogen ion in the solution, greater is the strength of acid. However, some acids do not dissociate to an appreciable extent in water such as carbonic acid. Therefore, these acids will have a low concentration of hydrogen ions present in a solution. Based on this, acids are classified as :
(i) Strong acids

An acid, which dissociates completely or almost completely in water, is classified as a strong acid.
Examples : Hydrochloric acid, sulphuric acid, nitric acid etc.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathrm{HCl}(\mathrm{aq}) & \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{H}^{+}(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{Cl}^{-}(\mathrm{aq}) \\
\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}(\mathrm{aq}) & \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{H}^{+}(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{SO}_{4}^{2-}(\mathrm{aq}) \\
\mathrm{HNO}_{3}(\mathrm{aq}) & \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{H}^{+}(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{NO}_{3}^{-}(\mathrm{aq})
\end{aligned}
$$

Note: It must be noted that in these acids all the hydrogen ions combine with water molecule and exist as hydronium ions, $\mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{O}^{+}$.

$$
\mathrm{H}^{+}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{O}^{+}
$$

## (ii) Weak acids

An acid which dissociates negligibly or almost incompletely in water, is classified as a weak acid.
Examples : Acetic acid, formic acid, carbonic acid etc.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOH}(\mathrm{aq}) & \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COO}^{-}(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{H}^{+}(\mathrm{aq}) \\
\text { Acetic acid } & \\
\mathrm{HCOOH}(\mathrm{aq}) & \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{HCOO}^{-}(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{H}^{+}(\mathrm{aq}) \\
\text { Formic acid } & \\
\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{CO}_{3}(\mathrm{aq}) & \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{H}^{+}(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{HCO}_{3}^{-}(\mathrm{aq}) \\
\text { Carbonic acid } &
\end{aligned}
$$

Or

$$
\underset{\text { Carbonic acid }}{\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{CO}_{3}(\mathrm{aq})} \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{H}^{+}(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{CO}_{3}^{2-}(\mathrm{aq})
$$

## Time to

## Check Your Knowledge

## - Hypobromous acid (HOBr) is a weak or strong acid?

## SOLUTION

It is a weak acid as it does not ionise completely in water.

## (3) Classification Based on the Concentration of the Acid

Concentration of an acid solution depends upon the percentage of the acid that is dissolved in water. Based on this, acids are classified as
CONNECTING TOPIC CONNECTING TOPIC CONNECTING TOPIC CONNECTING TOPIC CONNECTING TOPIC CONNECTING TOPIC
(i) Concentrated acid : An acid, that has a relatively high percentage of the acid dissolved in the aqueous solution is classified as a concentrated acid. Example concentrated hydrochloric acid, sulphuric acid, nitric acid, acetic acid etc.
(ii) Dilute acid : An acid, which has a relatively low percentage of the acid dissolved in the aqueous solution is classified as a dilute acid. Examples : dilute hydrochloric acid, sulphuric acid, nitric acid, acetic acid etc.

## (4) Classification Based on the Basicity of an Acid

As we have studied that acid on dissociation in water produce hydrogen ion, the number of these replaceable hydrogen ions in an acid is known as the basicity of an acid. It is also evident that, one hydrogen ion or hydronium ion can react with one hydroxyl ion ( $\mathrm{OH}^{-}$).
(i) Monobasic acid

A monobasic acid has one replaceable hydrogen ion. Hence, these acids combine with one hydroxyl group of a base to form salt and water.
Since, monobasic acids have only one replaceable hydrogen ion, they form only one kind of salt.

| Examples : | Hydrochloric acid | $;$ | $\mathrm{HCl} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{H}^{+}+\mathrm{Cl}^{-}$ |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
|  | Hydroiodic acid | $;$ | $\mathrm{HI} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{H}^{+}+\mathrm{I}^{-}$ |
|  | Nitric acid | $;$ | $\mathrm{HNO}_{3} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{H}^{+}+\mathrm{NO}_{3}^{-}$ |
|  | Acetic acid | $;$ | $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOH} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{H}^{+}+\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COO}^{-}$ |
|  | Hypochlorous acid | $;$ | $\mathrm{HOCl} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{H}^{+}+\mathrm{OCl}^{-}$ |

All the above acids have one replaceable hydrogen. Hence, they are monobasic acid and have basicity one.
(ii) Dibasic acid

A dibasic acid has two replaceable hydrogen ion. These acid combine with two hydroxyl groups of a base to form salt and water. It known as a dibasic acid. Such acids dissociate in two steps in water.
Example :
(i) $\quad \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}(\mathrm{aq}) \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{H}^{+}(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{HSO}_{4}^{-}(\mathrm{aq})$
(ii) $\quad \mathrm{HSO}_{4}^{-}(\mathrm{aq}) \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{H}^{+}(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{SO}_{4}^{2-}(\mathrm{aq})$

Since dibasic acid has two hydrogen ions, it can form two kinds of salts, i.e. the normal salt, and a hydrogen salt, as given below:

$$
\underset{\text { Sodium hydroxide }}{\mathrm{NaOH}(\mathrm{aq})}+\underset{\text { Sulphuric acid }}{\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}(\mathrm{aq})} \longrightarrow \underset{\text { Sodium hydrogen sulphate }}{\mathrm{NaHSO}_{4}(\mathrm{aq})}+\underset{\text { Water }}{\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l})}
$$

Example : $\quad$ Sulphuric acid $\quad ; \quad \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4} \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{H}^{+}+\mathrm{SO}_{4}^{2-}$

$$
\begin{array}{ll}
\text { Sulphurous acid } ; & \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{3} \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{H}^{+}+\mathrm{SO}_{3}^{2-} \\
\text { Carbonic acid } ; & \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{CO}_{3} \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{H}^{+}+\mathrm{CO}_{3}^{2-} \\
\text { Oxalic acid } ; & (\mathrm{COOH})_{2} \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{H}^{+}+\mathrm{COO}^{-} \\
\mathrm{COO}^{-}
\end{array}
$$

## (iii) Tribasic acids

The tribasic acids can combine with three hydroxyl groups. These acids have three replaceable hydrogen ions. Example, phosphoric acid $\left(\mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{PO}_{4}\right)$. Phosphoric acid dissociates in water in three steps:
(i) $\quad \mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{PO}_{4} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{H}^{+}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{PO}_{4}^{-}$
(ii) $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{PO}_{4}^{-} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{H}^{+}+\mathrm{HPO}_{4}^{2-}$
(iii) $\mathrm{HPO}_{4}^{2-} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{H}^{+}+\mathrm{PO}_{4}^{3-}$

Since, they are tribasic, therefore, they give three types of salts.

$$
\begin{array}{cc}
\underset{\text { Sodium Hydroxide }}{\mathrm{NaOH}(\mathrm{aq})}+\underset{\text { Phosphoric acid }}{\mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{PO}_{4}(\mathrm{aq})} \longrightarrow \underset{\text { Monosodium dihydrogen phosphate }}{\mathrm{NaH}_{2} \mathrm{PO}_{4}(\mathrm{aq})} & +\underset{\text { Water }}{\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l})} \\
\underset{\text { Sodium Hydroxide }}{2 \mathrm{NaOH}(\mathrm{aq})}+\underset{\text { Phosphoric acid }}{\mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{PO}_{4}(\mathrm{aq})} \longrightarrow \underset{\text { Disodium monohydrogen phosphate }}{\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{HPO}_{4}(\mathrm{aq})}+\underset{\text { Water }}{2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l})} & \\
\underset{\text { Sodium Hydroxide }}{3 \mathrm{NaOH}(\mathrm{aq})}+\underset{\text { Phosphoric acid }}{\mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{PO}_{4}(\mathrm{aq})} \longrightarrow \underset{\substack{\mathrm{Na}_{3} \mathrm{PO}_{4}(\mathrm{aq}) \\
\text { Trisodium phosphate }}}{\mathrm{Na}^{2}}+\underset{\text { Water }}{3 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l})}
\end{array}
$$

Note :- Acids containing more than one acidic hydrogen atom are called polyprotic acids.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-056.jpg?height=86&width=100&top_left_y=2558&top_left_x=1822)

## BASES

Like acids, bases also dissociate in water, but in place of $\mathrm{H}^{+}$they produce hydroxyl ion $\left(\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right)$. Thus, a base is a compound which on dissolving in water yields hydroxyl ions $\left(\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right)$. The characteristic property of a base is due to the formation of these negative hydroxyl ions. A base may be an oxide or a hydroxide of a metal. If a base is soluble in water, it dissociates to form a metal ion and negative hydroxyl ion. Substances that combine with acids to form salt and water are also called bases.

## Example:

Sodium hydroxide is a base, it gives sodium ion and hydroxyl ion when dissolved in water.

$$
\mathrm{NaOH}(\mathrm{aq}) \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{Na}^{+}(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{OH}^{-}(\mathrm{aq})
$$

Ammonium hydroxide is also a base. In water, it dissociates to form ammonium ion and a hydroxyl ion.

$$
\mathrm{NH}_{4} \mathrm{OH}(\mathrm{aq}) \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{NH}_{4}^{+}(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{OH}^{-}(\mathrm{aq})
$$

## Alkali

Alkalis are bases that dissociate in water to yield hydroxyl ion ( $\mathrm{OH}^{-}$). Sodium hydroxide, potassium hydroxide, calcium hydroxide and ammonium hydroxide are the common alkalis.
$\mathrm{NaOH}(\mathrm{aq}) \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{Na}^{+}(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{OH}^{-}(\mathrm{aq})$

$$
; \quad \mathrm{Ca}(\mathrm{OH})_{2}(\mathrm{aq}) \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{Ca}^{2+}(\mathrm{aq})+2 \mathrm{OH}^{-}(\mathrm{aq})
$$

$\mathrm{KOH}(\mathrm{aq}) \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{K}^{+}(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{OH}^{-}(\mathrm{aq})$
;

All alkalis are bases but not all bases are alkali. Alkali is a basic, ionic salt of an alkali or alkaline earth metal element. Alkalis are best known for being bases that dissolve in water. Whereas a base is most commonly thought of as a substance that can accept $\boldsymbol{H}+$ ions. A soluble base is also often referred to as an alkali if hydroxide ions $\left(\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right)$are involved.

Time to Check Your Knowledge

Whether calcium oxide $(\mathbf{C a O})$ is alkali, base or both?

## SOLUTION

It serves as both. As we know that alkalis are bases that dissociate in water to yield hydroxyl ion $\left(\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right)$. The calcium oxide dissolves in water to form calcium hydroxide which further ionises to give hydroxyl ion $\left(\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right)$.

$$
\mathrm{CaO}+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{Ca}(\mathrm{OH})_{2} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{Ca}^{2+}+2 \mathrm{OH}^{-}
$$

## Physical Properties of Bases

(a) Taste: Bases are bitter to taste. If you have ever gotten soap in your mouth, you have noticed its bitter taste. This bitter taste confirms presence of base in the soap.
(b) Feel: They are soapy and slippery to touch.
(c) Electrical conductivity: Some bases are good conductors of electricity. For example, soluble bases like sodium hydroxide and potassium hydroxide are used as electrolytes.
(d) Corrosive action : Strong alkalis like sodium hydroxide and potassium hydroxide are highly corrosive or caustic in nature. Sodium hydroxide and potassium hydroxide are commonly called caustic soda and caustic potash respectively. Organic tissues like skin, etc. get completely corroded by these two alkalis. However, the other alkalis are only mildly corrosive.
(e) Effect on indicator: Bases changes the colour of indicator as given in the table.

| Indicator | Change of colour |  |
| :--- | :---: | :---: |
| Litmus | From | To |
| Methyl orange | Red | Blue |
| Phenolphthalein | Orange | Yellow |
| Turmeric | Colourless | Pink |

## Chemical Properties of Bases

(a) Reaction with air

When exposed to air, alkalis combine with the carbon dioxide of the air, to form the respective carbonate.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\underset{\text { Sodium hydroxide }}{2 \mathrm{NaOH}(\mathrm{aq})}+\underset{\text { Carbon dioxide }}{\mathrm{CO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})} \longrightarrow \underset{\text { Sodium carbonate }}{\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{CO}_{3}(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l})} \\
\underset{\text { Water }}{2 \mathrm{KOH}(\mathrm{aq})}+\underset{\text { Potassium hydroxide }}{\mathrm{CO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})} \longrightarrow \underset{\text { Carbon dioxide }}{\mathrm{K}_{2} \mathrm{CO}_{3}(\mathrm{aq})}+\underset{\text { Potassium carbonate }}{\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l})}
\end{aligned}
$$

An application of an alkali reacting with carbon dioxide is the drying of lime i.e. white washing. The white washing of walls is due to the action of carbon dioxide of the air on calcium hydroxide. When milk of lime, a watery paste of calcium hydroxide, is applied on a wall, it reacts with the carbon dioxide of the air to form a white layer of calcium carbonate.

$$
\underset{\text { Calcium hydroxide }}{\mathrm{Ca}(\mathrm{OH})_{2}(\mathrm{aq})}+\underset{\text { Carbon dioxide }}{\mathrm{CO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})} \longrightarrow \underset{\text { Calcium carbonate }}{\mathrm{CaCO}_{3}(\mathrm{aq})}+\underset{\text { Water }}{\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l})}
$$

## (b) Reaction with ammonium salts

Alkalis combine with ammonium salts to liberate ammonia gas.
Alkali + Ammonium salt ⟶ Salt + Water + Ammonia

$$
\underset{\text { Calcium hydroxide }}{\mathrm{Ca}(\mathrm{OH})_{2}(\mathrm{aq})}+\underset{\text { Ammonium chloride }}{2 \mathrm{NH}_{4} \mathrm{Cl}(\mathrm{~g})} \longrightarrow \underset{\text { Calcium chloride }}{\mathrm{CaCl}_{2}(\mathrm{~s})}+\underset{\text { Water }}{2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l})}+\underset{\text { Ammonia }}{2 \mathrm{NH}_{3}(\mathrm{~g}) \uparrow}
$$

$$
\underset{\text { Sodium hydroxide }}{2 \mathrm{NaOH}(\mathrm{aq})}+\underset{\text { Ammonium sulphate }}{\left(\mathrm{NH}_{4}\right)_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}(\mathrm{aq})} \longrightarrow \underset{\text { Sodium sulphate }}{\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}(\mathrm{aq})}+\underset{\text { Water }}{2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l})}+\underset{\text { Ammonia }}{2 \mathrm{NH}_{3}(\mathrm{~g}) \uparrow}
$$

## Knowledge

- What will happen if we put aqueous solution of KOH and $\left(\mathrm{NH}_{4}\right)_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}$ in a thick glass tube and connect this tube with other glass vessel containing Nessler's reagent (as shown in figure below.)?
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-058.jpg?height=335&width=1137&top_left_y=1756&top_left_x=431)


## SOLUTION

Aqueous solution of KOH and $\left(\mathrm{NH}_{4}\right)_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}$ combines to form potassium sulphate with evolution of $\mathrm{NH}_{3}$.

$$
2 \mathrm{KOH}(\mathrm{aq})+\left(\mathrm{NH}_{4}\right)_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}(\mathrm{aq}) \longrightarrow \underset{\text { Potassiumsulphate }}{\mathrm{K}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}(\mathrm{aq})}+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l})+\underset{\text { Ammonia }}{2 \mathrm{NH}_{3}(\mathrm{~g})}
$$

This evolved ammonia combines with Nessler's reagent (alkaline solution of $\mathrm{K}_{2}\left[\mathrm{HgI}_{4}\right]$ ) and gives brown precipitate.

$$
2 \mathrm{~K}_{2} \mathrm{HgI}_{4}+\mathrm{NH}_{3}+3 \mathrm{KOH} \longrightarrow \underset{\text { Brown precipitate }}{\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{NHgOHgI}}+7 \mathrm{KI}+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}
$$

## (c) Reaction with acids

Bases combine with acids to form respective salt and water only.

$$
\begin{gathered}
\underset{\text { Sodium hydroxide }}{3 \mathrm{NaOH}(\mathrm{aq})}+\underset{\text { Phosphoric acid }}{\mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{PO}_{4}(\mathrm{aq})} \longrightarrow \underset{\text { Trisodium phosphate }}{\mathrm{Na}_{3} \mathrm{PO}_{4}(\mathrm{aq})}+\underset{\text { Water }}{3 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l})} \\
\underset{\text { Calcium hydroxide }}{\mathrm{Ca}(\mathrm{OH})_{2}(\mathrm{aq})}+\underset{\text { Acetic acid }}{2 \mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOH}(\mathrm{aq})} \longrightarrow \underset{\text { Calcium acetate }}{\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COO}\right)_{2} \mathrm{Ca}(\mathrm{aq})}+\underset{\text { Water }}{2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l})}
\end{gathered}
$$

A neutralization reaction is the reaction in which an acid and a base react to form salt and water. It involves the combination of $\mathrm{H}^{+}$ion and $\mathrm{OH}^{-}$ion to generate water. These reactions are known as neutralisation reaction.

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \text { Acid }+ \text { Base } \text { ⟶ Salt }+ \text { Water } \\
& \mathrm{HCl}+\mathrm{NaOH} \longrightarrow \mathrm{NaCl}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}
\end{aligned}
$$

Example :
Acid and base in the above reaction will ionize in the following way,

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathrm{HCl} & \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{H}^{+}+\mathrm{Cl}^{-} \\
\mathrm{NaOH} & \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{Na}^{+}+\mathrm{OH}^{-}
\end{aligned}
$$

Now, $\mathrm{H}^{+}$of acid and $\mathrm{OH}^{-}$of base will combine to form water $\left(\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}\right)$ and remaining ions will combine to form respective salt.

Non-metallic oxides also react in same manner, hence non-metallic oxides are acidic in nature.

$$
2 \mathrm{NaOH}+\mathrm{CO}_{2} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{CO}_{3}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}
$$

(d) Reaction with metal

Metals react with base with evolution of hydrogen gas.

$$
2 \mathrm{NaOH}+\mathrm{Zn} \longrightarrow \underset{\text { Sodium zincate }}{\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{ZnO}_{2}}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \uparrow
$$

## CONNECTING TOPIC

## CLASSIFICATION OF BASES

Bases can be classified in various ways, depending on the following factors:

## (1) Classification Based on the Strength of the Base

We have already studied that bases on dissociation produce hydroxyl ion ( $\mathrm{OH}^{-}$). The strength of a base depends on the concentration of the hydroxyl ion when it is dissolved in water. Based on this, bases are classified as,
(i) Strong base

A base that dissociates completely or almost completely in water is classified as a strong base. In other words, greater the tendency to loose hydroxyl ion in the solution, the stronger is the base.

Example: Sodium hydroxide : NaOH
Potassium hydroxide : KOH
Calcium hydroxide : $\mathrm{Ca}(\mathrm{OH})_{2}$
(ii) Weak base

A base that partially dissociates in water is classified as a weak base.
Example: Magnesium hydroxide : $\mathrm{Mg}(\mathrm{OH})_{2}$
Ammonium hydroxide : $\mathrm{NH}_{4} \mathrm{OH}$
CONNECTING TOPIC CONNECTING TOPIC CONNECTING TOPIC CONNECTING TOPIC CONNECTING TOPIC CONNECTING TOPIC

## Strontium hydroxide $\left(\mathbf{S r}(\mathbf{O H})_{\mathbf{2}}\right)$ is a strong or weak base?

## SOLUTION

$\mathrm{Sr}(\mathrm{OH})_{2}$ is a strong base as it ionises almost completely in water

## (2) Classification Based on the Concentration of a Base

Concentration of a basic solution depends upon the percentage of the base dissolved in water. Based on this fact, bases are classified as,
(i) Concentrated base : A base that has a relatively high percentage of the base in its aqueous solution is classified as a concentrated base. Example concentrated sodium hydroxide, potassium hydroxide, ammonium hydroxide etc.
(ii) Dilute base: A base that has a relatively low percentage of the base dissolved in its aqueous solution, is classified as a dilute base. Example dilute sodium hydroxide, potassium hydroxide, ammonium hydroxide etc.

## (3) Classification Based on the Acidity of a Base

The acidity of a base is equal to the number of hydroxyl ions it has. It is also the number of hydrogen ions with which a base can combine as one hydrogen ion combines with one hydroxyl ion.
Based on the acidity bases can be classified as,
(i) Monoacidic base

It is a base that has only one hydroxyl ion. It combines with only one hydrogen ion. Hence, its acidity will be one.
Example : Sodium hydroxide, Potassium hydroxide: KOH , Lithium hydroxide: LiOH , Ammonium hydroxide: $\mathrm{NH}_{4} \mathrm{OH}$.

$$
\underset{\text { Sodium hydroxide }}{\mathrm{NaOH}(\mathrm{aq})}+\underset{\text { Hydrochloric acid }}{\mathrm{HCl}(\mathrm{aq})} \longrightarrow \underset{\text { Sodium chloride }}{\mathrm{NaCl}(\mathrm{aq})}+\underset{\text { Water }}{\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l})}
$$

## (ii) Diacidic base

It is a base that has two hydroxyl groups and combines with two hydrogen ions. Hence, its acidity will be two.
Example : Iron (II) hydroxide: $\mathrm{Fe}(\mathrm{OH})_{2}$, Magnesium hydroxide: $\mathrm{Mg}(\mathrm{OH})_{2}$, Copper hydroxide: $\mathrm{Cu}(\mathrm{OH})_{2}$, Zinc hydroxide : $\mathrm{Zn}(\mathrm{OH})_{2}$, Lead hydroxide: $\mathrm{Pb}(\mathrm{OH})_{2}$.

$$
\underset{\text { Calcium hydroxide }}{\mathrm{Ca}(\mathrm{OH})_{2}(\mathrm{aq})}+\underset{\text { Hydrochloric acid }}{2 \mathrm{HCl}(\mathrm{aq})} \longrightarrow \underset{\text { Calcium chloride }}{\mathrm{CaCl}_{2}(\mathrm{aq})}+\underset{\text { Water }}{2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l})}
$$

## (iii) Triacidic base

A base that has three hydroxyl ions and can combines with three hydrogen ions. Hence, its acidity will be three.
Example: Aluminium hydroxide

$$
\underset{\text { Aluminium hydroxide }}{\mathrm{Al}(\mathrm{OH})_{3}(\mathrm{aq})}+\underset{\text { Hydrochloric acid }}{3 \mathrm{HCl}(\mathrm{aq})} \longrightarrow \underset{\text { Aluminium chloride }}{\mathrm{AlCl}_{3}(\mathrm{aq})}+\underset{\text { Water }}{3 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l})}
$$

## - - Time to Check Your Knowledge <br> - On the basis of acidity of bases, barium hydroxide is which type of base?

## SOLUTION

We know that acidity of a base is equal to number of hydroxyl ions. Thus, barium hydroxide $\mathrm{Ba}(\mathrm{OH})_{2}$ having two hydroxyl group is a diacidic base.

$$
\mathrm{Ba}(\mathrm{OH})_{2}(\mathrm{aq}) \longrightarrow \mathrm{Ba}^{2+}(\mathrm{aq})+2 \mathrm{OH}^{-}(\mathrm{aq})
$$

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-061.jpg?height=113&width=204&top_left_y=360&top_left_x=251)
Did You Know!

## Acid or a Base in a Water Solution:

The process of dissolving an acid or a base in water is a highly exothermic one. Care must be taken while mixing concentrated nitric acid or sulphuric acid with water. The acid must always be added slowly to water with constant stirring. If water is added to a concentrated acid, the heat generated may cause the mixture to splash out and cause burns. The glass container may also break due to excessive local heating. Mixing an acid or base with water results in decrease in the concentration of ions $\left(\mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{O}^{+} / \mathrm{OH}^{-}\right)$per unit volume. Such a process is called dilution and the acid or the base is said to be diluted.

Learn More

## Indicators are substances which undergoes change in colour, smell or taste with respect to the change in medium. It helps to differentiate between acidic and basic medium. Indicators are of following types depending upon its source:

- Natural indicators can be extracted from a wide variety offlowers, fruits, roots, leaves and other parts of plants, e.g. tea leaves, beet roots, rose petals of various colours, red cabbage leaves, turmeric, black berries, and delphinium flowers. Litmus (a natural dye) is one of the most common and naturally available indicator. It is extracted from lichens (combination of algae and fungus). When this litmus is added to an acidic solution, it turns red and when added to a basic solution, it turns blue. It possess a purple colour in distilled water.
- Synthetic indicators are prepared from man made chemical substances like phenolphtalein, methyl orange etc.
Phenolphthalein is a synthetic indicator. It turns acidic solutions colorless and basic solutions pink. If the concentration of indicator is particularly strong, it can appear purple.
Methyl orange is also a synthetic indicator used more oftenly because of clear colour change. In a solution becoming less acidic, methyl orange moves from red to orange and finally to yellow with the reverse occuring for a solution increasing in acidity.
Depending upon the change in properties indicators are classified as:
- Halochromic indicator show change in color with respect to the change in medium, e.g: litmus, phenolphtalein, methyl orange etc. These indicators are generally used as acid-base indicators.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-061.jpg?height=545&width=456&top_left_y=1235&top_left_x=1420)
- Olfactory indicator causes change in odour/smell with respect to the change in medium. e.g: clove oil, onion, vanilla extract etc.
- Gustatory indicator causes change in taste with respect to the change in medium. e.g: Sour taste of milk indicates that the milk has became acidic, hence it is spoiled.
Universal indicators : It is a mixture of indicators often sold ready made solution which can indicate pH values, usually over a range of 3-11, by successive changes of colour
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-061.jpg?height=149&width=420&top_left_y=2060&top_left_x=264)

Take 100 mL beaker and fill it upto 80\% with distilled water in your school laboratory. First add few drops of phenolphthalein solution and stir solution with glass rod properly then record your observation if there is any change. Now add few drops of ammonium hydroxide and stir solution with glass rod properly then again record your observation if there is any change. Then add few drops of vinegar solution and stir resulting solution properly with glass rod. Record change if any. Now in case you are not able to understand the reason of your recorded observations, consult your friend and discuss with your teacher.

## CONNECTING TOPIC

## THEORIES OF ACIDS AND BASES

## Arrhenius Concept of Acids and Bases

Svante Arrhenius in 1887, stated that acid ionizes in aqueous solution to produce hydrogen ions (which are protons), $\mathrm{H}^{+}$, and anions; and a base ionizes in aqueous solution to produce hydroxide ions $\left(\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right)$and cations. Later studies of aqueous solutions provided evidence of a small, positively charged hydrogen ion combining with a water molecule to form a hydrated proton, $\mathrm{H}^{+}\left(\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}\right)$ or $\mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{O}^{+}$, which is called the hydronium ion.

$$
\mathrm{H}^{+}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \longrightarrow \mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{O}^{+}
$$

Often, the hydronium ion or hydrated proton is represented as $\mathrm{H}^{+}(\mathrm{aq})$. Hydrogen chloride $(\mathrm{HCl})$, a gas, is an acid because it dissolves in water to yield hydrogen ions and chloride ions. This aqueous solution of HCl is referred to as hydrochloric acid.

$$
\mathrm{HCl}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \longrightarrow \mathrm{H}^{+}(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{Cl}^{-}(\mathrm{aq})
$$

## Problems with Arrhenius theory :

(i) The solvent has no role to play in Arrhenius theory. An acid is expected to be an acid in any solvent. This was found not to be the case. For example, HCl is an acid in water, behaving in the manner Arrhenius expected. However, if HCl is dissolved in benzene, there is no dissociation, the HCl remaining as undissociated molecules. The nature of the solvent plays a critical role in acid-base properties of substances.
(ii) All salts in Arrhenius theory should produce solutions that are neither acidic nor basic. This is not the case. If equal amounts of HCl and ammonia react, the solution is slightly acidic. If equal amounts of acetic acid and sodium hydroxide are reacted, the resulting solution is basic. Arrhenius had no explanation for this.
(iii) The need for hydroxide as the base led Arrhenius to propose the formula $\mathrm{NH}_{4} \mathrm{OH}$ as the formula for ammonia in water. This led to the misconception that $\mathrm{NH}_{4} \mathrm{OH}$ is the actual base, not $\mathrm{NH}_{3}$.
The Arrhenius theory of acids and bases will be fully supplanted by the theory proposed independently by Johnnies Bronsted and Thomas Lowry in 1923.

## Bronsted Lowry Theory of Acids and Bases

By the definition of Thomas Lowry and J.N. Bronsted working independently in 1923, "an acid is a material that donates a proton and a base is a material that can accept a proton".
Consider the example of ionization of hydrochloric acid in water. $\mathrm{HCl}(\mathrm{aq})$ acts as an acid by donating a proton to $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ molecule which acts as a base.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-062.jpg?height=273&width=776&top_left_y=1763&top_left_x=680)

Here, HCl act as an acid, because it has a proton available to be transferred.
$\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ act as a base, since it gets the proton that the acid lost.
Now, here comes an interesting data on considering the reverse reaction
$\mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{O}^{+}$- is an acid, because it can give a proton.
$\mathrm{Cl}^{-}$- is a base, since it has the capacity to receive a proton.
Notice that each pair, HCl and $\mathrm{Cl}^{-}$as well as $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ and $\mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{O}^{+}$, differ by one proton ( symbol $=\mathrm{H}^{+}$). These pairs are called conjugate pairs.

$$
\mathrm{HNO}_{3}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{O}^{+}+\mathrm{NO}_{3}^{-}
$$

The acids are $\mathrm{HNO}_{3}$ and $\mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{O}^{+}$and the bases are $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ and $\mathrm{NO}_{3}^{-}$.
Each conjugate pair has a proton donor and a proton acceptor. Acids are paired with bases. Each acid has, a proton available (an ionizable hydrogen) and conjugate base part. The acid ionizes releasing $\mathrm{H}^{+}$ion, whereas rest part of the original acid forms the conjugate base.

Nitric acid, $\mathrm{HNO}_{3}$, dissociates (splits) into a hydrogen ion and a nitrate ion. The hydrogen almost immediately joins to a water molecule to make a hydronium ion. The nitrate ion is the conjugate base of the $\mathrm{HNO}_{3}$ ion. In the second part of the reaction, water is a base (because it can accept a proton) and the hydronium ion is its conjugate acid.

$$
\underset{\text { Acid }}{\mathrm{HNO}_{3}+}+\underset{\text { Base }}{\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}} \longrightarrow \underset{\text { Conjugate base Conjugate acid }}{\mathrm{NO}_{3}^{-}}+\mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{O}^{+} ~
$$

## Problems with the theory

This theory works very nicely in all protic solvents (water, ammonia, acetic acid, etc.), but fails to explain acid base behavior in aprotic solvents such as benzene and dioxane. It also cannot explain reaction between acidic oxides ( $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$, $\mathrm{SO}_{2}$ ) and basic oxides ( $\mathrm{CaO}, \mathrm{MgO}$ etc).

$$
\mathrm{CaO}+\mathrm{SO}_{3} \longrightarrow \mathrm{CaSO}_{4}
$$

## ILLUSTRATION : 3

The following reactions occur in aqueous solution. Predict the products and identify the acids and bases and their conjugate species in the reaction of -
(a) $\mathrm{NH}_{3}$ with $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOH}$
(b) $\mathrm{N}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5}^{+}$with $\mathrm{CO}_{3}^{2-}$
(c) $\mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{O}^{+}$with $\mathrm{OH}^{-}$
(d) $\mathrm{HSO}_{4}^{-}$with $\mathrm{HCOO}^{-}$

## SOLUTION :

(a) $\underset{\text { Base }}{\mathrm{NH}_{3}}+\underset{\text { Acid }}{\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOH}} \longrightarrow \underset{\substack{\text { Conjugate } \\ \text { Acid }}}{\mathrm{NH}_{4}^{+}}+\underset{\substack{\text { Conjugate } \\ \text { Base }}}{\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COO}^{-}}$
(b) $\quad \underset{\text { Acid }}{\mathrm{N}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5}^{+}}+\underset{\text { Base }}{\mathrm{CO}_{3}^{2-}} \longrightarrow \underset{\substack{\text { Conjugate } \\ \text { Base }}}{\mathrm{N}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{4}}+\underset{\substack{\text { Conjugate } \\ \text { Acid }}}{\mathrm{HCO}_{3}^{-}}$
(c) $\underset{\text { Acid }}{\mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{O}^{+}}+\underset{\text { Base }}{\mathrm{OH}^{-}} \longrightarrow \underset{\substack{\text { Conjugate } \\ \text { Acid }}}{\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}}+\underset{\substack{\text { Conjugate } \\ \text { Base }}}{\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}}$
(d) $\underset{\text { Acid }}{\mathrm{HSO}_{4}^{-}}+\underset{\text { Base }}{\mathrm{HCOO}^{-}} \longrightarrow \underset{\substack{\text { Conjugate } \\ \text { Base }}}{\mathrm{SO}_{4}^{2-}}+\underset{\substack{\text { Conjugate } \\ \text { Acid }}}{\mathrm{HCOOH}}$

## Lewis Theory of Acids and Bases

In 1923, G.N. Lewis introduced another theory of acids and bases which could successfully explain the cases where there is no role for protons.
The G.N. Lewis idea of acids and bases is broader than the Bronsted Lowry model. According to Lewis, acids are electron pair acceptors and bases are electron pair donors.

## Postulates :

(i) An acid is a substance which can act as an electron pair acceptor. The molecule or ion should be associated with shortage of electrons to act as an acid.
Example : $\mathrm{BF}_{3}, \mathrm{H}^{+}$etc.
(ii) A base is a substance which can act as an electron pair donor. The molecule or ion should be associated with one or more lone pairs of electrons to act as a base.
Example : $\mathrm{NH}_{3}, \mathrm{OH}^{-}$etc.
(iii) Acid base neutralization can be defined as the transfer of an electron pair from a base to an acid.

$$
\mathrm{H}^{+}+: \mathrm{NH}_{3} \longrightarrow\left[\mathrm{H}^{+} \leftarrow: \mathrm{NH}_{3}\right]^{+}
$$

## Merits :

(i) This theory could explain even the acidic nature of those substances which do not contain hydrogen.
(ii) It could explain the neutralization reactions which do not involve any transfer of proton.

## Limitations:

(i) This theory failed to explain the acidic and basic nature of general acids and bases.
(ii) This theory could not explain the general neutralization reactions involving the formation of salt and water.
(iii) Generally, the acid-base reactions are fast reactions. But the formation of a coordinate bond is a slow process.
(iv) This theory cannot explain the relative strength of acids and bases.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-064.jpg?height=1374&width=36&top_left_y=420&top_left_x=193)

## Learn

## More

- Substance which are capable of donating as well accepting proton are amphoteric substance. For example $\mathbf{H}_{\mathbf{2}} \mathbf{O}, \mathbf{H C O}_{\mathbf{3}}^{-}, \mathbf{H S O}_{\mathbf{4}}^{-}$etc.
- Remember that an acid-base reaction is a competition between two bases for a proton. If the stronger of the two acids and the stronger of the two bases are reactants (appear on the left side of the equation), the reaction is said to proceed to a large extent.
- Amphiprotic solvents : These solvents influence the acid-base characteristics of solute i.e., they possess the tendency to accept proton (protophilic solvents e.g., $\mathbf{H}_{\mathbf{2}} \mathbf{O}, \mathbf{C}_{\mathbf{2}} \mathbf{H}_{\mathbf{5}} \mathbf{O H}$, liq. $\mathbf{N H}_{\mathbf{3}}$ etc.) or they posses the tendency to give protons (protogenic solvents e.g., $\mathbf{H}_{\mathbf{2}} \mathbf{O}$, liq. $\mathbf{H C l}, \mathbf{C H}_{\mathbf{3}} \mathbf{C O O H}$ etc.)
- Aprotic solvents : Solvents which do not influence the acid-base characteristic of solute are called aprotic solvents. In such systems acid-base equilibrium is established only by the introduction of second acid or base. In other words aprotic solvents are those which neither donate nor accept the proton e.g., $\mathbf{C C l}_{\mathbf{4}}, \mathbf{C}_{\mathbf{6}} \mathbf{H}_{\mathbf{6}}$, liquid $\mathbf{S O}_{\mathbf{2}}$, etc.
- The species: $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}, \mathrm{HCO}_{3}^{-}, \mathrm{HSO}_{4}^{-}$and $\mathrm{NH}_{3}$ can act both as Bronsted acids and bases. For each case give the corresponding conjugate acid and conjugate base.


## SOLUTION

The answer is given in the following Table:

| Species | Conjugate acid | Conjugate base |
| :--- | :---: | :---: |
| $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ | $\mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{O}^{+}$ | $\mathrm{OH}^{-}$ |
| $\mathrm{HCO}_{3}^{-}$ | $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{CO}_{3}$ | $\mathrm{CO}_{3}^{2-}$ |
| $\mathrm{HSO}_{4}^{-}$ | $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}$ | $\mathrm{SO}_{4}^{2-}$ |
| $\mathrm{NH}_{3}^{-}$ | $\mathrm{NH}_{4}^{+}$ | $\mathrm{NH}_{2}^{-}$ |

- Aluminium chloride $\left(\mathrm{AlCl}_{3}\right)$ behaves like which substance?


## SOLUTION

$\mathrm{AlCl}_{3}$ behaves like a Lewis acid because central aluminium atom being electron deficient can accept a lone pair of electron.

The three different theories of acids and bases explain the nature of various acids and bases in different perceptions. However, none of the theories could convincingly explain all the properties of acids and bases and hence cannot be universally applicable. Depending on the nature and conditions of reaction, an appropriate theory is made applicable. Thus all the three theories put together can cover the entire range of acids and bases.

According to both Arrhenius and Bronsted, acid is a substance which can give $\mathrm{H}^{+}$ions. Hence, all Arrhenius acids are also Bronsted acids. However, there is a difference in the definition of base. According to Arrhenius, a base has to be $\mathrm{OH}^{-}$ion donor while according to Bronsted, base is one which accepts proton. Thus this means NaOH is base according to Arrhenius theory but not according to Bronsted theory.

Bronsted bases are proton acceptor. While Lewis bases are electron donors; they generally contain one or more lone pair of electrons and therefore, they can also accept a proton. Thus, all Lewis bases are also Bronsted bases. Whereas Bronsted acids can donate a proton but they are not capable of accepting a pair of electrons. Thus, all Bronsted acids are not Lewis acids..

## THE PH SCALE

So far, we have explained the strength of an acid or base in terms of ion concentrations. We have used qualitative terms like "concentrated", "dilute", "strong", and "weak". To improve accuracy we prefer to use quantitative terms. Instead of descriptive terms which could be misinterpreted, we use a number scale called the pH scale.

The term p in pH stands for "potenz" in German; meaning power and is the amount of hydrogen ions in a particular solution. The more ions, the more acidic, the solution is the fewer ions, the more alkaline (basic) the solution is.
The pH scale is used to rank solutions in terms of acidity or basicity (alkalinity). Since the scale is based on pH values, as the value of pH increases, concentration of $\mathrm{H}^{+}$ions decreases. The pH scale is often said to range from 0 to 14 . Anything below 7.0 is acidic, and has higher concentration of hydrogen ion $\left(\mathrm{H}^{+}\right)$in the solution; whereas anything above 7.0 is alkaline, or basic and has higher concentration of hydroxide ion $\left(\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right)$. The pH of a neutral solution is 7 .
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-065.jpg?height=636&width=832&top_left_y=902&top_left_x=628)

| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Hydrochloric acid | 1.0 | Bread | 5.5 |
| Sulphuric acid | 1.2 | Potatoes | 5.8 |
| Gastric juice | 2.0 | Rain water | 6.2 |
| Lemon | 2.3 | Milk | 6.6 |
| Vinegar (Acetic acid) | 2.8 | Pure water | 7.0 |
| Soft drink | 3.0 | Egg | 7.8 |
| Apple | 3.1 | Sea water | 8.5 |
| Grape | 3.1 | Ammonium hydroxide | 11.1 |
| Tomato | 4.2 | Sodium hydroxide | 13.0 |
| Banana | 4.6 |  |  |
| Battery acid, 1M sulphuric acid | 0 | Stomach acid, 0.1 M hydrochloric acid | 1 |
| Milk of magnesia, $\mathrm{Mg}(\mathrm{OH})_{2}$ | 8 | Washing soda, $\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{CO}_{3}$ | 9 |
| Milk of magnesia, $\mathrm{Mg}(\mathrm{OH})_{2}$ | 10 | Aqueous household ammonia, $\mathrm{NH}_{3}$ | 11 |
| Lime water, $\mathrm{Ca}(\mathrm{OH})_{2}$ | 12 | Drano, 0.1 M NaOH | 13 |
| Drano, 1.0 M NaOH | 14 |  |  |

## ILLUSTRATION : 4

Five solutions A,B,C,D and E when tested with universal indicator showed $\mathbf{p H}$ as $\mathbf{4 , 1 , 1 1 , 7}$ and 9, respectively. Find the solution which is,
(a) neutral
(b) strongly alkaline
(c) strongly acidic
(d) weakly acidic
(e) weakly alkaline
and arrange the $\mathbf{p H}$ in increasing order of hydrogen-ion concentration.

## SOLUTION :

(a) 'D' with $\mathrm{pH}=7$ is neutral
(b) ' C ' with $\mathrm{pH}=11$ is strongly alkaline
(c) ' B ' with $\mathrm{pH}=1$ is strongly acidic
(d) 'A' with $\mathrm{pH}=4$ is weakly acidic
(e) ' E ' with $\mathrm{pH}=9$ is weakly alkaline

And $\mathrm{C}<\mathrm{E}<\mathrm{D}<\mathrm{A}<\mathrm{B}$ is increasing order of $\mathrm{H}^{+}$ion concentration.

## CONNECTING TOPIC

## CALCULATION OF pH VALUE

Sorenson defined pH as the negative logarithm (to the base 10) of the hydrogen ion concentration expressed in moles per litre,

$$
\mathrm{pH}=-\log \left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]
$$

where $\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]$is the hydrogen ion concentration in moles/litre.
Remember that sometimes $\mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{O}^{+}$is written, so $\mathrm{pH}=-\log \left[\mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{O}^{+}\right]$means the same thing. The pH scale runs from 0 to 14 . For acids, $\mathrm{pH}<7$; for bases, $\mathrm{pH}>7$; for neutral substances, $\mathrm{pH}=7$.
The negative logarithm is used because most of the pH values are positive numbers.
A change of one pH unit represents a ten-fold change in the concentration of hydronium and hydroxide ions. Thus, pH equal to 2 is ten times as acidic as pH equal to 3 , one hundred times as acidic as pH equal to 4 , one thousand times as acidic as pH equal to 5 , and so on. Similarly, pH equal to 13 is ten times as basic as pH equal to 12 and so on.
Example: The $\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]$in a solution is measured to be 0.010 M . What is the pH ?
The solution is pretty straightforward. Plug the [ $\mathrm{H}^{+}$] value into the pH definition:
An alternate way to write this is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{pH}=-\log 0.010 \\
& \mathrm{pH}=-\log 10^{-2} \\
& \mathrm{pH}=-(-2)=2
\end{aligned}
$$

Since the $\log$ of $10^{-2}$ is -2 , we have :
pH equal to 2 represents that solution is acidic.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-066.jpg?height=150&width=350&top_left_y=1546&top_left_x=251)

If concentration of hydrogen ion in a solution is 0.0015 . Find whether the solution is acidic or basic.
[Given: $\log 0.0015=-2.82$ ]

## SOLUTION

As we know that pH is defined as the negative logarithm of the hydrogen ion concentation present in a solution
$\mathrm{pH}=-\log \left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]$
$\mathrm{pH}=-\log 0.0015$
$\mathrm{pH}=-(-2.82)$
$\mathrm{pH}=2.82$
Since the value of pH is less than 7 . Thus, solution is acidic.

## THE pOH VALUE

Sorenson defined pH as the negative logarithm of the hydrogen ion concentration,
$\mathrm{pH}=-\log \left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]$
So, the pOH can be defined in a similar way: $\mathrm{pOH}=-\log \left[\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right]$
In words, the pOH is the negative logarithm of the hydroxide ion concentration.
So let's try a simple problem: The $\left[\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right]$in a solution is measured to be 0.0010 M . What is the pOH ?
The solution is pretty straightforward. Plug the [ $\mathrm{OH}^{-}$] into the pOH definition:
$\mathrm{pOH}=-\log 0.0010$
An alternate way to write this is: $\mathrm{pOH}=-\log 10^{-3}$
Since the $\log$ of $10^{-3}$ is -3 , we have: $\mathrm{pOH}=-(-3)$
pOH equal to 3 represents that solution is basic.

## CASE STUDY-2 : Distinguishing Arrhenius, Bronsted Lowry and Lewis Theory

CASE - I : We dissolved a compound, which does not contains hydrogen ion $\boldsymbol{H}^{+}$in basic solution but still neutralization reaction takes place. Why?

A compound can be an acid if it can accept electron even though it does not have ionisable hydrogen ion,according to Lewis concept. Hence, the dissolved compound must be lewis acid.

CASE - II : Now, we dissolved a compound in water which does not have hydroxyl ion in its formula, but it produced basic solution. Why?

Only compounds which produces hydroxyl ion are base, is the concept of Arrhenius. But, according to Bronsted and Lowry, the compounds which can accept a proton, whether it has hydroxyl ion or not, are bases. And according to Lewis, all compounds which can donate electron, are bases.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-067.jpg?height=609&width=717&top_left_y=1112&top_left_x=381)
CASE - III : A teacher gave a solution to a student referring it as levis acid, but the student noticed that the acid given to him was $\mathbf{H C l}$ and he recalled that compounds which has $\mathbf{H}^{+}$ion are Arrhenius acids. Then why did the teacher called it a lewis acid?
Lewis concept for acids is broader concept than Bronsted and Lowry Concept and Arrhenius Concept. All Arrhenius acids are Lewis acid as well as Bronsted acid, but all Lewis acids are not Bronsted or Arrhenius acid. This can be better understood by the given venn diagram.

CASE - IV : Acids and bases reacts to form an adduct. Why?

According to Lewis concept, acids are electron pair acceptor and bases are electron pair donor.
e.g. $\mathrm{BF}_{3}$ (acid) when reacted with $\mathrm{NH}_{3}$ (base), they donot undergo general acid base reaction, instead they slowly forms an adduct $\left(\mathrm{BF}_{3} \leftarrow: \mathrm{NH}_{3}\right)$.

CASE - $\mathbf{V}$ : To determine the strength of an acid or base.

Strong acids should easily give $\mathrm{H}^{+}$ion in aqueous solution and strong bases should easily give $\mathrm{OH}^{-}$ion in aqueous solution. Therefore, these theories can be used to determine the strength of acid containing $\mathrm{H}^{+}$ and bases containing $\mathrm{OH}^{-}$(i.e., alkali).
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-067.jpg?height=497&width=301&top_left_y=386&top_left_x=1150)
(a) $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}+2 \mathrm{NaOH} \rightarrow \mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$
(b) $\quad \mathrm{NH}_{3}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \rightarrow \mathrm{NH}_{4} \mathrm{OH}$
(c) $\quad \mathrm{Al}^{3+}+3 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \rightarrow\left[\mathrm{Al}\left(\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}\right)_{6}\right]^{3+}$

| JIdOL JNIIJヨNNOO | JIdOI SNIIJヨNNOS | JIdOI SNIIJヨNNOO | JIdOL SNIIJヨNNOO | JIdOL JNIIJヨNNOO | JIdOL JNIIJヨNNOO |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |

## CONNECTING TOPIC

## IONIC PRODUCT OF WATER（ $\mathbf{K}_{\mathbf{W}}$ ）

Ionic product of water $\left(\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{w}}\right)$ ，is the product of concentration of $\mathrm{H}^{+}$ion and $\mathrm{OH}^{-}$ion．

$$
\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{w}}=\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]\left[\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right]
$$

The value of $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{w}}$ is temperature dependent since it is an equilibrium constant．
Water is able to act both as acid and base．Water molecules in pure water can exist in both acidic and basic form which are equal in number and thus making it neutral．One $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ molecule donates proton and act as an acid and another $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ molecule accepts a proton and act as a base at the same time．This process can be better understood by the following reaction，

$$
\underset{\text { Acid }}{\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l})}+\underset{\text { Base }}{\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l})} \rightleftharpoons \underset{\substack{\text { Conjugate } \\ \text { Acid }}}{\mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{O}^{+}(\mathrm{aq})}+\underset{\substack{\text { Conjugate } \\ \text { Base }}}{\mathrm{OH}^{-}(\mathrm{aq})}
$$

Since pure water is neutral， pH of water $=7$ or $\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]=10^{-7}\left[\because \mathrm{pH}=-\log \left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]\right.$）
We know that，concentration of $\mathrm{H}^{+}$ion and $\mathrm{OH}^{-}$ion is equal，in pure water．

$$
\therefore \quad\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]=\left[\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right]=10^{-7}
$$

Taking negative log equation（i），

$$
\begin{aligned}
& -\log \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{w}}=-\log \left\{\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]\left[\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right]\right\} \\
& -\log \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{w}}=-\log \left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]-\log \left[\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

Putting the values of $\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]$and $\left[\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right]$from equation（ii）

$$
-\log \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{w}}=-\log 10^{-14}=14
$$

Equation（iii）can also be written as，

$$
\begin{array}{cc} 
& \mathrm{pK}_{\mathrm{w}}=\mathrm{pH}+\mathrm{pOH} \\
\therefore & \mathrm{pK}_{\mathrm{w}}=\mathrm{pH}+\mathrm{pOH}=14
\end{array}
$$

## ILLUSTRATION ： 5

## SOLUTION ：

$\mathrm{HCIO}_{4}$ is a strong acid，which means that it is $100 \%$ ionized in solution．Therefore，$\left[\mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{O}^{+}\right]$is equal to the original $\mathrm{HCIO}_{4}$ concentration．In this case，it is necessary to use a log table to determine the pH since the number is not an integral power of ten． Using a log table，the number is divided into two parts．

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{pH}=-\log \left(1.5 \times 10^{-2}\right)=-\log 1.5-\log 10^{-2}=-0.18+2.00=1.82 \\
& \mathrm{pOH}=14.00-1.82=12.18
\end{aligned}
$$

Since pure water is neutral， pH of
