ILLUSTRATIOM ：
－If concentration of hydroxide ion in a solution is $1 \times 10^{-12}$ ．Whether the solution is acidic or basic？

## SOLUTION

As we know that
$\mathrm{pOH}=-\log \left[\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right]$
$\mathrm{pOH}=-\log 1 \times 10^{-12}=12 \because \mathrm{pH}+\mathrm{pOH}=14$
$\therefore \mathrm{pH}=14-\mathrm{pOH}=14-12=2$
Hence the solution is strongly acidic
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-069.jpg?height=157&width=426&top_left_y=360&top_left_x=258)

Collect few routine household fruits like lemon, oranges, grapes, apple, papaya and vinegar. Make juice of every fruit seperately and then measure the pH of each juice separately by using pH papers or you can also get your pH measurements more accurately by using pH meter, in case if it is available. Now record your observations and arrange the different fruit juices including vinegar solution in the order of increasing pH. Also find out which acid is majorly present in those fruits. You can take help of internet, science magazines etc. You can also discuss with your friends and teacher.
Precaution: Use separate vessel for different fruit juices, otherwise it may lead to contamination which could effect result.

## IMPORTANCE OF PH IN EVERYDAY LIFE

## Blood pH

In order to function properly, the human body needs to maintain a blood pH between 7.35 and 7.45. Large deviation from this range is extremely dangerous. Values greater than 7.8 or less than 6.8 often results in death. Any condition in which the blood pH drops below 7.35 is known as acidosis. If the pH rises above 7.45 , it is known as alkalosis.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-069.jpg?height=240&width=990&top_left_y=1069&top_left_x=573)

## Acid Rain

When pH of rain water is less than 5.6, it is called acid rain. When acid rain flows into the rivers, it lowers the pH of the river water. The survival of aquatic life in such rivers becomes difficult.

## pH of the Soil

Plants require a specific pH range for their healthy growth. The effect of soil pH has a significant role in the solubility of minerals or nutrients. Before a nutrient can be used by plants it must be dissolved in the soil solution. Most minerals and nutrients are more soluble or available in acidic soils than in neutral or slightly alkaline soils. Phosphorus is not readily soluble in the soil but is most available in soil with a pH range around 6.5. Strongly acidic soils ( $\mathrm{pH} 4.0-5.0$ ) can have high concentrations of soluble aluminum, iron and manganese which may be toxic to the growth of some plants.
The soil pH can also influence plant growth by its effect on activity of beneficial microorganisms. Bacteria that decompose soil organic matter are as not found in strong acid soils. This prevents organic matter from breaking down, resulting in an accumulation of organic matter and the tie up of nutrients, particularly nitrogen, that are held in the organic matter.

## pH in Our Digestive System

It is very interesting to note that our stomach produces hydrochloric acid which helps in the digestion of food without harming the stomach. During indigestion the stomach produces too much acid which causes pain and irritation. To get rid of this pain, people uses bases called antacids. These antacids neutralise the excess acid.
Magnesium hydroxide (milk of magnesia), a mild base, is often used for this purpose.

## pH Change as the Cause of Tooth Decay

Tooth decay starts when the pH of the mouth is lower than 5.5. Tooth enamel, made up of calcium phosphate is the hardest substance in the body. Bacteria present in the mouth produces acids by degradation of sugar and food particles remaining in the mouth after eating. The best way to prevent this is to clean the mouth after eating food. Using toothpastes, which are generally basic, for cleaning the teeth can neutralise the excess acid and prevent tooth decay.

## Self Defence by Animals and Plants Through Chemical Warfare

When a honey bee or ant stings, they injects acid which causes pain and irrigation. In the similar way, stinging hair of nettle leaves cause pain when touched due to secretion of methanoic acid. Some naturally occuring acids are:

| Natural Source | Acid | Natural source | Acid |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Vinegar | Acetic acid | Sour milk (Curd) | Lactic acid |
| Orange | Citric acid | Lemon | Citric acid |
| Tamarid | Tartaric acid | Ant sting | Methanoic acid |
| Tomato | Oxalic acid | Nettle sting | Methanoic acid |

## SALT

Compounds formed by the neutralization reaction between an acid and a base are known as a salts.

## Acid + Base → Salt + Water

Salts are ionic compounds which dissociate to yield a positively charged ion other than hydrogen [ $\mathrm{H}^{+}$] and negatively charged ion other than hydroxide ion [ $\mathrm{OH}^{-}$]. During salt formation, positive ion comes from base and negative ion comes from acid.

## Example:

The most familiar salt is sodium chloride, the principal component of common table salt. Sodium chloride, NaCl , and water, $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$, are formed by neutralization of sodium hydroxide, NaOH (a base), with hydrogen chloride, HCl (an acid):

$$
\mathrm{HCl}+\mathrm{NaOH} \longrightarrow \mathrm{NaCl}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}
$$

In sodium chloride $(\mathrm{NaCl})$, formation of positively charged ion, (sodium $\mathrm{Na}^{+}$) occurs from sodium hydroxide and negatively charged ion, chloride ( $\mathrm{Cl}^{-}$) occurs from hydrochloric acid.
Note: Although best known salt is sodium chloride which is commonly known as common salt. It is important to know that salt is a general name and it does not refer only to sodium chloride. In fact sodium chloride is just one member of a huge family of salts. Some other examples of salts are magnesium sulphate, potassium nitrate, calcium carbonate etc.

## Family of Salts

The salts having same positive ions or same negative ions are said to belong to a family of salts. For example calcium chloride and calcium sulphate belong to same family of salts 'calcium salts' because they both contain the same positive ion, calcium ion. Similarly calcium chloride and sodium chloride belong to same family of salts 'chloride salts' as both contain the same negative ion, chloride ion.

A salt is a compound consisting of positive metallic ion and negative ion derived from an acid, if negative ions are capable of further ionization to yield $\mathrm{H}^{+}$the salt is an acid salt, but if not, the salt is a normal salt.

## CONNECTING TOPIC

## CLASSIFICATION OF SALTS

## (i) Acidic Salts

Salt which produces acidic solution when added to water is acidic salt. The pH of the acidic salt solution is less than 7 . If a polybasic acid (Example, $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}, \mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{PO}_{4}, \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{3}$ etc.) is neutralised partly by a base, the salt formed is acidic. In other words such salts are produced by the replacement of only a part of acidic hydrogen of the polybasic acids by a metal. Thus the anions of these salts retain one or more hydrogen atom of the parent acid. Acid salt exhibits all the properties of an acid in solution.

## Acidic Salts :

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{CO}_{3}+\mathrm{NaOH} & \longrightarrow \mathrm{NaHCO}_{3}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \\
\mathrm{NaHSO}_{3}(\text { Sodium bisulphite }) & ; \quad \mathrm{KHSO}_{3}(\text { Potassium bisulphite }) \\
\mathrm{NaHSO}_{4}(\text { Sodium bisulphate }) & ; \quad \mathrm{KHSO}_{4}(\text { Potassium bisulphate })
\end{aligned}
$$

## (ii) Normal Salts

When acid and base neutralise each other completely, the salt formed is a normal salt. The pH of the salt solution of normal salt is 7 . Normal salts are produced by the replacement of all replaceable hydrogen(s) present in the molecule of an acid by metal or $\mathrm{NH}_{4}{ }^{+}$group. Thus such salts contain neither replaceable hydrogen nor hydroxyl group in the formula.

$$
\underset{\text { (Carbonic acid) }}{\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{CO}_{3}}+2 \mathrm{NaOH} \longrightarrow \underset{\substack{\text { Sodium Carbonate } \\ \text { (normal salt) }}}{\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{CO}_{3}}+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}
$$

Aqueous solution of normal salts may be neutral, acid or alkaline depending upon the nature of the constituent acid and base.
Normal Salts :

$$
\begin{array}{ll}
\mathrm{KNO}_{3} \text { (Potassium nitrate), } & \mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{3} \text { (Sodium sulphite) } \\
\mathrm{KCl} \text { (Potassium chloride), } & \mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4} \text { (Sodium sulphate) }
\end{array}
$$

## (iii) Basic Salts

Salts which produces basic solution when added to water is basic salt. The pH of the basic salt solution is more than 7.
This type of salts are formed by incomplete neutralization of a base with an acid or by partial replacement of hydroxy radicals of a diacids or triacidic base with an acid radical.
This type of salts contain a metallic cation, a hydroxy ion of a base and anion of an acid.

$$
\left[\mathrm{Cu}(\mathrm{OH})_{2}+\mathrm{HNO}_{3} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Cu}(\mathrm{OH}) \mathrm{NO}_{3}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}\right]
$$

Basic salts: $\mathrm{Cu}(\mathrm{OH}) \mathrm{NO}_{3}$

$$
\mathrm{Pb}(\mathrm{OH}) \mathrm{NO}_{3}
$$

## Other types of Salt

## (1) Double Salts

Such salts are formed by mixing saturated solutions of two simple salts, followed by crystallisation of the saturated solution.

## Example :

$\mathrm{FeSO}_{4}\left(\mathrm{NH}_{4}\right)_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4} \cdot 6 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ : Mohr's salt, it a is mixture of $\mathrm{FeSO}_{4}$ (simple salt) and $\left(\mathrm{NH}_{4}\right)_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}$ (simple salt)
$\mathrm{K}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4} \cdot \mathrm{Al}_{2}\left(\mathrm{SO}_{4}\right)_{3} \cdot 24 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ : Potash alum, it is a mixture of $\mathrm{K}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}$ (simple salt) and $\mathrm{Al}_{2}\left(\mathrm{SO}_{4}\right)_{3}$ (simple salt).
Alum is used for purification of water.

## (2) Mixed Salts

Such a salt contains two or more acidic radicals or basic radicals other than $\mathrm{OH}^{-}, \mathrm{H}^{+}$
They consists of a fixed proportion of two salts, often sharing either a common cation or common anion.
Examples : Sodium potassium sulphate, $\mathrm{NaKSO}_{4}$ (two basic radicals, $\mathrm{Na}^{+}, \mathrm{K}^{+}$)
Bleaching powder - $\mathrm{CaOCl}_{2}$ (two acid radicals $\mathrm{OCl}^{-}, \mathrm{Cl}^{-}$)

## (3) Complex Salts

Like double salts, complex salts are formed by mixing saturated solutions of two simple salts, followed by crystallisation of the solution.
These salts give a simple and a complex ion on dissociation.
Examples : Potassium mercuric iodide - $\quad \mathrm{K}_{2}\left[\mathrm{Hg} \mathrm{I}_{4}\right]$

$$
\text { Simple ion }-\mathrm{K}^{+} \quad ; \quad \text { Complex ion }-\left[\mathrm{Hg} \mathrm{I}_{4}\right]^{2-}
$$

Sodium silver cyanide - $\mathrm{Na}\left[\mathrm{Ag}(\mathrm{CN})_{2}\right]$

$$
\text { Simple ion }-\mathrm{Na}^{+} \quad ; \quad \text { Complex ion }-\left[\mathrm{Ag}(\mathrm{CN})_{2}\right]^{-}
$$

## - $\quad \mathrm{K}_{4}\left[\mathrm{Fe}(\mathrm{CN})_{6}\right]$ is which type of salt?

## SOLUTION

It is a complex salt because on dissociation it gives simple and complex ion in solution.

$$
\mathrm{K}_{4}\left[\mathrm{Fe}(\mathrm{CN})_{6}\right] \longrightarrow 4 \mathrm{~K}^{+}+\left[\mathrm{Fe}(\mathrm{CN})_{6}\right]^{4-}
$$

## Learn More

(1) Deliquescent Salts :- These are water soluble salts which on exposure to the atmosphere, absorbs moisture from it and dissolves in it, and change into liquid state for example :- $\mathrm{CaCl}_{2}, \mathrm{MgCl}_{2}, \mathrm{FeCl}_{3}, \mathrm{KOH}$ etc.
(2) Efflorescent Salts :- These are crystalline hydrated salts which on exposure to the atmosphere lose their water of crystallization either partly or completely and change into a powder.

Examples :

| $\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{CO}_{3} \cdot 10 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ | (Washing soda) |
| :--- | :--- |
| $\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4} \cdot 10 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ | (Glauber's salt) |
| $\mathrm{CuSO}_{4} \cdot 5 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ | (Blue vitriol) |
| $\mathrm{MgSO}_{4} \cdot 7 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ | (Epsom salt) |

## Solubility of Salts

Some general observations about solubility of salts are listed below.
(i) All sodium, potassium and ammonium salts are soluble in water.
(ii) All nitrites, nitrates and bicarbonates are soluble in water.
(iii) Sulphates (except those of $\mathrm{Pb}^{2+}, \mathrm{Ba}^{2+}$ and $\mathrm{Ca}^{2+}$ ) are soluble in water.
(iv) All metallic oxides and sulphides are insoluble in water.
(v) Chlorides (except those of $\mathrm{Pb}^{2+}, \mathrm{Ag}^{+}, \mathrm{Hg}^{+}$) are soluble in water.
(vi) All metallic carbonates and phosphates are insoluble in water.

- Oxides and sulphides of $\mathrm{Na}^{+}$and $\mathrm{K}^{+}$are soluble in water.
- $\mathrm{PbCl}_{2}$ is soluble in hot water.
- Carbonates, hydroxides and phosphates of $\mathrm{Na}^{+}, \mathrm{K}^{+}$and $\mathrm{NH}_{4}^{+}$are soluble in water.


## Water of Crystallization

Water of crystallisation is the fixed number of water molecules present in the formula unit of a salt.
On the basis of water of crystallisation salts are classified as hydrated salts. Hydrated salts are those salts which contain a definite number of water molecules as water of crystallisation. Example
$\mathrm{CuSO}_{4} \cdot 5 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ - Copper sulphate penta-hydrate. It is also called blue vitriol.
$\mathrm{CaCl}_{2} \cdot 6 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ - Calcium chloride hexa-hydrate. It has 6 molecules of water of crystallisation.
$\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{CO}_{3} \cdot 10 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ - Sodium carbonate deca-hydrate. It is also called washing soda. It has 10 molecules of water of crystallisation.

## Hygroscopic Substances

These are substances which on exposure to atmosphere absorb moisture but do not change their original state after absorption.
For example :
CaO (quick lime), $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}$ (sulphuric acid), Silica gel.

## pH OF SALT SOLUTIONS

## Hydrolysis of Salts

It is commonly observed that different salts, on dissolution in water do not always form neutral solutions. For example, aqueous solution of copper sulphate is acidic whereas aqueous solution sodium acetate is basic in character. However, salts of strong acids and strong bases such as sodium chloride, potassium chloride etc. form neutral aqueous solution. Salt hydrolysis is the interaction of anion or cation of the salt with water molecules to produce an acidic, basic or neutral solution.
The pH of the solution gets affected by this interaction. The cations of strong bases (such as $\mathrm{Na}^{+}, \mathrm{K}^{+}, \mathrm{Rb}^{+}, \mathrm{Ca}^{2+}, \mathrm{Ba}^{2+}$, etc.) do not hydrolyze and therefore, the solutions of salts formed by strong acids and strong bases are neutral and their pH is 7 . On the other hand, the solutions of the salts formed by strong acids and weak bases are acidic i.e., their pH is less than 7 . While the solutions of strong bases and weak acids are basic and their pH is greater than 7 .
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-072.jpg?height=86&width=102&top_left_y=2558&top_left_x=1820)
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-073.jpg?height=199&width=320&top_left_y=370&top_left_x=258)

Since salt on dissolution in water undergoes dissociation to form ions, salt hydrolysis is reverse of neutralization.
(a) Hydrolysis of salts of weak acid and strong base e.g. sodium acetate $\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COONa}\right)$, sodium carbonate $\left(\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{CO}_{3}\right)$, sodium phosphate $\left(\mathrm{Na}_{3} \mathrm{PO}_{4}\right)$ etc. give aqueous solution in alkaline range because on hydrolysis they form weak acid (not completely) ionisable) and strong base which is approximately completely ionised. Thus in this case, base overpowers the effect of weak acid and resulting solution is basic in nature. For example

$$
\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COONa}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \rightleftharpoons \underset{\text { Weak acid }}{\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOH}}+\underset{\text { Strong base }}{\mathrm{NaOH}}
$$

(b) Hydrolysis of salts of weak bases and strong acids e.g. ammonium chloride $\left(\mathrm{NH}_{4} \mathrm{Cl}\right)$, copper sulphate $\left(\mathrm{CuSO}_{4}\right)$, aluminium chloride $\left(\mathrm{AlCl}_{3}\right)$ etc. give aqueous solution in acidic range because on hydrolysis they form weak base (not completely ionisable) and strong acid (get completely ionised). Thus in this case acid over powers the effect of weak base and resulting solution is acidic in nature.

$$
\mathrm{NH}_{4} \mathrm{Cl}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \rightleftharpoons \underset{\text { Weak base }}{\mathrm{NH}_{4} \mathrm{OH}}+\underset{\text { Strong acid }}{\mathrm{HCl}}
$$

(c) Salts of weak acid and a weak base e.g. ammonium acetate $\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COONH}_{4}\right.$, ammonium carbonate $\left(\mathrm{NH}_{4}\right)_{2} \mathrm{CO}_{3}$, aluminium phosphate $\left(\mathrm{Al}_{3} \mathrm{PO}_{4}\right)$ etc on hydrolysis give almost neutral, solution.

$$
\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COONH}_{4}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \rightleftharpoons \underset{\text { Weak acid }}{\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOH}}+\underset{\text { Weak base }}{\mathrm{NH}_{4} \mathrm{OH}}
$$

(d) Salts of strong acid and strong base e.g. sodium chloride ( NaCl ), potassium nitrate ( $\mathrm{KNO}_{3}$ ), sodium sulphate ( $\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}$ ). potassium chlorate $\left(\mathrm{KClO}_{4}\right)$ etc. Such salts on hydrolysis produce equally strong acids and bases. Thus their solutions are neutral.

$$
\mathrm{NaCl}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \rightleftharpoons \underset{\text { Strong base }}{\mathrm{NaOH}}+\underset{\text { Strong acid }}{\mathrm{HCl}}
$$

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-073.jpg?height=138&width=351&top_left_y=1809&top_left_x=255)

- If you dissolve ferric chloride in water. What is the nature of the resulting solution?
- What is the nature of solution obtained by dissolving ammonium sulphate in water?


## SOLUTION

When ferric chloride is dissolved in water, ferric hydroxide gets precipitated and HCl is formed. Thus, resulting solution is acidic in nature, as HCl is a strong acid and $\mathrm{Fe}(\mathrm{OH})_{3}$ is a weak base.

$$
\mathrm{FeCl}_{3}+3 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \rightleftharpoons \underset{\text { Ferric hydroxide (ppt) }}{\mathrm{Fe}(\mathrm{OH})_{3} \downarrow}+\underset{\text { Hydrochloric acid }}{3 \mathrm{HCl}}
$$

The solution obtained will be slightly acidic as it contains strong acid and weak base.

$$
\underset{\text { Ammonium sulphate }}{\left(\mathrm{NH}_{4}\right)_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}}+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \longrightarrow \underset{\text { Strong acid }}{\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}}+\underset{\text { Weak base }}{2 \mathrm{NH}_{4} \mathrm{OH}}
$$

## CONNECTING TOPIC

## COMMON ION EFFECT

Dissociation of acids/bases in solution is affected by the presence of other species in solution. Addition of some substances can increase or decrease dissociation of acid or base in solution. For example, acetic acid ( $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOH}$ ) dissociates as :

$$
\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOH}(\mathrm{aq}) \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{H}^{+}(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COO}^{-}(\mathrm{aq})
$$

Now if we add salt or acid that supplies any of the above ion, for example $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COONa}$ or HCl to pre existing equilibrium in solution, then the equilibrium will shift to left due to common ion. Hence, dissociation of $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOH}$ decreases.
Similarly, when a strong electrolyte like $\mathrm{NH}_{4} \mathrm{Cl}$ or NaOH is added to $\mathrm{NH}_{4} \mathrm{OH}$ solution then the equilibrium will shift to the left because of high concentration of either $\mathrm{NH}_{4}^{+}$ions or of $\mathrm{OH}^{-}$ions,

$$
\mathrm{NH}_{4} \mathrm{OH} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{NH}_{4}^{+}+\mathrm{OH}^{-}
$$

Thus in short the ionisation of weak acid or weak base is suppressed by the addition of its own ion is known as common ion effect.

Time to
Check Your
Knowledge

- What is the effect on below equilibria by addition of base which furnishes hydroxyl $\left(\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right)$ion?
$\mathrm{NH}_{4} \mathrm{OH} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{NH}_{4}^{+}+\mathrm{OH}^{-}$


## SOLUTION

As per the principle of common ion effect, addition of $\mathrm{OH}^{-}$ion will shift the equilibrium toward left side or in backward direction.

## SOME IMPORTANT COMPOUNDS

## Sodium Hydroxide (NaOH)

## Methods of preparation

(a) Causticisation process (Gossage process)

This is the most common method of preparation of NaOH . It is based on the interaction of a solution of soda (i.e. $\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{CO}_{3}$ ) and slaked lime (i.e $\mathrm{Ca}(\mathrm{OH})_{2}$ ).

$$
\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{CO}_{3}+\mathrm{Ca}(\mathrm{OH})_{2} \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{NaOH}+\mathrm{CaCO}_{3}
$$

(b) Chlor-alkali process

A chlor-alkali plant produces two principle products; chlorine ( $\mathrm{Cl}_{2}$ ) and sodium hydroxide, which is commonly known as caustic soda $(\mathrm{NaOH})$. The term chlor-alkali is derived from the two products produced; chlor from chlorine and alkali from sodium hydroxide (sodium hydroxide is part of a group of chemicals called alkalis). The chlorine and sodium hydroxide are produced from the electrolysis of an aqueous solution of sodium chloride brine (salt water solution). Hydrogen gas is produced as a by product of the chlor-alkali process.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-074.jpg?height=236&width=1625&top_left_y=2138&top_left_x=187)

The overall reaction taking place in the chlor - alkali process is :

$$
2 \mathrm{NaCl}(\mathrm{aq})+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \longrightarrow \mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+\mathrm{Cl}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+2 \mathrm{NaOH}(\mathrm{aq})
$$

The three products produced in this process are all useful. Figure shows the different uses of products.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-074.jpg?height=91&width=104&top_left_y=2553&top_left_x=1818)
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-075.jpg?height=556&width=1420&top_left_y=367&top_left_x=358)

## Properties of NaOH

(a) $\quad \mathrm{NaOH}$ (Caustic soda) is a white translucent solid available in pellets, flakes and granules as $50 \%$ saturated solution.
(b) The m.p. of NaOH is 519 K . It is an ionic compound.
(c) It is readily soluble in water and forms a strong alkaline solution with liberation of heat.
(d) It is deliquescent and readily absorbs $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ from air, so it should be stored in air-tight containers.
(e) It is also soluble in ethanol and methanol though the solubility in these solvents is much lower as compared to that in water.
(f) It is insoluble in ether and other non-polar solvents.
(g) It's solution leaves a yellow stain on fabric and paper.
(h) It reacts with acids to form corresponding salts.
(i) It's solution when exposed to air reacts with atmospheric $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ and form a crust of $\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{CO}_{3}$ at the surface.

## Uses of sodium hydroxide

(a) It is used in many industries, mostly as a strong base in the manufacture of pulp, paper, textiles, soaps, dyes, cellulose, detergents etc.
(b) It is used in petroleum refining.
(c) It is used as a drain cleaner.
(d) It is used in the preparation of sodium metal and many salts of the sodium.

## Sodium Carbonate (Washing Soda) $\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{CO}_{3} \cdot 10 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$

It exists in various forms, namely anhydrous sodium carbonate $\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{CO}_{3}$ (soda-ash), monohydrate $\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{CO}_{3}$. $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ (crystal carbonate), heptahydrate. $\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{CO}_{3} .7 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ and decahydrate $\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{CO}_{3} .10 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ (washing soda or sal soda)

## Methods of preparations

Sodium carbonate is generally prepared now a days by ammonia soda or Solvay process. The ingredients of this process are readily available and inexpensive. These are salt brine $(\mathrm{NaCl})$ (from sea), ammonia $\left(\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right)$ and lime stone $\mathrm{CaCO}_{3}$ (from mines).
Calcium chloride $\left(\mathrm{CaCl}_{2}\right)$ is an important by-product.

- The process was devised and first put to commercial use by Ernest Solvay, who built a plant in 1865 in Couillet, Belgium, and was improved in the 1870s by the German-born British Chemist Ludwig Mond.
- In this process, advantage is taken of low solubility of sodium bicarbonate whereby it gets precipitated in the reaction between sodium chloride and ammonium bicarbonate $\left[\mathrm{NH}_{4} \mathrm{HCO}_{3}\right]$


## Ammonia soda (Solvay) process

(i) For preparation of $\mathrm{NH}_{4} \mathrm{HCO}_{3}$, carbon dioxide is passed into a concentrated solution of ammonia when ammonium carbonate followed by ammonium bicarbonate is formed. The reactions can be represented by the following equations :

$$
\begin{gathered}
2 \mathrm{NH}_{3}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}+\mathrm{CO}_{2} \longrightarrow\left(\mathrm{NH}_{4}\right)_{2} \mathrm{CO}_{3} \\
\left(\mathrm{NH}_{4}\right)_{2} \mathrm{CO}_{3}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}+\mathrm{CO}_{2} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{NH}_{4} \mathrm{HCO}_{3}
\end{gathered}
$$

(ii) Addition of common salt $(\mathrm{NaCl})$ to the solution containing $\mathrm{NH}_{4}^{+}$and $\mathrm{HCO}_{3}^{-}$results in the precipitation of $\mathrm{NaHCO}_{3}$ which is least soluble. It is then filtered off.

$$
\mathrm{NH}_{4} \mathrm{HCO}_{3}+\mathrm{NaCl} \longrightarrow \mathrm{NH}_{4} \mathrm{Cl}+\mathrm{NaHCO}_{3} \downarrow
$$

## Nahcolite is naturally-occuring sodium bicarbonate generally found in evaporated lake basins, and is extracted by "solution mining".

(iii) Sodium bicarbonate is then heated to give $\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{CO}_{3}$,

$$
2 \mathrm{NaHCO}_{3} \xrightarrow{\Delta} \mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{CO}_{3}+\mathrm{CO}_{2}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}
$$

The $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ gas evolved can be re-used again.
(iv) Anhydrous sodium carbonate is dissolved in water and recrystallises to get washing soda crystals containing 10 molecules of water of crystallisation.

## Properties of sodium carbonate :

(a) Sodium carbonate is a white crystalline solid.
(b) Sodium carbonate is readily soluble in water. It hydrolyses in water to give an alkaline solution.

$$
\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{CO}_{3}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \longrightarrow \mathrm{NaOH}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{CO}_{3}
$$

(c) On heating, the decahydrate salt (washing soda)gradually loses water finally gives anhydrous salt $\left(\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{CO}_{3}\right.$ - soda ash).

$$
\underset{\text { Washing soda }}{\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{CO}_{3} \cdot 10 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}} \xrightarrow[-9 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}]{373 \mathrm{~K}} \mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{CO}_{3} \cdot \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \xrightarrow[-\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}]{\text { above } 373 \mathrm{~K}} \underset{\text { Soda ash }}{\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{CO}_{3}}
$$

## Uses of sodium carbonate

(a) It is used for softening of hard water. For this purpose hydrated sodium carbonate, $\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{CO}_{3} \cdot 10 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ known as washing soda is used.
(b) A mixture of sodium carbonate $\left(\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{CO}_{3}\right)$ and potassium carbonate $\left(\mathrm{K}_{2} \mathrm{CO}_{3}\right)$ is used as fusion mixture.
(c) It is used as an important laboratory reagent both in qualitative and quantitative analysis.
(d) It is used in paper, paint and textile industries.
(e) It is used for the manufacture of glass, borax, soap and caustic soda $(\mathrm{NaOH})$.

## ILLUSTRATION : $\mathbf{6}$

Name the sodium compound which is used for softening hard water.

## SOLUTION :

Sodium carbonate $\left[\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{CO}_{3}\right]$

## Sodium Hydrogen Carbonate (Baking Soda), $\mathrm{NaHCO}_{3}$

## Method of preparation :

Sodium bicarbonate is obtained as an intermediate product in Solvay's process for manufacture of sodium carbonate, whereby sodium bicarbonate being less soluble crystallizes out.

$$
\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{CO}_{3}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}+\mathrm{CO}_{2} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{NaHCO}_{3}
$$

Sodium hydrogen carbonate can also be prepared by ammonia - chloride process.

$$
\mathrm{NaCl}+\mathrm{NH}_{4} \mathrm{HCO}_{3} \longrightarrow \mathrm{NaHCO}_{3}+\mathrm{NH}_{4} \mathrm{Cl}
$$

## Properties of sodium bicarbonate

(a) It is a white powder with crystalline grains.
(b) It is weakly alkaline in nature.
(c) It neutralizes acids and breaks down proteins
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-077.jpg?height=199&width=323&top_left_y=370&top_left_x=255)

Do not add baking soda to vegetables because it will cause them to lose nutrients. Baking soda is also known as Saleratus.

Use of sodium hydrogen carbonate
(a) It is used as a component of baking powder.
(b) It is used as a tenderizer in baking (in combination with an acid it releases $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ ).
(c) It is used as a deodorizer because of its neutralizing action.
(d) It is used in laundry work for enhancing the detergents effectiveness because it stabilizes the pH level (acts as a buffer)
(e) It is added to swimming pool water to balance the pH and keep the water clear.
(f) In combination with an acid, baking soda undergoes a chemical reaction that releases bubbles of $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$. Trapped in butter these $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ bubbles enable the baked food to rise. Baked foods treated with baking soda, therefore, generally have a light crumb and are areated with many holes left by escaping bubbles of $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$
(g) It is used in fire extinguishers as baking soda undergoes a chemical reaction that gives off $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ which helps in extinguishing fire.

$$
2 \mathrm{NaHCO}_{3} \xrightarrow{\text { heat }} \mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{CO}_{3}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}+\mathrm{CO}_{2} \uparrow
$$

(h) It is used as abrasive cleaner. Baking soda's finely gritty texture makes it an excellent gentle abrasive cleaner. It is inexpensive, environment friendly, fragrance-free and safe for nearly all surfaces making it ideal for house-hold use.
(i) As a mild abrasive agent, baking soda can also be used in place of tooth-paste.
(j) When sprinkled around the exterior entrances and to foundations of homes, it may prohibit ants and other insects from crawling in, as it is irritating to their chitinous exoskeletons and they avoid it.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-077.jpg?height=195&width=344&top_left_y=1272&top_left_x=255)

- Soda - acid fire extinguisher contains a solution of $\mathrm{NaHCO}_{3}$ and $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}$
- A pinch of baking soda if added to tomato sauce while cooking or coffee while breeving, will reduce acidity.
- A small amount of baking soda if added to soaking water of beans will hasten the softening and cooking process and will reduce the beans propensity to cause flatulence in the eater.
- Baking soda is an effective meat tenderizer. It can be added to stews or rubbed directly on a cut of meat and then rinsed off before cooking to make the meat more tender.
- It is a mild antiseptic for skin infection.
- Dissolved into lukewarm bath, it will soothe the discomfort of sun-burn and itch.
- Made into a paste with cool water and applied directly to the skin, it will ease the pain of bee stings.
- One-half spoon baking soda mixed with 120 mL of water can be taken as antacid.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-077.jpg?height=44&width=69&top_left_y=1944&top_left_x=525)
- What is the difference between baking soda and washing soda?


## SOLUTION

Baking soda is $\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{CO}_{3}$ while washing soda is $\mathrm{NaHCO}_{3}$. Both baking soda and washing soda are alkaline (bases), but washing soda is stronger base. Although they have some uses in common, they cannot be used in place of each other. For example washing soda cannot be used for baking purpose in confectionary shop because being stronger base it will result into bitterness in flavour of cake.

## Calcium Sulphate, $\mathrm{CaSO}_{4}$

## Method of preparation

It occurs in nature as anhydrous $\mathrm{CaSO}_{4}$ or as dihydrate $\mathrm{CaSO}_{4} \cdot 2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ (Gypsum). In India large quantities of gypsum are found in Punjab and Rajasthan.

It can be prepared by the action of conc. sulphuric acid on calcium chloride.

$$
\mathrm{CaCl}_{2}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4} \longrightarrow \mathrm{CaSO}_{4}+2 \mathrm{HCl}
$$

The dihydrate $\mathrm{CaSO}_{4} \cdot 2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ is not soluble in water.

## Properties

Gypsum when heated to 390 K loses water to give $\mathrm{CaSO}_{4} \cdot \frac{1}{2} \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$. This is known as plaster of paris. When heated above 437 K , it becomes anhydrous $\mathrm{CaSO}_{4}$. Anhydrous $\mathrm{CaSO}_{4}$ is known as dead burnt plaster because it does not set like plaster of paris when moistened with water.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-078.jpg?height=201&width=284&top_left_y=740&top_left_x=224)

> Gypsum is used as raw material in manufacturing of portland cement. As it slows down the rate of quick setting of cement water paste by forming insoluble complex with alumina, one of the component of portland cement.
> Gypsum is a sedimentary rock, which is setted through the evaporation of sea water trapped in lagoons. According to the nature of its impurities, gypsum can show various colors, ranging from white to brown, yellow, grey and pink.

## Plaster of Paris $\mathrm{CaSO}_{4} \cdot \frac{1}{2} \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$

## Method of preparation :

It is prepared by heating gypsum ( $\mathrm{CaSO}_{4} \cdot 2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ ) to 373 K .

$$
\mathrm{CaSO}_{4} \cdot 2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \xrightarrow{373 \mathrm{~K}} \mathrm{CaSO}_{4} \cdot \frac{1}{2} \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}+\frac{3}{2} \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}
$$

The temperature should not be allowed to rise beyond 373 K as above this temperature whole of water of crystallization is lost.

## Properties

It is a white powder. When water is added to plaster of paris it evolves heat and quickly sets to a hard porous mass in about half an hour. The setting of plaster of Paris is accompanied by a slight expansion (about $1 \%$ ) in volume so that it fills the mould completely and takes a sharp impression. The process of setting occurs as follows:

$$
\underset{\text { Plaster of paris }}{\mathrm{CaSO}_{4} \cdot 1 / 2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}} \xrightarrow[\text { setting }]{\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}} \underset{\substack{\text { Gyssum } \\ \text { orthorhombic } \\ \text { dihydrate }}}{\mathrm{CaSO}_{4} \cdot 2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}} \xrightarrow[\text { hardening }]{\mathrm{CaSO}_{4} \cdot 2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}}
$$

The first step is called the setting stage, and the second, the hardening stage. The setting of plaster of Paris is catalyzed by sodium chloride, while it is reduced by borax, or alum.
Uses
(a) It is used for producing moulds for industries like pottery, ceramics, etc.
(b) It is used for setting broken and fractured bones in the body.
(c) It is used for making statues, models and other decorative material.
(d) It is mixed with cement and so used in the building industry.

## ILLUSTRATIOM : 7

Write an equation to show the reaction between Plaster of Paris and water.

## SOLUTION :

$\underset{\text { Plaster of paris }}{\mathrm{CaSO}_{4} \cdot \frac{1}{2} \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}}+\underset{\text { Water }}{\frac{3}{2}} \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \longrightarrow \underset{\text { Gypsum }}{\mathrm{CaSO}_{4} \cdot 2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}}$
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-078.jpg?height=149&width=353&top_left_y=2266&top_left_x=187)

- A person heated gypsum to a temperature upto $\mathbf{1 5 0} \boldsymbol{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. Will he get plaster of paris as a resultant product?


## SOLUTION

No that person will get anhydrous calcium sulphate. In order to obtain plaster of paris, gypsum should be heated upto $100^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$.

## Bleaching Powder, $\mathrm{CaOCl}_{2}$

## Method of preparation :

If chlorine is passed for a considerable time over solid slaked lime, the product formed is bleaching powder. It is also known as chloride of lime. Bleaching powder, a solid combination of chlorine and slaked lime, introduced in 1799 by the Scottish chemist,
Charles Tennant. Bleaching powder is represented as $\mathrm{CaOCl}_{2}$, though the actual composition is quite complex.

$$
\mathrm{Ca}(\mathrm{OH})_{2}+\mathrm{Cl}_{2} \longrightarrow \mathrm{CaOCl}_{2}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}
$$

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-079.jpg?height=227&width=350&top_left_y=612&top_left_x=251)

In the laboratory bleaching powder is prepared by taking a gas jar full of chlorine and shaking a spatula measure of freshly prepared slaked lime into it. The colour of the chlorine disappears immediately. The product may be used to absorb chlorine from several more gas-jars before absorption is complete.
[Note : bleaching powder is actually a mixture of calcium hypochlorite $\left(\mathrm{Ca}(\mathrm{ClO})_{2}\right)$, calcium chloride $\mathrm{CaCl}_{2} \cdot \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ with some slaked lime]
There are two types of calcium hypochlorite. These are (i) Dry form and (ii) Hydrated form
The hydrated form $\left(\mathrm{CaOCl}_{2} \mathrm{O}\right)$ is safer to handle. It was used as an transport substitute for free chlorine.

## Properties of bleaching powder

(a) It is a yellow white solid having a strong smell of chlorine.
(b) It is soluble in water. The lime present is always left behind as an insoluble salt.
(c) It has greater available chlorine than sodium hypochlorite, NaClO (liquid bleach). It contains about $36 \%$ of available chlorine.
[Note : Available chlorine is that which can be removed by dilute acids and even by carbonic acid $\left(\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{CO}_{3}\right)$ of the atmosphere.]
(d) Bleaching powder deteriorates if left in contact with the air and smells like chlorine because of action of $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ of atmosphere i.e. $\mathrm{CaOCl}_{2}$ reacts with $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ to form $\mathrm{CaCO}_{3}$ and release $\mathrm{Cl}_{2}$ and with HCl to form calcium chloride.

$$
\begin{aligned}
2 \mathrm{CaOCl}_{2}+2 \mathrm{CO}_{2} & \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{CaCO}_{3}+2 \mathrm{Cl}_{2}+\mathrm{O}_{2} \\
\mathrm{CaOCl}_{2}+4 \mathrm{HCl} & \longrightarrow \mathrm{CaCl}_{2}+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}+\mathrm{Cl}_{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

Extreme care should be used in handling bleaching powder. It should always be kept in dry place, away from any organic material. Bleaching powder undergoes self-heating and rapid decomposition accompanied by release of toxic chlorine gas.

## Uses:

(a) It is widely used as a bleaching agent for bleaching clothes.
(b) It is used for disinfection of drinking water or swimming pool water. For use in outdoor swimming pools, $\mathrm{CaOCl}_{2}$ can be used as a sanitizer in combination with cyanuric acid stabilizers. Two stabilizers will reduce the loss of chlorine because of u.v. radiation.
(c) It is also used for bleaching cotton and linen.
(d) It is used in manufacture of chloroform $\left(\mathrm{CHCl}_{3}\right)$.
(e) It is used as an oxidising agent.
(f) It is also used in bathroom cleaners, household disinfectant sprays, mass and algae removers, and weed killers.

## ILLUSTRATIOM : 8

Name the substance which on treatment with chlorine yields bleaching powder.

## SOLUTION :

Slaked lime $\mathrm{Ca}(\mathrm{OH})_{2}$ [Calcium hydroxide]

Time to
Check Your
Knowledge

## - How bleaching powder helps in disinfection of drinking water?

## SOLUTION

Bleaching powder dissolves in water to generate hypochlorous acid which has disinfecting property.

$$
\begin{gathered}
\mathrm{Ca}(\mathrm{OCl})_{2}+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \longrightarrow \underset{\text { Hypochlorous acid }}{2 \mathrm{HOCl}}+\underset{\text { Calcium hydroxide }}{\mathrm{Ca}(\mathrm{OH})_{2}} \\
2 \mathrm{HOCl} \rightarrow \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}+\mathrm{Cl}_{2}+[0]
\end{gathered}
$$

Nacent oxyegen [0] is responsible for disinfection of water.

## Multiple Choice Questions (MCQs)

DIRECTIONS : This section contains multiple choice questions. Each question has four choices (a), (b), (c) and (d) out of which ONLY ONE is correct.

1. During the preparation of hydrogen chloride gas on a humid day, the gas is usually passed through the guard tube containing calcium chloride. The role of calcium chloride taken in the guard tube is to:
(a) absorb the evolved gas.
(b) moisten the gas.
(c) absorb moisture from the gas.
(d) absorb $\mathrm{Cl}^{-}$ions from the evolved gas.
2. To protect tooth decay we are advised to brush our teeth regularly. The nature of the tooth paste commonly used is:
(a) Acidic
(b) Neutral
(c) Basic
(d) Corrosive
3. Which of the following is not a mineral acid?
(a) Hydrochloric acid
(b) Citric acid
(c) Sulphuric acid
(d) Nitric acid
4. Which of the following acid is present in sour milk ?
(a) glycolic acid
(b) lactic acid
(c) citric acid
(d) tartaric acid
5. An aqueous solution 'A' turns phenolphthalein solution pink. On addition of an aqueous solution ' B ' to ' A ', the pink colour disappears. The following statement is true for solution ' A ' and ' B '.
(a) A is strongly basic and B is a weak base.
(b) A is strongly acidic and B is a weak acid.
(c) A has pH greater than 7 and B has pH less than 7 .
(d) A has pH less than 7 and B has pH greater than 7 .

## Assertion \& Reason

DIRECTIONS : Each of these questions contains an assertion followed by reason. Read them carefully and answer the question on the basis of following options. You have to select the one that best describes the two statements.
(a) If both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of Assertion.
(b) If both Assertion and Reason are correct, but Reason is not the correct explanation of Assertion.
(c) If Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
(d) If Assertion is incorrect but Reason is correct.

1. Assertion : Aqueous solution of ammonium nitrate turns blue litmus red.
Reason : Ammonium nitrate is salt of strong acid and strong base.
2. Assertion : All alkalis are bases but all bases are not alkali.
Reason : Water soluble bases are alkali.
3. Assertion : Magnesium hydroxide is used as antacid.

Reason : Magnesium hydroxide is a strong base.
4. Assertion : Dry HCl gas does not change the colour of blue litmus paper to red.
Reason : Dry HCl gas is strongly basic.
5. Assertion : Sodium hydrogen carbonate is used in fire extinguisher.
Reason : Sodium hydrogen carbonate is a mild base.

## Passage Based Questions

DIRECTIONS : Study the given passage and answer the related question.

The strength of an acid depends on the concentration of the hydronium ions present in a solution. Greater the number of hydronium ion present, greater is the strength of acid. However, some acids do not dissociate to any appreciable extent in water. Therefore, these acids will have a low concentration of hydronium ions. Those acids which dissociate to give two or more than two hydronium ions are called polybasic acids.

1. What does it signify when red litmus paper turns blue?
2. What will happens if blue litmus paper is introduced in a solution of NaCl ?
3. Give one example of an acid which does not dissociate to any appreciable extent in water.
4. How the strength of an acid depends on the concentration of hydronium ions present in a solution?

11. Write the balanced equation in molecular form illustrating the complete neutralization of $\mathrm{Al}(\mathrm{OH})_{3}$ with $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}$.
12. Name two industries based use of washing soda.
13. Explain why, an aqueous solution of sodium sulphate is neutral while an aqueous solution of sodium carbonate is basic in nature.
14. Name the acid present in the following :
(i) Tomato
(ii) Vinegar
(iii) Tamarind
15. In one of the industrial processes used for manufactured of sodium hydroxide a gas X is formed as by product. The gas ' X ' reacts with dry slaked lime to give a compound ' $Y$ ' which is used as bleaching agent in textile industry.
16. (a) What is the action of litmus on :
(i) dry ammonia gas
(ii) solution of ammonia gas in water
(b) State the observations you would make on adding sodium hydroxide to aqueous solution of
(i) ferrous sulphate
(ii) aluminium chloride

Give balanced chemical equations
17. Identify the acid and the base from which sodium chloride is obtained. Which type of salt is it? When is it called rock salt? How is rock salt formed?
18. Blue litmus solution is added to two test tubes A and B containing dilute HCl and NaOH solution respectively. In which test tube a colour change will be observed? State the colour change and give its reason.
19. During electrolysis of brine, a gas ' G ' is liberated at anode. When this gas ' $G$ ' is passed through slaked lime, a compound ' C ' is formed, which is used for disinfecting drinking water.
(i) Write formula of ' $G$ ' and ' $C$ '.
(ii) State the chemical equation involved
(iii) What is common name of compound ' C '? Give its chemical name.

## Long Answer Questions

1. What is observed when - (i) dilute sulphuric acid is added to solid sodium carbonate, (ii) hot concentrated sulphuric acid is added to sulphur, (iii) sulphur dioxide is passed through lime water ? Also write chemical equations to represent the chemical reaction taking place in each case.
2. (i) Name the raw materials used in the manufacture of sodium carbonate by Solvay process. (ii) How is the sodiumhydrogencarbonate formed during Solvay process separated from a mixture of $\mathrm{NH}_{4} \mathrm{Cl}$ and $\mathrm{NaHCO}_{3}$ ? (iii) How is sodium carbonate obtained from sodium hydrogen carbonate ? Mention the name of scientist who develop Solvay process and list four important uses of sodium carbonate.
3. A compound X of sodium forms a white powder. It is a constituent of baking powder and is used in some antacid prescriptions. When heated, X gives out a gas and steam. The gas forms a white precipitate with limewater. Write the chemical formula and name of X and the chemical equation for its decomposition on heating. What is its role as baking powder and as an antacid?
4. Identify the compound of calcium which is yellowish white powder and is used for disinfecting drinking water. How is it manufactured ? Write the chemical equation for the reaction involved. What happens when it is left exposed to air? Mention its four important uses also.
5. Is it possible for a salt to behave as a base ? Explain.

## Reasoning Based Questions

1. Why does a solution of a strong acid conduct electricity better than a solution of a weak acid having the same concentration?
2. Why does tooth decay start when the pH of mouth is lower than 5.5 ?
3. Which of the two soil, $\mathrm{A}(\mathrm{pH}=8)$ or $\mathrm{B}(\mathrm{pH}=4)$ should be treated with powdered chalk to adjust its pH and why?
4. Bee sting should treated with baking soda while wasp sting should treated with vinegar, why?
5. Explain why the pH in a person's mouth becomes lower after each meal.
6. When $\mathrm{CuSO}_{4} \cdot 5 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ dehydrated, its characteristic blue colour disappeared. Why?
7. Explain why an aqueous solution of ammonium chloride is acidic in nature?

## HOTS Questions

1. "Sulphuric acid is a dibasic acid". Write the reaction equations to justify this statement and name the reaction products in the two cases.
2. How is chloride of lime chemically different from calcium chloride ? Why does chloride of lime gradually lose its chlorine when kept exposed to air ?
3. Describe the chemical reactions involved in the Solvay process for the manufacture of washing soda.
4. Suggest an explanation for why people once washed their hands with ashes.
5. Write balanced acid-base neutralization reactions that would lead to formation of the following salts or acid salts.
(a) $\mathrm{CaBr}_{2}$
(b) $\quad \mathrm{Sr}\left(\mathrm{ClO}_{2}\right)_{2}$
(c) $\mathrm{Li}_{2} \mathrm{~S}$
6. What happens to the corrosive properties of an acid and a base after they neutralize each other ? Defend your answer.
7. For making cake, baking powder is taken. If at home your mother uses baking soda instead of baking powder in cake,
(a) How will it affect the taste of the cake and why?
(b) How can baking soda be converted into baking powder?
(c) What is the role of tartaric acid added to baking soda?
8. Name the acid present in ant sting and give its chemical formula. Also give the common method to get relief from the discomfort caused by the ant sting.

## Case Study Based MCQs

1. Read the following and attempt any four questions from (i) to (v).
Acids produce hydrogen ions, $\mathrm{H}^{+}$(aq), in solution, which are responsible for their acidic properties. Hydrogen ions cannot exist alone, but they exist after combining with water molecules. when a base is dissolved in water. Bases generate hydroxide ( $\mathrm{OH}^{-}$) ions in water. Bases which are soluble in water are called alkalis. that all acids generate $\mathrm{H}^{+}(\mathrm{aq})$ and all bases generate $\mathrm{OH}^{-}(\mathrm{aq})$, Acid + Base ⟶ Salt + Water
(i) Ammonium hydroxide is a weak base because
(a) it has low vapour pressure
(b) it is only slightly ionised
(c) it is not a hydroxide of any metal
(d) it has low density
(ii) The poisonous effect of acid present in stings of bees and ants can be neutralised by use of a solution that contains
(a) acetic acid
(b) formic acid
(c) sodium hydroxide
(d) sodium chloride.
(iii) Which of the following is an alkali ?
(a) $\mathrm{Ca}(\mathrm{OH})_{2}$
(b) KOH
(c) $\mathrm{Mg}(\mathrm{OH})_{2}$
(d) $\mathrm{CaCO}_{3}$
(iv) Acids and bases are important because of
(a) their use in industry
(b) their effects on human health
(c) their effect on farmer's crop
(d) All the above are correct.
(v) Which of following compound is alkaline in aqueous medium.
(a) $\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{CO}_{3}$
(b) NaCl
(c) $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{CO}_{3}$
(d) $\mathrm{CuSO}_{4}$

## Text - Book Questions

1. You are provided with three test-tubes, one of them contains distilled water and the other two contain an acidic solution and a basic solution respectively. If you are given only red litmus paper how will you identify the contents of each test-tube?
2. Why should curd and sour substances not be kept in brass and copper vessels?
3. Which gas is usually liberated when an acid reacts with a metal? Illustrate with an example. How will you test for the presence of this gas?
4. Metal compound A reacts with dilute hydrochloric acid to produce effervescence. The gas evolved extinguishes a burning candle. Write a balanced chemical equation for the reaction if one of the compounds formed is calcium chloride.
5. Why do $\mathrm{HCl}, \mathrm{HNO}_{3}$ etc. show acidic character in aqueous solutions while solutions of compounds like alcohol and glucose do not show acidic character?
6. Why does aqueous solution of an acid conduct electricity?
7. Why does dry HCl gas not change the colour of dry litmus paper?
8. While diluting an acid, why is it recommended that acid should be added to water and not water to the acid?
9. How is concentration of hydronium ions $\left(\mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{O}^{+}\right)$affected when solution of an acid is diluted with water?
10. How is concentration of hydroxyl ions $\left(\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right)$affected when excess of base is dissolved in solution of sodium hydroxide?
11. You have two solutions A and B. The pH of solution A is 6 and that of solution $B$ is 8 . Which solutions has more hydrogen ion concentration? Which of this is acidic and which one is basic?
12. What effect does concentration of $\mathrm{H}^{+}(a q)$ ions have on the nature of a solution?
13. Do basic solutions also have $\mathrm{H}^{+}(a q)$ ions? If yes then why are these basic?
14. Under what soil condition do you think a farmer would treat the soil of his fields with quick lime (calcium oxide) or slaked lime (calcium hydroxide) or chalk (calcium carbonate)?
15. What is the common name of the compound $\mathrm{CaOCl}_{2}$ ?
16. Name the substance which upon treating with chlorine gas gives bleaching powder. Write the chemical equation for the reaction.
17. Name the sodium compound which is used for softening hard water.
18. What will happen if the solution of sodium hydrogen carbonate is heated? Write the chemical equation involved.
19. Write the chemical equation for reaction between plaster of paris and water.

## Text - Book Exercise

1. A solution turns red litmus blue. Its pH is likely to be -
(a) 2
(b) 4
(c) 5
(d) 10
2. A solution reacts with crushed egg-shells to give a gas that turns lime water milky. The solution contains -
(a) NaCl
(b) HCl
(c) LiCl
(d) KCl
3. 10 mL of a solution of NaOH is found to be completely neutralised by 8 mL of a given solution of HCl . If we take 20 mL of the same solution of NaOH , the amount of HCl solution (the same solution as before) required to neutralise will be -
(a) 4 mL
(b) 8 mL
(c) 12 mL
(d) 16 mL
4. Which of the following type of medicines is used for treating indigestion?
(a) Antibiotic
(b) Analgesic
(c) Antacid
(d) Antiseptic
5. Write word equations and the balanced equations for the reaction taking place when
(a) dilute sulphuric acid reacts with zinc granules.
(b) dilute hydrochloric acid reacts with magnesium ribbon.
(c) dilute sulphuric acid reacts with aluminium powder.
(d) dilute hydrochloric acid reacts with iron filings.
6. Compounds such as alcohol and glucose also contain hydrogen but are not categorised as acids. Describe an activity to prove it.
7. Why does distilled water not conduct electricity, whereas rain water does?
8. Why acids do not show any acidic behaviour in the absence of water ?
9. Five solutions $\mathrm{A}, \mathrm{B}, \mathrm{C}, \mathrm{D}$ and E when tested with universal indicator show pH as $4,2,12,7$ and 9 respectively. Which solution is
(a) Neutral
(b) Strongly alkaline
(c) Strongly acidic
(d) Weakly alkaline
(e) Weakly acidic

Arrange the pH in increasing order of $\mathrm{H}^{+}$ion concentration.
10. Equal lengths of magnesium ribbons are taken in test tube A and B . Hydrochloric acid $(\mathrm{HCl})$ is added to test tube A while acetic acid $\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOH}\right)$ is added to test tube B. In which case fizzing occurs more vigorously and why ?
11. Fresh milk has pH of 6 . How do you think pH will change as it turns into curd? Explain your answer.
12. A milkman adds very small amount of baking soda to fresh milk.
(a) Why does he shift the pH of milk from 6 to sligthtly alkaline ?
(b) Why does this milk take a long time to set as curd?
13. Why should plaster of paris be stored in a moisture proof container?
14. What is neutralisation reactions? Give two examples.
15. Give two important uses of washing soda and baking soda.

## Exemplar Questions

1. A student prepared solutions of (i) an acid and (ii) a base in two separate beakers. She forgot to label the solutions and litmus paper is not available in the laboratory. Since both the solutions are colourless, how will she distinguish between the two?
2. When zinc metal is treated with a dilute solution of a strong acid, a gas is evolved which is utilised in the hydrogenation of oil. Name the gas evolved. Write the chemical equation of the reaction involved and also write a test to detect the gas formed.
3. In one of the industrial processes used for manufacture of sodium hydroxide, a gas X is formed as by product. The gas X reacts with dry slaked lime to give a compound Y which is used as a bleaching agent in chemical industry. Identify X and Y giving the chemical equation of the reactions involved.
4. A metal carbonate X on reacting with an acid gives a gas which when passed through a solution Y gives the carbonate back. On the other hand, a gas G that is obtained at anode during electrolysis of brine is passed on dry Y , it gives a compound Z , used for disinfecting drinking water. Identity $\mathrm{X}, \mathrm{Y}, \mathrm{G}$ and Z .
5. Identify the compound X on the basis of the reactions given below. Also, write the name and chemical formulae of $\mathrm{A}, \mathrm{B}$ and C .

## Multiple Choice Questions (MCQs)

DIRECTIONS : This section contains multiple choice questions. Each question has four choices (a), (b), (c) and (d) out of which ONLY ONE is correct.

1. The product of complete neutralization of $\mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{PO}_{3}$ with NaOH is :
(a) $\mathrm{NaH}_{2} \mathrm{PO}_{3}$
(b) $\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{HPO}_{3}$
(c) $\mathrm{Na}_{3} \mathrm{PO}_{3}$
(d) $\mathrm{Na}_{3}\left(\mathrm{HPO}_{3}\right)_{2}$
2. Chemical A is used for water softening to remove temporary hardness. 'A' reacts with sodium carbonate to generate caustic soda. What is ' $A$ '?
(a) Gypsum
(b) Slaked lime
(c) Quick lime
(d) Lime stone
3. An aqueous solution turns red litmus solution blue. Excess addition of which of the following solution would reverse the change?
(a) Baking powder
(b) Lime
(c) Ammonium hydroxide solution
(d) Hydrochloric acid
4. A blue litmus paper was first dipped in dil. HCl and then in dil. NaOH solution. It was observed that the colour of the litmus paper -
(a) changed to red.
(b) changed first to red and then to blue.
(c) changed blue to colourless.
(d) remains blue in both the solutions.
5. The acid used in making vinegar is -
(a) formic acid
(b) acetic acid
(c) sulphuric acid
(d) nitric acid
6. $\quad \mathrm{CuO}+(\mathrm{X}) \rightarrow \mathrm{CuSO}_{4}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$. Here $(\mathrm{X})$ is -
(a) $\mathrm{CuSO}_{4}$
(b) HCl
(c) $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}$
(d) $\mathrm{HNO}_{3}$
7. Reaction of an acid with a base is known as -
(a) decomposition
(b) combination
(c) redox reaction
(d) neutralization
8. When $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ is passed through lime water, it turns milky. The milkiness in due to formation of -
(a) $\mathrm{CaCO}_{3}$
(b) $\quad \mathrm{Ca}(\mathrm{OH})_{2}$
(c) $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$
(d) $\quad \mathrm{CO}_{2}$
9. Antacids contain -
(a) weak base
(b) weak acid
(c) strong base
(d) strong acid
10. $2 \mathrm{NaOH}+\mathrm{MgSO}_{4} \longrightarrow$ ?
(a) $\mathrm{MgO}+\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}$
(b) $\mathrm{Mg}(\mathrm{OH})_{2}+\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}$
(c) $\mathrm{Mg}(\mathrm{OH})_{2}+\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{O}$
(d) $\mathrm{MgO}+\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{O}$
11. Bleaching powder gives smell of chlorine because it -
(a) is unstable.
(b) gives chlorine on exposure to atmosphere.
(c) is a mixture of chlorine and slaked lime.
(d) contains excess of chlorine.
12. Plaster of paris is made from -
(a) lime stone
(b) slaked lime
(c) quick lime
(d) gypsum
13. Chemical formula of baking soda is -
(a) $\mathrm{MgSO}_{4}$
(b) $\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{CO}_{3}$
(c) $\mathrm{NaHCO}_{3}$
(d) $\mathrm{MgCO}_{3}$
14. Washing soda has the formula -
(a) $\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{CO}_{3} \cdot 7 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$
(b) $\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{CO}_{3} \cdot 10 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$
(c) $\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{CO}_{3} \cdot \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$
(d) $\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{CO}_{3}$
15. Plaster of Paris hardens by -
(a) giving of $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$
(b) changing into $\mathrm{CaCO}_{3}$
(c) combining with water
(d) giving out water
16. Which of the following is acidic in nature?
(a) apple juice
(b) soap solution
(c) slaked lime
(d) lime
17. The reaction of metal with acid results in the formation of-
(a) only hydrogen gas
(b) only salt
(c) both salt and hydrogen gas
(d) none of these
18. Which of the following acid does not react with metals?
(a) sulphuric acid
(b) phosphoric acid
(c) carbonic acid
(d) nitric acid
19. When an oxide of a non-metal reacts with water which of the following is formed?
(a) Acid
(b) Base
(c) Salt
(d) None of these
20. 'Alum' is an example of -
(a) single salt
(b) double salt
(c) acids
(d) none of the above
21. Which of the following statements is correct about an aqueous solution of an acid and of a base?
(i) Higher the pH , stronger the acid
(ii) Higher the pH , weaker the acid
(iii) Lower the pH , stronger the base
(iv) Lower the pH , weaker the base
(a) (i) and (iii)
(b) (ii) and (iii)
(c) (i) and (iv)
(d) (ii) and (iv)
22. A sample of soil is mixed with water and allowed to settle. The clear supernatant solution turns the pH paper yellowish-orange. Which of the following would change the colour of this pH paper to greenish-blue?
(a) Lemon juice
(b) Vinegar
(c) Common salt
(d) An antacid
23. Plaster of paris is obtained -
(a) by adding water to calcium sulphate.
(b) by adding sulphuric acid to calcium hydroxide.
(c) by heating gypsum to a very high temperature.
(d) by heating gypsum to 373 K .
24. What is the term for the positive and negative ions of a compound breaking apart in solution -
(a) Conglomeration
(b) Oxidation
(c) Dissociation
(d) None of the Above
25. Of the aqueous solutions listed below, which would be the best conductor of an electric current?
(a) HCl
(b) $\mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{PO}_{4}$
(c) HOCl
(d) $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOH}$
26. Common salt besides being used in kitchen can also be used as the raw material for making
(i) washing soda
(ii) bleaching powder
(iii) baking soda
(iv) slaked lime
(a) (i) and (ii)
(b) (i), (ii) and (iv)
(c) (i) and (iii)
(d) (i), (iii) and (iv)
27. Which salt can be classified as an acid salt?
(a) $\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}$
(b) BiOCl
(c) $\mathrm{Pb}(\mathrm{OH}) \mathrm{Cl}$
(d) $\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{HPO}_{4}$
28. An element X reacts with dilute $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}$ as well as with NaOH to produce salt and $\mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})$. Hence, it may be concluded that:
[NTSE]
I. X is an electropositive element.
II. oxide of X is basic in nature.
III. oxide of X is acidic in nature.
IV. X is an electronegative element.
(a) I, II, III
(b) IV, I, II
(c) III, IV, I
(d) II, III, IV
29. The turmeric solution will turn red by an aqueous solution of -
[NTSE]
(a) potassium acetate
(b) copper sulphate
(c) sodium sulphate
(d) ferric chloride
30. The correct order of increasing pH values of the aqueous solutions of baking soda, rock salt, washing soda and slaked lime is
[NTSE]
(a) Baking Soda $<$ Rock Salt $<$ Washing Soda $<$ Slaked lime
(b) Rock Salt $<$ Baking Soda $<$ Washing Soda $<$ Slaked lime
(c) Slaked lime < Washing Soda < Rock Salt < Baking Soda
(d) Washing Soda $<$ Baking Soda $<$ Rock Salt $<$ Slaked lime
31. You are provided with aqueous solutions of three salts - A, B and C, 2-3 drops of blue litmus solution, red litmus solution and phenolphthalein were added to each of these solution in separate experiments. The change in colours of different indicators were recorded in the following table:

| Sample | With blue <br> litmus <br> solution | With red <br> litmus <br> solution | With <br> phenolphtha- <br> lein <br> solution |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| A | No change | No change | No change |
| B | Turns red | No change | No change |
| C | No change | Turns blue | Turns pink |

On the basis of above observations, identify $\mathrm{A}, \mathrm{B}$, and C from the following options:
[NTSE]
(a) $\mathrm{A}=\mathrm{NH}_{4} \mathrm{Cl}, \mathrm{B}=\mathrm{NaCl}, \mathrm{C}=\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COONa}$
(b) $\mathrm{A}=\mathrm{NH}_{4} \mathrm{Cl}, \mathrm{B}=\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COONa}, \mathrm{C}=\mathrm{NaCl}$
(c) $\mathrm{A}=\mathrm{NaCl}, \mathrm{B}=\mathrm{NH}_{4} \mathrm{Cl}, \mathrm{C}=\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COONa}$
(d) $\mathrm{A}=\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COONa}, \mathrm{B}=\mathrm{NH}_{4} \mathrm{Cl}, \mathrm{C}=\mathrm{NaCl}$
32. Aqua regia is the mixture of conc. HCl and conc. $\mathrm{HNO}_{3}$ in the ratio:
[JSTSE]
(a) $1: 3$
(b) $2: 3$
(c) $3: 1$
(d) $3: 2$
33. The chemical formula of 'Plaster of Paris' is
[NTSE]
(a) $\mathrm{CaSO}_{4} \cdot \frac{1}{2} \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$
(b) $\mathrm{CaSO}_{4} \cdot 2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$
(c) $\mathrm{CaSO}_{4} \cdot \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$
(d) $\mathrm{CaSO}_{4} \cdot \frac{3}{2} \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$

## More than One Option Correct

DIRECTIONS : This section contains multiple choice questions. Each question has four choices (a), (b), (c) and (d) out of which ONE OR MORE may be correct.

1. Which are bases as well as alkalies?
(a) NaOH
(b) KOH
(c) $\mathrm{Fe}(\mathrm{OH})_{3}$
(d) $\mathrm{Cu}(\mathrm{OH})_{2}$
2. Which of the following are mineral acids?
(a) nitric acid
(b) citrus acid
(c) formic acid
(d) hydrochloric acid
3. Which of the following are a property of acid?
(a) All acids have a sour taste.
(b) Acids turn blue litmus red.
(c) Acids turn red litmus blue.
(d) Acids form $\mathrm{H}^{+}$ions in water.
4. Which of the following is/are a weak base?
(a) NaOH
(b) KOH
(c) $\mathrm{NH}_{4} \mathrm{OH}$
(d) $\mathrm{Mg}(\mathrm{OH})_{2}$
5. Which of the following compounds are basic in nature -
(a) $\mathrm{Ca}(\mathrm{OH})_{2}$
(b) $\mathrm{CaCl}_{2} \cdot 6 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$
(c) $\mathrm{Mg}(\mathrm{OH})_{2}$
(d) $\mathrm{CuSO}_{4} \cdot 5 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$
6. Which of the following compounds are neutral to litmus -
(a) $\mathrm{NaNO}_{3}$
(b) NaCl
(c) $\mathrm{NaHCO}_{3}$
(d) $\mathrm{Ca}(\mathrm{OH})_{2}$
7. Acids and bases are important because of -
(a) their use in industry.
(b) their effects on human health.
(c) their effects on farmer's crops.
(d) their use in neutralization to form salts.
8. Acids -
(a) produce the hydroxyl ion $\left(\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right)$.
(b) produce the hydronium ion $\left(\mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{O}^{+}\right)$in aqueous solution.
(c) are proton donors.
(d) have low pH .
9. Bases -
(a) form the hydronium ion $\left(\mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{O}^{+}\right)$in aqueous solution.
(b) form the hydroxyl ion $(\mathrm{OH})^{-}$.
(c) are proton donors.
(d) have high pH value (>7).
10. The hydroxyl ion may be formed by
(a) acids
(b) bases
(c) water gaining a hydrogen ion
(d) alkali
11. NaOH is a strong base because -
(a) it gives $\mathrm{OH}^{-}$ion.
(b) it can be oxidised.
(c) it ionises almost completely.
(d) it completely neutralise acids.
12. Acids and bases -
(a) are electrolytes.
(b) are non-electrolytes.
(c) neutralize each other.
(d) cause indicators to change colors.

## Passage Based Questions

## DIRECTIONS : Study the given passage and answer the related questions.

The strength of a base depends on the concentration of the hydroxyl ions present in a solution. Greater the number of hydroxyl ion present, greater is the strength of base. However some bases do not dissociate to any appreciable extent in water, e.g. $\mathrm{NH}_{4} \mathrm{OH}$. Some bases dissolve in water to form alkali. Examples of such bases are sodium hydroxide and potassium hydroxide.

1. Which of the following is the characteristics of a base?
(a) Turns blue litmus to red.
(b) Turns phenolpthalein pink from colourless.
(c) Decomposes carbonates.
(d) Gives $\mathrm{H}^{+}$ions on dissociation.
2. Strength of base can be explained on the basis of -
(a) its concentration in solution
(b) its degree of ionisation
(c) (a) and (b) both required
(d) it is an inherent property of acid.
3. The acidity of barium hydroxide is -
(a) 1
(b) 2
(c) 3
(d) 4

## Assertion \& Reason

DIRECTIONS : Each of these questions contains an assertion followed by reason. Read them carefully and answer the question on the basis of following options. You have to select the one that best describes the two statements.
(a) If both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of Assertion.
(b) If both Assertion and Reason are correct, but Reason is not the correct explanation of Assertion.
(c) If Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
(d) If Assertion is incorrect but Reason is correct.

1. Assertion : $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{CO}_{3}$ is a strong acid.

Reason : A strong acid dissociates completely or almost completely in water.
2. Assertion : Salts are the products of an acid-base reaction.
Reason : Salt may be acidic or basic.
3. Assertion : On adding $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}$ to water the resulting aqueous solution get corrosive.
Reason : Hydronium ions are responsible for corrosive action.
1.

| Column I |  |  |  |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| (A) $\mathrm{NaHCO}_{3}$ |  |  |  |
| (B) NaOH |  |  |  |
| (C) $\mathrm{KHSO}_{4}$ |  |  |  |
| (D) $\mathrm{Ca}(\mathrm{OH})_{2}$ |  |  |  |
| $\mathrm{~A} \quad \mathrm{~B}$ | C | D |  |
| (a) $\mathrm{p}, \mathrm{q}, \mathrm{r} \quad \mathrm{q}, \mathrm{s}$ | $\mathrm{q}, \mathrm{r}$ | $\mathrm{q}, \mathrm{s}$ |  |
| (b) $\mathrm{p} \quad \mathrm{q}, \mathrm{s}$ | s | r |  |
| (c) $\mathrm{q} \quad \mathrm{s}$ | p | r |  |
| (d) $\mathrm{r} \quad \mathrm{q}$ | s | p |  |

## Column II

(p) Baking soda
(q) Alkaline
(r) Acidic salt
(s) Bitter taste

## Multiple Matching Questions

DIRECTIONS : Following question have Column I and Column II. Any given option in Column I can have correct matching with one or more option(s) given in Column II. Match the entries in Column I with entries in Column II.

## Integer/Numerical Value Type Questions

DIRECTIONS (Qs. 1-2) : Following are integer/numerical value based questions. Each question, when worked out will result in an integer/numerical value.

1. Ten solutions are given with their pH value.
$\mathrm{A}=7, \mathrm{~B}=1, \mathrm{C}=3, \mathrm{D}=9, \mathrm{E}=4, \mathrm{~F}=11, \mathrm{G}=14, \mathrm{H}=5$
$\mathrm{I}=10, \mathrm{~J}=13$
How many of them are acidic in nature?
2. Some compounds are given :
$\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOH}, \mathrm{NaOH}, \mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{PO}_{4}, \mathrm{NH}_{4} \mathrm{OH}, \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}, \mathrm{Ca}(\mathrm{OH})_{2}$.
How many of them is/are acid(s).

## Multiple Choice Questions

DIRECTIONS (Qs. 1-28) : This section contains multiple choice questions. Each question has four choices (a), (b), (c) and (d) out of which ONLY ONE is correct.

1. pH scale was introduced by :
(a) Arrhenius
(b) Sorensen
(c) Lewis
(d) Lowry
2. Species acting as both Bronsted acid and base, is :
(a) $\left(\mathrm{HSO}_{4}\right)^{-}$
(b) $\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{CO}_{3}$
(c) $\mathrm{NH}_{3}$
(d) $\mathrm{OH}^{-}$
3. The pH of $10^{-10} \mathrm{M} \mathrm{NaOH}$ solution is nearest to :
(a) 6
(b) -10
(c) 4
(d) 7
4. Diacidic base is :
(a) $\mathrm{CH}_{2}(\mathrm{OH})_{2}$
(b) $\mathrm{Ca}(\mathrm{OH})_{2}$
(c) $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CH}(\mathrm{OH})_{2}$
(d) All of these
5. Which of the following behaves as Lewis acid and not as Bronsted acid?
(a) HCl
(b) $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}$
(c) $\mathrm{HSO}_{3}^{-}$
(d) $\mathrm{SO}_{3}$
6. Conjugate acid of $\mathrm{NH}_{2}^{-}$is :
(a) $\mathrm{NH}_{4}^{+}$
(b) $\mathrm{NH}_{3}$
(c) $\mathrm{NH}_{2}$
(d) NH
7. The conjugate base of HBr is :
(a) $\mathrm{H}^{+}$
(b) $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{Br}^{+}$
(c) $\mathrm{Br}^{-}$
(d) $\mathrm{Br}^{+}$
8. An example of a Lewis acid is :
(a) $\mathrm{MgCl}_{2}$
(b) $\mathrm{SnCl}_{4}$
(c) $\mathrm{AlCl}_{3}$
(d) NaCl
9. Conjugate acid of $\mathrm{HF}_{2}^{-}$is :
(a) $\mathrm{H}^{+}$
(b) HF
(c) $\mathrm{F}_{2}$
(d) $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{~F}_{2}$
10. Which one of the following is the correct statement?
(a) $\mathrm{HCO}_{3}^{-}$is the conjugate base of $\mathrm{CO}_{3}^{2-}$.
(b) $\mathrm{NH}_{2}^{-}$is the conjugate acid of $\mathrm{NH}_{3}$.
(c) $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}$ is the conjugate acid of $\mathrm{HSO}_{4}^{-}$.
(d) $\mathrm{NH}_{3}$ is the conjugate base of $\mathrm{NH}_{2}^{-}$.
11. The pH value of a 10 M solution of HCl is :
(a) less than 0
(b) equal to 0
(c) equal to 1
(d) equal to 2
12. Would gaseous HCl be considered as an Arrhenius acid?
(a) Yes
(b) No
(c) Not known
(d) Gaseous HCl does not exist
13. pH of solution is defined by expression :
(a) $\quad \log \left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]$
(b) $\quad \log \left[\frac{1}{\mathrm{H}^{+}}\right]$
(c) $\frac{1}{\log \left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]}$
(d) $\frac{1}{-\log \left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]}$
14. Change of $\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{CO}_{3} \cdot 10 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ to $\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{CO}_{3} \cdot \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ on exposure to air is called :
(a) efflorescence
(b) effervescence
(c) fluorescence
(d) luminescence
15. In the reaction, $\mathrm{CO}_{3}{ }^{2-}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \rightarrow \mathrm{HCO}_{3}^{-}+\mathrm{OH}^{-}$, water is a :
(a) Bronsted acid
(b) Bronsted base
(c) conjugate acid
(d) conjugate base
16. The pH of a solution of hydrochloric acid is 4 . The molarity of solution is :
(a) 4.0
(b) 0.4
(c) 0.0001
(d) 0.04
17. In a solution of $\mathrm{pH}=5$, more acid is added in order to reduce the $\mathrm{pH}=2$. The increase in hydrogen ion concentration is.
(a) 100 times
(b) 1000 times
(c) 3 times
(d) 5 times
18. Acidity of ammonium hydroxide is -
(a) 1
(b) 2
(c) 4
(d) 3
19. Which of the following is not an acidic salt ?
(a) $\mathrm{CuSO}_{4}$
(b) $\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{CO}_{3}$
(c) $\mathrm{ZnSO}_{4}$
(d) $\mathrm{NH}_{4} \mathrm{NO}_{3}$
20. Basic salts are formed by neutralisation of -
(a) strong acid and strong base
(b) strong acid and weak base
(c) weak acid and weak base
(d) strong base and weak acid
21. The basic salt, $\mathrm{Cu}(\mathrm{OH}) \mathrm{NO}_{3}$ can be readily prepared by the reaction between
(a) $\mathrm{CuCl}_{2}+\mathrm{HCl}$
(b) $\mathrm{CuOH}+\mathrm{HNO}_{3}$
(c) $\mathrm{Cu}(\mathrm{OH})_{2}+\mathrm{HNO}_{3}$
(d) $\mathrm{Cu}\left(\mathrm{NO}_{3}\right)_{2}+\mathrm{OH}^{-}$
22. The number of electrons required to reduce chromium completely in $\mathrm{Cr}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{7}^{2-}$ to $\mathrm{Cr}^{3+}$ in acidic medium, is
[KVPY]
(a) 5
(b) 3
(c) 6
(d) 2
23. Which of the following is a Lewis acid?
[NTSE]
(a) $\mathrm{PH}_{3}$
(b) $\mathrm{NF}_{3}$
(c) NaH
(d) $\mathrm{B}\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3}\right)_{3}$
24. Which of the following salts is the most basic in aqueous solution?
[NTSE]
(a) $\mathrm{Al}(\mathrm{CN})_{3}$
(b) $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOK}$
(c) $\mathrm{FeCl}_{3}$
(d) $\mathrm{Pb}\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COO}\right)_{2}$
25. The number of water molecules(s) not coordinated to copper ion directly in $\mathrm{CuSO}_{4} \cdot 5 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$, is:
[NTSE]
(a) 2
(b) 3
(c) 1
(d) 4
26. The metal that gives hydrogen gas upon treatment with both acid as well as base is :
[NTSE]
(a) magnesium
(b) mercury
(c) zinc
(d) iron
27. Which of the following is an amphoteric hydroxide ?
[JSTSE]
(a) $\mathrm{Sr}(\mathrm{OH})_{2}$
(b) $\mathrm{Ca}(\mathrm{OH})_{2}$
(c) $\mathrm{Mg}(\mathrm{OH})_{2}$
(d) $\mathrm{Be}(\mathrm{OH})_{2}$
28. Conjugate base for Brönsted acids $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ and HF are :
(a) $\mathrm{OH}^{-}$and $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{~F}^{+}$, respectively
[JSTSE]
(b) $\mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{O}^{+}$and $\mathrm{F}^{-}$, respectively
(c) $\mathrm{OH}^{-}$and $\mathrm{F}^{-}$, respectively
(d) $\mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{O}^{+}$and $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{~F}^{+}$, respectively

## More than One Correct Option

DIRECTIONS (Qs. 29-33) : This section contains multiple choice questions. Each question has four choices (a), (b), (c) and (d) out of which ONE OR MORE may be correct.
29. Which of the following statement(s) is/are correct for sulphuric acid?
(a) It is a dibasic acid.
(b) It is hydracid due to presence of hydrogen in it.
(c) Sulphuric acid on reaction with iron releases hydrogen gas.
(d) pH of sulphuric acid is $<7$.
30. Consider the following reaction :
$\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}+\mathrm{NH}_{3} \xrightarrow{\text { Water }} \mathrm{NH}_{4}^{+}+\mathrm{HSO}_{4}^{-}$
Which of the following is/are correct for above equation?
(a) $\mathrm{HSO}_{4}^{-}$is a weak conjugate acid of $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}$
(b) $\mathrm{NH}_{3}$ is a stronger base.
(c) $\mathrm{NH}_{4}^{+}$is a weak conjugate acid of $\mathrm{NH}_{3}$
(d) $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}$ is a weak acid.
31. Which of the following is/are example(s) of Lewis acid?
(a) $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$
(b) $\mathrm{ZnCl}_{2}$
(c) $\mathrm{NH}_{3}$
(d) $\mathrm{BF}_{3}$
32. Which of the following is/are correctly matched?
(a) $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOH}-$
Weak acid
(b) $\mathrm{Cu}(\mathrm{OH})$
Strong base
(c) $\mathrm{Mg}(\mathrm{OH})_{2}$
Strong base
(d) $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{CO}_{3}$
Weak acid
33. Which of the following is/are acidic salts?
(a) $\mathrm{Pb}(\mathrm{OH}) \mathrm{NO}_{3}$
(b) $\mathrm{Cu}(\mathrm{OH}) \mathrm{NO}_{3}$
(c) $\mathrm{KHSO}_{3}$
(d) $\mathrm{NaHCO}_{3}$

## Matching Questions

DIRECTIONS (Qs. 34-35) : Each question contains two columns which have to be matched. Options ( $A, B, C, D$ ) in column I have to be matched with options ( $p, q, r, s$ ) in column II.
34. $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{w}}$ under conditions of high temperature and pressure is $1.0 \times 10^{-10}$. Match the following.

|  | Column I |  |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |

| (D) Solution of pH 7 | (s) Basic |  |


|  | A | B | C | D |

| (a) | s | q, s | p | r |
| (b) | s, r | q, s | p | r |
| (c) | p, s | q | r, s | p |
| (d) | s | p | q | r, s |

(A) Mono basic
(B) Dibasic
(C) Diacidic
(D) Mono acidic

(a) s
(b) p
(c) q
(d) r

## \section*{Assertion \& Reason <br> <br> Assertion \& Reason}

DIRECTIONS (Qs. 36-40) : Each of these questions contains an assertion followed by reason. Read them carefully and answer the question on the basis of following options. You have to select the one that best describes the two statements.
(a) If both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of Assertion.
(b) If both Assertion and Reason are correct, but Reason is not the correct explanation of Assertion.
(c) If Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
(d) If Assertion is incorrect but Reason is correct.
36. Assertion : pH of ammonium chloride solution is in acidic range.
Reason : Solution of a salt of weak base and strong acid is acidic.
37. Assertion : $\mathrm{pH}=7$ signifies pure water.

Reason : At this $\mathrm{pH},\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]=\left[\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right]=10^{-7}$.
38. Assertion : $\mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{PO}_{4}$ and $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}$ are known as polybasic acids.
Reason : They have two or more than two protons per molecule of the acid.
39. Assertion : The acidity of $\mathrm{Mg}(\mathrm{OH})_{2}$ is two.

Reason : The acidity of a base is equal to the number of hydroxyl ions.
40. Assertion : In water, Hydrochloric acid behaves as a weak monobasic acid.
Reason : In water, Hydrochloric acid acts as a proton donor.

## Passage Based Questions

DIRECTIONS (Qs. 41-43) : Study the given passage and answer the related questions.

Salts are produced as a result of neutralisation reaction between an acid and a base. Salts are broadly classified into three types.
(i) Neutral salts are those salts which when dissolved in water form a neutral solution. These are formed by the reaction of strong acids with strong bases. Sodium chloride $(\mathrm{NaCl})$ and sodium sulphate $\left(\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}\right)$ are two examples of neutral salt. The solutions of a neutral salt has no effect on litmus.

Column II
(p) KOH
(q) $\mathrm{Ca}(\mathrm{OH})_{2}$
(r) $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}$
(s) $\mathrm{HNO}_{3}$

D
IIIIII

s

(a) $\mathrm{MgCl}_{2}$
(b) NaCl
