(c) $\underset{\text { Column I }}{\mathrm{Pb}}(\mathrm{OH})$ ◯ ![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-089.jpg?height=39&width=0&top_left_y=1064&top_left_x=1674) $\mathrm{CaCl}_{2}$
43.
(ii) Acidic salts are those salts which when dissolved in water form an acidic solution. These are formed by the neutralisation reaction of a strong acid with a weak base. The solution of an acidic salt turns blue litmus to red. Two examples of acidic salts are ammonium chloride $\left(\mathrm{NH}_{4} \mathrm{Cl}\right)$ and ammonium sulphate $\left(\mathrm{NH}_{4}\right)_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}$.
(iii) Basic salts are those salts which when dissolved in water form a basic solution. These are formed by the neutralisation reaction of a weak acid with a strong base. The solution of a basic salt turns red litmus to blue. Two examples of basic salts are sodium carbonate ( $\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{CO}_{3}$ ) and sodium acetate $\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COONa}\right)$.

## Very Short Answer Questions

1. The products obtained are :
(i) Sodium zincate
(ii) Hydrogen gas
$\mathrm{Zn}(\mathrm{s})+2 \mathrm{NaOH}(\mathrm{aq}) \longrightarrow \mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{ZnO}_{2}(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})$
Sodium zincate
Hydrogen
2. As marble chips consist of calcium carbonate. Thus equation will be
$\mathrm{CaCO}_{3}(\mathrm{~s})+2 \mathrm{HCl}(\mathrm{aq}) \rightarrow \mathrm{CaCl}_{2}(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l})+\mathrm{CO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})$ Marble chips
3. Chemical name : Sodium hydrogen carbonate.

Formula : $\mathrm{NaHCO}_{3}$.
4. Raw materials required for the manufacture of bleaching powder are :
(i) Chlorine, $\mathrm{Cl}_{2}$
(ii) Dry slaked lime, $\mathrm{Ca}(\mathrm{OH})_{2}$.
5. The chemical formula of plaster of paris is $\mathrm{CaSO}_{4} \cdot \frac{1}{2} \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$
6. Consider a reaction between sodium carbonate and dilute Hydrochloric acid.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{CO}_{3}(\mathrm{~s})+2 \mathrm{HCl}(\mathrm{aq}) & \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{NaCl}(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l})+\mathrm{CO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \\
\mathrm{CO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+\underset{\text { Lime water }}{\mathrm{Ca}(\mathrm{OH})_{2}} & \rightarrow \underset{\text { (milkiness) }}{\mathrm{CaCO}_{3}(\mathrm{~s})+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}}
\end{aligned}
$$

7. Acidic aqueous solution contains more hydrogen ions than water. Higher the concentration of $\mathrm{H}^{+}$ions in a solution, more acidic is the solution.
8. Acids are sour in taste, acids turn blue litmus red.
9. $\mathrm{NaOH}(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{HCl}(\mathrm{aq}) \longrightarrow \mathrm{NaCl}(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l})$

In this reaction salt and water are formed. It is an acidbase neutralisation reaction.
10. Salts, having the same positive or negative radicals, are said to belong to the same family.
11. These products are $\mathrm{NaOH}, \mathrm{Cl}_{2}$ gas and $\mathrm{H}_{2}$ gas.
12. Carbon dioxide.

$\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{CO}_{3} \cdot 10 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \xrightarrow{\text { air }} \mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{CO}_{3} \cdot \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}+9 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$
15. Soluble bases in water are called alkalies e.g. NaOH , KOH are alkalies.
16. Water is a weak acid as it is ionised to a small extent and produces only a small number of $\mathrm{H}^{+}$.

## Short Answer Questions

1. An acid which is completely ionised in water and produces a large amount of hydrogen ions are called strong acids.
Example : $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}, \mathrm{HNO}_{3}$. An acid which is feebly ionised or remains almost unionised in aqueous solution is called weak acid. Example : $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOH}, \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{3}$ and $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{CO}_{3}$.
2. Water of crystallisation is the fixed number of water molecules present in one formula unit of a salt.
Example : $\mathrm{CuSO}_{4} \cdot 5 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$. Five water molecules are present in one formula unit of copper sulphate.
Some other salts with water of crystallisation are :

| $\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{CO}_{3} \cdot 10 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ | Sodium carbonate decahydrate |
| :--- | :--- |
|  | (Washing soda) |
| $\mathrm{CaSO}_{4} \cdot 2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ | Calcium sulphate dihydrate (Gypsum) |

3. 

(a) strong acid
(b) weak acid
(c) strong base
(d) weak base
4.
(a) KOH and $\mathrm{HClO}_{3}$
(b) $\mathrm{Al}(\mathrm{OH})_{3}$ and $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{3}$
(c) $\mathrm{Ba}(\mathrm{OH})_{2}$ and $\mathrm{HNO}_{2}$
(d) $\mathrm{NH}_{4} \mathrm{OH}$ and $\mathrm{HNO}_{3}$

(ii) Quick lime forms milky solution with water.

$$
\mathrm{CaO}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Ca}(\mathrm{OH})_{2}

6. Plaster of Paris is $\mathrm{CaSO}_{4} \cdot 1 / 2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$

Gypsum is $\mathrm{CaSO}_{4} \cdot 2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$
$\mathrm{CaSO}_{4} \cdot 2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \longrightarrow \mathrm{CaSO}_{4} \cdot \frac{1}{2} \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}+\frac{3}{2} \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$
$\underset{\substack{\text { Caster of Paris } \\ \mathrm{CaSO}_{4} \cdot 1 / 2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}}}{\text { Gypsum }}+\underset{\text { Plaster of Paris }}{3 / 2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}} \longrightarrow \underset{\text { Gypsum }}{\mathrm{CaSO}_{4} \cdot 2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}}$
It is used for plastering fractured bones.
7. (i) It is weakly alkaline in nature, therefore, it can neutralize excess of acid in the stomach.
(ii) It decomposes to form carbon dioxide which makes the bread or cake fluffy.
8. When a strong acid is mixed with water, most of the molecules of strong acid dissociate to produce hydrogen ions $\left(\mathrm{H}^{+}\right)$. These $\mathrm{H}^{+}$ions combine with water to produce hydronium ions $\left(\mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{O}^{+}\right)$. Hence most of the molecules of a strong acid get converted to hydronium ions when mixed with water.
9. When a weak acid is added to a concentrated solution of hydrochloric acid, the solution becomes more acidic because it increases the hydronium ion concentration of the solution.
10. Washing soda is $\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{CO}_{3} \cdot 10 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$. Its chemical name is sodium carbonate decahydrate. When washing soda is exposed to air, it loses water of crystallization and become amorphous.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-091.jpg?height=127&width=702&top_left_y=525&top_left_x=1151)
11. Complete neutralization requires one $\mathrm{H}^{+}$for each $\mathrm{OH}^{-}$. Since $\mathrm{Al}(\mathrm{OH})_{3}$ has three available $\mathrm{OH}^{-}$ions and $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}$ can only provide two $\mathrm{H}^{+}$ions, the reaction requires two moles of $\mathrm{Al}(\mathrm{OH})_{3}$ for three moles of $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}$.
$2 \mathrm{Al}(\mathrm{OH})_{3}+3 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Al}_{2}\left(\mathrm{SO}_{4}\right)_{3}+6 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$
12. Glass industry and cement industry are based on the use of washing soda.
13. Sodium sulphate get dissolved in water to form strong acid and strong base, so it is neutral.
$\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{NaOH}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}$
On the other hand, sodium carbonate gives strong base and weak acid in water, so it is basic in nature.
$\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{CO}_{3}+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \longrightarrow \underset{\text { strong base }}{2 \mathrm{NaOH}}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{CO}_{3}$
14. (i) Tomato: Malic acid and citric acid
(ii) Vinegar : Acetic acid
(iii) Tamarind : Tartaric acid and citric acid
15. (i) $\mathrm{X}=$ chlorine gas, $\mathrm{Y}=$ Calcium oxychloride
(ii) $\underset{\substack{\text { (Dry slaked } \\ \text { lime) }}}{\mathrm{Ca}(\mathrm{OH})_{2}}+\underset{\text { Chlorine }}{\mathrm{Cl}_{2}} \longrightarrow \underset{\substack{\text { (Calcium } \\ \text { oxychloride) } \\ \text { (Bleaching } \\ \text { agent) }}}{\mathrm{CaOCl}_{2}}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$
16. (a) (i) No change in colour
(ii) Red litmus turns blue
(b) (i) $2 \mathrm{NaOH}(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{FeSO}_{4}(\mathrm{aq}) \longrightarrow$
$\begin{array}{ll}\begin{array}{c}\text { Sodium } \\ \text { hydroxide }\end{array} & \begin{array}{l}\text { Ferrous } \\ \text { sulphate }\end{array}\end{array}$

$$
\mathrm{Fe}(\mathrm{OH})_{2}(\mathrm{~s})+\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}(\mathrm{aq})
$$

Ferrous Sodium sulphate hydroxide

| :---: |

\mathrm{Al}(\mathrm{OH})_{3}(\mathrm{~s})+\mathrm{NaCl}(\mathrm{aq})
$$

17. NaCl is obtained from the hydrochloric acid and sodium hydroxide base.
It is a neutral salt as it is obtained from strong acid and strong base.
Large crystals of solid salt are often brown due to impurities. This is called rock salt.
Beds of rock salt were formed when seas of bygone ages dried up.
18. Since blue litmus solution is added in both test tube, so the colour change is observed in test tube in which acid is present and color changes from blue to red.
Test tube A: Acid present (color change from blue to red)
Test tube B: Base present (color doesn't change, blue remains blue since NaOH is basic)
19. (i) ' G ' is Chlorine $(\mathrm{Cl})$ and ' C ' is bleaching powder $\left(\mathrm{CaOCl}_{2}\right)$.
(ii) Chemical equation :-

$$
\mathrm{Ca}(\mathrm{OH})_{2}+\mathrm{Cl}_{2} \rightarrow \mathrm{CaOCl}_{2}+\mathrm{H}_{2}
$$

(iii) Common name is bleaching powder and chemical name is calcium oxychloride.

## Long Answer Questions

1. (i) $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ gas is formed.

$$
\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{CO}_{3}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4} \text { (dil.) } \longrightarrow \mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}+\mathrm{CO}_{2}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}
$$

(ii) $\mathrm{SO}_{2}$ gas is formed.

$$
\mathrm{S}+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4} \text { (conc.) } \longrightarrow 3 \mathrm{SO}_{2}+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}
$$

(iii) Lime water turns milky due to formation of calcium sulphite.

$$
\mathrm{SO}_{2}+\mathrm{Ca}(\mathrm{OH})_{2} \longrightarrow \mathrm{CaSO}_{3}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}
$$

2. (i) Sodium chloride $(\mathrm{NaCl})$, Ammonia $\left(\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right)$, Limestone $\left(\mathrm{CaCO}_{3}\right)$.
(ii) It is done by filtration.
(iii) Sodium hydrogen carbonate is heated to get sodium carbonate.

$$
2 \mathrm{NaHCO}_{3} \xrightarrow{\text { heat }} \mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{CO}_{3}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}+\mathrm{CO}_{2}
$$

The process was developed by Ernest Solvay. Following are few important uses of $\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{CO}_{3}$.
(i) It is used for softening of hard water.
(ii) A mixture of $\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{CO}_{3}$ and $\mathrm{K}_{2} \mathrm{CO}_{3}$ is used as a fusion mixture.
(iii) It is used as an important laboratory reagent both in qualitative and quantitative analysis.
(iv) It is used for the manufacture of glass, borax, soap and caustic soda ( NaOH )
3. ' X ' is sodium bicarbonate $\left(\mathrm{NaHCO}_{3}\right)$. It is a constituent of baking powder and is used as an antacid. When ' X ' is heated, it gives out $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ and steam. $2 \mathrm{NaHCO}_{3}(\mathrm{~s}) \xrightarrow{\text { heat }} \mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{CO}_{3}(\mathrm{~s})+\mathrm{CO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \uparrow+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{g})$ When $\mathrm{CO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})$ is passed through lime water, it turns milky due to formation of calcium carbonate.
$\mathrm{Ca}(\mathrm{OH})_{2}+\mathrm{CO}_{2} \longrightarrow \mathrm{CaCO}_{3}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ Lime water Carbon dioxide White ppt.
Sodium bicarbonate as baking powder makes the cake and biscuits fluffy, and as antacid it neutralizes hyperacidity in the stomach.
4. The compound is bleaching powder. It is used for disinfecting drinking water.
It is manufactured by reaction of dry slaked lime with chlorine gas.

$$
\mathrm{Ca}(\mathrm{OH})_{2}+\mathrm{Cl}_{2} \longrightarrow \mathrm{CaOCl}_{2}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}
$$

If it is exposed to air, it reacts with $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ gas and liberates chlorine gas.
$\mathrm{CaOCl}_{2}+\mathrm{CO}_{2} \longrightarrow \mathrm{CaCO}_{3}+\mathrm{Cl}_{2}$ (Chlorine gas)

## Uses of bleaching power

(1) It is widely used as a bleaching agent for bleaching clothes.
(2) It is used in manufacture of chloroform $\left(\mathrm{CHCl}_{3}\right)$.
(3) It is used as an oxidising agent.
(4) It is also used in bath room cleaners, household disinfectant sprays, mass and algae removers and weed killers.
5. Yes, it is possible for a salt to behave as a base. Consider a salt formed by the action of a strong base ( NaOH ) and a weak acid $\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOH}\right)$ acetic acid.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-092.jpg?height=104&width=689&top_left_y=1233&top_left_x=1105)

This salt of a strong base and a weak acid behaves as a strong base in aqueous solution because when sodium acetate is dissolved in water it gives $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOH}$ (weakly ionised) and NaOH (completely ionised) so in solution there are more $\mathrm{OH}^{-}$ions than $\mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{O}^{+}$ions and the solution of this salt $\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COONa}\right)$ behaves as a base.

## Reasoning Based Questions

1. This is due to difference in number of ion produced by them in solution.
2. Tooth decay start when the pH of mouth is lower than 5.5 because the acid in our mouth which is formed by the bacterial break down of sugar molecules becomes strong enough to attack the enamel of our teeth and causes tooth decay.
3. Soil $\mathrm{A}(\mathrm{pH}=8)$ is too basic and soil $\mathrm{B}(\mathrm{pH}=4)$ is too acidic. Therefore plant growth in these soil affected badly. Most of the plant grow at the neutral $\mathrm{pH}(\mathrm{pH}=$ 7) of the soil. Soil B should be treated with powdered chalk to reduce its acidity. Powdered chalk act as base and react with excess acid present in the soil B.
4. When a bee stings a person, it inject an acidic liquid into the skin. To neutralise bee sting a mild base such as baking soda should be used. While wasp sting has alkaline nature therefore it should be treated with weak acid such as vinegar.
5. When we eat food, then the bacteria present in our mouth break down the sugar molecules in food to form acid. Because of the formation of acid from sugar molecule in the mouth, pH becomes less than 7 .
6. The presence of water of crystalisation in copper sulphate impart it blue colour. When $\mathrm{CuSO}_{4} \cdot 5 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ dehydrated water of crystalisation is lost therefore blue colour disappeared.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-093.jpg?height=113&width=604&top_left_y=724&top_left_x=351)
7. When ammonium chloride dissolved in water, it get hydrolysed to form ammonium hydroxide (weak base) and hydrochloric acid (strong acid). Hydrochloric acid completely ionised and produces large amount of $\mathrm{H}^{+}$ ions in the solution. Ammonium hydroxide is slightly ionised and produces small amount of $\mathrm{OH}^{-}$ions. Aqueous solution of $\mathrm{NH}_{4} \mathrm{Cl}$ contains more of $\mathrm{H}^{+}$ions than $\mathrm{OH}^{-}$ions therefore it is acidic.
$\mathrm{NH}_{4} \mathrm{Cl}(\mathrm{s})+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l}) \rightleftharpoons \underset{\text { weak base }}{\mathrm{NH}_{4} \mathrm{OH}(\mathrm{aq})}+\underset{\text { strong acid }}{\mathrm{HCl}(\mathrm{aq})}$

## HOTS Questions

1. $\mathrm{NaOH}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4} \longrightarrow \mathrm{NaHSO}_{4}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$
$2 \mathrm{NaOH}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4} \longrightarrow \underset{\text { Sodium sulphate }}{\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}}+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$
2. Chloride of lime is $\mathrm{CaOCl}_{2}$ whereas calcium chloride is $\mathrm{CaCl}_{2}$. Chloride of lime ( $\mathrm{CaOCl}_{2}$ ) reacts with $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ of atmosphere to form $\mathrm{CaCO}_{3}$ and $\mathrm{Cl}_{2}$.
$\mathrm{CaOCl}_{2}+\mathrm{CO}_{2} \longrightarrow \mathrm{CaCO}_{3}+\mathrm{Cl}_{2} \uparrow$
3. Sodium chloride $(\mathrm{NaCl})$, ammonia $\left(\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right)$ and limestone $\left(\mathrm{CaCO}_{3}\right)$ are raw materials used for the manufacture of washing soda by Solvay process.
$\mathrm{NH}_{3}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}+\mathrm{CO}_{2} \longrightarrow\left(\mathrm{NH}_{4}\right) \mathrm{HCO}_{3}$
$\left(\mathrm{NH}_{4}\right) \mathrm{HCO}_{3}+\mathrm{NaCl} \longrightarrow \mathrm{NaHCO}_{3}+\mathrm{NH}_{4} \mathrm{Cl}$
$\mathrm{CaCO}_{3} \xrightarrow{\text { heat }} \mathrm{CaO}+\mathrm{CO}_{2}$
$\mathrm{CaO}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Ca}(\mathrm{OH})_{2}$
$\mathrm{Ca}(\mathrm{OH})_{2}+2 \mathrm{NH}_{4} \mathrm{Cl} \longrightarrow \mathrm{CaCl}_{2}+2 \mathrm{NH}_{3}+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$
$2 \mathrm{NaHCO}_{3} \xrightarrow{\text { heat }} \mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{CO}_{3}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}+\mathrm{CO}_{2}$
$\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{CO}_{3}+10 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \longrightarrow \underset{\text { Washing soda }}{\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{CO}_{3}} \cdot 10 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$
4. Ashes contain potassium carbonate $\left(\mathrm{K}_{2} \mathrm{CO}_{3}\right)$ which gives a basic solution when dissolved in water because when $\mathrm{K}_{2} \mathrm{CO}_{3}$ is dissolved in water it forms KOH (strong base) and $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{CO}_{3}$ (weak acid)
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-093.jpg?height=106&width=663&top_left_y=2437&top_left_x=349)

Because of its basic nature people once used ashes to wash their hands.
5. (a) $2 \mathrm{HBr}+\mathrm{Ca}(\mathrm{OH})_{2} \longrightarrow \mathrm{CaBr}_{2}+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$
(b) $2 \mathrm{HClO}_{2}+\mathrm{Sr}(\mathrm{OH})_{2} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Sr}\left(\mathrm{ClO}_{2}\right)_{2}+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$
(c) $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{~S}+2 \mathrm{LiOH} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Li}_{2} \mathrm{~S}+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$
6. The corrosive properties of an acid and a base get very much diminished after they (i.e. acid and base) neutralize each other. Generally, salts are much less corrosive as compared to the acid and the base from which they are formed.
7. (a) Baking soda is sodium hydrogen carbonate. On heating, it is converted into sodium carbonate which is bitter to taste.

$$
2 \mathrm{NaHCO}_{3} \xrightarrow{\text { Heat }} \mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{CO}_{3}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}+\mathrm{CO}_{2}
$$

(b) Baking soda can be converted into baking powder by the addition of appropriate amount of tartaric acid to it.
(c) The role of tartaric acid is to neutralise sodium carbonate and cake will not taste bitter.
8. The acid present in ant sting is methanoic acid (formic acid). The chemical formula is HCOOH . To get relief one should apply any available basic salt e.g., baking soda $\left(\mathrm{NaHCO}_{3}\right)$ on it.

Exercise 2
Master NCERT (Textbook \& Exemplar)

## Text - Book Questions

1. Dip a piece of red litmus paper separately in each of the given test-tubes. Note the changes in colour of litmus paper.
(i) The test-tube in which the colour of red litmus paper turns purple indicates neutral nature i.e. it contains water.
(ii) The test-tube in which the colour of red litmus paper turns blue indicates basic nature i.e. it contains the basic solution.
(iii) The test-tube in which the colour of red litmus paper remains unchanged indicates acidic nature i.e. it contains acidic solution.
2. Sour substances generally contain an acid. Curd also contains an acid. The acid present in curd and sour
substances is likely to react with copper or brass vessels and forms certain salts which are poisonous in nature (i.e. toxic compounds). To avoid any such possibility, it is advisable not to store curd and sour substances in brass and copper vessels.
Note : Brass is an alloy of copper and zinc.
3. The gas usually liberated by the action of an acid with a metal is hydrogen $\left(\mathrm{H}_{2}\right)$.
Metal + Acid ⟶ Salt + Hydrogen
$\mathrm{Zn}(\mathrm{s})+2 \mathrm{HCl}(\mathrm{aq}) \longrightarrow \mathrm{ZnCl}_{2}(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})$
Test for presence of gas :
When the gas is passed through soap solution, the gas filled bubbles are formed. When a burning match stick is brought near the hydrogen gas filled soap bubbles, the bubbles burn with a pop sound. It shows the presence of hydrogen gas.
4. $\quad \mathrm{A}+\mathrm{HCl}($ dil $) \longrightarrow \mathrm{CaCl}_{2}+$ gas

Since the gas is formed with effervescence and extinguishes a burning candle, so the gas is carbon dioxide.
$\mathrm{A}+$ dil. $\mathrm{HCl} \longrightarrow \mathrm{CaCl}_{2}+\mathrm{CO}_{2}$
Since the metal compound reacts with dil. HCl to produce $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$, the metal compound is a carbonate. Because $\mathrm{CaCl}_{2}$ is one of the products so the metal salt is $\mathrm{CaCO}_{3}$ (calcium carbonate). The reaction that occurs is :
$\mathrm{CaCO}_{3}+\mathrm{HCl} \longrightarrow \mathrm{CaCl}_{2}+\mathrm{CO}_{2}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$
Balancing the equation, we get

$$
\mathrm{CaCO}_{3}+2 \mathrm{HCl} \longrightarrow \mathrm{CaCl}_{2}+\mathrm{CO}_{2} \uparrow+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}
$$

5. The acids $\left(\mathrm{HCl}, \mathrm{HNO}_{3}\right.$ etc.) have replaceable hydrogen atoms in their molecule and they release hydrogens ion $\left(\mathrm{H}^{+}\right)$in aqueous solution. Since they release $\mathrm{H}^{+}$ion so they show acidic character.
Compounds, alcohol $\left(\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{OH}\right)$ and glucose $\left(\mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{12} \mathrm{O}_{6}\right)$ fail to release $\mathrm{H}^{+}$ions in their aqueous solution because of absence of replaceable hydrogen atom in their molecule.
Since they do not release $\mathrm{H}^{+}$ion in aqueous solution so they do not show acidic properties in solution.
6. In an aqueous solution, an acid dissociates to produce $\mathrm{H}^{+}$ions and some other anion. Due to the presence of ions the aqueous solution of an acid conducts electricity.

$$
\mathrm{HCl} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{H}^{+}+\mathrm{Cl}^{-}
$$

7. The change in colour of litmus is observed only in presence of $\mathrm{H}^{+}$ions (acidic nature) or $\mathrm{OH}^{-}$ions (basic nature).
Since dry HCl gas fails to produce any $\mathrm{H}^{+}$ions so the colour of dry litmus paper does not change when it comes in contact of dry HCl gas.
Note : Acids produce $\mathrm{H}^{+}$ions in aqueous solution.
8. It is because if water is added to acid, heat evolved will be very high and bottle may break. On the other hand, if acid is added to water heat evolved will be absorbed by water as it has high specific heat. Thus, it prevents any accidental chances.
9. The concentration of hydronium ions $\left(\mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{O}^{+}\right)$decreases when solution of an acid is diluted with water.
Note : Mixing of an acid or base with water results in decrease in the concentration of ions ( $\mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{O}^{+} / \mathrm{OH}^{-}$) per unit volume. Such a process is called dilution and the acid or base is said to be diluted.
10. Sodium hydroxide $(\mathrm{NaOH})$ is a strong base (i.e. it is completely ionised in solution). When more of NaOH is dissolved, more $\mathrm{OH}^{-}$will be produced and thus there will be an increase in concentration of $\mathrm{OH}^{-}$ions.
11. We know that $p H$ of a solution is inversely proportional to its $H^{+}$ion concentration (i.e. lesser the pH more will be $\mathrm{H}^{+}$ion concentration).
Thus the solution $\mathbf{A}$ with pH 6 has more $\mathrm{H}^{+}$ion concentration than the solution $\mathbf{B}$ with pH 8 .
We also know that for an acidic solution the pH lies between 0 to 7 and for a basic solution the pH range is 7-14.

Thus $\mathbf{A}$ with pH 6 is acidic and $\mathbf{B}$ with pH 8 is basic.
12. The acidic nature of a solution is directly related to the concentration of $\mathrm{H}^{+}(a q)$ ions in solution. More the concentration of $\mathrm{H}^{+}(a q)$ ions in solution, more acidic will be the solution.
Thus if the concentration of $\mathrm{H}^{+}(a q)$ ions increases in a solution, the solution becomes more acidic in nature and if the concentration of $\mathrm{H}^{+}(a q)$ ions in a solution decreases it becomes less acidic in nature.
13. Yes, basic solution also have $\mathrm{H}^{+}(a q)$ ions present in them. Actually these solutions are prepared in water. Water is a weak electrolyte (slightly ionised) and produces $\mathrm{H}^{+}$and $\mathrm{OH}^{-}$ions.

$$
\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{H}^{+}+\mathrm{OH}^{-}
$$

However, the number of $\mathrm{H}^{+}$ions released by water is small in comparison to $\mathrm{OH}^{-}$ions released by a base and also by water. Because of this (number of $\mathrm{OH}^{-}>$number of $\mathrm{H}^{+}$), the solution is basic in nature.
14. The farmer will treat the soil of his fields with quick lime (a basic oxide) or slaked lime (basic hydroxide) or chalk (a metal carbonate) if the soil is acidic. An acidic soil will react with these basic substances and a neutralization reaction will occur. In this way the acidic nature of the soil will get neutralized.
15. Common name of this compound is bleaching powder.
16. Slaked lime or calcium hydroxide $\left[\mathrm{Ca}(\mathrm{OH})_{2}\right]$

$$
\underset{\text { Slaked lime }}{\mathrm{Ca}(\mathrm{OH})_{2}(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{Cl}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \longrightarrow \underset{\text { Bleaching powder }}{\mathrm{CaOCl}_{2}(\mathrm{~s})+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}}}
$$

17. Washing soda or sodium carbonate $\left(\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{CO}_{3} \cdot 10 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}\right)$
18. Whence solution of sodium hydrogen carbonate is heated, it decomposes to give sodium carbonate with the evolution of carbondioxide.

$$
2 \mathrm{NaHCO}_{3}(\mathrm{aq}) \xrightarrow{\text { heat }} \mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{CO}_{3}(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{CO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l})
$$

19. $\mathrm{CaSO}_{4} \cdot \frac{1}{2} \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}+\frac{3}{2} \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \longrightarrow \mathrm{CaSO}_{4} \cdot 2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$

Plaster of Paris Gypsum

## Text - Book Exercise

1. (d) The red litmus solution turns blue in basic solution. The pH of basic solution is more than 7 .
2. (b) Since the gas produced turns lime water milky so the gas is $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$. The egg-shell is made of $\mathrm{CaCO}_{3}$ which reacts with an acid (dil. HCl ) to produce $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$.
$\mathrm{CaCO}_{3}+2 \mathrm{HCl} \longrightarrow \mathrm{CaCl}_{2}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}+\mathrm{CO}_{2} \uparrow$

$$
\begin{aligned}
20 \mathrm{~mL} \text { of } \mathrm{NaOH} \text { will require } \mathrm{HCl} & =\frac{8}{10} \times 20 \mathrm{~mL} \\
& =16 \mathrm{~mL}
\end{aligned}
$$

4. (c) We use antacids for treating indigestion.
5. (a) Zinc + Sulphuric acid ⟶

$$
\mathrm{Zn}(\mathrm{~s})+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}(\mathrm{aq}) \xrightarrow{\substack{\text { Zinc sulphate }+ \text { Hydrogen } \\ \mathrm{ZnSO}_{4}(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})}}
$$

(b) Magnesium + Hydrochloric acid ⟶

Magnesium chloride + Hydrogen

$$
\mathrm{Mg}(\mathrm{~s})+2 \mathrm{HCl}(\mathrm{aq}) \longrightarrow \mathrm{MgCl}_{2}(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})
$$

(c) Aluminium + Sulphuric acid ⟶

Aluminium sulphate + Hydrogen

$$
2 \mathrm{Al}(\mathrm{~s})+3 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}(\mathrm{aq}) \longrightarrow \mathrm{Al}_{2}\left(\mathrm{SO}_{4}\right)_{3}(\mathrm{aq})+3 \mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})
$$

(d) Iron + Hydrochloric acid ⟶

$$
\mathrm{Fe}(\mathrm{~s})+2 \mathrm{HCl}(\mathrm{aq}) \xrightarrow{\text { Iron (II) chloride }+\mathrm{FeCl}_{2}(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})}
$$

6. From their chemical formula it is evident that both alcohol ( $\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{OH}$ ) and glucose ( $\mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{12} \mathrm{O}_{6}$ ) contain hydrogen. But in their aqueous solution they do not produce any $\mathrm{H}^{+}$ion so they are not categorised as acids.

## ACTIVITY

Materials required: Beaker, cork, nails (iron), battery $(6 \mathrm{~V})$, connecting wires, bulb, switch, ethyl alcohol or glucose.

## Procedure :

1. Set up the apparatus as shown in figure.

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-095.jpg?height=472&width=496&top_left_y=374&top_left_x=1231)
Figure

2. Take ethyl alcohol/glucose solution in the beaker such that the upper ends of the nails remain slightly above the level of the liquid.
3. Switch on and observe if the bulb glows (If bulb glows then the solution is conducting i.e., it contains $\mathrm{H}^{+}$ion otherwise not).
4. Repeat it with the other solution.

In both the cases we find that the bulb does not glow. It proves that there are no $\mathrm{H}^{+}$ion in solution and so the substance is not acidic.
7. Distilled water does not conduct electricity because it does not contain any ionic compound like acids, bases or salts, dissolved in it. Rain water, while falling to the earth through the atmosphere, dissolves acidic gases like $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$, $\mathrm{SO}_{2}$ etc. from the air thus forms acids like carbonic acid $\left(\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{CO}_{3}\right)$, sulphurous acid $\left(\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{3}\right)$ etc. These acids provides ions to rain water. So, due to presence of these acids, the rain water conducts electricity.
8. The ionisation of an acid occures only in an aqueous solution (i.e., in presence of water). Thus an acid releases $\mathrm{H}^{+}$ions only in presence of water and so it shows acidic behaviour only in presence of water and not in absence of water.
9. (a) $\mathrm{D}(\mathrm{pH} 7)$, The pH of a neutral solution is 7 .
(b) $\mathrm{C}(\mathrm{pH} 12)$, Higher the value of pH , stronger is the alkaline nature of the solution.
(c) $\mathrm{B}(\mathrm{pH} 2)$, Lower the value of pH , more acidic the solution is.
(d) E, The pH of an alkaline solution lies in the range of 7 to 14 .
(e) $\mathrm{A}(\mathrm{pH} 4)$, The pH of an acidic solution lies in the range of 0 to 7 .
pH of a solution is inversely proportional to its $\mathrm{H}^{+}$ion concentration. Thus the required arrangement is

$$
\mathrm{C}<\mathrm{E}<\mathrm{D}<\mathrm{A}<\mathrm{B} .
$$

10. Fizzing in the reaction occurs due to the evolution of the $\mathrm{H}_{2}$ gas by the action of acid on a metal (magnesium).

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \underset{\text { (test tube A) }}{\mathrm{Mg}(\mathrm{~s})+2 \mathrm{HCl}(\mathrm{aq})} \longrightarrow \mathrm{MgCl}_{2}(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \\
& \mathrm{Mg}(\mathrm{~s})+2 \mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOH}(\mathrm{aq}) \longrightarrow \\
& \text { (test tube B) } \\
& \left(\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COO}\right)_{2} \mathrm{Mg}(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})
\end{aligned}
$$

Since HCl is a stronger acid as compared to acetic acid, Fizzing occurs more vigorously in test tube A.
Note : HCl is mineral acid (completely ionised) whereas $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOH}$ is an organic acid (slightly ionised).
11. When milk changes into curd there occurs a decrease in pH . It happens because of the fact that lactose (a carbohydrate) present in milk gets converted into lactic acid (an acid). As more of acid is formed, the pH of medium decreases (i.e., it becomes more acidic)
12. (a) We know that the pH of fresh milk is 6 , thus it is slightly acidic due to the presence of lactic acid in it. Upon standing, its pH decreases slowly and it becomes more acidic because more of lactic acid is produced. Milkman adds a very small amount of baking soda $\left(\mathrm{NaHCO}_{3}\right)$ to fresh milk to make the medium slightly alkaline. The base released by baking soda will neutralise the effect of lactic acid present in milk and helps to check the milk getting sour.
(b) When milk sets as curd, the pH decreases and so it becomes more acidic. In case of alkaline medium due to addition of $\mathrm{NaHCO}_{3}$ it will take longer time to achieve acidic medium back so that milk may set as curd.
13. Plaster of paris should be stored in a moisture-proof container because the presence of moisture can cause slow setting of Plaster of paris by bringing out its hydration. This will make the Plaster of paris useless after sometime.
$\mathrm{CaSO}_{4} \cdot \frac{1}{2} \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}+\frac{3}{2} \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \longrightarrow \mathrm{CaSO}_{4} \cdot 2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$.
Plaster of paris
14. A reaction which occurs between an acid and a base to produce salt and water is known as neutralisation reaction.
Acid + Base → Salt + Water
Examples :

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{NaOH}(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{HCl}(\mathrm{aq}) \longrightarrow \mathrm{NaCl}(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l}) \\
& \mathrm{KOH}(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{HNO}_{3}(\mathrm{aq}) \longrightarrow \mathrm{KNO}_{3}(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l})
\end{aligned}
$$

15. Uses of washing soda
(i) Used as a cleansing agent for domestic purposes.
(ii) Used in manufacture of soap, paper, chemicals such as NaOH , borax etc.
Uses of baking soda
(i) Used in baking powder.
(ii) Used in antacids.

## Exemplar Questions

1. Using chemical indicator like phenolphthalein or natural indicators like turmeric, china rose etc. Alternatively, with $\mathrm{NaHCO}_{3}$ solution which gives effervescences of $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ with acid, but not with base.
2. When zinc reacts with dilute solution of strong acid, it forms salt and hydrogen gas is evolved.
$\mathrm{Zn}+2 \mathrm{HCl} \rightarrow \mathrm{ZnCl}_{2}+\mathrm{H}_{2}$
When a burning splinter is brought near the mouth of the test tube, the gas burns with a pop sound.
3. In the manufacture of sodium hydroxide, hydrogen gas and chlorine gas ( X ) are formed as by-products. When chlorine gas ( X ) reacts with dry slaked lime, it forms calcium oxychloride (bleaching powder) Y. The reactions are
$2 \mathrm{NaCl}(\mathrm{aq})+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l}) \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{NaOH}(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{Cl}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+\mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})$
$\mathrm{X}-\mathrm{Cl}_{2}$ (Chlorine gas)
$\mathrm{Ca}(\mathrm{OH})_{2}(\mathrm{~s})+\mathrm{Cl}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \longrightarrow \mathrm{CaOCl}_{2}(\mathrm{~s})+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$
Y -Calcium oxychloride (bleaching powder)
4. The gas evolved at anode during electrolysis of brine is chlorine ( G ). When chlorine gas is passed through dry $\mathrm{Ca}(\mathrm{OH})_{2}(\mathrm{Y})$ produces bleaching powder (Z), used for disinfecting drinking water.

$$
\underset{\text { Slaked lime (Y) }}{\mathrm{Ca}(\mathrm{OH})_{2}}+\mathrm{Cl}_{2} \longrightarrow \underset{\text { Bleaching powder (Z) }}{\mathrm{CaOCl}_{2}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}}
$$

Since Y and Z are calcium salts, therefore X is also a calcium salt and it is calcium carbonate.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-096.jpg?height=76&width=584&top_left_y=1480&top_left_x=1082)
$\underset{\mathrm{Y}}{\mathrm{Ca}(\mathrm{OH})_{2}}+\mathrm{CO}_{2} \longrightarrow \underset{\mathrm{X}}{\mathrm{CaCO}_{3}}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$
5. $\mathrm{X}-\mathrm{NaOH}$ (Sodium hydroxide)
$\mathrm{A}-\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{ZnO}_{2}$ (Sodium zincate)
B - NaCl (Sodium chloride)
$\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COONa}$ (Sodium acetate)

## Exercise 4

Foundation Builder +

1. (b)
2. (a) $\left(\mathrm{HSO}_{4}\right)^{-}$can accept and donate a proton
$\left(\mathrm{HSO}_{4}\right)^{-}+\mathrm{H}^{+} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}$ (acting as base)
$\left(\mathrm{HSO}_{4}\right)^{-}-\mathrm{H}^{+} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{SO}_{4}^{2-}$ (acting as acid)
3. (d) Given concentration of $\mathrm{NaOH}=10^{-10} \mathrm{M}$
$\underset{10^{-10} \mathrm{M}}{\mathrm{NaOH}} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Na}^{+}+\underset{10^{-10}}{\mathrm{OH}^{-}}$
$\therefore\left[\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right]$from $\mathrm{NaOH}=10^{-10}$
We have to consider dissociation of $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$
[ $\mathrm{OH}^{-}$] from $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}=10^{-7}$
Total $\left[\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right]=10^{-7}+10^{-10}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
& =10^{-7}(0.001+1)=10^{-7}\left(\frac{1001}{1000}\right) \\
& =10^{-10} \times 1001
\end{aligned}
$$

$\therefore \mathrm{pOH}=-\log \left[\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right]$
$=-\left(\log 1001 \times 10^{-10}\right)=-3.004+10=6.9996$
$\mathrm{pH}=14-\mathrm{pOH}=14-6.996=7.004$
$\therefore \mathrm{pH}$ of $10^{-10} \mathrm{M} \mathrm{NaOH}$ solution is nearest to 7 .
4. (b) Diacidic base has two replaceable hydroxyl groups. - OH groups present in compounds (a) and (c) represent alcoholic group, not base.
5. (d) Lewis acids are electron pair acceptor whereas Bronsted acids are proton donor.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-098.jpg?height=104&width=372&top_left_y=452&top_left_x=328)
6. (b) Because $\mathrm{NH}_{3}$ after losing a proton $\left(\mathrm{H}^{+}\right)$gives $\mathrm{NH}_{2}^{-}$.

$$
\mathrm{NH}_{3}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{NH}_{2}^{-}+\mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{O}^{+}
$$

(Conjugate acid-base pair differs only by a proton)
7. (c) Proton donors are acids. Thus conjugate base of an acid has one $\mathrm{H}^{+}$less than the acid.

$$
\underset{\text { Acid }}{\mathrm{HBr}} \longrightarrow \underset{\text { Conjugate base }}{\mathrm{Br}^{-}}+\mathrm{H}^{+}
$$

8. (c) Electron acceptors are Lewis acids.
$\mathrm{AlCl}_{3}$ is electron deficient compound (Al has 6 electrons in valence shell), so it is electron acceptor and Lewis acid.
9. (d) Conjugate acid of $\mathrm{HF}_{2}^{-}$is $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{~F}_{2}$.

\underset{\text { Base }}{\mathrm{HF}_{2}^{-}}+\mathrm{H}^{+} \longrightarrow \underset{\text { Conjugate acid }}{\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{~F}_{2}}
$$

10. (c) $\mathrm{HSO}_{4}^{-}$accepts a proton to form $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}$.

Thus $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}$ is the conjugate acid of $\mathrm{HSO}_{4}^{-}$.

$$
\underset{\text { Base }}{\mathrm{HSO}_{4}^{-}} \xrightarrow{+\mathrm{H}^{+}} \underset{\substack{\text { Conjugate acid } \\ \text { of } \mathrm{HSO}_{4}^{-}}}{\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}}
$$

11. (a) Molarity (M) of $\mathrm{HCl}=10 \mathrm{M}$. HCl is a strong acid and it is completely dissociated in aqueous solutions as :

$$
\mathrm{HCl} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{H}^{+}+\mathrm{Cl}^{-}
$$

So, for every mole of HCl , there is one $\mathrm{H}^{+}$. Therefore $\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]=[\mathrm{HCl}]$ or $\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]=10$.
$\mathrm{pH}=-\log \left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]=-\log [10]=-1$.
12. (b) According to Arrhenius, acids are those substances which give proton in aqueous solution, hence gaseous HCl is not an Arrhenius acid.
13. (b) $\mathrm{pH}=-\log \left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]=\log \left[\frac{1}{\mathrm{H}^{+}}\right]$

## Think out of the box

## Case Study - 1

1. Only $\mathrm{HNO}_{3}$ solution will conduct electricity because being an acid it will produce $\mathrm{H}^{+}$ion, which participate in conductance.
2. Acid shows acidic property only in water, hence those acids which are in aqeuous form will show colour change using universal indicator. Therefore, all the given substance, except HCl (which is in gaseous state) and $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ (which is in solid state), could be determined by indicator.
3. Sulphuric acid should be stored in ceramic container instead of metallic container because acids are corrosive in nature and thus will react with metal.

## Case Study - 2

1. $\underset{\text { (Lewis Acid) }}{\mathrm{AlCl}_{3}}+\underset{\text { (Lewis base) }}{\mathrm{Cl}^{-}} \longrightarrow \underset{\text { (Adduct) }}{\mathrm{AlCl}_{4}^{-}}$
2. (a) $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}$, acid comes under all three types of acids i.e., Arrhenius, Bronsted and Lewis acid.
3. $\quad \mathrm{NaOH}$, base comes under all three types of bases i.e., Arrhenius, Bronsted and Lewis base.
(b) $\mathrm{NH}_{3}$, here is a base according to both Bronsted Lowry concept and Lewis concept. $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$, here is an acid according to all three theories.
(c) $\mathrm{Al}^{3+}$, here is a Lewis acid and $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ is Lewis base.

| Arrhenius |  | Bronsted |  | Lewis |  |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Acid | Base | Acid | Base | Acid | Base |
| $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{3}$ | $\mathrm{OH}^{-}$ | $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{3}$ | $\mathrm{Cl}^{-}$ | $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{3}$ | $\mathrm{Cl}^{-}$ |
| $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ | NaOH | $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ | $\mathrm{OH}^{-}$ | $\mathrm{Ag}^{2+}$ | $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ |
|  |  |  | $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ | $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ | NaOH |
|  |  |  | NaOH | $\mathrm{Cl}^{+}$ | $\mathrm{I}_{2}$ |
|  |  |  | $\mathrm{I}_{2}$ |  | $\mathrm{CO}_{3}^{2-}$ |

## 3. Metals and

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-100.jpg?height=378&width=444&top_left_y=383&top_left_x=1370)
Foundation

## C C O N CEPT M AP

| ${ }^{\star}$ Alloy : mixture of two or more |
| :--- |
| elements in solid state. |
| Brass: $\mathrm{Cu}+\mathrm{Zn}$ |
| Bronze $: \mathrm{Cu}+\mathrm{Sn}$ |
| Solder: $\mathrm{Sn}+\mathrm{Pb}$ |
| Duralumin $: \mathrm{Al}+\mathrm{Cu}+\mathrm{Mn}+\mathrm{Mg}$ |

## Physical properties of metals

- Metallic lusture
- Usually solid (except Hg which is a liquid)
- Show ductility and malleability.
- Good conductor of heat and electricity.


## Chemical properties of metals

Chemical properties of metals

- Form basic oxides with oxygen (Al and Zn forms amphoteric oxides)
- Form metal oxide and $\mathrm{H}_{2}$ gas with $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$
- Na and K reacts violently with cold water.
- Mg reacts only with hot water.
- $\mathrm{Al}, \mathrm{Zn}$ and Fe react with steam.
- Form salts and $\mathrm{H}_{2}$ gas with acids.
- Reactive metals displaces less reactive metals from their solution.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-100.jpg?height=573&width=249&top_left_y=1521&top_left_x=520)


## Reaction of metals with non metals

- Metal loses the valence electrons while non-metal accepts these electron to form ionic bond.
- Properties of ionic compounds :
- Generally solids and exist in the form of crystals.
- High melting and boiling points.
- Soluble in water, insoluble in kerosene, petrol etc.
- Conduct electricity in molten state and not in solid state.


## Chemical properties of non-metals

- with chlorine form covalent compounds.
- do not react with acids on room temperature. On heating oxidised to respective oxides.
- chlorine dissolves in water and form acidic solution.
- Hydrogen burns with a pop sound.
- C and S form oxide with oxygen.


## Physical properties of Non-metal

- poor conductors, weak and brittle.
- low density and non-sonorous.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-100.jpg?height=486&width=935&top_left_y=836&top_left_x=861)

Now-a-days we use large number of materials in our routine life. Only few of them occur naturally rest are obtained by converting naturally occuring raw material into more useful substances via chemical modification. At present the number of elements known to us is around 118. Out of which 92 elements are naturally occuring and the rest are man-made. Most of the elements are solid, but eleven out of them are in gaseous state at room temperature and two are liquid at room temperature (i.e., mercury and bromine).

## METALS

## Physical Properties of Metals

Some of the physical properties of metals are listed below.
(a) They have a shining surface called lustre when freshly prepared.
(b) They are ductile (they can be drawn into wires) and malleable (they can be beaten into thin sheets, due to this property metals can be rolled into sheets)
(c) They usually have a high melting points.
(d) They are usually hard and solid at room temperature.
(e) Metals are generally good conductors of heat, electricity and sound.
(f) Metals have high tensile strength and density.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-101.jpg?height=250&width=344&top_left_y=1055&top_left_x=255)

Alkali and alkaline earth metals are extremely reactive, and are rarely encountered in their elemental or metallic form. Some metals like sodium are so soft that they can be cut even with knife. Some metals are magnetic in nature such as iron, cobalt and nickel. Steel is also magnetic in nature since it is an alloy with major composition of iron. Whereas, other metals are not magnetic.

## ILLUSTRATION : 1

## Why cutting tools are generally made up of metals ?

## SOLUTION :

This is because of hard and strengthful character of metal.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-101.jpg?height=151&width=424&top_left_y=1487&top_left_x=260)

Take pieces of iron, copper and aluminium, the three common metals you came across very often in routine life. Now place first piece of iron on hard steel blog and hammer it with full power. Notice change in shape if any. Now place pieces of copper and aluminium on steel blog and hammer it in a similar manner and record your observations. What conclusion will you obtain about physical nature of metals?

Noble metals : Noble metals are those metals which are resistant to corrosion or oxidation, unlike most base metals. They are referred to noble metals, often due to their inertness. Examples include tantalum, gold, platinum, and rhodium. Precious metals: A precious metal is a rare metallic chemical element of high economic value. Chemically, the precious metals are less reactive than most elements, they have high lustre and high electrical conductivity. Historically, precious metals were important as currency, but are now regarded mainly as investment and industrial commodities. The best-known precious metals are gold and silver. While both have industrial uses, they are better known for their uses in art, jewellery, and coinage. Other precious metals include the platinum group metals: ruthenium, rhodium, palladium, osmium, iridium, and platinum, of which platinum is the most widely traded.
Base metals : In chemistry, the term 'base metal' is used informally to refer to a metal that oxidizes or corrodes relatively easily, and reacts variably with dilute hydrochloric acid $(\mathrm{HCl})$ to form hydrogen. Examples include iron, nickel, lead and zinc. Copper is considered as a base metal because it oxidizes relatively easily, although it does not react with HCl .
Ferrous metals : The term "ferrous" is derived from the latin word meaning "containing iron". This can include pure iron, such as wrought iron, or an alloy such as steel. Ferrous metals are often magnetic in nature.

## CONNECTING TOPIC

Alloy : An alloy is a mixture of two or more elements in solid state in which the major component is a metal. Most pure metals are either too soft, brittle or chemically reactive for practical use. Combining different ratios of metals in alloys modify the properties of pure metals to produce desirable characteristics. The aim of making alloys is generally to make them less brittle, harder, resistant to corrosion, or have a more desirable color and lustre. Examples of alloys are steel (iron and carbon), brass (copper and zinc), bronze (copper and tin), and duralumin (aluminium and copper). Alloys specially designed for highly demanding applications, such as jet engines, may contain more than ten elements. Stainless steel ( $\mathrm{Fe}+\mathrm{C}+\mathrm{Cr}+\mathrm{Ni}$ ) is used where resistance to corrosion is important. Al and Mg alloys are used for strength and light applications. Nickel based super alloys are used in high temperature appliances such as turbochargers, pressure vessels and heat exchangers. Generally, electrical conductivity of an alloy is less than that of pure metal.

Alloys have wide domestic and industrial applications, as alloy formation enhances the applicability of metals in various fields.

|  | Alloy | Composition | Used for |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1. | Brass | $\mathrm{Cu}=80 \%, \mathrm{Zn}=20 \%$ | Making utensils and cartridges. |
| 2. | Bronze | $\mathrm{Cu}=90 \%, \mathrm{Sn}=10 \%$ | Making statues, medals, ships, coins and machines. |
| 3. | Solder | $\mathrm{Sn}=50 \%, \mathrm{~Pb}=50 \%$ | Joining metals, soldering wire and electronic components, etc. |
| 4. | Duralumin | $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{Al}=95.5 \%, \mathrm{Cu}=3 \% \\ & \mathrm{Mn}=1.0 \%, \mathrm{Mg}=0.5 \% \end{aligned}$ | Making bodies of aircrafts, kitchenware and automobile parts, etc. |
| 5. | German silver | $\mathrm{Cu}=60 \%, \mathrm{Zn}=20 \%, \mathrm{Ni}=20 \%$ | Making utensils and ornaments |
| 6. | Gun metal | $\mathrm{Cu}=90 \%, \mathrm{Sn}=10 \%$ | Making gears and castings, etc. |
| 7. | Bell metal | $\mathrm{Cu}=80 \%, \mathrm{Sn}=20 \%$ | Making bells, gangs, etc. |
| 8. | Magnalium | $\mathrm{Al}=90 \%, \mathrm{Mg}=10 \%$ | Making beam balance, light instruments |
| 9. | Type metal | $\mathrm{Pb}=82 \%, \mathrm{Sb}=15 \%, \mathrm{Sn}=3 \%$ | Making casting type |
| 10. | Stainless steel | Fe, Ni, Cr, C | Making utensils, cutlery, etc. |

## Chemical Properties of Metals

## 1. Metal burns in air :

Many metals burn in the oxygen of air to produce a metal oxide
Metal + Oxygen → Metal oxide
The more reactive a metal is, the more violent the reaction when it is burnt in oxygen or air.
Example : Magnesium + Oxygen → Magnesium oxide

$$
2 \mathrm{Mg}(\mathrm{~s})+\mathrm{O}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{MgO}(\mathrm{~s})
$$

Many metals tarnish in air. The metal slowly reacts with the oxygen of the air forming a dull layer of the metal oxide on the surface of the metal.

Very unreactive metals like gold and silver do not tarnish, they stay shiny.
The reactivity series :

$\left.\begin{array}{l}\mathrm{K}-\text { Potassium } \\ \mathrm{Na}-\text { Sodium } \\ \mathrm{Ca}-\text { Calcium } \\ \mathrm{Mg}-\text { Magnesium }\end{array}\right]$ Burn very easily with a bright flame $\left.\begin{array}{ll}\text { Al-Aluminium } & \left.\begin{array}{l}\text { Zn-Zinc } \\ \text { Fe-Iron } \\ \text { Pb-Lead } \\ \text { Cu-Copper }\end{array}\right] \text { React slowly with air when heated } \\ \end{array} \quad \begin{array}{l}\text { Ag-Silver } \\ \text { Au-Gold }\end{array}\right]$ No reaction

Metal oxides are usually basic in nature but there are some metal oxides which show both acidic and basic behaviour. i.e. they react with both acids and bases. These are called amphoteric oxides. One of the most common example of an amphoteric oxide is $\mathrm{Al}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$. It reacts in the following manner with acids and bases -

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathrm{Al}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}+6 \mathrm{HCl} & \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{AlCl}_{3}+3 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \\
\mathrm{Al}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}+2 \mathrm{NaOH} & \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{NaAlO}_{2}+3 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}
\end{aligned}
$$

Zinc, tin, lead and beryllium can also form amphoteric oxides.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-102.jpg?height=93&width=102&top_left_y=2553&top_left_x=1820)

Most metal oxides are insoluble in water but some of these dissolve in water to form alkali. For example, sodium oxide and potassium oxide dissolve in water to produce alkali as follows,

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{~s})+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l}) & \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{NaOH}(\mathrm{aq}) \\
\mathrm{K}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{~s})+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l}) & \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{KOH}(\mathrm{aq})
\end{aligned}
$$

All metals do not react with oxygen at same rate, some metals are more reactive than others. This is because very reactive metals lose electrons easily. Metals such as sodium are very reactive and explosive in air. Hence, to protect them and to prevent accidental fires, they are kept immersed in kerosene oil. Whereas, metals such as gold are very unreactive, and therefore do not corrode or tarnish in air.

## Learn More

At ordinary temperature, the surfaces of metals such as magnesium, aluminium, zinc, lead, etc., are covered with a thin layer of oxide. The protective oxide layer prevents the metal from further oxidation. Iron does not burn on heating but iron fillings burn vigorously when sprinkled in the flame of the burner. Copper does not burn, but the hot metal is coated with a black coloured layer of copper(II) oxide. Silver and gold do not react with oxygen even at high temperatures.

## SOLUTION

Calcium reacts vigrously and burns with a bright flame when heated with air. On the other hand, zinc reacts slowly with air when heated to form oxide.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-103.jpg?height=154&width=421&top_left_y=1361&top_left_x=260)

Take small pieces of aluminium, copper and zinc metal respectively. Now leave these pieces in open humid air for two days. After that take dilute sulphuric acid in three glass beakers $\mathrm{A}, \mathrm{B}$ and C respectively. Put pieces of metals of aluminium, copper and zinc in beakers $\mathrm{A}, \mathrm{B}$ and C respectively for few minutes. Take out the metal pieces separately in three petridishes. Record your observations and answer the following questions
(i) Which metal is consumed most in acid?
(ii) Which metal form toughest protective oxide layer when metals left in open air?
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-103.jpg?height=204&width=325&top_left_y=1841&top_left_x=251)

Anodising is an electrolytic passivation process used to increase the thickness of the natural oxide layer on the surface of metal parts. The process is called "anodising" because the part to be treated forms the anode electrode of an electrical circuit. Anodizing increases corrosion resistance and provides better adhesion for paint primers and glues than the bare metal. Anodic films are most commonly applied to protect aluminium alloys, although process also applied for titanium, zinc, magnesium, niobium, and tantalum.
2. Reaction of metal with water :

Metals react with water and produce a metal oxide and hydrogen gas. Metal oxides that are soluble in water dissolve in it to form metal hydroxides. But all metals do not react with water.

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \text { Metal + Water } \text { ⟶ Metal oxide + Hydrogen } \\
& \text { Metal oxide + Water } \text { ⟶ Metal hydroxide }
\end{aligned}
$$

Metals like potassium and sodium react violently with cold water. In case of sodium and potassium, the reaction is so violent and exothermic that the evolved hydrogen immediately catches fire.

$$
\begin{aligned}
2 \mathrm{~K}(\mathrm{~s})+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l}) & \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{KOH}(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+\text { Heat energy } \\
2 \mathrm{Na}(\mathrm{~s})+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l}) & \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{NaOH}(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+\text { Heat energy }
\end{aligned}
$$

The reaction of calcium with water is less violent. The heat evolved is not sufficient for the hydrogen to catch fire.

$$
\mathrm{Ca}(\mathrm{~s})+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l}) \longrightarrow \mathrm{Ca}(\mathrm{OH})_{2}(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})
$$

Calcium starts floating because the bubbles of hydrogen gas formed stick to the surface of the metal.
Magnesium does not react with cold water. It reacts with hot water to form magnesium hydroxide and hydrogen. It also starts floating due to the bubbles of hydrogen gas sticking to its surface.
Metals like aluminium, iron and zinc do not react either with cold or hot water. But they react with steam to form the metal oxide and hydrogen.
Eg. Magnesium + Steam ⟶ Magnesium oxide + Hydrogen

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{Mg}(\mathrm{~s})+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{~g}) \longrightarrow \mathrm{MgO}(\mathrm{~s})+\mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \\
& 2 \mathrm{Al}(\mathrm{~s})+3 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{~g}) \longrightarrow \mathrm{Al}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}(\mathrm{~s})+3 \mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \\
& 3 \mathrm{Fe}(\mathrm{~s})+4 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{~g}) \longrightarrow \mathrm{Fe}_{3} \mathrm{O}_{4}(\mathrm{~s})+4 \mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})
\end{aligned}
$$

Metals such as lead, copper, silver and gold do not react with water at all.

Freshly cut sodium has a metallic shine. However, it reacts with water (present in air) to form NaOH which forms a layer on the exposed metallic surface. The reaction so strongly exothermic that the piece of sodium melts (melting point of sodium: $97.8{ }^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ ). The heat transferred to the local environment ignites the hydrogen that is produced. The hydrogen burns with a bright flame. This is why elemental sodium is stored under kerosene oil or a similar substance.

$$
\text { Sodium reacts with water : } \quad 2 \mathrm{Na}+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{NaOH}+\mathrm{H}_{2}
$$

The phenolphthalein solution indicator shows that sodium hydroxide is produced. The solution changes to deep red.

A metal reacts with oxygen and gives a compound, having a high melting point. The compound is soluble in water and the reaction is not that violent to burn the evolved hydrogen. Predict the name of metal.

## SOLUTION

Calcium is that metal

## 3. Reaction of metal with acids :

When an acid reacts with metal, a salt and hydrogen are produced:
For example :

$$
\text { Acid + Metal } \longrightarrow \text { Salt + Hydrogen }
$$

Sulfuric acid + Calcium ⟶ Calcium sulfate + Hydrogen

The salt that is produced depends upon the reactivity of metals with acids. Potassium, sodium, lithium and calcium all react violently with dilute sulphuric acid and dilute hydrochloric acid. It is dangerous to put these metals into an acid. The reaction is similar to the reaction with water, forming the metal salt (either sulphate or chloride) and hydrogen gas.
For example :

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \text { Sodium }+ \text { Hydrochloric acid } \longrightarrow \text { Sodium chloride }+ \text { Hydrogen } \\
& 2 \mathrm{Na}(\mathrm{~s})+2 \mathrm{HCl}(\mathrm{aq}) \quad \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{NaCl}(\mathrm{aq}) \quad+\quad \mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})
\end{aligned}
$$

Magnesium, aluminium, zinc, iron, tin and lead react safely with dilute acid. Magnesium is the fastest and lead is the slowest among the six.

The reaction of zinc with sulphuric acid is often used to make a small amount of hydrogen in the laboratory.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-104.jpg?height=93&width=104&top_left_y=2553&top_left_x=1818)

$$
\mathrm{Zn}(\mathrm{~s})+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}(\mathrm{aq}) \longrightarrow \mathrm{ZnSO}_{4}(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \uparrow
$$

The reaction is slow at room temperature but its rate can be increased by the addition of a small amount of copper(II) sulphate. Zinc displaces copper metal, which acts as a catalyst.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-105.jpg?height=197&width=323&top_left_y=525&top_left_x=255)

Hydrogen gas is not evolved when a metal reacts with nitric acid. It is because $\mathrm{HNO}_{3}$ is a strong oxidising agent. It oxidises the $H_{2}$ produced to water and itself gets reduced to any of the nitrogen oxides ( $\mathrm{N}_{2} \mathrm{O}, \mathrm{NO}, \mathrm{NO}_{2}$ ). But magnesium ( Mg ) and manganese (Mn) react with very dilute $\mathrm{HNO}_{3}$ to evolve $\mathrm{H}_{2}$ gas.
Metals react with dil HCl to form corresponding chloride salt and hydrogen which is evolved as bubbles. The rate of formation of bubbles was the fastest in case of magnesium and the reaction is exothermic. The reactivity decreases in the order $\mathrm{Mg}>\mathrm{Al}>\mathrm{Zn}>\mathrm{Fe}$. In the case of copper, no bubbles were seen and the temperature also remains unchanged. This shows that copper does not react with dilute HCl .

Time to Check Your Knowledge
Four experiments are set-up as shown below. A rapid evolution of $\mathbf{H}_{2}$ gas will be observed in the test-tube.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-105.jpg?height=857&width=1204&top_left_y=1126&top_left_x=466)

## SOLUTION

The most rapid evolution of $\mathrm{H}_{2}$ gas will take place in (ii) because magnesium has highest reactivity towards dil HCl in comparision to zinc granules, copper turnings and iron fillings.

## 4. Reaction of metals with nitric acid :

Dilute nitric acid is added to zinc, aluminium and copper. Immediately thereafter, zinc and aluminum show no reaction. Copper, on the other hand, reacts immediately and produces brown $\mathrm{NO}_{2}$ according to the following reaction.

$$
\mathrm{Cu}+2 \mathrm{HNO}_{3}+2 \mathrm{H}^{+} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Cu}^{2+}+2 \mathrm{NO}_{2}+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}
$$

Aluminum and zinc do not react even with concentrated nitric acid since, a thick and hard to dissolve, oxidative layer builds (passive oxidation) on the metal, that protects the metal against further assaults.

Acids are often used to dissolve metals or organic material. However, certain material are more difficult to dissolve. They require more potent and specialized acid solutions like aqua regia.
Aqua regia consists of 3 parts HCl to 1 part $\mathrm{HNO}_{3}$, it is an excellent oxidant, can dissolve gold and platinum, it produces yellow fumes to produce nitrosyl chloride, NOCl, chlorine gas, $\mathrm{Cl}_{2}$, and water, $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$.
Acid Piranha consists $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}$ and $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{2}$ in the ratios, $3: 1$ to $7: 1$, it is also an excellent oxidant and removes trace amount of organic residues.

For preparing piranha always add peroxide to sulfuric acid, never vice versa. This is a selfheating solution that may even boil when mixed.

## 5. Reaction of metal with solution of other metal salts:

A metal will displace (take the place of) a less reactive metal in a metal salt solution.
For example,

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \text { Iron + Copper(II) sulphate } \longrightarrow \text { Iron sulphate + Copper. } \\
& \mathrm{Fe}(\mathrm{~s})+\mathrm{CuSO}_{4}(\mathrm{aq}) \quad \longrightarrow \mathrm{FeSO}_{4}(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{Cu}(\mathrm{~s}) \downarrow
\end{aligned}
$$

Copper(II) sulphate is blue, iron sulphate is pale green in colour.
During the reaction the blue solution loses its colour and the iron metal is seen to turn pink-brown as the displaced copper becomes deposited on it.

If a less reactive metal is added to a metal salt solution, there will be no reaction - nothing will happen.
For example, iron is less reactive than magnesium.
Iron + Magnesium sulphate ⟶ No reaction
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-106.jpg?height=195&width=323&top_left_y=1416&top_left_x=187)

In these displacement reactions the metals are competing for the non-metal anion. Reactions using chlorides or nitrates can also be used.

The order of the metals in the reactivity series can be worked out by using these type of reactions.
For example, tin would be seen to displace lead from lead chloride but would not react with iron(II) chloride.

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \text { Tin }+ \text { Lead chloride } \longrightarrow \text { Tin chloride }+ \text { Lead } \\
& \mathrm{Sn}(\mathrm{~s})+\mathrm{PbCl}_{2}(\mathrm{aq}) \longrightarrow \mathrm{SnCl}_{2}(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{Pb}(\mathrm{~s}) \\
& \text { Tin }+ \text { Iron }(\mathrm{II}) \text { chloride } \longrightarrow \text { No reaction } \\
& \mathrm{Sn}(\mathrm{~s})+\mathrm{FeCl}_{2}(\mathrm{aq}) \longrightarrow \text { No reaction }
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore, tin must be above lead but below iron in the reactivity series.
Copper would be seen to displace silver from silver nitrate but would not react with lead nitrate.

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \text { Copper }+ \text { Silver nitrate } \longrightarrow \text { Copper nitrate }+ \text { Silver } \\
& \mathrm{Cu}(\mathrm{~s})+2 \mathrm{AgNO}_{3}(\mathrm{aq}) \longrightarrow \mathrm{Cu}\left(\mathrm{NO}_{3}\right)_{2}(\mathrm{aq})+2 \mathrm{Ag}(\mathrm{~s}) \\
& \text { Copper }+ \text { Lead nitrate } \longrightarrow \text { No reaction } \\
& \mathrm{Cu}(\mathrm{~s})+\mathrm{Pb}\left(\mathrm{NO}_{3}\right)_{2}(\mathrm{aq}) \longrightarrow \text { No reaction }
\end{aligned}
$$

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-106.jpg?height=593&width=266&top_left_y=1754&top_left_x=1537)

Therefore, copper must be above silver but below lead in the reactivity series.
The arrangement of metals in the decreasing order of their activities is known as activity series. It is also known as reactivity series. A portion of this series is given as follows.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-106.jpg?height=93&width=104&top_left_y=2553&top_left_x=1818)
