CASE III : Few metals like Na, Mg, Al and Zn reacts easily with dilute HCl whereas copper and silver do not react with dilute HCl or even with dilute $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}$. Why?
All those metals which are more reactive viz. lose electron more easily than hydrogen, displaces hydrogen from dilute acids to produce hydrogen gas. This happens because the more reactive metal gives electron easily and these electrons reduce the hydrogen ion of the acids to $\mathrm{H}_{2}$ gas. So, $\mathrm{Na}, \mathrm{Mg}, \mathrm{Al}$ or Zn gives electrons easily required for the reduction while Cu and Ag do not give electrons for the reduction. Therefore, Cu and Ag do not react with acids.

1. Generally, metals react with dil HCl or dil $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}$ to give their chloride sulphates and when a metal reacts with dilute $\mathrm{HNO}_{3}$, then $\mathrm{H}_{2}$ gas is not evolved. Why?
2. A zinc plate was dipped in $\mathrm{FeSO}_{4}$ solution. After certain time, iron from the solution was deposited on the zinc plate. State the reason why it happened. Write the chemical equation too.

CASE II : Potassium reacts violently with cold water to form KOH and $\mathrm{H}_{2}$ gas. Ca and Mg has different reactivity with water, Ca forms $\mathrm{Ca}(\mathrm{OH})_{2}$ whereas ${ }^{2}$ g does not react, it reacts only in hot condition. Metals like Al, Zn and Fe do not react with cold water or hot water. They react with steam to form a metal oxide and hydrogen. Why?

The reaction of potassium metal with $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ is highly exothermic due to which the hydrogen gas released during the reaction catches fire immediately. Thus potassium metal is highly reactive.
$2 \mathrm{~K}(\mathrm{~s})+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l}) \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{KOH}(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+$ Heat In case of calcium, heat produced is less which is not sufficient to burn $\mathrm{H}_{2}$ gas which is released during the reaction. Thus, this reaction is less violent calcium reacts with cold $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ but magnesium reacts only with hot $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$. This shows that magnesium is less reactive than calcium.

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{Ca}(\mathrm{~s})+\underset{(\text { Cold })}{2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l})} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Ca}(\mathrm{OH})_{2}(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \\
& \text { Calcium hydroxide } \\
& \mathrm{Mg}(\mathrm{~s})+\underset{(\text { Cold })}{\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l})} \longrightarrow \text { No reaction } \\
& \mathrm{Mg}(\mathrm{~s})+\underset{(\mathrm{Hot})}{2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l})} \longrightarrow \begin{array}{c}
\mathrm{Mg}(\mathrm{OH})_{2}(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \\
\text { Magnesium hydroxide }
\end{array} \\
& \mathrm{Mg}(\mathrm{~s})+\underset{(\text { Steam })}{\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{~g})} \longrightarrow \begin{array}{c}
\mathrm{MgO}(\mathrm{~s})+\mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \\
\text { Magnesium oxide }
\end{array}
\end{aligned}
$$

Aluminium, zinc and iron are not reactive enough to react with cold water or hot water. Some energy needs to be added for the reaction to take place.

So, when $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ is converted to steam, it has more energy and now has the ability to react with $\mathrm{Al}, \mathrm{Zn}$ or Fe .

$$
\begin{aligned}
2 \mathrm{Al}(\mathrm{~s})+3 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{~g}) \longrightarrow & \mathrm{Al}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}(\mathrm{~s})+3 \mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \\
(\text { steam }) & \text { Aluminium oxide } \\
\mathrm{Zn}(\mathrm{~s})+\underset{(\text { steam })}{\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{~g})} \longrightarrow & \mathrm{ZnO}(\mathrm{~s})+\mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \\
& \text { Zinc oxide } \\
3 \mathrm{Fe}(\mathrm{~s})+4 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{~g}) \longrightarrow & \mathrm{Fe}_{3} \mathrm{O}_{4}(\mathrm{~s})+4 \mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \\
& \text { Iron oxide }
\end{aligned}
$$

## OCCURRENCE OF METALS

The earth's crust is a very big source of the metals. Metals occur in nature sometimes free but mostly in combined state. Metals like silver, gold, platinum etc. occur in free or native state. Most of the metals are found in the form of the compounds in the nature. These naturally occuring compounds of metals are known as minerals. In certain minerals, the percentage of a particular metal is high and these metals can be extracted profitably and such minerals are called ores. The process of extraction of metals from their respective ores is called metallurgy.
The unwanted rocky, earthy or sandy materials almost always associated with ores as impurities are called gangue or matrix.

## Learn

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-109.jpg?height=99&width=111&top_left_y=790&top_left_x=1027)
More

Different metallurgical operations may be divided into three classes :
(a) Pyrometallurgy : Pyro means very high temperature and this class includes the methods of extraction which take place at very high temperature. Metals like $\mathrm{Cu}, \mathrm{Fe}, \mathrm{Zn}, \mathrm{Pb}, \mathrm{Sn}, \mathrm{Ni}, \mathrm{Cr}, \mathrm{Hg}$ etc. are extracted by pyrometallurgical process.
(b) Hydrometallurgy is the extraction of metals from ores using aqueous solution. It includes
(a) Leaching
(b) Reduction

Generally, Ag and Au are extracted by this method.
(c) Electrometallurgy is the process of obtaining metals through electrolysis.

The electropositive metals are obtained by this method.
e.g. ⟶ $\mathrm{Na}, \mathrm{Mg}$, etc.
The table given here lists some common ores of some metals.

| S. No. | Name of the ore | Formula of the ore | Type of ore | Metal obtained from the ore |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1. | Bauxite | $\mathrm{Al}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3} \cdot 2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ | Oxide | Aluminium (Al) |
| 2. | Haematite | $\mathrm{Fe}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$ | Oxide | Iron (Fe) |
| 3. | Magnetite | $\mathrm{Fe}_{3} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ | Oxide | Iron (Fe) |
| 4. | Zincite | ZnO | Oxide | Zinc (Zn) |
| 5. | Cuprite | $\mathrm{Cu}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ | Oxide | Copper (Cu) |
| 6. | Litharge | PbO | Oxide | Lead (Pb) |
| 7. | Malachite | $\mathrm{CuCO}_{3} \cdot \mathrm{Cu}(\mathrm{OH})_{2}$ | Carbonate | Copper (Cu) |
| 8. | Magnesite | $\mathrm{MgCO}_{3}$ | Carbonate | Magnesium (Mg) |
| 9. | Lime stone | $\mathrm{CaCO}_{3}$ | Carbonate | Calcium (Ca) |
| 10. | Cinnabar | HgS | Sulphide | Mercury (Hg) |
| 11. | Chalcopyrite | $\mathrm{CuFeS}_{2}$ | Sulphide | Copper (Cu) |
| 12. | Zinc blende | ZnS | Sulphide | Zinc (Zn) |
| 13. | Galena | PbS | Sulphide | Lead (Pb) |
| 14. | Chalcocite | $\mathrm{Cu}_{2} \mathrm{~S}$ | Sulphide | Copper (Cu) |
| 15. | Common salt | NaCl | Chloride (Halide) | Sodium (Na) |
| 16. | Fluorspar | $\mathrm{CaF}_{2}$ | Fluoride (Halide) | Calcium (Ca) |
| 17. | Horn silver | AgCl | Chloride (Halide) | Silver (Ag) |

CONNECTING TOPIC CONNECTING TOPIC CONNECTING TOPIC CONNECTING TOPIC CONNECTING TOPIC CONNECTING TOPIC

## CONNECTING TOPIC

Extraction of ore is a complex process which comprises various steps. So before studying the topic of extraction we should understand the general principles of metallurgy. Although we will study all these principles in detail in class XIIth, for your better understanding a summary is given here.

## GENERAL PRINCIPLES OF METALLURGY

Metallurgy is an art of extracting metals from the ores. The ores are generally associated with a number of impurities and earthy matter. The extraction of metals from ores involves various steps as discussed below :

## 1. Grinding or Pulverisation

The large pieces of ores are broken into small pieces with the help of hammer. These pieces are crushed with crusher and finally powdered in stamp mills. This powdered form of ore is used for further processes.

## 2. Concentration

The ores, obtained from ore mine contain various types of impurities. The removal of various types of impurities from ores is known as concentration. The process by which ore is concentrated depends on the type of ore and gangue. This concentration is carried out by using one or more of the following methods :
(a) Gravity separation or hydraulic washing : This method is based on the difference in the density of ore and the density of impurities. The ore is crushed and finely powdered, sieved and then levigated in a stream of water. The lighter impurities are carried away by the stream of water and heavier ore particles settle down. Ores of tin, lead and iron are concentrated by this process.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-110.jpg?height=424&width=766&top_left_y=1210&top_left_x=662)

## Gravity separation method

(b) Froth floatation process : This process is used in the concentration of sulphide ores like galena ( PbS ), zinc blende (ZnS) etc. The well powdered ore (taken in a tank) is mixed with water to which an oil (pine oil or eucalyptus oil) has been added. The whole mass is agitated by a blast of air sent through pipe as shown in figure. The froth (containing impurities) floats on the surface of the water, while heavy ore particles settle down. The froth along with the ore is removed from the surface of the water.

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-110.jpg?height=456&width=616&top_left_y=1676&top_left_x=1187)
Froth floatation process for the concentration of sulphide ores.

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-110.jpg?height=200&width=325&top_left_y=2179&top_left_x=258)
Froth stabilizers are those chemicals which stabilise the froth e.g. cresols and aniline.

## Collectors :

These compounds attach themselves by polar groups to grains of minerals which then become water repellent and thus pass on into the froth. Examples are ethyl xanthate and potassium ethyl xanthate.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-110.jpg?height=95&width=104&top_left_y=2553&top_left_x=1818)

## Activators and depressants :

These compounds activate or depress the floating property of one of the components of the ore and thus help in the separation of different minerals present in the same ore. Copper sulphate is an example of activator, while sodium and potassium cyanides are the examples of depressants.

## ILLUSTRATIOM : 2

## How NaCN acts as a depressant?

## SOLUTION :

NaCN when added to ore containing ZnS and PbS , it forms a complex with ZnS as $\mathrm{Na}_{2}\left[\mathrm{Zn}(\mathrm{CN})_{4}\right]$ and prevent it from forming froth. PbS is then easily separated from ZnS .
(c) Electromagnetic separation : This method is used when either the ore or the gangue is magnetic in nature. For example, tin stone contains $\mathrm{Fe}_{3} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ as impurity. The powdered ore is dropped on a moving belt passing over two magnetic rollers. The magnetic impurities (like $\mathrm{Fe}_{3} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ ) get collected in a heap nearer the magnetic rollers, while the ore (free from impurity) is collected in a heap, away from the magnetic rollers.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-111.jpg?height=656&width=1139&top_left_y=918&top_left_x=536)

Some metals are attracted to magnet and can behave as a magnet. Thus Iron, cobalt and nickel are described as magnetic materials.
There are two types of permanent magnets those from "hard" magnetic materials and those from "soft" magnetic materials. Hard magnetic materials tend to stay magnetized over a long period while soft magnetic metals can be magnetized but lose their magnetism quickly.
(d) Leaching : It is a chemical method used for concentration of ore in which crude powdered ore is allowed to react with suitable reagent. Ore reacts with added reagent to form soluble complex while impurities remain undissolved. Insoluble impurities are separated by filtration and ore is reprecipitated from soluble complex.
Note : This process is used for concentration of Al, Ag, Au ores etc.

## 3. Extraction of Metals

Metals are extracted from their concentrated ore by the reduction of metal oxides. The ore is converted to its oxide by one of following methods.
(a) Calcination : Calcination is the process of heating the ore in the presence of limited supply of air to a temperature insufficient to melt it. During calcination, moisture and volatile impurities are removed and the whole mass becomes porous. Here carbonates are converted into oxides by giving out carbon dioxide. Apart from carbonate ores, hydrated ores are also subjected to calcination for the removal of water of crystallization. Some examples are given below:
(i) The metal zinc and calcium are obtained from calamine $\left(\mathrm{ZnCO}_{3}\right)$ and lime stone $\left(\mathrm{CaCO}_{3}\right)$ respectively. When these ores are calcined, they are converted into oxides.

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{ZnCO}_{3} \longrightarrow \mathrm{ZnO}+\mathrm{CO}_{2} \\
& \mathrm{CaCO}_{3} \longrightarrow \mathrm{CaO}+\mathrm{CO}_{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

(ii) Haemetite $\left(\mathrm{Fe}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}\right)$ is an ore of iron. Concentrated ore when calcined in a shallow kiln -
(1) Moisture and carbon dioxide are removed.
(2) Ferrous oxide present is oxidised to ferric oxide.
(3) Sulphur is removed as sulphur dioxide gas.
(4) The whole mass becomes porous.
(b) Roasting: It is a process which involves heating of ore in presence of excess of air. In this process, moisture and volatile impurities are removed. The ore itself is converted into oxides.

## Examples :

(i) Zinc blende ( ZnS ), when roasted, is converted into zinc oxide.

$$
2 \mathrm{ZnS}+3 \mathrm{O}_{2} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{ZnO}+2 \mathrm{SO}_{2}
$$

(ii) Copper metal is extracted from copper pyrite ore. When this ore is roasted :
(1) Most of the sulphur is removed as sulphur dioxide.
(2) Arsenic and antimony are volatilised.
(3) Copper pyrite ore is converted into sulphides of iron and copper

$$
2 \mathrm{CuFeS}_{2}+\mathrm{O}_{2} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{FeS}+\mathrm{Cu}_{2} \mathrm{~S}+\mathrm{SO}_{2}
$$

Partial oxidation of FeS and $\mathrm{Cu}_{2} \mathrm{~S}$ also takes place

$$
\begin{aligned}
2 \mathrm{FeS}+3 \mathrm{O}_{2} & \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{FeO}+2 \mathrm{SO}_{2} \\
2 \mathrm{Cu}_{2} \mathrm{~S}+3 \mathrm{O}_{2} & \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{Cu}_{2} \mathrm{O}+2 \mathrm{SO}_{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

Both calcination and roasting may be carried out in same reverberatory furnace. In calcination, air holes are partially or completely closed, while in roasting they are open.

## - Why sulphide ores are roasted first before reduction ?

## SOLUTION

It is because oxides of metals can be reduced more easily to free metals rather than sulphides of metals.

The differences between roasting and calcination are given below:

| Roasting | Calcination |
| :--- | :--- |
| 1. Heating in the presence of excess air. | 1. Heating in the presence of limited air. |
| 2. Roasting is applied on sulphide ores. | 2. Calcination is applied on carbonate ores. |
| 3. Sulphur dioxide is released. | 3. Carbon dioxide is released. |
| 4. Not used for dehydrating an ore. | 4. Can be used to drive out moisture from an ore. |

## 4. Reduction of Metal Oxides

(a) Smelting : Calcined or roasted ore is mixed with carbon or any other suitable reducing agent and flux, and heated strongly. Metal oxides are reduced to metal. Smelting is opposite of roasting which involves an oxidizing reaction.

Examples : When tin stone $\left(\mathrm{SnO}_{2}\right)$ is heated strongly with carbon, tin metal is obtained.

$$
\mathrm{SnO}_{2}+2 \mathrm{C} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Sn}+2 \mathrm{CO}
$$

The zinc oxide obtained by calcination of zinc blende, when heated strongly with carbon gives zinc metal.

$$
\mathrm{ZnO}+\mathrm{C} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Zn}+\mathrm{CO}
$$

Similarly, lead metal is obtained by the reduction of lead oxide.

$$
\mathrm{PbO}+\mathrm{C} \longrightarrow \mathrm{~Pb}+\mathrm{CO}
$$

CONNECTING TOPIC CONNECTING TOPIC CONNECTING TOPIC CONNECTING TOPIC CONNECTING TOPIC CONNECTING TOPIC

- Carbon is good reducing agent for oxides but not for sulphides.
- Due to cheapness and high availability, carbon is widely used as a reducing agent. The process is known as carbon reduction. The metals which are less electropositive, do not form carbides with carbon, they are reduced by this method e.g. oxides of Ph, Fe, Zn, Sn etc.
- Below $710^{\circ} \mathrm{C}, \mathrm{CO}$ is better reducing agent than carbon and reverse is true at temperature higher than $710^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$.

Flux :
Ores contain some fusible impurities. These impurities are removed by adding certain substances. These substances are called flux. The flux to be used depends on the nature of impurities. If acidic impurities (such as $\mathrm{SiO}_{2}$ ) are present, basic flux (e.g., $\mathrm{CaO}, \mathrm{FeO}, \mathrm{MgO}$ etc.) are mixed and if the impurities present are of basic nature the acidic flux are used.

Slag :
Fusible compounds, formed by the combination of flux and impurities are known as slag. For example, if ferrous oxide is present as impurity in a metal it may be removed by adding silica as a flux. Ferrous oxide is removed as $\mathrm{FeSiO}_{3}$ (slag).

$$
\mathrm{FeO}+\mathrm{SiO}_{2} \longrightarrow \mathrm{FeSiO}_{3}
$$

Generally, slags possess melting points lower than the metals. The slags are generally lighter and hence floats on the surface of molten metals from where it can be skimmed off from time to time.
(b) Aluminothermic process :

There are certain metals whose oxides can not be reduced by carbon. These metals are extracted by aluminothermic process. The metal oxides are thoroughly mixed with aluminium powder and a little $\mathrm{BaO}_{2}$ in a crucible. The reaction is brought about by introducing a lighted magnesium ribbon. Since the reaction is exothermic, temperature reaches to about $3000^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. Metals like chromium and manganese can be extracted by this method.

$$
\begin{gathered}
\mathrm{Cr}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}+2 \mathrm{Al} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Al}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}+2 \mathrm{Cr} \\
3 \mathrm{MnO}_{2}+4 \mathrm{Al} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{Al}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}+3 \mathrm{Mn}
\end{gathered}
$$

The reaction between iron (II) oxide and aluminium is called thermite reaction. Commercially, it is used in welding of iron tracks. It was discovered by German chemist Dr. Hans Goldschmidt. In this reaction, iron oxide (rust) and aluminium metal powder undergo a redox (oxidation-reduction) reaction to form molten iron metal.

$$
\mathrm{Fe}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}(\mathrm{~s})+2 \mathrm{Al}(\mathrm{~s}) \longrightarrow \mathrm{Al}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}(\mathrm{~s})+2 \mathrm{Fe}(\mathrm{l})
$$

In fact, thermite reaction has been used for underwater welding, joining railway tracks or cracked machine parts.
(c) Precipitation method :

Those metals which are more reactive tend to displace less reactive metals. If an iron rod is placed in a copper sulphate solution the copper metal is precipitated and iron goes in solution. Zinc is more reactive than silver and gold in reactivity series and hence it precipitates these metals from their salt solutions. This method is used in the extraction of silver and gold.

## 5. Refining of Metals

The metals obtained by anyone of the above methods are not pure. The metals are refined by the following methods :
(a) By liquation : This method is used for the easily fusible metals like tin etc. Impure metal is placed on the inclined bed of a furnace and heated. The metal melts and flows out leaving behind the impurities.
For example, purification of Sn containing impurities of high m.p. like $\mathrm{Fe}, \mathrm{Cu}, \mathrm{W}$. It is also used for purification of $\mathrm{Pb}, \mathrm{Bi}$ etc.

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-114.jpg?height=533&width=801&top_left_y=589&top_left_x=641)
Purification of tin metal by liquation process

(b) By distillation : Volatile metals like Zn and Hg etc. can be purified by this method. When these metals are heated, their vapours are formed leaving behind the impurities. The vapours are condensed to get pure metals.
(c) By oxidation : If the impurities present in the metals can be easily oxidised, these are removed by this method. Molten metal is stirred thoroughly in the presence of air. The impurities float on the surface of the molten mass in the form of a scum which can be easily separated.
(d) Electrolytic refining : The impure metal is taken as anode and the cathode consists of a pure metal. Cathodes and anodes are placed in a soluble salt of the metal taken as an electrolyte. Pure metal is gradually deposited on the cathode and impurities either settle down or dissolved in the solution and collected as anode mud. Large number of metals like copper, silver, gold and zinc are purified by this method. The metal of highest purity are obtained by this method.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-114.jpg?height=497&width=700&top_left_y=1400&top_left_x=1107)
(e) Thermal decomposition method : In these methods, the metal is converted into suitable volatile compound which is decomposed at high temperature to give pure metal.
(i) Mond's process or carbonyl process (Purification of Ni) :

This method is used for the purification of nickel. Impure nickel is allowed to react with carbon monooxide at $60-80^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. It gives $\mathrm{Ni}(\mathrm{CO})_{4}$, which is a volatile compound. Vapours of $\mathrm{Ni}(\mathrm{CO})_{4}$ decomposes at about $180^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ giving pure Ni ( $\approx 99.9 \%$ purity).
(ii) van-Arkel process :

It is also known as iodide refining method. This method is used for obtaining metals of very high purity. Common metals which are refined by this method are Ti, Zr, Hf, Si etc. In this method, metal is converted into a volatile compound (e.g., iodides) which is decomposed to obtain pure metal. Impurities are left behind as they do not react with $\mathrm{I}_{2}$.

$$
\text { Impure metal }+\mathrm{I}_{2} \longrightarrow \text { Metal iodide } \xrightarrow{\text { Very high temp. }} \text { Pure metal }+\mathrm{I}_{2}
$$

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-114.jpg?height=93&width=104&top_left_y=2553&top_left_x=1818)

## EXTRACTION OF METALS ON THE BASIS OF REACTIVITY SERIES

The reaction of metals or their position in activity series can also give a great idea about extraction of metals.

## Extraction of Metals Placed Low in the Activity Series

Metals that are low in the activity series are very unreactive. The oxides of these metals can be reduced to metals by heating alone. For example, mercury is obtained from its ore, cinnabar (HgS), by the process of heating.

## Reduction using heat :

$$
\begin{gathered}
2 \mathrm{HgS}(\mathrm{~s})+3 \mathrm{O}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \xrightarrow{\text { Heat }} 2 \mathrm{HgO}(\mathrm{~s})+2 \mathrm{SO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \\
2 \mathrm{HgO}(\mathrm{~s}) \xrightarrow{\text { Mercuric oxide }}
\end{gathered}
$$

Copper can also be obtained in a similar manner from its sulphide ore $\left(\mathrm{Cu}_{2} \mathrm{~S}\right)$.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-115.jpg?height=205&width=326&top_left_y=913&top_left_x=255)

When concentrated cinnabar ore (HgS) is roasted the ore is converted to oxide. The reduction of cinnabar ore to oxide is a combination of roasting and reduction.

## Extraction of Metals Placed in the Middle of the Activity Series

Metals such as iron, zinc, lead, copper, etc., are present in the middle of the reactivity series. These are moderately reactive metals and are usually present as sulphides or carbonates. It is easier to reduce an oxide ore as compared to its sulphides and carbonates. If the ore is not an oxide ore, it is first converted to the oxide by the process of calcination or roasting.
Roasting : Sulphide ores are converted into oxides by heating strongly in presence of excess air, to form the corresponding oxides. Sulphur impurities escape as $\mathrm{SO}_{2}$ gas. For example,

$$
\begin{aligned}
& 2 \mathrm{ZnS}(\mathrm{~s})+3 \mathrm{O}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{ZnO}(\mathrm{~s})+2 \mathrm{SO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \\
& 2 \mathrm{PbS}(\mathrm{~s})+3 \mathrm{O}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{PbO}(\mathrm{~s})+2 \mathrm{SO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \\
& 4 \mathrm{FeS}(\mathrm{~s})+7 \mathrm{O}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{Fe}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}(\mathrm{~s})+4 \mathrm{SO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})
\end{aligned}
$$

Calcination : In calcination, ore is heated to a high temperature in absence of air. Usually, carbonate ores or ores containing water are calcined to drive out carbonate and moisture impurities.

$$
2 \mathrm{ZnCO}_{3}(\mathrm{~s}) \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{ZnO}(\mathrm{~s})+\mathrm{CO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})
$$

The zinc oxide is then reduced to zinc by a suitable reducing agent such as carbon.

$$
\mathrm{ZnO}(\mathrm{~s})+\mathrm{C}(\mathrm{~s}) \longrightarrow \mathrm{Zn}(\mathrm{~s})+\mathrm{CO}(\mathrm{~g})
$$

Besides using carbon to reduce metal oxides to metals, highly reactive metals like sodium, calcium, aluminium etc. are also used as reducing agents because they can displace the metal of lower reactivity from their compounds, e.g. when manganese dioxide is heated with aluminium powder, the following reaction takes place.

$$
3 \mathrm{MnO}_{2}(\mathrm{~s})+4 \mathrm{Al}(\mathrm{~s}) \longrightarrow 3 \mathrm{Mn}(\mathrm{~s})+2 \mathrm{Al}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}(\mathrm{~s})+\text { Heat }
$$

A great amount of heat is evolved in such reactions, hence metals are produced in the molten state.
Another example of this type of the reaction is the thermite reaction in which iron oxide reacts with aluminium to give iron and aluminium oxide evolving large amount of heat

$$
\mathrm{Fe}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}(\mathrm{~s})+2 \mathrm{Al}(\mathrm{~s}) \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{Fe}(\mathrm{~s})+\mathrm{Al}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}(\mathrm{~s})+\text { Heat }
$$

## Extraction of Metals Placed at the Top of the Activity Series

Metals such as sodium, magnesium, calcium, aluminium present on the top of the reactivity series are very reactive and cannot be obtained from their compounds by heating with carbon. This is because these metals have more affinity for oxygen than for carbon. Such highly reactive metals are obtained by electrolytic reduction of fused chlorides, e.g. sodium from sodium chloride in a "Downs cell". Similarly, calcium is obtained by the reduction of fused calcium chloride.
At cathode :

$$
\mathrm{Na}^{+}+\mathrm{e}^{-} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Na}
$$

At anode :

$$
2 \mathrm{Cl}^{-} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Cl}_{2}+2 \mathrm{e}^{-}
$$

## STEPS INVOLVED IN THE EXTRACTION OF METALS FROM ORES

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-116.jpg?height=1132&width=1282&top_left_y=445&top_left_x=356)

## CORROSION OF METALS

We have learnt that metals which are chemically active get corroded in presence of moist air. Corrosion is an oxidation reaction with atmospheric oxygen in the presence of water on the surface of a metal.

Rusting is the corrosion of iron on exposure to atmosphere.

$$
2 \mathrm{Fe}(\mathrm{~s})+\frac{3}{2} \mathrm{O}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+x \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l}) \longrightarrow \mathrm{Fe}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3} \cdot x \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{~s})
$$

Hydrated iron (III) oxide
Rusting is called as oxidation reaction because here iron gains oxygen $\mathrm{Fe} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Fe}^{3+}+3 \mathrm{e}^{-}$or iron atoms are losing electrons ( $\mathrm{Fe}-3 \mathrm{e}^{-} \rightarrow \mathrm{Fe}^{3+}$ ). The equation is not need to be balanced and the amount of water ' $x$ ' is variable, from dry to soggy.

The major problem of corrosion occurs with iron (or steel) which is used as a structural material in industries like construction, infrastructure, bridges, rail transport, power transmission, ship building, automobiles, heavy industries etc.
Aluminium, another useful structural metal, also undergoes an oxidation reaction, but does not oxidize and corrode as quickly as its reactivity suggests.

- Iron corrodes more quickly than most other metals. Corrosion or rusting of iron is accelerated

Did You Know! in the presence of $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ and salt solution.

- Once a thin oxide layer of $\mathrm{Al}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$ has formed on the surface of aluminium, it forms a barrier to oxygen and water to prevent further corrosion of aluminium.

Aluminium can be made harder, stronger and stiffer by mixing it with small amounts of other metals (e.g., magnesium) to make alloys. The alkali metals like sodium, used in chemistry laboratories and in some chemical industries, rapidly corrodes in air and needs to be stored under oil.

Copper and lead are both used in roofing solutions because neither is very reactive. The compounds formed on the surface do not flake away as easily as rust does from iron. Lead corrodes to a white lead oxide or carbonate and copper corrodes to form a basic green carbonate (combination of the hydroxide $\mathrm{Cu}(\mathrm{OH})_{2}$ and carbonate $\mathrm{CuCO}_{3}$ ).

In the past, both metals, copper and lead, have been used for piping but as lead is considered too toxic, copper is usually used. Non reactive metals like gold, platinum, mercury do not corrode.

Why people who live in coastal areas encounter more corrosion problems.

## SOLUTION

It is because the air in those areas is more humid and thus corrosion occurs faster in the presence of moisture.

## Prevention of Corrosion

Iron and steel (alloy of iron) are most easily protected by paint which provides a barrier between the metal and air/water. Moving parts of machines can be protected by a water repellent oil or grease layer. Covering the surface with enamel and lacquers is another method.

Did You
Know?

- Material selection is the first step to eliminate or minimize corrosion. The following rule can help as initial guidelines for materials selection for corrosion prevention concerns.

1. Avoid dissimilar metals to prevent galvanic corrosion.
2. Select materials with compact insulating oxide or passive layer such as stainless steel or aluminum.
3. Avoid the usage of materials with localized non uniform stresses by stress relief heat treatment process.
(a) Alloying :

Iron or steel along with other metals can also be protected by 'alloying' or mixing with other metals (e.g., chromium) to make non-rusting alloys. Stainless steel is an example of a non-rusting alloy of iron and carbon. Brass, an alloy of copper with zinc is less expensive and non reactive.
(b) Galvanizing :

Coating iron or steel with a thin zinc layer is called 'galvanizing'. A thin layer of Zn is deposited by electrolysis in which iron/steel object is dipped in molten zinc using Zn as cathode. Zinc preferentially corrodes or oxidizes to form a zinc oxide layer that does not flake off like iron oxide rust. However, if the coated surface is scratched, the zinc again corrodes.
(c) Electroplating :

Electroplating is very common method to coat surface of iron by another metal to prevent corrosion. The process is usually done from aqueous electrolytes at room temperature. It is one of the most popular method to prevent corrosion on steel. Chromium, nickel or aluminium are some common plating metals for corrosion protection.

## CASE STUDY-2 : Extraction and Refining of Metals

| Metal | $\mathbf{K}, \mathbf{N a}, \mathbf{C a}, \mathbf{M g}, \mathbf{A l}$ | $\mathbf{Z n}, \mathbf{F e}, \mathbf{P b}, \mathbf{C u}$ | $\mathbf{C u}, \mathbf{H g}$ | $\mathbf{A g}, \mathbf{A u}, \mathbf{P t}$ |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| "Method of <br> "Extraction" | Electrolysis of <br> molten chloride <br> or oxide | "Reduction of <br> oxide "with <br> carbon" | "Heating <br> "sulphide <br> "in air" | "Found <br> in "native <br> state" |

CASE I : The highly reactive metals such as sodium is extracted by the electrolysis of their molten chloride. Aqueous solution of sodium chloride cannot be used in place of molten sodium chloride. Why?
Molten NaCl contains free $\mathrm{Na}^{+}$and $\mathrm{Cl}^{-}$ions. Following two reactions take place at the two electrodes:
At cathode : $\quad 2 \mathrm{Na}^{+}+2 \mathrm{e}^{-} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{Na}$ (Reduction reaction)

At anode : $\quad$| (Negative electrode) |
| :--- |
| $2 \mathrm{Cl}^{-} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Cl}_{2}+2 \mathrm{e}^{-}$(Oxidation reaction) |

(Positive electrode)

During the electrolysis of a aqueous solution of NaCl , as soon as the sodium metal is formed at cathode, it will react with $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ present in aqueous solution to form NaOH instead of sodium metal.

CASE II : In thermite process aluminium is used as a reducing agent instead of carbon while in case of reduction of ZnO, carbon is used instead of Al. Why?

When the reactivity of Zn and Fe is considered, Fe is less reactive than Zn. So strong reducing agent is required for iron oxide. During the thermite process large amount of heat is evolved, therefore this reaction is exothermic and is used in welding for joining the broken pieces of heavy iron objects.

Due to high reactivity, ZnO is easily reduced by carbon, hence, no need of Al. Aluminium is used as a reducing agent in those cases where the metal oxide is comparatively less reactive metal than zinc.
$\mathrm{ZnO}(\mathrm{s})+\mathrm{C}(\mathrm{s}) \longrightarrow \mathrm{Zn}(\mathrm{s})+\mathrm{CO}(\mathrm{g})$

$$
\mathrm{Fe}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}(\mathrm{~s})+2 \mathrm{Al}(\mathrm{~s}) \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{Fe}(\mathrm{l})+\mathrm{Al}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}(\mathrm{~s})+\text { Heat }
$$

CASE III : General metallurgical process like (i) conversion of ore to oxide (ii) Refining (iii) Dressing of ore (iv) Extraction of metal (v) Concentration of ore, are used to find economically valuable metals. Arrange them in correct sequence and give the reason why these are selected for implementation? Metals are extracted from their ores through certain processes. The first is dressing of ore in this process, the racks of ore are broken down into small pieces and ground to powder. In order to remove gangue (in purities) second process that is called concentration of ore is used. This process increase the concentration of metal in the ore. After that ore is converted to oxide by using next step that is called conversion of ore to oxide. Generally two methods (roasting and calination) are used for this conversion. The oxide ores obtained from this process needs to be reduced to metal so extraction of metal is required. The metal so obtained in this process is not $100 \%$ pure and needs to be further purified. Thus, the proess that is called refining of ore is used. Therefore, correct sequence is (iii) $\rightarrow(\mathrm{v}) \rightarrow(\mathrm{i}) \rightarrow(\mathrm{iv}) \rightarrow($ iii $)$.

## Think Out of the Box

1. Four metals $\mathrm{A}, \mathrm{B}, \mathrm{C}$ and D are all obtained by the reduction of their oxides with carbon. Metal A is used to form a thin layer over the sheets of metal D to prevent its corrosion. Metal B is used for electroplating tiffin boxes made of metal D whereas metal C is used in making car batteries. Metals B and C form an alloy called solder. What are metals $\mathrm{A}, \mathrm{B}, \mathrm{C}$ and D ?
2. For the refining of an impure metal like copper by electrolysis; the impure copper metal is used as an anode whereas pure copper is cathode. It is observed that one of the electrode gradually becomes thinner whereas other becomes thicker. Why do you think this happens?
3. $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ is generally used as a material in fire extinguisher it can not be used to put out fires caused by metals like $\mathrm{K}, \mathrm{Na}$ and Mg . Why $\mathrm{K}, \mathrm{Na}$ and Mg are generally active metals get oxidized by $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ to form respective oxides so these metals continue to burn in atmosphere of $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$. That's why $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ is not used to put out fires caused by these metals.

## NON-METALS

Seventeen elements are generally classified as non-metals; most are gases (hydrogen, helium, nitrogen, oxygen, fluorine, neon, chlorine, argon, krypton, xenon and radon) : and a few are solids (carbon, phosphorus, sulfur, selenium and iodine). Bromine is the only non-metal which is a liquid at room temperature.
All non-metals have following properties in common:

- They are dull.
- They are poor conductors of heat and electricity (they are insulators).
- They are weak and brittle (they easily break or shatter).
- They have a low density (they feel light for their size).
- They do not make a ringing sound when they are hit.

The most abundant non-metal in the earth's crust is oxygen, which constitutes about $50 \%$ of the earth's crust. The next abundant non metal is silicon which constitutes about $26 \%$ of the earth's crust.

$$
\text { Oxygen }>\text { Silicon }>\text { Phosphorus }>\text { Sulphur }
$$

Non-metals are major constituents of earth, air and oceans. Oxygen and nitrogen are the main constituents of air. Oxygen and silicon are the two major constituents of earth. Hydrogen and oxygen are the two major constituents of oceans.
Carbon is a solid non-metal that exists in two very different forms. These are diamond and graphite. The table shows some differences between them.

|  | Diamond | Graphite |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| (i) | It is hardest substance known and its density is equal to $3.5 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{mL}$ | Graphite is soft and slippery with the density of $2.3 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{mL}$ |
| (ii) | Its crystals are octahedral, colourless and transparent. | It is black colored, opaque and has hexagonal crystals. |
| (iii) | In a diamond, each carbon atom is covalently bonded to four other carbon atoms along four corners of regular tetrahedron. This pattern extends in three dimensions. Diamond is hard due to strong covalent bonds present in it. | In graphite, carbon atoms are bonded together in flat layers by strong covalent bonds in a regular hexagon. These layers are held together by much weaker van der Wall's forces, therefore the crystals of graphite are soft and slippery. |
| (iv) | Diamond is a non conductor of electricity. | Graphite is a conductor of electricity. |
| (v) | The standard heat of formation ( $\mathrm{DH}_{\mathrm{f}}^{\circ}$ ) of diamond is 29 $\mathrm{kJ} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}$.![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-119.jpg?height=292&width=324&top_left_y=1622&top_left_x=562) <br> Structure of diamond | It is thermodynamically most stable. Its $\mathrm{DH}_{\mathrm{f}}^{\circ}=0$![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-119.jpg?height=320&width=217&top_left_y=1579&top_left_x=1373) <br> Structure of graphite |

- Diamond is the hardest natural substance on earth, but it is also very brittle and will shatter if hit with a hammer. Graphite is unusual because it is a non-metal that conducts electricity. Knowledge
- Name a non-metal which is a good conductor of heat and electricity?


## SOLUTION

Carbon in the form of graphite is the only non-metal which is a good conductor of electricity. Graphite is an allotrope of carbon and is used for making electrodes.

## Chemical Properties of Non-metals

Non-metals are more reactive with metals than with other non-metals. Generally, non-metals react with each other at a high temperature.

1. Action of air : Non-metals do not react with air at room temperature except white phosphorus which burns in air at room temperature to form its oxide. (other allotropic form of phosphorus, such as red or black do not burn).
Hydrogen burns in air and produces a pop sound and form water

$$
2 \mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+\mathrm{O}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \xrightarrow{\text { Ignition }} 2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l})
$$

Carbon, sulphur etc. burn in oxygen to form their oxides.

$$
\begin{gathered}
\mathrm{C}(\mathrm{~s})+\mathrm{O}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \xrightarrow{\text { Ignition }} \mathrm{CO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \\
\mathrm{S}_{8}(\mathrm{~s})+8 \mathrm{O}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \xrightarrow{\text { Ignition }} 8 \mathrm{SO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \\
\mathrm{P}_{4}(\mathrm{~s})+5 \mathrm{O}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \xrightarrow[\text { Ignition }]{ } \mathrm{P}_{4} \mathrm{O}_{10}(\mathrm{~s})
\end{gathered}
$$

These non-metal oxides react with water to form acids and therefore, these are said to be acidic in nature.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathrm{CO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l}) \longrightarrow & \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{CO}_{3}(\mathrm{aq}) \\
& \text { Carbonic acid } \\
8 \mathrm{SO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+8 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l}) \longrightarrow & 8 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{3}(\mathrm{aq})
\end{aligned}
$$

Chlorine is used as an antimicrobial agent or disinfectant for drinking water due to formation of hypochlorous acid on reaction with water.
2. Action of water: Generally, non-metals do not react with water. However, chlorine dissolves in water and forms an acidic solution.

$$
2 \mathrm{Cl}_{2}+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \xrightarrow[\text { or heat }]{\text { Sunlight }} 2 \mathrm{HCl}+\underset{\text { Hypochlorous acid }}{2 \mathrm{HOCl}}
$$

On passing steam over red hot coke (carbon), we get a gaseous mixture of carbon monoxide and hydrogen which is known as water gas.

$$
\mathrm{C} \text { (red hot) }+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \text { (steam) } \longrightarrow \underbrace{\mathrm{CO}+\mathrm{H}_{2}}_{\text {water gas }}
$$

This proces of obtaining water gas from coal or coke is called coal gasification. Earlier, $1: 1$ mixture of CO and $\mathrm{H}_{2}$ was named as water gas but now the term synthesis gas or syn gas is used for all mixtures of $\mathbf{C O}$ and $\boldsymbol{H}_{2}$, irrespective of their composition.
3. Displacement of one non-metal by another from salt solution : Just like metals, non-metals also differ in their reactivities. Among halogen family (i.e. $\mathrm{Cl}, \mathrm{Br}, \mathrm{I}$ and F ), the order of reactivity is $\mathrm{Cl}>\mathrm{Br}>\mathrm{I}$. Thus chlorine can displace Br and I from solutions of bromides ( NaBr ) and iodides ( NaI ).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathrm{Cl}_{2}+2 \mathrm{NaBr} & \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{NaCl}+\mathrm{Br}_{2} \\
\mathrm{Cl}_{2}+2 \mathrm{NaI} & \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{NaCl}+\mathrm{I}_{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

4. Reaction with acids : Non-metals do not react with acids. However, on heating with conc. sulphuric acid and concentrated nitric acid, some non-metals are oxidised to form respective oxides.

$$
\begin{gathered}
\mathrm{S}(\mathrm{~s})+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4} \text { (conc.) } \xrightarrow{\text { heat }} 3 \mathrm{SO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l}) \\
\mathrm{S}(\mathrm{l})+6 \mathrm{HNO}_{3} \text { (conc.) } \xrightarrow{\text { heat }} \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}(\mathrm{aq})+6 \mathrm{NO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l})
\end{gathered}
$$

5. Reaction with chlorine : Non-metals react with chlorine to form covalent chlorides.

Note : Phosphorus forms two types of chlorides

$$
\begin{gathered}
2 \mathrm{P}+3 \mathrm{Cl}_{2} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{PCl}_{3} \\
\text { Phosphorus trichloride } \\
2 \mathrm{P}+5 \mathrm{Cl}_{2} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{PCl}_{5} \\
\text { Phosphorus pentachloride }
\end{gathered}
$$

6. Reaction with hydrogen : Non-metals when heated with hydrogen form hydrides, e.g. hydrogen combines with nitrogen in the presence of iron catalyst to form ammonia gas.

$$
\mathrm{N}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+3 \mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \stackrel{500^{\circ} \mathrm{C}}{\rightleftharpoons} \underset{\text { Ammonia }}{2 \mathrm{NH}_{3}(\mathrm{~g})}
$$

## Difference in physical properties of metals and non-metals

| Property | Metal | Non-metal |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| State | They are solids at ordinary temperature and usually non-volatile <br> (Exception: mercury which is a liquid) | They exist in all the three states, i.e. solid, liquid and gas. They are either gases or volatile liquids at low temperatures. <br> (Exception: diamond and boron which are hard solids) |
| Density | Their density is generally high. | Their density is generally low. |
| Metallic lustre | They possess metallic lustre | Generally they do not possess metallic lustre. <br> Exception: graphite and iodine |
| Malleability and ductility | They are malleable and ductile. They can be beaten into thin sheets and drawn into wires. <br> Exception: Bi | They are neither malleable nor ductile. |
| Alloy formation | They form alloys with other metals and some non-metals | They do not form alloys (except carbon, silicon and phosphorus). |
| Thermal and electrical conductivity | They are good conductors of heat and electricity. <br> Exception: lead which is a poor conductor of electricity. | They are poor conductors of heat and electricity. <br> Exception: graphite and gas carbon |

## Differences In Chemical Properties of Metal And Non-metal

| Property | Metal | Non-metal |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Nature of oxides | They generally form basic oxides which form alkalis with water. For example: <br> $4 \mathrm{Na}+\mathrm{O}_{2} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ <br> $\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{O}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{NaOH}$ | They form acidic oxides which form acids on hydrolysis. <br> $\mathrm{SO}_{2}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{g}) \longrightarrow \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{3}$ |
| Hydrides | They either form no compound with hydrogen or form unstable hydrides. | They form stable hydrides with hydrogen. |
| Electrochemical nature | They are electropositive elements and form cations. They are liberated at cathode during electrolysis. Some metals like chromium and manganese form some anions also with other elements like oxygen. | They are electronegative elements and form anions. They are liberated at the anode during electrolysis. Exception: Hydrogen. |
| Action of acids | They are generally dissolved in mineral acids and forming a salt with the evolution of a gas. For example: $\mathrm{Zn}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4} \longrightarrow \mathrm{ZnSO}_{4}+\mathrm{H}_{2}$ | They either do not dissolve in mineral acids or form the corresponding oxy-acids. <br> For example: $\mathrm{P}_{4}+20 \mathrm{HNO}_{3} \longrightarrow 4 \mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{PO}_{4}+20 \mathrm{NO}_{2}+4 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ |
| Solubility | They are generally dissolved by a chemical reaction. | Many non-metals are dissolved without any chemical reaction. For example: Chlorine, bromine or iodine dissolves in water. |

## HOW DO METALS AND NON-METALS REACT

In earlier classes we learnt that, noble gases have a completely filled valence shell and hence show little chemical activity. We can, therefore, explain the reactivity of elements as a tendency to attain a completely filled valence shell. Let us have a look at the electronic configuration of noble gases and some metals and non-metals.
A sodium atom has one electron in its outermost shell. If it loses the electron from its M shell then its L shell now becomes the outermost shell and has a stable octet. The nucleus of this atom still has 11 protons but the number of electrons has become 10 , so there is a net positive charge giving us a sodium cation $\mathrm{Na}^{+}$. On the other hand, chlorine atom has seven electrons in its outermost shell and requires one more electron to complete its octet. When sodium and chlorine react with each other, the electron is lost by sodium atom which could be taken up by chlorine atom. After gaining an electron, the chlorine atom gets a unit negative charge, because its nucleus has 17 protons and 18 electrons in its $\mathrm{K}, \mathrm{L}$ and M shells. This gives us a chloride anion
$\mathrm{C} 1^{-}$. So both of these elements can have a give-and-take relation between them as follows (Fig.).
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-122.jpg?height=321&width=1364&top_left_y=408&top_left_x=411)

Sodium and chloride ions, being oppositely charged, attract to each other and are held by strong electrostatic forces of attraction to exist as sodium chloride ( NaCl ).
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-122.jpg?height=127&width=168&top_left_y=829&top_left_x=179)

It should be noted that sodium chloride does not exist as molecules but aggregates of oppositely charged ions.

Let us see the formation of one more ionic compound, magnesium chloride.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-122.jpg?height=108&width=435&top_left_y=1064&top_left_x=616)
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-122.jpg?height=90&width=583&top_left_y=1174&top_left_x=621)
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-122.jpg?height=177&width=556&top_left_y=1260&top_left_x=621)

## Formation of magnesium chloride

The compounds formed in this manner by the transfer of electrons from a metal to a non-metal are known as ionic compounds or electrovalent compounds and these ionic compounds exhibit electrostatic intermolecular forces.

## Properties of Ionic Compounds

(a) Physical nature : Ionic compounds are solids and are somewhat hard because of the strong forces of attraction between the positive and negative ions. These compounds are generally brittle and break into pieces when pressure is applied.
(b) Melting and Boiling points : Ionic compounds have high melting and boiling points . This is because a considerable amount of energy is required to break the strong inter-ionic attraction.

## Melting and Boiling Points of Some Ionic Compounds

| Ionic compound | Melting point (K) | Boiling point (K) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| NaCl | 1074 | 1686 |
| LiCl | 887 | 1600 |
| $\mathrm{CaCl}_{2}$ | 1045 | 1900 |
| CaO | 2850 | 3120 |
| $\mathrm{MgCl}_{2}$ | 981 | 1685 |

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-122.jpg?height=221&width=185&top_left_y=2196&top_left_x=350)

Magnesium oxide, MgO has higher melting point than sodium chloride which is around $2800^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. This is because its $\mathrm{Mg}^{2+}$ and $\mathrm{O}^{2-}$ ions have a greater number of charges, and so form stronger ionic bonds, than the $\mathrm{Na}^{+}$and $\mathrm{Cl}^{-}$ions in sodium chloride. Because it stays solid at such high temperatures, magnesium oxide remains unconductive, and is used for hightemperature electrical insulation.
(c) Solubility : Electrovalent compounds are generally soluble in water (polar solvent) and insoluble in solvents such as kerosene, petrol, (nonpolar solvents) etc.
(d) Conduction of electricity : The conduction of electricity through an aqueous solution involves the movement of charged particles. A solution of an ionic compound in water contains ions, which move to the opposite electrodes when electricity is passed through the solution. Ionic compounds in the solid state do not conduct electricity because movement of ions in the solid state is not possible due to their rigid structure. But ionic compounds conduct electricity in the molten state. This is possible in the molten state since the elecrostatic forces of attraction between the oppositely charged ions overcomes due to the heat. Thus, the ions move freely and conduct electricity.

Time to
Check Your Knowledge
Why potassium bromide (KBr) is non conducting in solid state but it conducts electricity in molten state or in aqueous solution.

## SOLUTION

KBr is an ionic compound with high melting point. In solid state, movement of ions is not possible due to rigid structure. But in molten state or in aqueous solution electrostatic force of attraction between oppositely charged ions is less, thus, conduct electricity due to movement of ions.

Some common items you came across at home or in your chemistry lab are table salt, vinegar, sugar, marble, blue vitrol, potassium ferrocyanide, dilute hydrochloric acid, gelatine, alcohol and baking soda. Now classify ionic compound (s) out of these items. Also provide reasons to support your answer that why have you classified those compounds in category of ionic compounds.

## METALLOIDS

Metalloids are those chemical elements that exhibit properties of both metals and non-metals. They behave as non-metals physically and chemically and show electrical conductivity like metals. However, they are not good conductors of electricity like metals and are known as semiconductors.
The concepts of metalloid and semiconductor should not be confused. Metalloid refers to the properties of certain elements in relation to the periodic table. Semiconductor refers to the physical properties of materials (including alloys, compounds) and there is only partial overlap between the two.
The following elements are generally considered as metalloids :

- Boron (B)
- Germanium (Ge)
- Antimony (Sb)
- Silicon (Si)
- Arsenic (As)
- Tellurium (Te)
- Polonium (Po)

Metalloids characteristics include ability to form amphoteric oxides and ability to behave as semiconductors and semimetals. The metalloids are placed between metals and non-metals in the periodic table. There are seven metalloids in the periodic table that are placed in Group 13, 14, 15, 16 and 17. They form a zig zag step line in the periodic table. They are separated from the metals and non-metals by a line called the 'amphoteric line'. Metalloids like boron, germanium, arsenic are used as dopants in semiconductor chips. Metalloids are usually brittle in nature and behave as electrical insulators at room temperature.

## Uses of Metalloids

- Boron is found in boric acid - a mild antiseptic and insect killer.
- Silicon is used in computers and other electronics and it is used to make rubbery cookware that does not stick to the food. Silicon is also used in making solar energy devices.
- Germanium is used in fiber optics.
- Arsenic can be used with gallium in semiconductors, but it is generally used as an insecticide.
- Antimony is used in batteries, antifriction alloys, cable sheathing, matches, medicines, antiprotozoan drugs, plumbing, soldering (some "lead-free" solders contain $5 \% \mathrm{Sb}$ ), and a couple of other things.
- Tellurium is used in ceramics, semiconductors, and blasting caps.
- Polonium is a very rare natural element. It is found in uranimum ores but it is uneconomical to extract it. These are used in antistatic devices and for research purposes.
Note : Boron, germanium and arsenic, all are used as a dopant in semiconductor chips.

## Multiple Choice Questions (MCQs)

DIRECTIONS : This section contains multiple choice questions. Each question has four choices (a), (b), (c) and (d) out of which ONLY ONE is correct.

(a) Aluminium
(b) Mercury
(c) Magnesium
(d) Potassium

(a) 16
(b) 17
(c) 18
(d) 20

(a) Barium
(b) Aluminium
(c) Carbon
(d) Phosphorus

(b) Acidified $\mathrm{CuSO}_{4}$ solution is used as electrolyte.
(c) Impure Cu is taken as cathode
(d) $\mathrm{Cu}^{2+}$ ion gets collected at anode.

(b) Ammonium chloride
(c) Lithium chloride

## Assertion \& Reason

DIRECTIONS : Each of these questions contains an assertion followed by reason. Read them carefully and answer the question on the basis of following options. You have to select the one that best describes the two statements.
(a) If both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of Assertion.
(b) If both Assertion and Reason are correct, but Reason is not the correct explanation of Assertion.
(c) If Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
(d) If Assertion is incorrect but Reason is correct.

1. Assertion: Metals are electropositive elements.

Reason: Metals form positive ions by losing electrons.
2. Assertion: Electric wires are made up of copper.

Reason: Non metals are bad conductor of electricity.
3. Assertion: Potassium oxide is a basic oxide.

Reason: Solution of potassium oxide in water turns red litmus blue.
4. Assertion: Sodium, potassium and magnesium are never found as free elements in nature.
Reason: Sodium, potassium and magnesium are reactive elements.
5. Assertion: Carbonate ores are changed into oxides by roasting process.
Reason: It is easier to obtain a metal from its oxides.

## Match the Following

1. Column-I
(A) CaO
(B) $\mathrm{Al}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$
(C) $\mathrm{SO}_{2}$
(D) $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$
2. Column-I
(A) Iodine
(B) Diamond
(C) Mercury
(D) Bromine

DIRECTIONS : Each question contains two columns. column I have to be matched with column II.

## Column-II

(p) Amphoteric oxide
(q) Neutral oxide
(r) Basic oxide
(s) Acidic oxide

## Column-II

(p) Liquid metal
(q) Liquid non-metal
(r) Lustrous
(s) Hardest substance

## Passage Based Questions

DIRECTIONS : Study the given paragraph ( $s$ ) and answer the following questions.

Metals are electropositive elements. They can easily lose electrons to form ions. Metals show distinguished physical as well as chemical properties. Generally most of the metals are ductile and malleable with exception such as mercury. These properties make them valuable for commercial as well as domestic uses. Reaction of a metal with water is one of important chemical property. Metals like sodium and potassium reacts with cold water while magnesium reacts with hot water. Metals like aluminium, zinc do not react with hot/cold water but they easily react with steam. When a metal react with hot/cold water the products are metal hydroxide and hydrogen, and when it react with steam, the product are metal oxide and hydrogen. Some metals like sodium, potassium react violently with water.

1. When zinc reacts with steam it produces:
(a) $\mathrm{Zn}(\mathrm{OH})_{2}$
(b) ZnO
(c) $\mathrm{O}_{2}$
(d) $\mathrm{ZnO}_{2}$
2. Most ductile metal among the following is:
(a) Au
(b) Ag
(c) Cu
(d) Al
3. During the reaction of calcium with water, pieces of metal start floating due to the formation of:
(a) $\mathrm{Ca}(\mathrm{OH})_{2}$
(b) $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$
(c) $\mathrm{H}_{2}$
(d) none of these
4. Consider the reactions:

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{Na}(\mathrm{~s})+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l}) \longrightarrow \mathrm{NaOH}(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \\
& \mathrm{Ca}(\mathrm{~s})+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l}) \longrightarrow \mathrm{Ca}(\mathrm{OH})_{2}(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})
\end{aligned}
$$

(a) Reaction (i) is endothermic reaction.
(b) Reaction (ii) is endothermic reaction.
(c) Reaction (ii) is more exothermic than reaction (i).
(d) Reaction (i) is more exothermic than reaction (ii).
5. Metals can be converted into thin sheet by hammering. This property is known as:
(a) Ductility
(b) Sonorous
(c) Malleability
(d) Both (a) and (c)

## Very Short Answer Questions

1. Name two metals which catch fire if kept in open air.
2. Name the three metals which do not react with water at all.
3. A metal ' X ' displaces metal ' Y ' from $\mathrm{Y}_{2}\left(\mathrm{SO}_{4}\right)_{3}$ aqueous solution. Which metal is more reactive ' X ' or ' Y '?
4. Name a non-metallic element which conducts electricity.
5. What are metalloids?
6. What is meant by galvanisation ?
7. Define gangue.
8. What is an amalgam ?
9. Which one of the following metals does not react with oxygen even at high temperatures ?
Calcium, Gold, Sodium
10. Name two metals which are both ductile as well as malleable.
11. Write the chemical equation for the reaction of hot aluminium with steam.
12. A green layer is gradually formed on a copper plate left exposed to air for a week in a bathroom. What could this green substance be ?
13. Name one non-metal and one metal which are in liquid state at room temperature.
14. Which one of the metal in the following metals is (a) most reactive (b) least reactive
$\mathrm{Au}, \mathrm{Na}, \mathrm{Cu}, \mathrm{Ca}$
15. Which chemical element is alloyed with copper to make bronze?
16. Name the main constituents of common brass.
17. Which two major constituents form the alloy duralumin?
18. Name the constituents of an alloy called German silver.
19. Choose a metal out of the following which reacts with hot water but not with cold water : Sodium, Magnesium, Iron
Mention the products formed during the reaction.
20. Choose the metal (from the list given below) which can displace zinc from zinc sulphate solution.
Lead, Copper, Magnesium, Silver.
Write the equation of the chemical reaction involved.
21. Which one is more reactive, Ag or Pt ?
22. Name two metals which can displace hydrogen from dilute HCl .
23. Write the chemical equation to represent the reaction taking place between sodium metal and cold water.
24. How is corrosion of iron prevented by coating it with a layer of oil?
25. Why do silver ornaments turn blackish after some time ?

## Short Answer Questions

## DIRECTIONS : Give answer in two or three sentences.

1. An atom X has seven valence electrons and another atom Y has two valence electrons. Write the formula of the compound formed between X and Y . Also predict the type of bond formed between them.
2. What happens when
(i) Iron nail is placed in silver nitrate solution.
(ii) Iron strip is dipped in zinc sulphate solution.
3. What is 24 carat gold ? How will you convert it into 18 carat gold ?
4. Name an ore of zinc other than zinc oxide. By what process can this ore be converted to zinc oxide.
5. What is the activity series of metals ? Rearrange the following metals in an increasing order of reactivity : Aluminium, Zinc, Mercury.
6. Write chemical equations for the reactions taking place when :
(i) Zinc sulphide is heated in air.
(ii) Reduction of lead oxide by carbon
7. Define the term 'alloy'. Write two advantages of making alloys.
8. Give reasons for each of the following :
(i) Germanium is called a metalloid.
(ii) Gold is known as a precious metal.
9. Give reasons why metals conduct electricity.
10. Write chemical equations to show the reactions taking place when :
(i) Zinc carbonate is calcined.
(ii) Carbon dioxide gas is bubbled into lime water for a long time.
11. State three reasons for counting sulphur amongst the non-metals.
12. Explain how the following metals are obtained from their compounds by the reduction process :
(a) Metal X, which is low in reactivity series.
(b) Metal Y, which is middle of the series.
(c) Metal Z, which is high up in the reactivity series.
13. How does the term ore differ from mineral? Give an example.
14. What are the three major steps involved in extraction of a metal after its ore is mined?
15. A metal X combines with a non-metal Y by the transfer of electrons to form a compound Z .
(i) State the type of bond in compound Z .
(ii) What can you say about the melting point and boiling point of compound Z ?
(iii) Will this compound dissolve in kerosene or petrol?
(iv) Will this compound be a good conductor of electricity?
16. Write one example of each of the following :
(i) Most malleable metal and most ductile metal.
(ii) The best conductor of heat and the poorest conductor of heat.
(iii) A metal with highest melting point and a metal with lowest melting point.
17. (i) A non-metal X exists in two different forms Y and $\mathrm{Z} . \mathrm{Y}$ is the hardest natural substance whereas Z is a good conductor of electricity. Identify $\mathrm{X}, \mathrm{Y}$ \& Z .
(ii) Anelement X , onreactionwithoxygen forms anoxide $\mathrm{XO}_{2}$. The oxide when dissolved in water turns blue litmus red. State whether element X is a metal or a non-metal.
(iii) Name the metal which is alloyed with copper to make bronze.
18. A metal ' $M$ ' is found in nature as its carbonate. It is used in the galvanization of iron. Identify ' $M$ ' and name its ore. How will you convert this ore into free metal?
19. What is cinnabar? How is a metal extracted from cinnabar? Explain briefly.

## Long Answer Questions

1. Give reason for the following :

When a piece of copper metal is added to a solution of zinc sulphate, no change takes place, but the blue colour of copper sulphate fades away when a piece of zinc is placed in its solution.
2. Which of the following metals can displace hydrogen from hydrochloric acid solution? Write appropriate equations for reaction that occurs.
$\mathrm{Al}, \mathrm{Cu}, \mathrm{Ag}$
Also write a short note on active metals which can displace hydrogen from water.
3. Name the following:
(i) A metal that catches fire in open air and gives off white fumes.
(ii) A metal that forms two types of oxides and rusts in moisture; write their formulae also.
(iii) A metal used in stainless steel.
(iv) A metal used in long distance cable wires.
(v) A metal added to gold to harden it.
4. (i) Explain the process electrolytic refining.
(ii) Differentiate between the roasting and calcination processes used in metallurgy. Give an example of each.
5. Why does metal not evolve hydrogen gas with nitric acid?
6. (i) Write the steps involved in the extraction of pure metals in the middle of the activity series from their carbonate ores.
(ii) How is copper extracted from its sulphide ore? Explain the various steps supported by chemical equations. Draw labelled diagram for the electrolytic refining of copper.
7. Give reasons for the following:
(i) Most metals conduct electricity well.
(ii) The reaction of iron oxide $\left[\mathrm{Fe}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}\right]$ with heated aluminium is used to join cracked machine parts.
8. List in tabular form three chemical properties on the basis of which we can differentiate between a metal and a non-metal.
9. Write balanced chemical equations to explain what happens, when
(i) Mercuric oxide is heated.
(ii) Mixture of cuprous oxide and cuprous sulphide is heated.
(iii) Aluminium is reacted with manganese dioxide.
(iv) Ferric oxide is reduced with aluminium.
(v) Zinc carbonate undergoes calcination.

## Reasoning Based Questions

1. Explain why the surface of some metals acquire dull appearance when exposed to air for a long time ?
2. Why are metals highly electropositive in nature ?
3. Why does zinc liberate hydrogen gas when it reacts with dilute hydrochloric acid but copper does not?
4. Why is an ionic bond also called an electrovalent bond ?
5. Why is sulphide ore converted to its oxide before reduction?
6. Why a salt which does not conduct electricity in the solid state becomes a good conductor in molten state ? Explain.
7. Explain why, the galvanised iron article is protected against rusting even if the zinc layer is broken.
8. An iron knife kept dipped in a blue copper sulphate solution, colour of solution changes to light green. Why?
9. Articles made of aluminium do not corrode even though aluminium is an active metal.
10. Why do gold ornameants look new even after several years of use ?
11. Why does aluminium not react with water under ordinary conditions.
12. Hydrogen is not metal but still it has been assigned a place in the reactivity series of metal why?
13. When calcium metal is added to water, the gas evolved close not catch fire but same gas evolved on adding sodium metal to water catches fire. why?
14. Why, a solution of cane sugar does not conduct electricity but a solution of common salt is a good conductor of electricity.
15. Why are ionic compounds usually hard?

## HOTS Questions

1. A copper plate was dipped into a solution of $\mathrm{AgNO}_{3}$. After sometime, a black layer was deposited on the copper plate. State the reason for it. Write the chemical equation of the reaction involved.
2. The oxide $\mathrm{X}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$ is unaffected by water. Outline a method by which a sample of metal X can be obtained from its ore. Give one reason as to why have you chosen this method.
3. Give reasons for the following :
(i) Silicon is considered as metalloids.
(ii) Carbon is not used for extraction of aluminium from aluminium oxide.
(iii) For producing hydrogen gas by reaction with hydrochloric acid, granulated zinc is preferred over block of zinc.
4. Give reasons for the following :
(i) $\mathrm{Na}, \mathrm{K}$ and Ca metals form hydrides by combination with hydrogen gas, but most other metals do not.
(ii) Zinc oxide is called an amphoteric oxide.
5. (a) What is corrosion of metals ? Name one metal which does not corrode and one which corrodes on being kept open.
(b) How will you show that the rusting of iron needs oxygen and moisture at the same time ?
6. Which of the following combinations would result in a displacement reaction? Write appropriate equations for any reactions that occurs.
(a) $\mathrm{I}_{2}(\mathrm{~s})+\mathrm{NaBr}(\mathrm{aq}) \longrightarrow$
(b) $\mathrm{Cl}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+\mathrm{NaI}(\mathrm{aq}) \longrightarrow$
(c) $\mathrm{Br}_{2}(\mathrm{l})+\mathrm{NaCl}(\mathrm{aq}) \longrightarrow$
7. Metals replace hydrogen from acid, whereas non-metals do not. Why?

## Case Study Based MCQs

1. Read the following and answer any four questions from (i) to (v).

Elements can be classified as metals or non-metals on the basis of their properties. The easiest way to start grouping substances is by comparing their physical properties. Metals, in their pure state, have a shining surface. This property is called metallic luster. metals are generally hard. The hardness varies from metal to metal. some metals are used for making cooking vessels.
(i) Metals generally are
(a) reducing agents
(b) oxidising agent
(c) both oxidising and reducing agents
(d) None of these
(ii) The most abundant metal in the earth's crust is -
(a) iron
(b) copper
(c) aluminium
(d) mercury
(iii) The metal that reacts with cold water is -
(a) mercury
(b) sodium
(c) zinc
(d) tungsten
(iv) Metal present in chloroplast is
(a) Iron
(b) Copper
(c) Magnesium
(d) Cobalt
(v) Which of the following metal(s) catch fire on reaction with water?
(a) Sodium
(b) Potassium
(c) Magnesium
(d) both (a) and (b)

## Text - Book Questions

1. Give example of a metal which
(a) is liquid at room temperature.
(b) can be easily cut with knife.
(c) is the best conductor of heat.
(d) is the poorest conductor of heat.
2. Explain the meaning of malleable and ductile.
3. Why is sodium kept immersed in kerosene oil ?
4. Write the equations for the reactions of
(a) iron with steam
(b) calcium with water
(c) potassium with water
5. You are provided with following information :

| Metal | Solution to which metal is added |  |  |  |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
|  | Iron (II) sulphate | Copper (II) sulphate | Zinc sulphate | Silver nitrate |
| A | No reaction | Displacement | - | - |
| B | Displacement | - | No reaction | - |
| C | No reaction | No reaction | No reaction | Displacement |
| D | No reaction | No reaction | No reaction | No reaction |

Answer the following questions.
(a) Which is the most reactive metal ?
(b) What would you observe when B is added to solution of copper (II) sulphate ?
(c) Arrange the metals $\mathrm{A}, \mathrm{B}, \mathrm{C}$ and D in order of increasing reactivity.
6. Which gas is produced when a reactive metal reacts with dilute hydrochloric acid ? Write the chemical reaction when iron reacts with dilute $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}$.
7. What would you observe when zinc is added to a solution of iron (II) sulphate ? Write chemical equation that takes place.
8. Metallic oxides of zinc, magnesium and copper were heated with zinc, Mg and Cu . In which case the displacement reaction will take place ?

| Metals oxide | Zinc | Magnesium | Copper |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Zinc oxide |  |  |  |
| Magnesium oxide |  |  |  |
| Copper oxide |  |  |  |

9. (i) Write electron-dot structures for sodium, magnesium and oxygen.
(ii) Show the formation of $\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ and MgO by transfer of electrons.
(iii) What are the ions present in these compounds?
10. Why do ionic compounds have high melting points?
11. Define the following terms :
(i) minerals
(ii) ores
(iii) gangue
12. Name two metals which are found in free state in nature?
13. What chemical process is used for obtaining a metal from its oxide ?
14. Which metals do not corrode easily ?
15. What are alloys?

## Text - Book Exercise

1. Which of the following will give displacement reactions?
(a) NaCl solution and copper metal
(b) $\mathrm{MgCl}_{2}$ solution and aluminium metal
(c) $\mathrm{FeSO}_{4}$ solution and silver metal
(d) $\mathrm{AgNO}_{3}$ solution and copper metal
2. Which of the following methods is suitable for preventing an iron frying pan from rusting?
(a) Applying grease
(b) Applying paint
(c) Applying a coat of zinc
(d) All the above
3. An element can react with oxygen to give a compound with high melting point. This compound is also water soluble. The element is likely to be
(a) Calcium
(b) Carbon
(c) Silicon
(d) Iron
4. Food cans are coated with tin and not with zinc because
(a) zinc is costlier than tin.
(b) zinc has higher melting point than tin.
(c) zinc is more reactive than tin.
(d) zinc is less reactive than tin.
5. You are given a hammer, a battery, a bulb, wire and a switch:
(a) How would you use them to distinguish between samples of metals and non-metals ?
(b) Assess the usefulness of these tests to distinguish between metals and non-metals.
6. What are amphoteric oxides ? Give examples of two amphoteric oxides.
7. Name two metals which can displace hydrogen from dilute acids and two metals which can not do so.
8. In the electrolytic refining of metal (M), name anode, cathode and electrolyte.
9. Pratyush took sulphur powder on a spatula and heated it. He collected the gas by inverting a test-tube over it as shown in the figure.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-129.jpg?height=650&width=654&top_left_y=1007&top_left_x=331)

What will be the action of gas on
(a) dry litmus paper ?
(b) moist litmus paper ?

Write balanced chemical equation for the reaction taking place.
10. State two ways to prevent rusting of iron.
11. What type of oxides are formed when non-metals combine with oxygen ?
12. Give reasons for the following:
(a) Platinum, silver and gold are used to make jewellery.
(b) Sodium, potassium and lithium are stored under oil.
(c) Aluminium is highly reactive metal but still used for making cooking utensils.
(d) Carbonate and sulphide ores are usually converted into oxides during the process of extraction.
13. You must have seen tarnished copper vessels being cleaned with lemon or tamarind juice. Explain why these sour substances are effective in cleaning vessels.
14. Differentiate between metals and non-metals on the basis of their chemical properties.
15. A man went door to door posing as goldsmith. He promised to bring back the glitter on dull gold ornaments. An unsuspecting lady gave a set of gold bangles to him which he dipped in a particular solution. The bangles sparkled like new but their weight was reduced drastically. The lady got upset and after a futile argument, the man beat a hasty retreat. Can you predict the nature of solution used by the imposter?
16. Give reason as to why copper is used to make hot water tanks and not steel (an alloy of iron).

## Exemplar Questions

1. Iqbal treated a lustrous, divalent element M with sodium hydroxide. He observed the formation of bubbles in reaction mixture. He made the same observations when this element was treated with hydrochloric acid. Suggest how can he identify the produced gas. Write chemical equations for both the reactions.
2. A non-metal A is an important constituent of our food and forms two oxides B and C. Oxide B is toxic, whereas C causes global warming.
(i) Identify $\mathrm{A}, \mathrm{B}$ and C .
(ii) To which group of periodic table does A belong?
3. An alkali metal A gives a compound B (molecular mass $=40)$ on reacting with water. The compound B gives a soluble compound C on treatment with aluminium oxide. Identify $\mathrm{A}, \mathrm{B}$ and C and give the reaction involved.
4. Of the three metals $\mathrm{X}, \mathrm{Y}$ and $\mathrm{Z}, \mathrm{X}$ reacts with cold water, Y with hot water and Z with steam only. Identify X , Y and Z and also arrange them in order of increasing reactivity.
5. A non-metal A which is the largest constituent of air, when heated with $\mathrm{H}_{2}$ in 1:3 ratio in the presence of catalyst ( Fe ) gives a gas B . On heating with $\mathrm{O}_{2}$ it gives an oxide C . If this oxide is passed into water in the presence of air it gives an acid D which acts as a strong oxidising agent.
(i) Identify $\mathrm{A}, \mathrm{B}, \mathrm{C}$ and D
(ii) To which group of periodic table does this nonmetal belong?

## Exercise 3

## Multiple Choice Questions

DIRECTIONS : This section contains multiple choice questions. Each question has four choices (a), (b), (c) and (d) out of which ONLY ONE is correct.

1. A student by mistake used a wet gas jar to collect sulphur dioxide. Which one of the following tests of the gas is likely to fail ?
(a) Odour
(b) Effect on acidified $\mathrm{K}_{2} \mathrm{Cr}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{7}$ solution
(c) Solubility test
(d) None of these
2. Silicon is used in :
(a) solar energy devices
(b) semiconductors
(c) transistors
(d) all of these
3. Which of the following is not a characteristics of metal ?
(a) Malleable
(b) Electropositive nature
(c) Ductile
(d) None of these
4. $\mathrm{Zn}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ (Steam) $\longrightarrow \mathrm{A}+\mathrm{B}$, In the equation A and B are -
(a) $\mathrm{Zn}, \mathrm{H}$ only
(b) $\mathrm{ZnH}_{2}$ and $\mathrm{O}_{2}$
(c) $\mathrm{ZnO}_{2} \& \mathrm{O}_{2}$
(d) $\mathrm{ZnO} \& \mathrm{H}_{2}$
5. Removal of impurities from ore is known as -
(a) crushing and grinding
(b) concentration of ore
(c) calcination
(d) roasting
6. Froth floatation method is used for the concentration of-
(a) oxide ores
(b) sulphide ores
(c) sulphate ores
(d) halide ores
7. Heating of concentrated ore in absence of air for conversion into oxide ore is known as -
(a) roasting
(b) calcination
(c) reduction
(d) none of these
8. Pure gold is -
(a) 24 carats
(b) 22 carats
(c) 20 carats
(d) 18 carats
9. What is anode mud ?
(a) Fan of anode
(b) Metal of anode
(c) Impurities collected at anode in electrolysis during purification of metals
(d) All of these
10. Which of the following pairs will give displacement reactions?
(a) $\quad \mathrm{ZnSO}_{4}$ solution and aluminium metal
(b) $\mathrm{MgCl}_{2}$ solution and aluminium metal
(c) $\mathrm{FeSO}_{4}$ solution and silver metal
(d) $\mathrm{AgNO}_{3}$ solution and copper metal.
11. Which of the following is a chemical method for preventing an iron frying pan from rusting?
(a) applying grease
(b) applying paint
(c) applying a coating of zinc
(d) all of the above
12. An element reacts with oxygen to give a compound with a high melting point. This compound is also soluble in water. The element is likely to be -
(a) calcium
(b) carbon
(c) silicon
(d) iron
13. Aluminium does not oxidise readily in air because -
(a) it is high in the electrochemical series.
(b) it is low in the electrochemical series.
(c) the metal does not combine with oxygen.
(d) the metal is covered with a layer of oxide which does not rub off.
