(a) $\mathrm{Zn}<\mathrm{Fe}<\mathrm{Mg}<\mathrm{K}$
(b) $\mathrm{Fe}<\mathrm{Mg}<\mathrm{Zn}<\mathrm{K}$
(c) $\mathrm{Fe}<\mathrm{Mg}<\mathrm{K}<\mathrm{Zn}$
(d) $\mathrm{Fe}<\mathrm{Zn}<\mathrm{Mg}<\mathrm{K}$
15. The least malleable is -
(a) aluminium
(b) silver
(c) gold
(d) carbon
16. The metal that reacts with cold water is -
(a) mercury
(b) sodium
(c) zinc
(d) tungsten
17. The only metal that is liquid at room temperature is -
(a) mercury
(b) sodium
(c) zinc
(d) tungsten
18. The process of extraction of metal from its ores, is known as -
(a) concentration
(b) calcination
(c) purification
(d) metallurgy
19. The compound from which metal is extracted economically is -
(a) slag
(b) gangue
(c) ore
(d) mineral
20. The process to remove unwanted impurities from the ore is called -
(a) purification
(b) calcination
(c) bassemerisation
(d) concentration
21. The process to heat the ore in the presence of excess supply of air below its melting point is called -
(a) roasting
(b) calcination
(c) smelting
(d) liquation
22. Graphite is a/an -
(a) alloy
(b) metal
(c) metalloid
(d) non-metal
23. One of the constituents of amalgam is -
(a) aluminium
(b) copper
(c) iron
(d) mercury
24. Which of the following metal reacts with water/steam to produce oxide instead of hydroxide ?
(a) Sodium
(b) Potassium
(c) Calcium
(d) Magnesium
25. The white phosphorus is stored
(a) in air
(b) under water
(c) under kerosene
(d) under $\mathrm{CS}_{2}$
26. Sodium is obtained by the electrolysis of -
(a) an aqueous solution of sodium chloride
(b) an aqueous solution of sodium hydroxide
(c) fused sodium chloride
(d) fused sodium sulphate

(a) chloride
(b) bromide
(c) oxide
(d) sulphide
28. Which of the following is incorrect?
(a) Chalcocite

- Copper
(b) Magnetite
- Iron

- Aluminium
(d) Galena
- Lead

29. Among $\mathrm{Mg}, \mathrm{Cu}, \mathrm{Fe}, \mathrm{Zn}$ the metal that does not produce hydrogen gas in reaction with hydrochloric acid is
(a) Cu
(b) Zn
(c) Mg
(d) Fe

[KVPY]
$\mathrm{ZnS}(\mathrm{s})+\mathrm{O}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \xrightarrow{\text { Heat }} \ldots \ldots \ldots$. are
(a) ZnO and $\mathrm{SO}_{2}$

[JSTSE]
(a) Iron
(b) Copper
(c) Magnesium
(d) Cobalt

[NTSE]
(a) remove moisture condensed over the surface of ribbon.
(b) generate heat due to exothermic reaction.

[NTSE]
(a) carbon
(b) aluminium
(c) sulphur
(d) sodium

[KVPY]
(a) only water
(b) only acids
(c) only alkalis
(d) both acids and alkalis

[JSTSE]

(b) $\mathrm{Al}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$
(c) CO
(d) $\mathrm{NO}_{2}$
37. A metal 'M' of moderate reactivity is present as its sulphide ' X '. On heating in air, ' X ' converts into its oxide ' Y ' and a gas evolves. On heating ' Y ' and ' X ' together, the metal ' M ' is produced. ' X ' and ' Y ' respectively are
[NTSE]
(a) ' X ' cuprous sulphide, ' Y ' cuprous oxide
(b) ' X ' cupric sulphide, ' Y ' cupric oxide
(c) ' X ' sodium sulphide, ' Y ' sodium oxide
(d) ' X ' calcium sulphide, ' Y ' calcium oxide

[JSTSE]
(a) Cu
(b) Zn
(c) Fe
(d) Mg

[JSTSE]
(a) $\mathrm{MNO}_{3}$

## Passage Based Questions

DIRECTIONS : Study the given paragraph(s) and answer the following questions.

## PASSAGE - 1

The huge annual loss due to corrosion is a national waste and should be minimized.
Following are some methods which are helpful to prevent corrosion
(i) Coating the iron surface with paint or oil or grease prevents moist oxygen from coming in contact with the metal and thus effectively prevents rusting of iron.
(ii) Galvanisation : Iron is blasted with fine sand to make the surface rough dipped in molten zinc and then cooled. A thin layer of zinc forms on the iron surface. Since zinc is more reactive than iron, it acts as a sacrificial metal and is preferentially oxidised thus preventing oxidation of iron.
(iii) Electroplating with tin, nickel or chromium also prevents rusting.
(iv) Alloying (mixing iron in its molten state with other metals) prevents rusting. Stainless steel is an alloy of iron with Cr or Ni .

1. The most durable metal plating on iron to protect against corrosion is :
(a) nickel plating
(b) copper plating
(c) tin plating
(d) zinc plating
2. The most convenient method to protect the bottom of ship made of iron is :
(a) coating it with red lead oxide.
(b) white tin plating.
(c) connecting it with Mg block.
(d) connecting it with Pb block.
3. The best way to prevent rusting of iron is :
(a) making it cathode
(b) putting in saline water
(c) both of these
(d) none of these

## PASSAGE-2

Some metals are chemically very reactive, whereas others are less reactive or unreactive. On the basis of vigourness of reactions of various metals with oxygen, water and acids, as well as displacement reactions, the metals have been arranged in a group or series according to their chemical reactivity. The arrangement of metals in a vertical column in the order of decreasing reactivities is called reactivity series of metals (or activity series of metals). In reactivity series, the most reactive metal is placed at the top whereas the least reactive metal is placed at the bottom. As we come down in the series, the chemical reactivity of metals decreases. Since the metals placed at the bottom of the reactivity series (like silver and gold) are less reactive, so they are usually found in free state (native state) in nature.

1. When metal Z is added to dilute HCl solution, there is no evolution of gas. Metal is :
(a) K
(b) Na
(c) Ag
(d) Zn
2. Copper sulphate solution can be safely kept in a container made of :
(a) aluminium
(b) lead
(c) silver
(d) zinc
3. Metal always found in free state is :
(a) gold
(b) silver
(c) copper
(d) sodium

## Assertion \& Reason

DIRECTIONS : Each of these questions contains an Assertion followed by Reason. Read them carefully and answer the question on the basis of following options. You have to select the one that best describes the two statements.
(a) If both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of Assertion.
(b) If both Assertion and Reason are correct, but Reason is not the correct explanation of Assertion.
(c) If Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
(d) If Assertion is incorrect but Reason is correct.

1. Assertion : Iron is found in the free state in nature.

Reason : Iron is highly reactive element.
2. Assertion : Different metals have different reactivities with water and dilute acids.
Reason : Reactivity of a metal depends on its position in the reactivity series.
3. Assertion : Zinc becomes dull in moist air.

Reason : Zinc is coated by a thin film of its basic carbonate in moist air.
4. Assertion : Zinc is used in the galvanisation of iron.

Reason : Its coating on iron articles increases their life by protecting them from rusting.
5. Assertion : Nitrate ores are rarely available.

Reason : Bond dissociation energy of nitrogen is very high.
[NTSE]
1.

(b) Roasting

(c) $2 \mathrm{PbS}+3 \mathrm{O}_{2} \xrightarrow{-\mathrm{SO}_{2}} 2 \mathrm{PbO} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Pb}$

## Assertion \& Reason

DIRECTIONS (Qs. 46-50) : Each of these questions contains an Assertion followed by reason. Read them carefully and answer the question on the basis of following options. You have to select the one that best describes the two statements.
(a) If both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of Assertion.
(b) If both Assertion and Reason are correct, but Reason is not the correct explanation of Assertion.
(c) If Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
(d) If Assertion is incorrect but Reason is correct.
46. Assertion : Leaching is a process of reduction.

Reason : Leaching involves treatment of the ore with a suitable reagent so as to make it soluble, while impurities remains insoluble.
47. Assertion : Lead, tin and bismuth are purified by liquation method.
Reason : Lead, tin and bismuth have low m.p. as compared to impurities.
48. Assertion : Froth floatation process is based on the different wetting nature of ore and gangue particles.
Reason : Mustard oil is used as frother in froth floatation process.
49. Assertion : In smelting, the roasted ore is heated with powdered coke in presence of flux.
Reason : Oxides are reduced to metals by C or CO and impurities are removed as slag.
50. Assertion: For the extraction of iron, haematite ore is used.
Reason: Haematite is a carbonate ore of iron.
[NTSE]

13. Bromine is a non-metal and mercury is a metal which are liquid at room temperature.
14. (a) Sodium (Na) is the most reactive,
(b) Gold ( Au ) is the least reactive.
15. Tin
16. Copper and zinc are major constituents of brass.
17. Aluminium and copper
18. German silver contains Copper $(\mathrm{Cu})$, zinc $(\mathrm{Zn})$ and nickel (Ni).
19. Magnesium reacts with hot water.

$$
\mathrm{Mg}+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Mg}(\mathrm{OH})_{2}+\mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})
$$

20. Among all the given metals only Mg is more reactive than Zn .

$$
\mathrm{Mg}+\mathrm{ZnSO}_{4} \longrightarrow \mathrm{MgSO}_{4}+\mathrm{Zn}
$$

21. Silver ( Ag ) is more reactive than platinum ( Pt ) as silver is above Pt in the reactivity series.
22. Zinc and iron.
23. $2 \mathrm{Na}(\mathrm{s})+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l}) \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{NaOH}(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})$
24. A layer of oil prevents exposure of metal to oxygen and moisture, therefore, corrosion of iron is prevented.
25. Silver ornaments gradually turn black due to the formation of a thin silver sulphide layer on their surface by the action of hydrogen sulphide gas present in air.

## Short Answer Questions

1. The bond formed between X and Y will be electrovalent. The formula of the compound is $\mathrm{YX}_{2}$

$$
\mathrm{Y}:+2 \cdot \ddot{\mathrm{X}}: \rightarrow\left[\mathrm{Y}^{2+}\right][: \ddot{\mathrm{X}}:]^{-} \text {or } \mathrm{YX}_{2}
$$

2. (i) Iron is more reactive than silver. Thus ferrous nitrate is formed.

$$
\mathrm{Fe}(\mathrm{~s})+2 \mathrm{AgNO}_{3}(\mathrm{aq}) \longrightarrow \mathrm{Fe}\left(\mathrm{NO}_{3}\right)_{2}(\mathrm{aq})+2 \mathrm{Ag}(\mathrm{~s})
$$

(ii) Iron is below zinc in the reactivity series, therefore iron cannot displace zinc from zinc sulphate solution. No reaction takes place.
3. 24 carat gold is pure gold. Pure gold is very soft and not suitable for making jewellery. Therefore, to increase its hardness, it is alloyed either with copper or silver. 18 carat gold is prepared by alloying 18 parts pure gold with 6 parts of either copper or silver ionic compound.
4. Calamine i.e., $\mathrm{ZnCO}_{3}$. It can be converted into zinc oxide by calcination.

$$
\mathrm{ZnCO}_{3} \xrightarrow{\text { heat }} \mathrm{ZnO}+\mathrm{CO}_{2}
$$

5. The series in which metals are arranged in decreasing order of their reactivity is called activity series of metals. Mercury < Zinc < Aluminium
6. (i) $2 \mathrm{ZnS}+3 \mathrm{O}_{2} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{ZnO}+2 \mathrm{SO}_{2}$
(ii) $\mathrm{PbO}+\mathrm{C} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Pb}+\mathrm{CO}$
7. Alloys are homogeneous mixture of two or more metals. One of them can be non-metal also.
Advantages :
(i) Alloy with required properties can be made.
(ii) Alloys have properties different from the constituting elements.
(iii) Alloys are resistant to corrosion.
(iv) Alloys have high tensile strength.
8. (i) Ge shows the properties of both metals and nonmetals, therefore, it is regarded as metalloid.
(ii) Gold is highly valuable for economy of country as it is very useful in art, jewellery and coinage, therefore, it is called precious metal.
9. Metals conduct electricity due to presence of free electrons which can easily move.
10. (i) $\mathrm{ZnCO}_{3} \xrightarrow{\text { heat }} \mathrm{ZnO}+\mathrm{CO}_{2}$
(ii) $\mathrm{Ca}(\mathrm{OH})_{2}+\mathrm{CO}_{2} \longrightarrow \mathrm{CaCO}_{3}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$

Milkiness will disappear if $\mathrm{CO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})$ is passed through lime water for long time due to formation of $\mathrm{Ca}\left(\mathrm{HCO}_{3}\right)_{2}$.

$$
\mathrm{CaCO}_{3}+\mathrm{CO}_{2}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Ca}\left(\mathrm{HCO}_{3}\right)_{2}
$$

11. (i) Sulphur can gain electrons easily to form $\mathrm{S}^{2-}$ ion, therefore it is considered as non-metal.
(ii) Secondly, sulphur forms $\mathrm{SO}_{2}$ and $\mathrm{SO}_{3}$ which are acidic oxides.
(iii) Sulphur does not displace $\mathrm{H}_{2}$ from water and dilute acids.
12. (a) Metal X can be obtained by self reduction by heating alone.
(b) Metal Y can be obtained by chemical reduction using C or Al .
(c) Metal Z will be obtained by electrolytic reduction.
13. Minerals are naturally occurring chemical compounds of a metal, which may be associated with more impurities. But ore is a chosen mineral of a metal, from which metal is extracted profitably on a large scale, in pure form. For e.g., the ore of Fe is haemetite, while the minerals having Fe may be magnetite, iron pyrites, spathic iron etc.
14. The three steps involved in extraction are:
(i) Concentration of the ore to remove impurities.
(ii) Reduction of the ore to get the metal.
(iii) Purification of the ore.
15. (i) The bond which is formed by loss and gain of electron is called ionic or electro valent bond.
(ii) Melting point and boiling point of ionic compounds are high.
(iii) Ionic compounds are soluble in water not in organic solvents.
(iv) Ionic compounds are good conductors of electricity.
16. (i) Gold and silver are most malleable metals and gold is most ductile metal.
(ii) Best conductor of heat - Silver Poorest conductor of heat - Lead
(iii) Metal with highest melting point - Tungsten Metal with lowest melting point-Gallium
17. (i) X -Carbon, Y - Diamond, Z -Graphite
(ii) Acids turn blue litmus into red. Non-metals from acidic oxides and form acid when dissolved in water hence X is a non-metal.
(iii) Tin is mixed with copper to make bronze.
18. The metal ' M ' is Zinc. Ore of zinc is zinc blend ( ZnS ). Zn is found as $\mathrm{ZnCO}_{3}$ in nature. Zn is obtained from zinc blend from the following steps.
(i) Roasting: $2 \mathrm{ZnS}(\mathrm{s})+3 \mathrm{O}_{2}(\mathrm{~s}) \xrightarrow{\Delta}$

$$
2 \mathrm{ZnO}(\mathrm{~s})+2 \mathrm{SO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})
$$

(ii) Reduction: $\mathrm{ZnO}(\mathrm{s})+\mathrm{C}(\mathrm{s}) \xrightarrow{\Delta} \mathrm{Zn}(\mathrm{s})+\mathrm{CO}(\mathrm{g})$
19. Cinnabar $(\mathrm{HgS})$ is an ore of mercury. When cinnabar is heated in air, it is first converted into HgO and on further heating reduces into Hg .

$$
\begin{gathered}
2 \mathrm{HgS}+3 \mathrm{O}_{2} \xrightarrow{\Delta} 2 \mathrm{HgO}+2 \mathrm{SO}_{2} \\
2 \mathrm{HgO} \xrightarrow{\Delta} 2 \mathrm{Hg}+\mathrm{O}_{2}
\end{gathered}
$$

## Long Answer Questions

1. (i) It is because copper is less reactive than Zn , therefore, it cannot displace Zn from $\mathrm{ZnSO}_{4}$ solution.
$\mathrm{Cu}+\mathrm{ZnSO}_{4} \longrightarrow$ No reaction
But when Zn is dipped in copper sulphate solution, it displaces copper from $\mathrm{CuSO}_{4}$ to form $\mathrm{ZnSO}_{4}$ which is colourless. Reddish brown copper metal gets precipitated because Zn is more reactive than Cu.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-138.jpg?height=108&width=566&top_left_y=2165&top_left_x=1155)
2. The activity series of the metals, tells us that copper and silver do not displace hydrogen from solutions of acids. Aluminum is an active metal that can displace $\mathrm{H}_{2}$ from HCl and form aluminium chloride.

$$
6 \mathrm{HCl}(\mathrm{aq})+2 \mathrm{Al}(\mathrm{~s}) \longrightarrow 3 \mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+2 \mathrm{AlCl}_{3}(\mathrm{aq})
$$

$6\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{Cl}^{-}(\mathrm{aq})\right]+2 \mathrm{Al}(\mathrm{s})$

$$
\longrightarrow 3 \mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+2\left[\mathrm{Al}^{3+}(\mathrm{aq})+3 \mathrm{Cl}^{-}(\mathrm{aq})\right]
$$

$6 \mathrm{H}^{+}(\mathrm{aq})+2 \mathrm{Al}(\mathrm{s}) \longrightarrow 3 \mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+2 \mathrm{Al}^{3+}(\mathrm{aq})$
Very active metals can even displace hydrogen from water. However, such reactions of very active metals of Group IA are dangerous because they generate enough heat to cause explosive ignition of the hydrogen. The reaction of potassium, or another metal of Group IA, with water is also a displacement reaction:

$$
2 \mathrm{~K}(\mathrm{~s})+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l}) \longrightarrow 2\left[\mathrm{~K}^{+}(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{OH}^{-}(\mathrm{aq})\right]+\mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})
$$

3. (i) The metal that catches fire in open air and gives off white fumes is sodium.
(ii) The metal that forms two types of oxides and rusts in moisture is iron. The formulas of its oxides are: $\mathrm{FeO} ; \mathrm{Fe}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$.
(iii) The metal used in stainless steel is chromium besides iron and carbon as it increases corrosion resistance character of steel.
(iv) The metal used in long distance cable wires is aluminium, because it is a light metal and a very good conductor of electricity.
(v) The metal added to gold to harden, is copper.
4. (i) Electrolytic refining : The impure metal is taken as anode and the cathode consists of a pure metal. Cathodes and anodes are placed in a soluble salt of the metal taken as an electrolyte. Pure metal gradually passes on to the cathode and impurities either settles down or dissolved in the solution called anode mud. Large number of metals like copper, silver, gold and zinc are purified by this method. The metal of highest purity are obtained by this method.
(ii) Roasting is a process in which ore is heated in presence of oxygen so as to convert it into oxide, e.g.,

$$
2 \mathrm{ZnS}(\mathrm{~s})+3 \mathrm{O}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{ZnO}(\mathrm{~s})+2 \mathrm{SO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})
$$

Calcination is a process in which ore is heated in absence of air, e.g.,

$$
\mathrm{ZnCO}_{3}(\mathrm{~s}) \xrightarrow{\text { heat }} \mathrm{ZnO}(\mathrm{~s})+\mathrm{CO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})
$$

5. When metal reacts with nitric acid, hydrogen gas is not evolved. It is because $\mathrm{HNO}_{3}$ is a strong oxidising agent. It oxidises $\mathrm{H}_{2}$ to produce water and itself reduced to any of the oxides of nitrogen ( $\mathrm{N}_{2} \mathrm{O}, \mathrm{NO}$ or $\mathrm{NO}_{2}$ ).

For example,

$$
\begin{aligned}
3 \mathrm{Cu}(\mathrm{~s})+ & 8 \mathrm{HNO}_{3}(\mathrm{aq}) \longrightarrow \\
& 3 \mathrm{Cu}\left(\mathrm{NO}_{3}\right)_{2}(\mathrm{aq})+2 \mathrm{NO}(\mathrm{~g})+4 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l})
\end{aligned}
$$

6. (i) Extraction of Zn

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{ZnCO}_{3}(\mathrm{~s}) \xrightarrow{\Delta} \mathrm{ZnO}(\mathrm{~s})+\mathrm{CO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \\
& \mathrm{ZnO}(\mathrm{~s})+\mathrm{C}(\mathrm{~s}) \longrightarrow \mathrm{Zn}(\mathrm{~s})+\mathrm{CO}(\mathrm{~g})
\end{aligned}
$$

(ii) Copper is found as $\mathrm{Cu}_{2} \mathrm{~S}$ in nature and can be obtained from its ore just by heating in air.

$$
2 \mathrm{Cu}_{2} \mathrm{~S}+3 \mathrm{O}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \xrightarrow[2 \mathrm{Cu}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{~s})+2 \mathrm{SO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})]{\Delta}
$$

(2) $2 \mathrm{Cu}_{2} \mathrm{O}+\mathrm{Cu}_{2} \mathrm{~S} \xrightarrow{\Delta} 6 \mathrm{Cu}(\mathrm{s})+\mathrm{SO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})$

Step - 1 is the heating of sulphide ore of copper in the presence of excess of air. This process is known as roasting.
Step - 2 is the self reduction of copper.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-139.jpg?height=389&width=533&top_left_y=1071&top_left_x=1215)
7. (i) Metals are electropositive elements. They can easily lose electrons to form ions.

$$
\mathrm{M} \longrightarrow \mathrm{M}^{+}+\mathrm{e}^{-}
$$

These electrons are relatively free to move. Due to mobility of electrons most metals conduct electricity.
(ii) The reaction of $\mathrm{Fe}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$ with heated alumina is known as thermite
$\mathrm{Fe}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}(\mathrm{~s})+2 \mathrm{Al}(\mathrm{s}) \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{Fe}(\mathrm{l})+\mathrm{Al}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}(\mathrm{~s})+$ Heat The reaction is highly exothermic. In the reaction aluminium acts as reducing agent and reduces $\mathrm{Fe}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$ to Fe and itself oxidises to $\mathrm{Al}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$. Due to large amount of heat produced, the iron is produced in molten state. Molted iron is used to join cracked machine parts.
8. Difference between metal and a non-metal -

|  | Metal | Non-metal |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1. | They react with oxygen to form basic oxides, which forms alkalies with water, $\begin{aligned} & 4 \mathrm{Na}+\mathrm{O}_{2} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{O} \\ & \mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{O}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{NaOH} \end{aligned}$ | They react with oxygen to form acidic or neutral oxides. $\mathrm{C}+\mathrm{O}_{2} \longrightarrow \mathrm{CO}_{2}$ |
| 2. | They are electropositive elements and form cation. They are liberated at cathode during electrolysis. | They are electronegative elements and form anions. They are liberated at the anode during electrolysis. |
| 3. | They generally dissolve in mineral acids forming a salt with the evolution of hydrogen gas. e.g. $2 \mathrm{Na}+2 \mathrm{HCl} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{NaCl}+\mathrm{H}_{2}$ | They either do not dissolve in mineral acids or form the corresponding oxy acids. e.g. $\mathrm{P}_{4}+20 \mathrm{HNO}_{3} \longrightarrow 4 \mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{PO}_{4}+20 \mathrm{NO}_{2}+4 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ |

9. (i) Mercuric oxide decomposes to mercury and oxygen gas on heating.
$2 \mathrm{HgO} \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{Hg}+\mathrm{O}_{2}$
(ii) When cuprous oxide is heated with cuprous sulphide, copper metal is obtained.
$2 \mathrm{Cu}_{2} \mathrm{O}+\mathrm{Cu}_{2} \mathrm{~S} \rightarrow 6 \mathrm{Cu}+\mathrm{SO}_{2}$
(iii) When manganese dioxide reacts with aluminium then it results into the formation of aluminium trioxide and manganese.
$4 \mathrm{Al}+3 \mathrm{MnO}_{2} \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{Al}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}+3 \mathrm{Mn}$
(iv) When Ferric oxide is heated with aluminium, iron metal is obtained.
$\mathrm{Fe}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}+\mathrm{Al} \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{Fe}+\mathrm{Al}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$
(v) When zinc carbonate is heated in calcination process it converted to zinc oxide then it can be easily converted to metal.
$\mathrm{ZnCO}_{3} \rightarrow \mathrm{ZnO}+\mathrm{CO}_{2}$

## Reasoning Based Questions

1. Due to the formation of a thin layer of oxide, carbonate or sulphide on the surface some metal acquires dullness. For example, silver on exposure to air acquire a blackish tinge due to the formation of silver sulphide.
2. Metals are electropositive elements because they lose electrons very easily to form positively charged ions.

$$
\begin{gathered}
\mathrm{K} \rightarrow \mathrm{~K}^{+}+\mathrm{e}^{-} \\
\mathrm{Mg} \rightarrow \mathrm{Mg}^{2+}+2 \mathrm{e}^{-}
\end{gathered}
$$

3. Zn is above hydrogen while copper is below hydrogen in the activity series of metals. Therefore, zinc displaces hydrogen from dilute HCl while copper does not.

$$
\begin{gathered}
\mathrm{Zn}(\mathrm{~s})+2 \mathrm{HCl}(\mathrm{aq}) \rightarrow \mathrm{ZnCl}_{2}(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \\
\mathrm{Cu}(\mathrm{~s})+2 \mathrm{HCl}(\mathrm{aq}) \rightarrow \text { No reaction }
\end{gathered}
$$

4. An ionic bond is also called as electrovalent bond because of the involvement of electrostatic forces of attraction between oppositely charged ions in the formation of ionic bond.
5. It is because oxygen can be easily removed from metal oxides by reacting it with carbon or aluminium or by electrolytic reduction, sulphur cannot be removed from metal sulphides easily.
6. In solid state, ionic compounds do not conduct electric current because the ions are held together in fixed positions by strong electrostatic forces and cannot move freely. When ionic solid is dissolved in water or melted, the crystal structure is broken down and ions become free to move and conduct electricity.
7. The galvanised iron article remains protected against rusting even if the zinc layer is broken because zinc is more easily oxidised than iron. So when zinc layer on the surface of galvanised iron article is broken, then zinc continues to corrode but iron article does not corrode or rust.
8. Iron displaces copper from copper sulphate solution as it lie above copper in reactivity series and forms ferrous sulphate solution which is light green.

$$
\underset{\text { (Blue) }}{\mathrm{Fe}}+\underset{\text { (Light green) }}{\mathrm{CuSO}_{4}} \longrightarrow \underset{4}{\mathrm{FeSO}_{4}}+\mathrm{Cu}
$$

9. It is because oxide layer is formed on the surface of aluminium due to which it does not react further because it is non-penetrating layer.
10. Gold is highly unreactive metal which remains unaffected by air, water vapour and other gases in this atmosphere. So gold does not corrode. That is why gold ornaments look new even after several years of use.
11. Aluminium does not react with water at ordinary temperature due to the presence of a thin but strong layer of aluminium oxide on its surface.
12. Hydrogen is not a metal but like metal it loses electrons and forms positive ions, $\mathrm{H}^{+}$. Therefore, it has been assigned a place in the reactivity series.
13. Calcium reacts with water to produce calcium hydroxide and hydrogen gas. The heat produced during the reaction is not sufficient to burn the hydrogen gas.
$\mathrm{Ca}(\mathrm{s})+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l}) \longrightarrow \mathrm{Ca}(\mathrm{OH})_{2}(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})$
Sodium reacts vigorously with water to produce sodium hydroxide and hydrogen gas. Large amount of heat is produced during the reaction which causes ignition of hydrogen gas.
$\mathrm{Z} \mathrm{Na}(\mathrm{s})+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l}) \longrightarrow \mathrm{Z} \mathrm{NaOH}(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+$ Heat
14. Common salt is an ionic solid. When ionic solid is dissolved in water, its crystal structure is break down and ions become free to move and conduct electricity. Cane sugar is a covalent compound therefore in its aqueous solution ions are not present hence do not conduct electricity.
15. Ionic compounds consists of oppositely charged ions and they attract each other very strongly. It forms a regular crystal structure. Therefore, ionic compounds are usually hard.

## HOTS Questions

1. The black layer was deposited on copper due to deposition of silver metal on it.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-140.jpg?height=108&width=604&top_left_y=2074&top_left_x=1110)
2. $\mathrm{X}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$ is unaffected by water. It must be $\mathrm{Al}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$. ' X ' is obtained from its ore by electrolytic reduction because aluminium itself is good reducing agent and cannot be obtained from $\mathrm{Al}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$ by chemical reduction.
3. (i) Silicon is a metalloid because its properties resembles both with metals as well as non-metals.
(ii) Aluminium is stronger reducing agent than carbon, therefore, carbon cannot be used for extraction of Al from its oxide.
(iii) Granulated zinc has more surface area than block of zinc metal, therefore, it is used for preparation of $\mathrm{H}_{2}$ gas.
4. (i) $\mathrm{Na}, \mathrm{K}$ and Ca are strongly electropositive, therefore, they form hydrides with hydrogen.
(ii) Zinc oxide reacts with both acids as well as bases, therefore, it is called an amphoteric oxide.
5. (a) Corrosion is a process in which metal reacts with air and moisture from the atmosphere along with some dilute acid to produce coating on its surface. Iron corrodes readily when placed in open atmosphere whereas aluminium does not corrode easily.
(b) Take three test tubes and put iron nails in each of them. In the first test tube add $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ up to brim and leave no space for air. In the second test tube add fused $\mathrm{CaCl}_{2}$ so as to absorb moisture. In the third test tube add water and leave space for air. Leave them for few days. Rusting will not take place in first and second test tubes. But rusting will take place in third test tube showing that rusting of iron requires both air and moisture.
6. The activity of the halogens decreases from top to bottom in the periodic table. Br is above I and Cl is above Br in the periodic table. Therefore, neither combination (a) nor combination (c) could result in reaction. Cl is above I in the periodic table, and so combination (b) results in a displacement reaction.
The more active halogen, chlorine, displaces the less active halogen, iodine from its compounds.

$$
\begin{aligned}
& 2 \mathrm{NaI}(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{Cl}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \longrightarrow \mathrm{I}_{2}(\mathrm{~s})+2 \mathrm{NaCl}(\mathrm{aq}) \\
& 2\left[\mathrm{Na}^{+}(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{I}^{-}(\mathrm{aq})\right]+\mathrm{Cl}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\longrightarrow \mathrm{I}_{2}(\mathrm{~s})+2\left[\mathrm{Na}^{+}(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{Cl}^{-}(\mathrm{aq})\right]
$$

$2 \mathrm{I}^{-}(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{Cl}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \longrightarrow \mathrm{I}_{2}(\mathrm{~s})+2 \mathrm{Cl}^{-}(\mathrm{aq})$
7. Non-metals cannot supply electrons to convert $\mathrm{H}^{+}$to $\mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})$ whereas metals can give electrons for conversion.

$$
2 \mathrm{H}^{+}+2 \mathrm{e}^{-} \rightarrow \mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})
$$

## Case Study Based MCQs

1. (i) (a) As metals are electropositive in nature and have tendency to lose electrons.
(ii) (c)
(iii) (b) It is because of extremely high reactivity of sodium.
(iv) (c) In chloroplast Mg is present.
(v) (d) Sodium and potassium both are extremely reactive and react with water so vigorously. The reaction is highly exothermic so the hydrogen evolved will catch fire.

## Master NCERT (Textbook \& Exemplar)

## Text - Book Questions

1. 

(a) Mercury (Hg)
(b) Sodium (Na)
(c) Silver (Ag)
(d) Lead (Pb)
2. Malleable : The property by virtue of which a substance can be beaten into sheets is known as malleability. The
substance that shows the property of malleability is known as malleable substance. Metals are malleable in nature.
Ductile : The property by virtue of which a substance can be drawn into wires is known as ductility. A substance that shows the property of ductility is known as ductile. Metals are ductile in nature.
3. Sodium is a very reactive metal. It reacts with air and water. If kept open in air it reacts readily with oxygen (present in air) to form sodium oxide. It also reacts with water (moisture present in air) to form sodium hydroxide.
$4 \mathrm{Na}(\mathrm{s})+\mathrm{O}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{s})$
$2 \mathrm{Na}(\mathrm{s})+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l}) \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{NaOH}(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})$
To preserve sodium metal, it is generally kept under kerosene oil so that neither air nor moisture may come in contact with metal.
4. (a) $3 \mathrm{Fe}(\mathrm{s})+4 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}($ steam $) \longrightarrow \mathrm{Fe}_{3} \mathrm{O}_{4}(\mathrm{~s})+4 \mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})$
(b) $\mathrm{Ca}(\mathrm{s})+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l}) \longrightarrow \mathrm{Ca}(\mathrm{OH})_{2}(\mathrm{~s})+\mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})$
(c) $\mathrm{K}(\mathrm{s})+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l}) \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{KOH}(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})$
5. In the reactivity series we find that order of reactivity of metals present in the salt solutions is $\mathrm{Zn}>\mathrm{Fe}>\mathrm{Cu}>$ Ag. Thus we can say that by looking at the information provided in the table, we can conclude that
(i) Metal A is more reactive than copper as it displaces copper from copper (II) sulphate and is less reactive than iron as it fails to displace iron from iron (II) sulphate.
(ii) Metal B is more reactive than iron and less reactive than zinc.
(iii) Metal C is more reactive than silver and less reactive than other metals (i.e. $\mathrm{Fe}, \mathrm{Cu}$ and Zn ).
(iv) Metal D is least reactive.

Thus
(a) The most reactive metal is B .
(b) Since B is more reactive than iron so it is also more reactive than copper hence it will displace copper from copper (II) sulphate solution. The blue colour of copper (II) sulphate solution will slowly fade.
(c) The arrangement of metals $\mathrm{A}, \mathrm{B}, \mathrm{C}$ and D in decreasing order of reactivity is $\mathrm{B}>\mathrm{A}>\mathrm{C}>\mathrm{D}$.
6. When a reactive metal reacts with dilute hydrochloric acid, the gas produced is hydrogen $\left(\mathrm{H}_{2}\right)$.
Iron and dilute $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}$ react to produce $\mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})$.

$$
\mathrm{Fe}(\mathrm{~s})+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}(\mathrm{dil}) \longrightarrow \mathrm{FeSO}_{4}(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})
$$

7. The green colour of iron (II) sulphate solution will disappear. Zinc will gradually dissolve and iron will get precipitated at the bottom of the container.
$\mathrm{Zn}(\mathrm{s})+\mathrm{FeSO}_{4}(\mathrm{aq}) \longrightarrow \mathrm{ZnSO}_{4}(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{Fe}(\mathrm{s})$
8. From the reactivity series we find the order of reactivity of $\mathrm{Zn}, \mathrm{Mg}$ and Cu as
$\mathrm{Mg}>\mathrm{Zn}>\mathrm{Cu}$
i.e., magnesium is most reactive and so it will displace zinc from zinc oxide and copper from copper oxide
$\mathrm{Mg}+\mathrm{ZnO} \longrightarrow \mathrm{MgO}+\mathrm{Zn}$ (displacement)
$\mathrm{Mg}+\mathrm{CuO} \longrightarrow \mathrm{MgO}+\mathrm{Cu}$ (displacement)
Zinc is less reactive than magnesium so it will not displace magnesium from magnesium oxide.
$\mathrm{Zn}+\mathrm{MgO} \longrightarrow$ No reaction
Zinc is more reactive than copper so it will displace copper from copper oxide.
$\mathrm{Zn}+\mathrm{CuO} \longrightarrow \mathrm{ZnO}+\mathrm{Cu}$ (displacement)
Since copper is least reactive it will neither displace Mg nor Zn from their oxides.
$\mathrm{Cu}+\mathrm{MgO} \longrightarrow$ No reaction
$\mathrm{Cu}+\mathrm{ZnO} \longrightarrow$ No reaction
9. (i) Sodium $(Z=11) ; \quad 2,8,1 \quad \stackrel{\bullet}{N a}$

Magnesium ( $\mathrm{Z}=12$ ); 2, 8, $2 \quad \bullet$ Mg •
Oxygen $(\mathrm{Z}=8) ; \quad 2,6 \quad \mathbf{:} \mathbf{O} \boldsymbol{:}$
(ii) Formation of $\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{O}$
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-142.jpg?height=170&width=533&top_left_y=1105&top_left_x=322)

Formation of MgO
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-142.jpg?height=168&width=547&top_left_y=1324&top_left_x=322)
(iii) The ions present in $\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ are $\mathrm{Na}^{+}$and $\mathrm{O}^{2-}$ i.e. sodium ion ( $\mathrm{Na}^{+}$) and oxide ion ( $\mathrm{O}^{2-}$ ).
The ions present in MgO are $\mathrm{Mg}^{2+}$ and $\mathrm{O}^{2-}$ i.e. magnesium ion ( $\mathrm{Mg}^{2+}$ ) and oxide ions ( $\mathrm{O}^{2-}$ )
10. Ionic compounds consists of oppositely charged ions i.e. positively charged cations and negatively charged anions. They are held together by strong electrostatic force of atraction. Therefore, ionic compounds have high melting points and boiling points.
11. (i) The elements or compounds, which occur naturally in the earth's crust are known as minerals.
(ii) Those minerals from which an element can be extracted economically is called an ore.
(iii) The unwanted rocky, earthy or sandy materials almost always associated with ores as impurities are called gangue or matrix.
12. Gold ( Au ) and Platinum ( Pt ).
13. Reduction process.
14. Au (gold) and Pt (Platinum), which are placed near the bottom of the activity series do not corrode easily.
15. Alloys are homogeneous mixtures of more than one metal or metals and nonmetals.

## Text - Book Exercise

1. (d) Copper will displace silver from silver nitrate solution because copper lies above silver in reactivity series of metals.
2. (d) All the above methods are helpful in preventing iron pan from rusting.
3. (a) Calcium (Ca) combines with oxygen to form calcium oxide ( CaO ) which has a high melting point and dissolves in water to form $\mathrm{Ca}(\mathrm{OH})_{2}$.
4. (c) Zinc is more reactive than tin (zinc is above tin in reactivity series) so it will react with organic acids (present in food) to form poisonous compounds. To avoid this food cans are coated with tin and not with zinc.
5. (a) Using the hammer, strike both metal and non-metal (solid) and observe if any sound is produced. Metals produce sound when struck with hammer whereas non-metals do not produce sound when struck with hammer.
Note: Metals are sonorous; Non-metals are nonsonorous.
Also if we use hammer to convert the metal and non-metal (solid) in the form of sheet we will find that metals get converted to a sheet but non-metals will break. Note : Metals are malleable and ductile while non-metals are brittle.
The given material can be used to distinguish between metal and non-metal if we set up the electric circuit as shown below :
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-142.jpg?height=419&width=766&top_left_y=1347&top_left_x=1053)

Insert the sample of metal and non-metal between the terminals A and B as shown in figure. We will observe that the bulb glows, when the sample introduced is that of a metals and the bulb does not glow when the sample introduced is that of a non-metal.
Note: Metals are good conductors of electricity whereas non-metals are bad conductors.
(b) From these tests it can be easily concluded that

- metals are malleable where as non-metals are not.
- metals are good conductors of electrictity whereas non-metals are not.

6. Amphoteric oxides are those oxides which have both acidic as well as basic characters. Therefore, amphoteric oxides can read both acid and a base.
Examples: Aluminium oxide $\left(\mathrm{Al}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}\right)$ and zinc oxide $(\mathrm{ZnO})$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathrm{ZnO}(\mathrm{~s})+2 \mathrm{HCl}(\mathrm{aq}) & \longrightarrow \mathrm{ZnCl}_{2}(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l}) \\
\mathrm{ZnO}(\mathrm{~s})+2 \mathrm{NaOH}(\mathrm{aq}) & \longrightarrow \mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{ZnO}_{2}(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l})
\end{aligned}
$$

7. Sodium and calcium can displace hydrogen from dilute acids as they lie above hydrogen in the reactivity series. Copper and silver can not displace hydrogen from dilute acids as they lie below hydrogen in the reactivity series.
8. Anode : It is a rod or wire of impure metal M

Cathode : It is a rod or wire of pure metal M
Electrolyte : It is an aqueous solution of a soluble salt of the metal M.
9. On heating sulphur will produce $\mathrm{SO}_{2}$ gas

$$
\underset{\text { (air) }}{\mathrm{S}}+\underset{2}{\mathrm{O}_{2}} \longrightarrow \mathrm{SO}_{2}
$$

(a) It will not change the colour of dry litmus paper.
(b) It will change the colour of moist litmus paper from blue to red.
Note: The colour change of litmus paper is due to the formation of $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{3}$ (sulphurous acid) by dissolution of $\mathrm{SO}_{2}$ in $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$

$$
\mathrm{SO}_{2}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \longrightarrow \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{3}
$$

10. The rusting of iron can be prevented by
(i) applying grease
(ii) applying paint
(iii) galvanisation (applying a thin coat of zinc)
11. When non-metals combine with oxygen the oxides formed are either acidic or neutral e.g.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathrm{C}+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{O}_{2} & \rightarrow \mathrm{CO}(\text { neutral oxide }) \\
\mathrm{C}+\mathrm{O}_{2} & \rightarrow \mathrm{CO}_{2}(\text { acidic oxide })
\end{aligned}
$$

12. (a) Platinum, silver and gold are very less reactive (found near the bottom in reactivity series) and are not affected by air, water or dil. acids. Because of
their low reactivity and their bright lustre, these metals are used for making jewellary.
(b) Sodium, potassium and lithium react with water (or moisture in air) producing a lot of heat along with evolution of hydrogen. As a result, the hydrogen gas thus produced catches fire. To avoid accidents these metals are not kept in air. They are kept under kerosene to avoid contact with air and water.
(c) When aluminium is exposed to air it reacts with oxygen (present in air) to form aluminium oxide $\left(\mathrm{Al}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}\right)$. This $\mathrm{Al}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$ formed gets deposited on the surface of aluminium metal in the form of a thin coating which acts as a protective coating and due to its presence metal becomes unreactive. The formation of protective layer of aluminium oxide on the surface make highly reactive aluminium metal suitable for making cooking utensils.
(d) Since we can easily reduce the metal oxides to the metal using coke (C) or any other suitable reducing agent, so carbonates and sulphides are converted to the oxides form by process of roasting or calcination.
13. Copper metal reacts slowly with water, carbon dioxide and oxygen (all these present in air) and forms basic copper carbonate $\left[\mathrm{Cu}(\mathrm{OH})_{2} \mathrm{CuCO}_{3}\right]$ which gets deposited as a green layer on its surface. Lemon juice contains citric acid and tamarind juice contains tartaric acid. Both these juices which contain an acid react with basic copper carbonate to form soluble copper salts which are removed from the surface of copper metal and the surface of copper metal begins to shine.
14. Differences in chemical properties of metals and non-metals:

| Properties | Metals | Non-metals |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Nature of oxides | They generally form basic oxides which form alkalies with water. For example:![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-143.jpg?height=67&width=413&top_left_y=1796&top_left_x=623) <br> $\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{O}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{NaOH}$ <br> Sodium hydroxide (alkali) | They form acidic oxides. $\mathrm{S}_{8}(\mathrm{~s})+\mathrm{O}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \longrightarrow 8 \mathrm{SO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})$ <br> $8 \mathrm{SO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+8 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l}) \longrightarrow \underset{\text { (acid) }}{8 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{3}(\mathrm{aq})}$ |
| Electrochemical nature | They are electropositive elements and form cation. They are liberated at cathode during electrolysis. Some metals like chromium \& manganese form some anions also with other elements like oxygen. | They are electronegative elements and form anions. They are liberated at the anode during electrolysis (exception: hydrogen). |
| Action of acids | They generally dissolve in mineral acids forming a salt with the evolution of a gas. <br> For example: $\mathrm{Zn}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4} \longrightarrow \mathrm{ZnSO}_{4}+\mathrm{H}_{2}$ | They either do not dissolve in mineral acids or form the corresponding oxyacids. For example: $\mathrm{P}_{4}+20 \mathrm{HNO}_{3} \xrightarrow[4 \mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{PO}_{4}]{ }+20 \mathrm{NO}_{2}+4 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ |
| Solubility | They generally dissolve by a chemical reaction. | Many non-metals dissolve without any chemical change taking place. For example: chlorine, bromine or iodine dissolves in water. |

15. The man has used an aqua-regia solution (mixture of conc. HCl and conc. $\mathrm{HNO}_{3}$ in the ratio of $3: 1$ by volume). Gold dissolves in aqua-regia and forms gold (III) chloride, $\mathrm{AuCl}_{3}$ (auric chloride). Since, gold dissolves in aqua-regia, so a loss in mass of gold bangles occurred. Since the dull layer of gold was removed from its surface so there was original shine on the bangles. It is an excellent oxidant and highly corrosive.
16. Copper does not react easily with water unlike iron (constituent of steel) and it is better conductor of heat than steel so it can be used to heat the water uniformly. It is less efficient in conserving heat but this disadvantage is corrected with heat insulation.

Saline water is highly conducting and hence accelerates the formation of rust.

1. (b) Leaching is a chemical method of concentration of ores. Leaching involves the treatment of the ore with a suitable reagent so as to make it soluble while impurities remain insoluble. The ore is recovered from the solution by suitable chemical method.
2. (d) Smelting involves the reduction of the ore to the molten metal at a high temperature. For the extraction of less electropositive metal powerful reducing agents such as $\mathrm{C}, \mathrm{H}_{2}, \mathrm{CO}$ (water gas), Na , $\mathrm{K}, \mathrm{Mg}, \mathrm{Al}$ may be used.
3. (a)
4. (b) This process is also called auto reduction process or air reduction process. The sulphide ores of less electropositive metals are heated in air to convert part of the ore into oxide or sulphate which then react with the remaining sulphide ore to give the metal and sulphur dioxide.

$$
\begin{aligned}
& 2 \mathrm{Cu}_{2} \mathrm{~S}+3 \mathrm{O}_{2} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{Cu}_{2} \mathrm{O}+2 \mathrm{SO}_{2} \\
& \mathrm{Cu}_{2} \mathrm{~S}+2 \mathrm{Cu}_{2} \mathrm{O} \longrightarrow 6 \mathrm{Cu}+\mathrm{SO}_{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

## Think out of the box

## CASE STUDY-1

1. $\mathrm{HNO}_{3}$ is a strong oxidising agent so it oxidises hydrogen gas to $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ and itself is reduced to any of the nitrogen oxide (such as $\mathrm{N}_{2} \mathrm{O}, \mathrm{NO}$ or $\mathrm{NO}_{2}$ ). Therefore, no hydrogen gas is evolved.
2. The reactivity order of metals, $\mathrm{Mg}, \mathrm{Fe}$ and Cu is $\mathrm{Mg}>\mathrm{Fe}>\mathrm{Cu}$, therefore reaction (i) will not occur because Fe is less reactive than Mg ; Reaction (ii) will also not occur because Cu is least reactive among them.
3. When zinc plate is dipped in $\mathrm{FeSO}_{4}$ solution, then the green colour of $\mathrm{FeSO}_{4}$ fades gradually due to the formation of zinc sulphate solution and iron metal is deposited on zinc plate. $\mathrm{Zn}(\mathrm{s})+\underset{\text { Green solution }}{\mathrm{FeSO}_{4}(\mathrm{aq})} \longrightarrow \underset{\substack{\text { Zinc sulphate } \\ \text { (colourless solution) }}}{\mathrm{ZnSO}_{4}(\mathrm{aq})}+\mathrm{Fe}(\mathrm{s})$

## CASE STUDY-2

1. Solder is an alloy of tin and lead. Lead is used in making car batteries that means lead is metal C whereas tin is metal B.
Metals A and D are Zn and Fe because Zn is used to prevent Fe from corrosion.
2. In this process, cathode is attached to the negative terminal of the battery so it is negatively charged whereas anode is attached to the positive terminal of the battery so it has positive charge. During electrolysis, $\mathrm{Cu}^{2+}$ ion of $\mathrm{CuSO}_{4}$ used as an electrolyte reaches the cathode and get reduced by receiving electrons from the cathode terminal.

$$
\text { Cathode : } \quad \mathrm{Cu}^{2+}+2 \mathrm{e}^{-} \longrightarrow \begin{gathered}
\mathrm{Cu} \\
\text { Copper metal }
\end{gathered}
$$

Resulted copper metal is deposited on the cathode. The impure anode is oxidised to the $\mathrm{Cu}^{2+}$ ions by leasing electrons hence anode is dissolved into the electrolyte.

$$
\text { Anode }: \mathrm{Cu} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Cu}^{2+}+2 \mathrm{e}^{-}
$$

Therefore, the pure metal from the impure anode dissolves into the electrolyte that means it becomes thinner and thinner whereas an equivalent amount of pure metal from the electrolyte is deposited on the cathode that means it becomes thicker and thicker.

## 4. Carbon and its Compounds

A

Strong
Foundation
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-148.jpg?height=1737&width=1621&top_left_y=814&top_left_x=190)

## TETRAVALENCY IN CARBON

A carbon atom has a total of six electrons occupying the first two shells, i.e., the K-shell has two electrons and the L-shell has four electrons. This distribution indicates that in the outermost shell there are one completely filled ' $s$ ' orbital and two half-filled ' $p$ ' orbitals, showing carbon to be a divalent atom. But in reality, carbon displays tetravalency in the combined state. Therefore, a carbon atom has four valence electrons. It could gain four electrons to form C anion or lose four electrons to form C cation. Both these conditions require large amount of energy. Thus it has a very little tendency to form ionic compounds. To overcome this problem carbon undergoes bonding by sharing its valence electron with other carbon atoms or with atoms of other elements. This allows it to be covalently bonded to one, two, three or four carbon atoms or atoms of other elements or groups of atoms (molecule).

This characteristic of carbon atom by virtue of which it form four covalent bonds is generally referred to as tetracovalency of carbon.

Not just carbon, but many other elements form molecules by sharing valence electrons. The shared electrons 'belong' to the outer shells of both the atoms and lead to both atoms attaining the noble gas configuration.

## CONNECTING TOPIC

To understand the bonding of carbon with other atoms in its compound we should understand the concept of covalent bonding.

## COVALENT BOND

According to Lewis, covalent bond is formed by the sharing of same number of electron between two atoms to complete their octet".

If one electron is shared between two atoms then it forms single bond. Similarly double \& triple bonds are formed by the sharing of two and three electrons respectively. For example:-
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-149.jpg?height=376&width=883&top_left_y=1452&top_left_x=648)

Only the electrons of valence shell take part in sharing. The electrons of valence shell which do not take part in chemical bonding are called lone pair or non bonding electrons. For example, in oxygen six valence electrons of oxygen atom (2, 6) can take part in bonding. Out of six electrons two electrons, are shared by one oxygen atom thus after bond formation each oxygen possesses four non bonding electrons (or two lone pairs).
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-149.jpg?height=104&width=570&top_left_y=2014&top_left_x=831)

## Octet Rule

The octet rule requires all atoms in a molecule to have 8 valence electrons; either by sharing, losing or gaining electrons; to become stable. For covalent bonds, atoms tend to share their electrons with each other to satisfy the octet rule. It requires 8 electrons because that is the amount of electrons needed to fill its outer most orbital (electron configuration); also known as a noble gas configuration. Each atom wants to become as stable as the noble gases that have their outer valence shell filled. Although it is important to remember the "magic number", 8, yet there are many octet rule exceptions.

Example: There are many stable molecules in which central atom have more than $8 \mathrm{e}^{-}$in its valence shell e.g. $\mathrm{IF}_{7}$ ( I has $14 \mathrm{e}^{-}$in valence shell), $\mathrm{PCl}_{5}$ ( P has $10 \mathrm{e}^{-}$in valence shell), $\mathrm{SF}_{6}$ ( S has $12 \mathrm{e}^{-}$in valence shell) etc.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-150.jpg?height=241&width=255&top_left_y=365&top_left_x=529)

## General properties of covalent molecules

(a) Solubility : These compounds are usually insoluble or less soluble in water and in other polar solvents. Polar covalent molecules are more soluble in water in comparison to that of non polar covalent molecules.
(b) Low melting point and boiling point: The intermolecular forces of attractions between the molecules of covalent compounds are generally weak. As a result, lesser amount of energy is needed to overcome these intermolecular forces. Thus, these compounds usually have low melting point and boiling point than ionic compounds.
(c) Conducting nature: These are generally poor conductors of electricity in the fused or dissolved state due to unavailability of ions. or gases at room temperature. However, a few covalent compounds also exist in the solid state e.g. glucose, urea, thiourea \& sugar etc.

## Electron Dot (Lewis Dot) Structures

Lewis dot structures for many species can be written according to the following steps :
(i) Pick up the central atom of the species in question.
(ii) Recall the number of electrons in the outermost orbit (valence electrons) in each atom of the species.
(iii) Recall the valency of the central atom and other atoms too.
(iv) Write down the structural formula of the species on the basis of point no. (ii) and (iii).
(v) Supply the required number of electrons to each bond (1 pair of electrons for a single bond, 2 pairs for a double bond and 3 pairs for a triple bond).
(vi) Verify that each atom of the species has inert gas configuration, i.e., either 2 (in case of hydrogen) or 8 electrons in the outermost orbit. Let us apply these points on few examples.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-150.jpg?height=245&width=257&top_left_y=365&top_left_x=1306)
<img class="imgSvg" id = "mpcazmdac5o2fh0w63b" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,"/>
<img class="imgSvg" id = "mpcazmdcavlvjfvnwo" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,"/>
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-150.jpg?height=195&width=341&top_left_y=1809&top_left_x=258)

For main group elements, the number of valence electrons which an atom contributes in a Lewis structure is the same as its group number in the periodic table.

## 1. Lewis dot structure of $\mathbf{H C N}$ :

(i) In the given compound central atom is carbon.
(ii) The valence electron in $\mathrm{H}, \mathrm{C}$ and N are 1, 4 and 3 respectively.
(iii) The valencies of $\mathrm{H}, \mathrm{C}$ and N are 1, 4 and 3 respectively.
(iv) Thus the structural formula of HCN can be written as : $\mathrm{H}-\mathrm{C} \equiv \mathrm{N}$
(v) Now supply the required number of electrons for each bond, the above formula can be represented as below,

$$
\mathrm{H} \times \mathrm{C}: \underset{\times}{\times} \mathrm{N}
$$

(vi) Note that in the above formula, N does not have complete octet and moreover its 5 valence electrons are not accounted for. Hence it must have a lone pair of electrons. Thus the complete Lewis dot structure for HCN can be represented as below :

$$
\mathrm{H} \times \mathrm{C}: \underset{\times}{\times} \mathrm{N} \underset{\times}{\times}
$$

2. Lewis dot structures of few other compounds :

|  | Molecule | Structural formula | Lewis dot formula |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |

| (ii) | Ozone ( $\mathrm{O}_{3}$ ) | $\mathrm{O}=\mathrm{O} \rightarrow \mathrm{O}$ | :Ö :: Ö: Ö: |

| (iv) | Carbon dioxide ( $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ ) | $\mathrm{O}=\mathrm{C}=\mathrm{O}$ | $\stackrel{\times \times}{\times} \stackrel{\times}{\times}: \mathrm{C}: \underset{\times}{\times} \stackrel{\times}{\mathrm{O}} \times \frac{\times}{\times}$ |
| (v) | Sulphur dioxide ( $\mathrm{SO}_{2}$ ) | $\mathrm{O}=\mathrm{S} \rightarrow \mathrm{O}$ | ${ }_{\times}^{\times \times \mathrm{O}} \times: \quad \ddot{\mathrm{S}}: \underset{\times \times}{\mathrm{O}_{\times}^{\times} \times}$ |
| (vi) | Sulphur trioxide ( $\mathrm{SO}_{3}$ ) | <smiles>O=S(=O)=O</smiles> | ${ }_{\times}^{\times \times} \mathrm{O} \times: \mathrm{S}: \underset{\times \times}{\times}{ }_{\times}^{\times} \underset{\times \times}{\mathrm{O}_{\times}}$ |

Methane is the simplest hydrocarbon and is known as marsh gas.

## Methane Molecule $\left(\mathrm{CH}_{4}\right)$

Carbon atom has four electrons in its outermost shell. Thus, it requires four more electrons to acquire a stable noble gas configuration. Each of the hydrogen atom has only one electron in its outermost shell and requires one more electron to complete its outermost shell (to acquire He configuration). This is done as follows

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-151.jpg?height=248&width=1364&top_left_y=1685&top_left_x=322)
Methane is the simplest hydrocarbon and is known as marsh gas.

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-151.jpg?height=141&width=117&top_left_y=1991&top_left_x=477)
Methane is the simplest hydrocarbon and is known as marsh gas.

## Carbon Dioxide Molecule (CO2)

Carbon atom has four electrons in its outermost shell and oxygen atom has six electrons in its outermost shell.
Thus, each carbon atom requires four, and each oxygen atom requires two more electrons to acquire noble gas configurations. To achieve this, two oxygen atoms form a covalent double bond with carbon as follows

$$
\underset{(2,6)}{: \ddot{\mathrm{O}}:}+\underset{(2,4)}{\times \underset{\times}{\times} \mathrm{C}_{\times}^{\times}}+\underset{(2,6)}{: \ddot{\mathrm{O}}:} \longrightarrow \underset{(2,8)}{: \ddot{\mathrm{O}}: \times \underset{(2,8)}{\mathrm{C}} \times: \underset{(2,8)}{\ddot{\mathrm{O}}:}: \text { or } \quad \begin{array}{c}
\mathrm{O}=\mathrm{C}=\mathrm{O} \\
\text { Carbondioxide molecule }
\end{array}}
$$

## Acetylene Molecule ( $\mathbf{C}_{\mathbf{2}} \mathbf{H}_{\mathbf{2}}$ )

Carbon atom has four electrons in its outermost shell and hydrogen atoms have only one electron in its outermost shell. Carbon share one of its electrons with each hydrogen to form single bonds. Each carbon then requires three more electrons to acquire a stable configuration of the nearest noble gas (neon). This is done by mutual sharing of three pairs of electrons between the two carbon atoms to form a triple bond as shown below

$$
\underset{(1)}{\mathrm{H} \cdot}+\underset{(2,6)}{2 \times} \mathrm{C}_{\times}^{\times}+\mathrm{H}^{\cdot} \longrightarrow \underset{(1)}{\mathrm{H} \dot{\times}} \underset{(2)(2,8)(2,8)(2)}{\times \mathrm{C}_{\times}^{\times} \times} \mathrm{C}{ }^{\times} \cdot \mathrm{H} \longrightarrow \mathrm{H}: \mathrm{C} \equiv \mathrm{C}: \mathrm{H} \quad \text { or } \quad \mathrm{CH} \equiv \mathrm{CH}
$$

- Explain the formation of carbon carbon double bond in ethylene molecule.


## SOLUTION

Carbon atom has four electrons in its outermost shell and hydrogen atoms have only one electron in its outermost shell. Two carbon atoms share four electrons (two each atom) with four electrons of four hydrogen atoms. Each carbon then requires two more electrons to acquire a stable configuration which is done by mutual sharing of two pairs of electrons between two carbon atoms to form a double bond as shown below.

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-152.jpg?height=93&width=732&top_left_y=1171&top_left_x=635)
Ethylene molecule

## ALLOTROPES OF CARBON

Carbon (Latin carbo means charcoal) is an element of prehistoric discovery. It is very widely distributed in nature. It is found in abundance in the sun, stars, comets, and atmospheres of most planets.

Carbon can either be in structural crystalline forms or may occur in a structure-less amorphous form. Some elements like carbon, sulphur, tin, oxygen, etc. are found in more than one structural forms. Therefore, some elements can have several different structural forms while in the same physical state. These forms are called allotropes and such phenomenon is called allotropy. The various allotropic forms of carbon can be broadly classified into two classes : wurtzite are polymorphs of ZnS.
(a) Crystalline form : Pure carbon is found as graphite, diamond, Buckministerfullerene

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-152.jpg?height=239&width=257&top_left_y=2151&top_left_x=415)
Structure of Diamond

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-152.jpg?height=332&width=173&top_left_y=2060&top_left_x=858)
Structure of Graphite

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-152.jpg?height=266&width=262&top_left_y=2122&top_left_x=1244)
Structure of Buckministerfullerene

- There is another form of crystalline carbon known as "white" carbon, but not much is known about it. Conditions such as temperature and pressure determine which allotrope of carbon occurs.
- A diamond is hard, clear, and shiny substance. It is formed under very high temperatures and pressure. Diamond has ability to reflect and refract light which makes it an important material for making jewellary.
Graphite is black, with a slippery, greasy feel. Graphite is formed on heating coke or coal.
- Though non-metallic, graphite possesses a metallic lustre. It is insoluble in ordinary solvents. Graphite is used in making electrodes, in electric furnances and electric ores. It is also used in lead pencils.


## (b) Amorphous form : Coal, coke, charcoal (or wood charcoal), animal charcoal (or bone black), lamp black, carbon black,

gas carbon and petroleum coke.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-153.jpg?height=149&width=158&top_left_y=959&top_left_x=443)

## Comparison between Diamond and Graphite

|  | Diamond | Graphite |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| i. | Diamond is the hardest material known. | Graphite is one of the softest materials known. |
| ii. | Diamond is the ultimate abrasive. | Graphite is a very good lubricant. |
| iii. | Diamond is an excellent electrical insulator. | Graphite is a conductor of electricity. |
| iv. | Diamond is the best known naturally occurring thermal conductor. | Some forms of graphite are used for thermal insulation. (i.e. firebreaks and heat shields) |
| v. | Diamond is highly transparent. | Graphite is opaque. |
| vi. | Diamond crystallizes in the cubic system. | Graphite crystallizes in the hexagonal system. |

These differences in the properties of diamond and graphite are due to the difference in their structures.
In diamond, each C atom is linked to its neighbours by four single covalent bonds. This leads to a three-dimensional network of covalent bonds. In graphite, the carbon atoms are arranged in flat parallel layers as regular hexagons. Each carbon in these layers is bonded to three others by covalent bonds. Graphite thus acquires some double bond character.
Each layer is bonded to adjacent layers by weak van der Waal's forces. Moreover, one electron is free at each carbon atom. This allows each layer to slide over the other easily. Due to this type of structure graphite is soft and slippery, and can act as a lubricant. Graphite is also a good conductor of electricity due to mobile electrons in it.

Graphite is also used as a industrial lubricant because it possesses high melting point. Thus no change in structure occur even if machineries are working at high temperature.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-153.jpg?height=143&width=351&top_left_y=2229&top_left_x=255)

Why graphite is used as lubricant?

## SOLUTION

As structure of graphite contains flat hexagonal parallel layers joined together by weak van der Waal's forces, thus, these layers can slide over one another easily making graphite a good lubricant.

Buckminister fullerenes contain 60 carbon atoms arranged in a round molecule resembling a soccer ball. $\mathrm{C}_{60}$ molecule has marvelously symmetrical structure. $\mathrm{C}_{60}$ molecule is a fused-ring of aromatic system containing 20 hexagons and 12 pentagons of C atoms. The five membered rings are connected to six membered rings while the six membered rings are fused to both five and six membered rings.
The structure bends around and closes to form a soccer ball shaped molecule. It is therefore, called buckyball also. Fullerenes were first prepared by the evaporation of graphite using a laser. These can be conveniently made by heating graphite in an electric arc in the presence of inert gas like helium or argon. Fullerene looks different from diamond and graphite. It is a yellow powdery substance which is soluble in organic solvents and form coloured solution. It turns pink on dissolution in non-polar solvents like toluene. It can be polymerised on exposure to U.V. radiations. Fullerenes are fascinating because they show unusual characteristics and applications like: They are wonderful lubricants because the balls can roll between the surfaces.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-154.jpg?height=150&width=350&top_left_y=762&top_left_x=187)

- How many hexagonal and pentagonal rings are present in C60 molecules?


## SOLUTION

Fullerene contains 20 hexagonal and 12 pentagonal rings which are fused together

## CATENATION IN CARBON

Carbon atom is unique amongst all the elements that are found in nature. It can form long chain molecules. The ability of carbon to form a long chain is called catenation. Carbon forms strong bond because of its small size which enables the nucleus to held on to the shared pairs of electrons strongly. The chains are formed because carbon atoms form tetravalent bonds with other carbon atoms. This structure can be repeated endlessly without disturbing the stability of the bonds and the compounds formed. The chains can form branches, and sub-branches. The carbon atoms also form rings. The rings themselves can have more rings attached to them. Protein molecules and amino acids are long chain carbon molecules.

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-154.jpg?height=380&width=1191&top_left_y=2133&top_left_x=406)
Figure : Variety of carbon chains formed when carbon atoms join together.

In addition, carbon atoms may be linked by single, double or triple bonds. Compounds of carbon, which has only single bonds between the carbon atoms are called saturated compounds. Compounds of carbon having double or triple bonds between their carbon atoms are called unsaturated compounds.

## Learn

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-155.jpg?height=86&width=108&top_left_y=497&top_left_x=1014)
More

- A carbon atom attached to only one other (or no other) carbon is called primary carbon (written as $1^{\circ}$ carbon).
- A carbon atom attached to two other carbon atoms is called secondary carbon (written as $2^{\circ}$ carbon).
- A carbon atom attached to three other carbon atoms is called tertiary carbon (written as $3^{\circ}$ carbon).
- A carbon atom attached to four other carbon atoms is called quarternary carbon (written as $4^{\circ}$ carbon).

Hydrogen atoms attached to $1^{\circ}, 2^{\circ}$ and $3^{\circ}$ carbon atoms are referred to as primary, secondary and tertiary hydrogen atoms respectively. The following example clearly differentiates the various carbon atoms:
<img class="imgSvg" id = "mpcazmdh3ba3oat62no" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,"/>

Time to
Check Your
Knowledge
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-155.jpg?height=157&width=158&top_left_y=1107&top_left_x=443)

What is so special about carbon so that one entire major branch of chemistry is devoted to its compound with hydrogen?

## SOLUTION

Carbon has a unique property of catenation because of which it is capable to form long straight or branched carbon chains and also rings of carbon atoms. Thus it forms large number of compounds with hydrogen called hydrocarbons. Chemistry of these hydrocarbons are studied under separate branch called organic chemistry.

## ILLUSTRATION : 2

In the following compound carbon atom with * mark is primary, secondary, tertiary or quaternary carbon atom?

$$
\mathrm{CH}_{3}-\mathrm{CH}_{2}-\mathrm{CH}-\mathrm{CH}_{3}
$$

## SOLUTION :

The carbon atom with * mark is tertiary carbon atom because it is attached to three other carbon atoms.

## COMPOUNDS OF CARBON

Compounds of carbon can broadly classified into two main categories, namely inorganic and organic compounds.
Inorganic compounds : These include compounds of carbon with metals and non-metals (other than hydrogen) such as oxygen, halogens etc. These do not have carbon-carbon bonds.
Organic compounds : Organic compounds are those chemical compounds in which one or more atoms of carbon are covalently linked to atoms of other elements, most commonly oxygen or nitrogen.

## Inorganic Compounds of Carbon

Carbon combines with a variety of other elements like oxygen, halogens, metals, etc., to form binary compounds respectively named as oxides, halides and carbides.

## Oxides of carbon :

(a) Carbon monoxide, CO
(i) Carbon monoxide is a colourless, tasteless gas with faint odour.
(ii) Carbon monoxide is extremely poisonous in nature. The poisonous nature of CO is due to the fact that it combines with haemoglobin (a red colouring matter of blood), oxygen carrier of blood, to form a stable compound carboxyhaemoglobin. As a result, oxygen transporation is disturbed and thus brain and body tissues do not get necessary oxygen and ultimately death occurs. This explains, why people sleeping in closed rooms with charcoal fire burning inside, dies.

A victim of carbon monoxide poisoning, if unconscious, should be brought in the open place and Know!
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-156.jpg?height=168&width=122&top_left_y=721&top_left_x=388)
given artificial respiration with carbogen (mixture of oxygen and 5-10\% $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ ).

## Uses :

(i) Carbon monoxide is used as a fuel in the form of water gas ( $\mathrm{CO}+\mathrm{H}_{2}$ ) and producer gas ( $\mathrm{CO}+\mathrm{N}_{2}$ ).
(ii) It is used in making metal carbonyls among which nickel tetracarbonyl is used in the extraction of nickel (Mond's process).
(iii) It is used in the manufacture of methanol, synthetic petrol and phosgene.
(iv) It is used as a reducing agent in metallurgy of iron and in the manufacture of hydrogen.
(v) Iron carbonyl is used in the manufacture of magnetic tapes for videos and tape recorders.
(b) Carbon dioxide, $\mathbf{C O}_{\mathbf{2}}$

It is present in air to the extent of $0.03 \%$ by volume. It is found in caves, mines, etc., and it evolves from volcanoes.
(i) Carbon dioxide can be easily liquefied under pressure (50-60 atmospheres) and also solidified. Solid carbon dioxide is technically known as dry ice because it evaporates (sublimes) without liquefying (it sublimes at 195 K ). It is used as a refrigerant (coolant) under the commercial name of drikold. When it is placed in an organic solvent like ether, it gives a constant temperature of 173 K .
(ii) Although $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ is not poisonous in nature, it does not support life.
(iii) It is neither combustible nor a supporter of combustion. But certain active metals like $\mathrm{Na}, \mathrm{K}$ and Mg continue burning in atmosphere of $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$. It is due to the fact that the active metals combine with $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ to form the oxides. The latter combine with $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ to form carbonates.

$$
\begin{aligned}
2 \mathrm{Mg}+\mathrm{CO}_{2} & \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{MgO}+\mathrm{C} \\
\mathrm{MgO}+\mathrm{CO}_{2} & \longrightarrow \mathrm{MgCO}_{3}
\end{aligned}
$$

(iv) Carbon dioxide is absorbed by plants in the presence of sunlight and chlorophyll (green colouring matter) to form glucose and higher carbohydrates. This process is known as photosynthesis.

$$
6 \mathrm{CO}_{2}+6 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \xrightarrow[\text { chlorophyll }]{\text { light }} \mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{12} \mathrm{O}_{6}+6 \mathrm{O}_{2}
$$

## Learn More

## Reaction of carbon with the halogens :

Graphite reacts with fluorine, $\mathrm{F}_{2}$, at high temperatures to make a mixture of carbon tetrafluoride, $\mathrm{CF}_{4}$, together with some $\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{~F}_{6}$ and $\mathrm{C}_{5} \mathrm{~F}_{12}$.

$$
\mathrm{C}(\mathrm{~s})+\text { excess } \mathrm{F}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \longrightarrow \mathrm{CF}_{4}(\mathrm{~g})+\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{~F}_{6}+\mathrm{C}_{5} \mathrm{~F}_{12}
$$

At room temperature, the reaction with fluorine is a complex reaction. The 'graphite fluoride', a non-stoichiometric species with formula $\mathrm{CF}_{\mathrm{x}}$ is formed.

## Reaction of carbon with the transition metals :

The usual method of preparing polycrystalline transition metal carbides on the research scale involves the direct reaction of metal or metal hydride powders with carbon.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathrm{M}+\mathrm{C} & \longrightarrow \mathrm{MC} \\
\mathrm{MH}+\mathrm{C} & \longrightarrow \mathrm{MC}+\mathrm{H}_{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

## Organic Compounds

## Origin and Definition of Organic Compounds :

According to the earlier definition, organic chemistry was defined as the chemistry of substances found in the living matter (Berzelius in 1808). Further, Berzelius in 1815 also suggested that the organic compounds could be prepared only by living organisms under the influence of a mysterious force known as the vital force (life-force). The vital force theory remain unchallanged until 1828, when Friedrich Wohler, a German chemist, reported the synthesis of a typical organic compound urea by heating an inorganic compound, ammonium cyanate.

$$
\underset{\substack{\text { Ammonium } \\ \text { chloride }}}{\mathrm{NH}_{4} \mathrm{Cl}}+\underset{\substack{\text { Potassium } \\ \text { cyanate }}}{\mathrm{KCNO}} \longrightarrow \underset{\substack{\text { Ammonium } \\ \text { cyanate }}}{\mathrm{NH}_{4} \mathrm{CNO}+\mathrm{KCl}}
$$

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-157.jpg?height=115&width=451&top_left_y=701&top_left_x=1192)

The synthesis of urea from inorganic compounds gave a blow to the vital force theory and thus the distinction between organic and inorganic compounds based on vital force soon started fading away. The vital force theory was completely discarded after the synthesis of acetic acid from C, H and O (Kolbe, 1845) and methane (Berthelet, 1856). Subsequently, a large number of naturally occurring organic compounds were synthesized in the laboratory and the vital force theory was decisively disproved.
However, all the organic compounds were found to contain carbon as one of the elements. Thus the emphasis for the definition of organic compounds was shifted from origin to composition. Accordingly, organic chemistry has now been defined as the chemistry of carbon compounds.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-157.jpg?height=204&width=337&top_left_y=1133&top_left_x=255)

On the basis of this definition, the study of compounds like $\mathrm{CO}, \mathrm{CO}_{2}, \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{CO}_{3}$, carbonates, cyanides, cyanates, carbides, etc., should be included in organic chemistry, but due to their greater resemblance with inorganic compounds these are studied under inorganic chemistry.

Further, since all the organic compounds could be considered as derivatives of hydrocarbons, a more appropriate definition for the organic chemistry is the study of hydrocarbons and their derivatives.

## HYDROCARBONS

Compounds containing only carbon and hydrogen are known as hydrocarbons. Petroleum and coal are the two important natural sources of hydrocarbons. Petroleum is a major source of aliphatic hydrocarbons while coal is a source of aromatic hydrocarbons.
On the basis of structure and properties, hydrocarbons can be classified broadly into two classes.

## 1. Open Chain or Acyclic Hydrocarbons

As the name represents, these hydrocarbons contain open chain of carbon atoms in their molecules. These are also called aliphatic hydrocarbons. These are further classified into three types depending on the nature of carbon-carbon bonds in their molecules namely alkanes, alkenes and alkynes.
(i) Alkanes : These contain carbon-carbon single bonds e.g.

$$
\begin{array}{cc}
\mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{C}-\mathrm{CH}_{3} & \mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{C}-\mathrm{CH}_{2}-\mathrm{CH}_{2}-\mathrm{CH}_{3} \\
\text { Ethane } & \text { Butane }
\end{array}
$$

General formula of alkane is $\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{n}} \mathrm{H}_{2 \mathrm{n}+2}$ and are commonly called paraffins.
(ii) Alkenes : These contain at least one carbon-carbon double bonds, e.g.

$$
\begin{array}{lc}
\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{C}=\mathrm{CH}_{2} & \mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{C}-\mathrm{CH}=\mathrm{CH}-\mathrm{CH}_{3} \\
\text { Ethene } & \text { Butene-2 }
\end{array}
$$

General formula of alkene is $\mathrm{C}_{n} \mathrm{H}_{2 n}$. They are commonly called olefins.
(iii) Alkynes : These contain at least one carbon-carbon triple bonds, e.g.

$$
\mathrm{HC} \equiv \mathrm{CH}
$$

Ethyne
<img class="imgSvg" id = "mpcazmdiwmugzgbtut9" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,"/>

Propyne
$\mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{C}-\mathrm{C} \equiv \mathrm{C}-\mathrm{CH}_{3}$
Butyne-2

General formula of alkyne is $\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{n}} \mathrm{H}_{2 \mathrm{n}-2}$. They are traditionally called acetylenes, although the name acetylene also refers specifically to $\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{2}$.

## 2. Closed Chain or Cyclic Hydrocarbons

As the name represents, these compounds contain closed chain (ring) of carbon atoms in their molecules. These are further divided into two types namely cycloalkanes and aromatic hydrocarbons or arenes.
(i) Cycloalkanes : Those saturated hydrocarbons in which carbon atoms are joined together to form ring are known as cycloalkanes. For example,

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-158.jpg?height=102&width=127&top_left_y=609&top_left_x=520)
Cyclopropane

<img class="imgSvg" id = "mpcazmdjig6jy8q8aga" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,"/>

Cyclopentane
<img class="imgSvg" id = "mpcazmdkcgme7cfv73g" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,"/>

Cyclohexane
(ii) Aromatic hydrocarbons or Arenes: Those hydrocarbons which contain at least one benzene ring in their molecules are called arenes. For example,
<img class="imgSvg" id = "mpcazme10zg46qpwdpc" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,"/>

Toluene
<img class="imgSvg" id = "mpcazme3rn17znpiyhh" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,"/>

Naphthalene

All the above types of hydrocarbons may also be grouped in a different manner into two types depending upon the basis of nature of carbon-carbon bonds in their molecules.
(i) Saturated hydrocarbons: These contain carbon-carbon single bonds in their molecules. Alkanes and cycloalkanes are grouped in this category.
(ii) Unsaturated hydrocarbons: These contains carbon-carbon multiple bond (double or triple) in their molecules. Alkenes, alkynes and arenes are grouped in this category.

## Classification of Hydrocarbons :

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-158.jpg?height=787&width=1520&top_left_y=1523&top_left_x=239)

## ISOMERISM IN HYDROCARBONS
