## FUNCTIONAL GROUPS

In organic chemistry, functional groups (or moieties) are specific groups of atoms within molecules that are responsible for the characteristic chemical reactions of those molecules. The same functional group will undergo the same or similar chemical reaction(s) regardless of the size of the molecule for example, - OH (alcohol), - CHO (aldehyde) etc.

## Important Functional Groups and the Corresponding Classes of Compound :

| Compound Name | Structure of Compound and Functional Group (red) | Example |  |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
|  |  | Formula | Name |
| Alkene | $\mathrm{C}=\mathrm{C}$ | $\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{4}$ | Ethene |
| Alkyne | $\mathrm{C} \equiv \mathrm{C}$ | $\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{2}$ | Ethyne |
| Alcohol | $\mathrm{R}-\ddot{\mathrm{O}}-\mathrm{H}$ | $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{OH}$ | Ethanol |
| Ether | $\mathrm{R}-\underset{0}{0}-\mathrm{R}^{\prime}$ | $\left(\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5}\right)_{2} \mathrm{O}$ | Diethyl ether |
| Aldehyde | ![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-159.jpg?height=100&width=173&top_left_y=1086&top_left_x=590) | $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CHO}$ | Ethanal |
| Ketone | ![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-159.jpg?height=100&width=178&top_left_y=1210&top_left_x=590) | $\mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{COCH}_{2} \mathrm{CH}_{3}$ | Methyl ethyl ketone |
| Carboxylic acid | <smiles></smiles> | $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOH}$ | Acetic acid |
| Ester | ![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-159.jpg?height=112&width=247&top_left_y=1479&top_left_x=590) | $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CO}_{2} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{CH}_{3}$ | Ethyl acetate |
| Amine | <smiles>[R]N[R]N([R])[R]N([R])[R]</smiles> | $\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{NH}_{2}$ | Ethylamine |
| Amide | <smiles>[R]N[14NH]C([R])=O</smiles> | $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CONH}_{2}$ | Acetamide |

## ILLUSTRATION : 3

Why mercapto (-SH) are also called thioalcohols?

## SOLUTION :

It is due to resemblance with the functional group of alcohols -OH . The only difference is the presence of sulphur atom (also known as thio) instead of oxygen atom that's why these are known as thio alcohol.

Check Your Knowledge

- What is the similarity in the following pair of organic compounds?


## HCHO and CH3CH2CHO.

## SOLUTION

Both of the above compounds are aldehydes as they contain - CHO functional group.

## HOMOLOGOUS SERIES

In chemistry, a homologous series is a series of organic compounds with a similar general formula, possessing similar chemical properties due to the presence of the same functional group, and shows a gradation in physical properties as a result of increase in molecular size and mass (see relative molecular mass). For example, ethane has a higher boiling point than methane since it has more van der Waal's forces(intermolecular forces) with neighbouring molecules. This is due to increase in the number of atoms making up the molecule.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-160.jpg?height=211&width=339&top_left_y=653&top_left_x=187)

Organic compounds in the same homologous series differ by a $\mathrm{CH}_{2}$ group. Alkanes (paraffins), alkenes (olefins), methoxyethane (ethers), and alkynes (acetylenes) form such series in which members differ in mass by 14 atomic mass units. For example, the alkane homologous series have methane $\left(\mathrm{CH}_{4}\right)$, ethane $\left(\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{6}\right)$, propane $\left(\mathrm{C}_{3} \mathrm{H}_{8}\right)$, butane $\left(\mathrm{C}_{4} \mathrm{H}_{10}\right)$, and pentane $\left(\mathrm{C}_{5} \mathrm{H}_{12}\right)$, each member differing from the previous one by a $\mathrm{CH}_{2}$ group (or 14 atomic mass units).
Similarly, alcohol homologous series starts with methanol ( $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{OH}$ ), ethanol ( $\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{OH}$ ), propylalcohol ( $\boldsymbol{C}_{3} \boldsymbol{H}_{7} \mathbf{O H}$ ) etc.

- A homologation reaction is any chemical process converting one member of a homologous series to the next.


## SOLUTION

In homologous series each member differ from the previous one by a $\mathrm{CH}_{2}$ group (or 14 atomic mass units).

## NOMENCLATURE OF CARBON COMPOUNDS

In the early stages of the development, the various organic compounds were named on the basis of either the source of origin of the compounds or on some specific property of the compounds. For example, methane gas is found to get released in marshy places. Hence it is called marsh gas. These names are called trivial or common names.

In order to give names for the huge number of organic compounds used all over the world, a more systematic method was required. The International Union of Pure and Applied Chemistry (IUPAC) developed a systematic approach to the nomenclature of organic compounds based on its structure and reflects the physical and chemical properties. This IUPAC system has three parts: root word, suffix and prefix.

## For detail explanation please refer chapter-8

## CHEMICAL PROPERTIES OF CARBON COMPOUNDS

## Combustion

Combustion usually occurs when a hydrocarbon reacts with oxygen to produce $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ and $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$.
In more general sense, combustion involves a reaction between any combustible materials and an oxidizer to form an oxidized product. Combustion is an exothermic reaction, so it release heat. General reaction is:
Hydrocarbon + Oxygen ⟶ Carbondioxide + Water

For examples:

$$
\begin{array}{ll}
\mathrm{CH}_{4}+2 \mathrm{O}_{2} \longrightarrow \mathrm{CO}_{2}+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} ; & \mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{6}+\frac{7}{2} \mathrm{O}_{2} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{CO}_{2}+3 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \\
\mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{12} \mathrm{O}_{6}+6 \mathrm{O}_{2} \longrightarrow 6 \mathrm{CO}_{2}+6 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} ; & \mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{OH}+3 \mathrm{O}_{2} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{CO}_{2}+3 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}
\end{array}
$$

- If compound undergoing combustion contains nitrogen (it burns to form $\mathrm{NO}_{2}$ ) or sulfur (it burns to form $\mathrm{SO}_{2}$ ).

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{NO}_{2}+\mathrm{O}_{2} \longrightarrow \mathrm{CO}_{2}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}+\mathrm{NO}_{2} \\
& \mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{SH}+\mathrm{O}_{2} \longrightarrow \mathrm{CO}_{2}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}+\mathrm{SO}_{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

- There are complexities with combustion as you get deeper into it. For example, a combustion with insufficient oxygen yields carbon monoxide rather than carbon dioxide.
- In a very limited supply of air, methane gives carbon black.

$$
\mathrm{CH}_{4}+\underset{\text { Air (very limited) }}{\mathrm{O}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})} \xrightarrow{\Delta} \underset{\text { Carbon black }}{\mathrm{C}}+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}
$$

Unsaturated hydrocarbons (alkenes, alkynes and arenes) also undergo combustion to form carbon dioxide and water. For example,

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{CH}_{2}=\mathrm{CH}_{2}+3 \mathrm{O}_{2} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{CO}_{2}+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}+1411 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1} \\
& \text { Ethene } \\
& 2 \mathrm{CH} \equiv \mathrm{CH}+5 \mathrm{O}_{2} \longrightarrow 4 \mathrm{CO}_{2}+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}+1300 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1} \\
& \text { Acetylene } \\
& 2 \mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{6}+15 \mathrm{O}_{2} \longrightarrow 12 \mathrm{CO}_{2}+6 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}+3300 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1} \\
& \text { Benzene }
\end{aligned}
$$

The combustion of acetylene is used industrially for welding and cutting of metals in which oxyacetylene flame having high temperature ( 3270 K ) is produced.

- Compressed Natural Gas (CNG) which is used these days in automobiles undergoes spontaneous or rapid combustion?


## SOLUTION

CNG (a mixture of methane, ethane, propane and butane) undergoes rapid combustion in automobile's internal combustion engine, it does not ignite by itself.

## Learn More

Saturated hydrocarbons will generally give a clean flame while unsaturated carbon compounds will give a yellow flame with lots of black smoke,limiting the supply of air results in incomplete combustion of even saturated hydrocarbons giving a sooty flame. The gas/kerosene stove used at home has inlets for air so that a sufficiently oxygen-rich mixture is burnt to give a clean blue flame. If you observe the bottom of cooking vessels getting blackened, it means that the air holes are blocked and fuel is getting wasted. Fuels such as coal and petroleum have some amount of nitrogen and sulphur in them. Their combustion results in the formation of oxides of sulphur and nitrogen which are major pollutants in the environment.

## ILLUSTRATION : 4

What are the products obtained on combustion of ethylene?

## SOLUTION :

Carbon dioxide and water vapour

$$
\underset{\text { Ethylene }}{\mathrm{CH}_{2}}=\mathrm{CH}_{2}+3 \mathrm{O}_{2} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{CO}_{2}+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}
$$

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-162.jpg?height=362&width=369&top_left_y=566&top_left_x=616)
(a)

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-162.jpg?height=371&width=371&top_left_y=559&top_left_x=1009)
(b)

Which set-up will produce smoke?

## SOLUTION

Set-up (b) will produce smoke as the oil taken in spatula contains unsaturated hydrocarbons which burn with luminous flame and produce smoke.

## Oxidation

Carbon undergoes oxidation by combining with oxygen at higher temperature to form oxides, viz., carbon monoxide (CO) and carbon dioxide ( $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ ). Carbon monoxide is formed, when incomplete combustion of carbon or carbon containing fuels takes place.

$$
\mathrm{C}+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{O}_{2} \longrightarrow \mathrm{CO}(\mathrm{~g})
$$

CO is present in automobile exhausts (when there is incomplete combustion), volcanic gases, chimney gases etc. Carbon dioxide is formed by the complete combustion of carbon, hydrocarbons, carbon monoxide etc.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathrm{C}+\mathrm{O}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) & \longrightarrow \mathrm{CO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+\text { Heat } \\
2 \mathrm{CO}+\mathrm{O}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) & \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{CO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+\text { Heat } \\
\mathrm{CH}_{4}+2 \mathrm{O}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) & \longrightarrow \mathrm{CO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}
\end{aligned}
$$

Carbon containing compounds undergo oxidation reactions when burnt in air or oxygen in the presence of catalyst to give useful products other then $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ and $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$. For example, when methane is mixed with oxygen and heated in presence of molybdenum oxide, it gets oxidized to methanal or formaldehyde.

$$
2 \mathrm{CH}_{4}+\mathrm{O}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \frac{350-500^{\circ} \mathrm{C}}{\text { Molybdenum oxide }} \mathrm{HCHO}+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}
$$

Oxidation of carbon compounds is used for producing other carbon compounds like alcohols, carboxylic acids, ethers etc. Methanol, an industrial alcohol, for instance, is prepared by the oxidation of methane.

$$
2 \mathrm{CH}_{4}+\mathrm{O}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \xrightarrow[\text { Copper tube }]{200^{\circ} \mathrm{C}} 2 \mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{OH}(\mathrm{~g})
$$

Acetic acid is manufactured by the oxidation of fermented liquors ( $10-15 \%$ alcohol) in air along with the presence of mycoderma aceti. A 3-7\% solution of acetic acid is obtained and it is called vinegar.

$$
\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{OH}+\mathrm{O}_{2} \longrightarrow \mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOH}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}
$$

## Addition Reaction

Reactions in which an atoms or group of atoms are added to a molecule (i.e., there is simply a net gain of the reagent atoms in product molecule) are called addition reactions. The important examples of addition reactions are summarised below.
(a) Addition of hydrogen (Catalytic hydrogenation, hydrogenation in presence of catalyst): Hydrogen adds to unsaturated hydrocarbons (alkenes, and alkynes) in presence of metallic catalyst, such as finely divided platinum, palladium or nickel and form the corresponding alkane. For example,

$$
\underset{\text { Ethene }}{\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{C}=\mathrm{CH}_{2}}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \frac{\mathrm{Ni}}{473-573 \mathrm{~K}} \underset{\text { Ethane }}{\mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{C}-\mathrm{CH}_{3}}
$$

This reaction is used industrially in preparation of vegetable ghee which contains mainly saturate fatty acids from vegetable oils which mainly contains unsaturated fatty acids.
(b) Addition of halogens : When an alkene is treated at room temperature with chlorine or bromine in an inert solvent (usually chloroform or carbon tetrachloride), halogen adds rapidly to the double bond of the alkene and produces a vicinal dihalide.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-163.jpg?height=122&width=602&top_left_y=776&top_left_x=762)

Vicinal dibromide

Reaction of alkenes with $\mathrm{Br}_{2}$ with fluorine is explosive whereas iodine does not react under normal conditions. This reaction can be used as a test for unsaturation of alkenes because the brown colour of bromine will get decolourised by alkenes.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-163.jpg?height=147&width=570&top_left_y=1117&top_left_x=922)

This reaction is also given by alkynes.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-163.jpg?height=149&width=424&top_left_y=1366&top_left_x=260)

In your school lab, take two beakers containing bromine solution and label them A and B. Then add vanaspati ghee in beaker A and vegetable oil in B respectively. In beaker A , color of bromine solution remains same but in beaker B color of solution gets faded up or discharged. What is the reason for this observation? Consult your friends and teacher in case of any problem.
(c) Addition of hydrogen halides (hydrohalogenation) : On addition of hydrogen halide to an alkene, it forms an alkyl halides, e.g.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-163.jpg?height=58&width=490&top_left_y=1763&top_left_x=829)

If the alkene is unsymmetrical, then the negative part of attacking molecule joins with the carbon atom which carries lesser number of hydrogen atoms as per Markovnikov's rule.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-163.jpg?height=156&width=681&top_left_y=1912&top_left_x=724)

In the presence of organic peroxides $(\mathrm{R}-\mathrm{O}-\mathrm{O}-\mathrm{R})$ e.g. benzoyl peroxide the reaction takes place opposite to the Markovnikov's rule. This is known as an anti Markovnikov's rule or Kharasch effect,

$$
\underset{\text { Propene }}{\mathrm{CH}_{3}-\mathrm{CH}=\mathrm{CH}_{2}}+\mathrm{HBr} \xrightarrow{(\mathrm{R}-\mathrm{O}-\mathrm{O}-\mathrm{R})} \mathrm{CH}_{3}-\mathrm{CH}_{2}-\mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{Br}
$$

Only HBr shows this peroxide effect, but not HF, HCl and HI, where addition occur according to Markovnikov's rule even in presence of peroxides.
(d) Addition of hypohalous acids : Alkenes react with hypochlorous and hypobromous acids readily to form the corresponding halohydrin.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-163.jpg?height=140&width=597&top_left_y=2426&top_left_x=767)

Complete the following reaction.

$$

$$

## SOLUTION :

Addition of HOBr occurs according to Markovnikov's rule. Thus reaction will be
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-164.jpg?height=163&width=750&top_left_y=676&top_left_x=623)

## CONNECTING TOPIC

When the reagent has two different atoms, e.g. but addition of unsymmetrical reagent to unsymmetrical alkene (when the two doubly bond carbon atoms have different number of H atoms, e.g. $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CH}=\mathrm{CH}_{2}$ ) may form two different products.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-164.jpg?height=136&width=866&top_left_y=1082&top_left_x=603)

But in practice, one of the products (here 2-chloropropane formed in major amount, while the other here 1-chloropropane is formed in minor amount.

Additions in such reactions, is governed by Markovnikov's rule according to which negative part of the reagent (e.g. Cl in $\mathrm{H}-\mathrm{Cl}, \mathrm{HSO}_{4}$ in $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}, \mathrm{Br}$ in $\mathrm{H}-\mathrm{Br}, \mathrm{OH}$ in $\mathrm{H}-\mathrm{OH}$, etc ) adds on that carbon atom of the alkene which has lesser number of carbon atoms. Thus,
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-164.jpg?height=140&width=891&top_left_y=1455&top_left_x=587)
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-164.jpg?height=157&width=351&top_left_y=1589&top_left_x=246)

Write the product of following reaction on the basis of Markownikov's addition.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-164.jpg?height=104&width=464&top_left_y=1777&top_left_x=804)

## SOLUTION

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-164.jpg?height=164&width=736&top_left_y=1982&top_left_x=667)

## Substitution Reaction

The reactions in which an atom or group of atoms in a molecule is replaced or substituted by different atoms or group of atoms are called substitution reaction. For example,

$$
\begin{gathered}
\left.\mathrm{CH}_{4}+\mathrm{Cl}_{2} \longrightarrow \mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{Cl}+\mathrm{HCl} \text { (H replaced by } \mathrm{Cl}\right) \\
\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{I}+\mathrm{KOH}(\mathrm{aq}) \longrightarrow \mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{OH}(\mathrm{I} \text { replaced by } \mathrm{OH})
\end{gathered}
$$

## CONNECTING TOPIC

## Important Substitution Reactions

In substitution reactions the hydrogen of the alkane molecule is replaced by another atom or a group of atoms (like alkyl) resulting in the formation of the derivatives of that hydrocarbon. Substitution by halogen atom is generally called halogenation. This type of substitution results in chlorination, bromination or iodination.
Like methane, ethane also forms a series of substitution products in the presence of excess chlorine and sunlight.

$$
\underset{\text { Ethane }}{\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{6}+\mathrm{Cl}_{2}} \xrightarrow{\text { uv light }} \underset{\text { Chloroethane }}{\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{Cl}}+\mathrm{HCl}
$$

(a) Bromination : Bromination of alkanes is similar to chlorination but not so vigorous.
(b) Iodination : Iodination of alkanes is reversible and slow.

$$
\mathrm{CH}_{4}+\mathrm{I}_{2} \stackrel{\Delta}{\rightleftharpoons} \mathrm{CH}_{3}-\mathrm{I}+\mathrm{HI}
$$

Iodination may be carried out in the presence of an oxidising agent such as $\mathrm{HIO}_{3}, \mathrm{HNO}_{3}, \mathrm{HgO}$, etc. which destroys the HI as it is formed and so drives the reaction to the right.

$$
5 \mathrm{HI}+\mathrm{HIO}_{3} \longrightarrow 3 \mathrm{I}_{2}+3 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}
$$

(c) Nitration : When a mixture of alkane and vapours of fuming nitric acid is heated between $150^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and $475^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, a complex mixture of nitroalkanes is obtained.

$$
\mathrm{R}-[\mathrm{H}+\mathrm{HO}]-\mathrm{NO}_{2} \xrightarrow{\text { high temp. }} \mathrm{R}-\mathrm{NO}_{2}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}
$$

(d) Sulphonation : In this reaction, hydrogen atom of the $\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{H}$ bond is replaced by $-\mathrm{SO}_{3} \mathrm{H}$ group.

$$
\mathrm{R} \underset{\mathrm{~L}--+---\rfloor}{\mathrm{H}+\mathrm{HO}}+\mathrm{SO}_{3} \mathrm{H} \text { (fuming) } \longrightarrow \quad \mathrm{R}-\underset{\text { Alkane sulphonic acid }}{\mathrm{SO}_{3} \mathrm{H}}+\underset{2}{\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}}
$$

Fuming $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}=$ Mixture of $\mathrm{SO}_{3}+$ Conc. $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}=\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{~S}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{7}$ (Oleum)

## ETHYL ALCOHOL (ETHANOL)

## Preparation of ethanol by fermentation of sugar

Fermentation is a process whereby an organic compound is broken into smaller molecules by enzymes such as yeast. Ethanol is prepared by fermentation of molasses. Molasses, a by product of sugar industry, has traces of sugar. The fermentation is initiated by yeast, which secretes two enzymes called invertase and zymase. These enzymes act as catalyst for converting sugar into ethanol. The reaction is exothermic and carbon dioxide is released in the process.

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \underset{\text { Sucrose }}{\mathrm{C}_{12} \mathrm{H}_{22} \mathrm{O}_{11}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}} \xrightarrow{\text { Invertase }} \underset{\text { Glucose }}{\mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{12} \mathrm{O}_{6}}+\underset{\text { Fructose }}{\mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{12} \mathrm{O}_{6}} \\
& \underset{\text { Glucose or fructose }}{\mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{12} \mathrm{O}_{6}} \xrightarrow{\text { Zymase }} \underset{\text { Ethanol }}{2 \mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{OH}+2 \mathrm{CO}_{2}}
\end{aligned}
$$

- Presence of air will tend to oxidise the mixture whose resultant is vinegar (acetic acid or ethanoic acid) $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOH}$. This is undesirable.
- Ethanol can also be made by fermentation of starchy materials such as barley, potatoes and maize. The starch is first converted to sugar by fermentation and then to ethanol.


## Ethanol preparation by hydration of ethene

An addition of a water molecule will convert ethene into ethanol $\left(\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{OH}\right)$.

$$
\underset{\text { Ethene }}{\mathrm{CH}_{2}}=\mathrm{CH}_{2}+\underset{\text { Water }}{\mathrm{H}-\mathrm{OH}} \frac{\mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{PO}_{4}}{300^{\circ} \mathrm{C}, 60 \mathrm{~atm}} \underset{\substack{\text { Ethanol } \\ \text { (Ethyl alcohol) }}}{\mathrm{CH}_{3}-\mathrm{CH}_{2}-\mathrm{OH}}
$$

Addition of water to a molecule is known as hydration.

## ILLUSTRATION : $\mathbf{6}$

Preparation of ethyl alcohol from ethene, is which type of reaction?

## SOLUTION :

It is an example of addition reaction in which addition of water molecule to ethene takes place.

## Physical Properties :

Ethanol is a versatile solvent, miscible in all proportions with water and many organic solvents, including acetic acid, acetone, benzene, carbon tetrachloride, chloroform. Solubility of higher alcohols ( $\mathrm{C}_{5}$ or more) decreases rapidly as the number of carbons increases. The polar nature of the hydroxyl group causes ethanol to dissolve in many ionic compounds.

## Reactions of Ethanol

(a) Reaction with HX :

Ethanol reacts with hydrogen halides to produce ethyl halides such as ethyl chloride and ethyl bromide:

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{OH}+\mathrm{HCl} \xrightarrow{\text { anh. } \mathrm{ZnCl}_{2}} \mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{Cl}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \\
& \mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{OH}+\mathrm{HBr} \xrightarrow{\text { anh. } \mathrm{ZnBr}_{2}} \mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{Br}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}
\end{aligned}
$$

Hydrogen chloride in the presence of zinc chloride is known as Lucas reagent.

Ethyl halides can also be produced by reacting ethanol with more specialized halogenating agents, such as thionyl chloride for preparing ethyl chloride, or phosphorus tribromide for preparing ethyl bromide.
(b) Ester formation :

Under acid-catalyzed conditions, ethanol reacts with carboxylic acids to produce ethyl esters and water:

$$
\mathrm{RCOOH}+\mathrm{HOCH}_{2} \mathrm{CH}_{3} \xrightarrow{\mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{O}^{+}} \mathrm{RCOOCH}_{2} \mathrm{CH}_{3}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}
$$

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-166.jpg?height=193&width=323&top_left_y=1594&top_left_x=187)

For this reaction to produce useful yields it is necessary to remove water from the reaction mixture as it is formed.

Ethanol can also form esters with inorganic acids. Diethyl sulfate and triethyl phosphate, prepared by reacting ethanol with sulfuric and phosphoric acid respectively, are both useful ethylating agents in organic synthesis. Ethyl nitrite, prepared from the reaction of ethanol with sodium nitrite and sulfuric acid, was formerly a widely-used diuretic.

Time to
Check Your Knowledge

- How can you prepare ethyl propanoate from ethanol?


## SOLUTION

By reaction of ethanol with propanoic acid in acidic media.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-166.jpg?height=145&width=1182&top_left_y=2247&top_left_x=404)
(c) Dehydration :

$$
\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{OH} \xrightarrow[453 \mathrm{~K}]{\text { conc. } \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}} \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{C}=\mathrm{CH}_{2}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}
$$

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-167.jpg?height=209&width=329&top_left_y=367&top_left_x=251)

Dehydration of alcohols to ethers or alkenes can also be brought about by passing the vapour of alcohols over heated alumina catalyst under different conditions
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-167.jpg?height=195&width=784&top_left_y=484&top_left_x=813)
(d) Oxidation :

Ethanol is oxidized first to acetaldehyde (also known as ethanal, and further to acetic acid. This oxidation is brought about by common oxidising agent like alkaline $\mathrm{KMnO}_{4}$, acidified $\mathrm{K}_{2} \mathrm{Cr}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{7}$, chromic acid etc.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-167.jpg?height=77&width=883&top_left_y=817&top_left_x=625)
(e) Reaction with chlorine :

When exposed to chlorine, ethanol forms chloral.
(f) Combustion
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-167.jpg?height=88&width=576&top_left_y=1011&top_left_x=772)

Combustion of ethanol forms carbon dioxide and water:

$$
\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{OH}+3 \mathrm{O}_{2} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{CO}_{2}+3 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}
$$

## Learn

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-167.jpg?height=81&width=106&top_left_y=1233&top_left_x=1016)

## More

## How Do Alcohols Affect Living Beings?

Superficially, ethanol evokes a distinctive heat-like sensation in the mouth and a stinging sensation on the skin. In the body it is metabolized to other substances, affecting the central nervous system in particular. The effect varies between individuals, and can be worse when applied in addition to certain drugs. When applied to open wounds (as for disinfection) it produces a strong stinging sensation. Pure or highly concentrated ethanol may permanently damage living tissue on contact. When large quantities of ethanol are consumed, it tends to slow metabolic processes and depresses the central nervous system. This results in lack of coordination, mental confusion, drowsiness, lowering of the normal inhibitions, and finally stupour.
The individual ethanol consuming may feel relaxed but does not realise that his sense of judgement, sense of timing, and muscular coordination have been seriously impaired. Ethanol within the human body is converted into acetaldehyde by alcohol dehydrogenase and then into acetic acid by acetaldehyde dehydrogenase. The product of the first step of this breakdown, acetaldehyde, is more toxic than ethanol. It has been shown to increase the risk of developing cirrhosis of the liver, multiple forms of cancer, and alcoholism. Death from ethyl alcohol consumption is possible when blood alcohol level reaches $0.4 \%$. A blood alcohol level of $0.5 \%$ or more is commonly fatal.

## Uses of Ethanol

(a) Ethanol is used in manufacturing of paints, dyes, varnishes etc.
(b) Ethanol is used in medicines especially for disinfecting area on the skin before giving an injection. It is used for sterilization of syringes in hospitals.
(c) Ethanol is used for preparation of compounds such as chloroform and ether.
(d) Ethanol is used for making thermometer that is used for measuring low temperatures.
(e) Ethanol is used in spirit lamps.
(f) Ethanol blended fuel called rectified spirit is used as a substitute for fuels in vehicles that are environmental pollutant. They give off low emissions of carbon monoxide gas that is harmful to the environment. Ethanol is the main component of alcoholic beverages such as rum, whisky and beer.
(g) Ethanol is used as an organic solvent.
(h) Ethanol is used as an antifreeze mixture for radiators in cars. Ethanol mixed with water freezes at a lower temperature than water. Such an antifreeze mixture is used for radiators in cold countries.

Make a project report on the use of ethyl alcohol as a fuel. Mention which type of ethyl alcohol blended fuels are currently or in near by future sold in market. Also mention the composition of these fuels. Make into account the merits and demerits of using ethyl alcohol as fuel. ## ACETIC ACID (ETHANOIC ACID)

Acetic acid, also known as ethanoic acid, is an organic chemical compound best recognized for giving vinegar its sour taste and pungent smell. Its chemical formula is represented as $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOH}$. The trivial name acetic acid is commonly used. Glacial acetic acid is a trivial name for water-free acetic acid, the name comes from the ice-like crystals formed slightly below room temperature at $16.7^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ (about $62^{\circ} \mathrm{F}$ ).

It is a weak acid because at standard temperature and pressure the dissociated acid exists in
Did You Know! equilibrium with the undissociated form in aqueous solutions, in contrast to strong acids, which are fully dissociated.

## Physical Properties

Pure, acetic acid (glacial acetic acid) is a colourless liquid that attracts water from the environment (hygroscopy), and freezes below $16.7^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ ( $62{ }^{\circ} \mathrm{F}$ ) to a colourless crystalline solid. Acetic acid is the second simplest carboxylic acids (first being formic acid, HCOOH ). Acetic acid is corrosive, and its vapour causes irritation to the eyes, a dry and burning nose, sore throat and congestion to lungs.

## Chemical Properties

Carboxylic acids $(-\mathrm{COOH})$ ionises in aqueous solution to give H , hence these are acidic in nature. Acetic acid is a weak, monoprotic acid. Acetic acid is corrosive to metals including iron, magnesium, and zinc, forming hydrogen gas and metal salts called acetates.

## Reactions of Acetic Acid (Ethanoic Acid) :

(a) Esterification reaction : Esterification is the reaction between an acid and an alcohol producing ester( sweet-smelling substances).

$$
\underset{\text { Alkanol }}{\mathrm{R}-\mathrm{OH}}+\underset{\text { Alkanoic acid }}{\mathrm{HOOC}-\mathrm{R}^{\prime}} \rightleftharpoons \underset{\text { Ester }}{\mathrm{R}-\mathrm{OOC}-\mathrm{R}^{\prime}}+\underset{\text { Water }}{\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}}
$$

Esters are used in making perfumes and as flavouring agents.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-168.jpg?height=200&width=325&top_left_y=1886&top_left_x=185)

Esters react in the presence of an acid or a base to give back the alcohol and carboxylic acid. This reaction is known as ester hydrolysis or saponification (hydrolysis in presence of base) because it is used in the preparation of soap.
(b) Reaction with a base: Like mineral acids, ethanoic acid reacts with a base such as sodium hydroxide to give a salt (sodium ethanoate or commonly called sodium acetate) and water:

$$
\mathrm{NaOH}+\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOH} \longrightarrow \mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COONa}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}
$$

(c) Reaction with carbonates and hydrogencarbonates: Ethanoic acid reacts with carbonates and hydrogencarbonates to give rise to a salt, carbon dioxide and water. The salt produced is commonly called sodium acetate.

$$
\begin{aligned}
2 \mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOH}+\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{CO}_{3} & \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COONa}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}+\mathrm{CO}_{2} \\
\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOH}+\mathrm{NaHCO}_{3} & \longrightarrow \mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COONa}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}+\mathrm{CO}_{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-169.jpg?height=151&width=346&top_left_y=370&top_left_x=255)

- What are the products obtained on reaction of acetic acid with magnesium carbonate?


## SOLUTION

Magnesium acetate, water and carbon dioxide are formed during the reaction of acetic acid with magnesium carbonate.

$$
\underset{\text { Acetic acid }}{2 \mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOH}}+\underset{\text { Magnesium carbonate }}{\mathrm{MgCO}_{3}} \longrightarrow \underset{\text { Magnesium acetate }}{\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COO}\right)_{2} \mathrm{Mg}}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}+\mathrm{CO}_{2} \uparrow
$$

(d) Reaction with $\mathrm{PCl}_{5}, \mathrm{PCl}_{3}$ and $\mathrm{SOCl}_{2}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOH}+\mathrm{PCl}_{5} & \longrightarrow \mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COCl}+\mathrm{POCl}_{3}+\mathrm{HCl} \\
\text { Acetic acid } & \text { Acetylchloride } \\
{ }_{\text {Acetic acid }} 3 \mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOH}+\mathrm{PCl}_{3} & \longrightarrow \mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COCl}+\mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{PO}_{3} \\
\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOH}+\mathrm{SOCl}_{2} & \longrightarrow \mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COCl} \\
\text { Acetic acid } & \mathrm{CH}^{2}+\mathrm{SO}_{2} \uparrow+\mathrm{HCl} \uparrow \\
\text { Acetylchloride } &
\end{aligned}
$$

(e) Decarboxylation : Sodium or potasium salts of carboxylic acids on heating with soda lime $(\mathrm{NaOH}+\mathrm{CaO})$ give alkanes which contain one carbon atom less than the parent acid.

$$
\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COONa}+\mathrm{NaOH} \xrightarrow{\mathrm{CaO}, \text { Heat }} \mathrm{CH}_{4}+\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{CO}_{3}
$$

## Uses

It is an important chemical reagent and industrial chemical that is used in the production of polyethylene terephthalate, mainly used in soft drink bottles; cellulose acetate, mainly for photographic film; and polyvinyl acetate for wood glue, as well as synthetic fibres and fabrics. In households, dilute acetic acid is often used in descaling agents. In the food industry, acetic acid is used under the food additive code E260 as an acidity regulator.

## SOAPS AND DETERGENTS

Soaps are sodium or potassium salts of higher fatty acids, produced from the alkaline hydrolysis of fats (higher fatty acids) in a chemical reaction. The reaction is called saponification. They are of biological origin. Each soap molecule has a long hydrocarbon chain, sometimes called its 'tail', with a carboxylate 'head'. In water, the sodium or potassium ions float free, leaving a negatively charged head.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-169.jpg?height=205&width=871&top_left_y=1909&top_left_x=630)

- When the alkali is sodium hydroxide, a sodium soap is formed. Sodium soaps are "hard" soaps. When the alkali is potassium hydroxide, a potassium soap is formed. Potassium soaps are softer and are found in some liquid hand soaps and shaving creams.
- Soap is an excellent cleanser because of its ability to act as an emulsifying agent. An emulsifier is capable of dispersing one liquid into another immiscible liquid. This means that while oil (which attracts dirt) doesn't naturally mix with water, soap can suspends oil/dirt in such a way that it can be removed.

Detergents (syndets) are emulsifying agents that are very similar to soaps but they do not have a biological origin and they do not have a carboxylate group instead of it they have sulphonate group as they are sodium salt of long chain sulphonic acids. Synthetic detergents usually have a long hydrocarbon chain of $12-20$ carbon atoms. At the head of this chain there is a polar group that forms a strong hydrogen bond with water but does not form strong ionic bonds with the ions of hard water.
Before dealing with cleansing action of soap let us understand few points:
(i) The salts of fatty acids have an ionic polar head group, the carboxylate and a long non-polar hydrocarbon chain.
<img class="imgSvg" id = "mpcazme97p4ybcz37sd" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,"/>

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-170.jpg?height=1342&width=529&top_left_y=625&top_left_x=1292)
Figure : Structure of a detergent molecule and it's cleansing action

(ii) The polar group is hydrophilic (i.e. water-loving), the non-polar portion is hydrophobic (i.e. water-hating) or lipophilic.
(iii) An aqueous solutions of this type of compound tend to form ordered aggregates such as micelles and bilayers.
(iv) Here the polar groups are exposed to the water and the hydrocarbon portions are buried within the core, away from the water.

## How Soap Cleans :

The things we call "dirt" are actually grease, oils, and fats which are insoluble in water. When cleaning, soap micelles come into contact with the "dirt" and because like dissolves like, a minute amount of "dirt" dissolves within the central region of the soap micelle. Nonpolar dirts are emulsified and washed away in polar wash water. This is why soaps are said to be good emulsifying agents.

## How Does Detergent Remove Grease From Clothing ?

Detergent molecules contain hydrophobic tails (hydrocarbon chain) which are water hating and will attach themselves to any substance which is non-polar (grease). The tails surround the grease with the polar heads in the aqueous phase, forming the structure shown. On agitation, the grease is pulled from the clothing allowing the grease to be completely surrounded and moved into the aqueous phase. This is then washed away leaving only clean clothes behind.

Figure : Structure of a detergent molecule and it's cleansing action
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-170.jpg?height=111&width=374&top_left_y=2012&top_left_x=810)

## Surface Tension/Surfactants :

The property of a liquid due to which its free surface tries to have minimum surface area and behaves as if it were under tension some what like a stretched elastic membrane is called surface tension. Chemicals that are able to reduce the surface tension of the solvent effectively are called surface active agents, or surfactants. Surfactants perform other important functions in cleaning, such as loosening, emulsifying (dispersing in water) and holding soil in suspension until it can be rinsed away. Surfactants can also provide alkalinity, which is useful in removing acidic soils. Surfactants are classified by their ionic (electrical charge) properties in water: anionic (negative charge), nonionic (no charge), cationic (positive charge) and amphoteric (either positive or negative charge). Soap is an anionic surfactant. Carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC) is a compound which is added to synthetic detergent to keep the dust particles suspended in water, and helps in cleansing process.

## CASE STUDY : Chemistry Behind Soaps and Detergents

CASE I : The dirty clothes has grease or oil particles which hold dirt on clothes. For washing these clothes soap is required because grease particles do not dissolve in water. Only water is not sufficient to remove dirt even at hot condition. Why?

When soap is dissolved in $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$, it forms a colloidal suspension in water in which the soap molecules comes together to form spherical micelles. The soap micelle entraps the oily or greasy particle by using its hydrocarbon ends whereas ionic end of the soap molecules remain attached to $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$. These micelles get dispersed in $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ due to which the soap water becomes dirty but the cloth gets cleaned. No such interaction of greasy or oil particles is possible with water even at hot condition. will be effective in both, soft water as well as hard water. why?
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-171.jpg?height=728&width=630&top_left_y=1092&top_left_x=442)

## CASE III : Detergents have number of

advantages over soaps but detergents are the main pollutants to cause water pollution. Why?

An important disadvantage of detergents over soap is that some of the detergents are non biodegradable, that is they cannot be decomposed by micro-organisms and hence cause water pollution in water bodies. Detergents sometimes could be carcinogenic, so they should be eliminated from the water e.g. phosphate salt. It is the main constituent of the laundry detergents, which tends to inhibit the biodegradation of organic substances. Non-biodegradable substances cannot be eliminated by public waste water treatment. In addition to that, some phosphate based detergents can also cause eutrophication.
CASE II : The cleaning action of soap is very effective in soft water but it is not effective in hard water. If detergents are used for cleaning purpose, it
Hard water contains calcium and magnesium salts whereas soft water contains negligible amount of calcium and magnesium ions. Generally, soaps are sodium or potassium salts of long chain fatty acids. When soap is added to hard water, the $\mathrm{Ca}^{2+}$ and $\mathrm{Mg}^{2+}$ ions present in hard water react with soap. The sodium salts present in soap are converted to their corresponding calcium and magnesium salts which are precipitated as scum. The insoluble scum sticks on the clothes thus the cleaning capacity of soap is reduced. Soft water, however, does not contain any calcium or magnesium ions, therefore, lathers easily when soap is added. A detergent is the sodium salt of long chain benzene sulphonic acid. Detergent can form lather easily even in hard water because they do not form insoluble $\mathrm{Ca}^{2+}$ or $\mathrm{Mg}^{2+}$ salts on reacting with $\mathrm{Ca}^{2+}$ or $\mathrm{Mg}^{2+}$ ions present in hard water.

1. What is the difference between temporary and permanent hardness? Is it possible to remove these hardness? If possible please suggest suitable method.
2. Give the chemical equation involved in the formation of scum.
3. White material get deposited in the interior of boilers and electric kettle with their prolong use. What is the reason for this observation?

## True / False

DIRECTIONS : Read the following statements and write your answer as true or false.

1. Carbon is a versatile element.
2. Carbon forms covalent bonds with itself and other elements such as hydrogen, oxygen, sulphur, nitrogen and chlorine.
3. Carbon and its compounds are some of our major sources of fuels.
4. Graphite is a good conductor of electricity.
5. The simplest saturated hydrocarbon is methane.
6. Ethanol is the first member of the alcohol homologous series.
7. Diamond is a good conductor of electricity.
8. Graphite is used in pencils.
9. When hydrocarbons burn in air, carbon dioxide and hydrogen are produced with heat energy.
10. If a hydrocarbon has double or triple covalent bond, it is saturated.
11. Unsaturated hydrocarbons give addition reactions.
12. By hydrogenation, vegetable oils are converted into vanaspati ghee.
13. Invertase and amylase are two enzymes involved in fermentation of ethanol from sugar.

## Column I

(A) -CHO compounds
(B) $-\mathrm{CONH}_{2}$
(C) $-\mathrm{NH}_{2}$
(D) $-\mathrm{N}=\mathrm{N}-$

## Column II

(p) A
(q) Aldehydes
(r) Acid amides
(s) Amines

## Match the Following

DIRECTIONS : Each question contains option given in two columns. options ( $A, B, C, D$ ) in column I have to be matched with options (p, q, r, s) in column II.
1.

|  | Column I | Column II |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| (A) | Combustion reaction | (p) |
| $\mathrm{C}_{3} \mathrm{H}_{8}+\mathrm{Cl}_{2}$ |  |  |

$$
\xrightarrow{\text { UV light }} \mathrm{C}_{3} \mathrm{H}_{7} \mathrm{Cl}+\mathrm{HCl}
$$

(B) Oxidation reaction
(q) $\mathrm{CH}_{2}=\mathrm{CH}_{2}+\mathrm{H}_{2}$
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-173.jpg?height=58&width=293&top_left_y=804&top_left_x=1585)
(C) Addition reaction
(r) $2 \mathrm{CH}_{4}+\mathrm{O}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})$

$$
\xrightarrow[\text { Molybdenum oxide }]{300^{\circ} \mathrm{C}} \mathrm{HCHO}+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}
$$

(D) Substitution reaction
(s) $\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{OH}+3 \mathrm{O}_{2} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{CO}_{2}+3 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$
2.

## Passage Based Questions

DIRECTIONS: Give answer in one word or one sentence.

## Passage - 1

The given diagram represent an experiment in which a test tube contains 1 mL of ethanol (absolute alcohol) and 1 mL glacial acetic acid along with a few drops of concentrated $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}$. Observe the diagram and answer the following questions.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-173.jpg?height=448&width=579&top_left_y=1841&top_left_x=1190)

1. Name the type of reaction taking place in this experiment.
2. Write the chemical equation.
3. Why reverse of this reaction is known as saponification reaction?
4. Give two uses of the resulting product.

## Short Answer Questions

## DIRECTIONS : Give answer in 2-3 sentences.

1. What is meant by homologous series? State any four characteristics.
2. Draw the electron dot structure of ethyne and also draw its structural formula.
3. Write the name of all amorphous form of carbon.
4. Explain the toxic effects of carbon monoxide.
5. Explain with example how aromatic unsaturated organic compound undergoes addition reaction?
6. What are heterocyclic compounds. Explain with example?
7. Which two of the following compounds could belong to the same homologous series?
$\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{6} \mathrm{O}_{2}, \mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{6} \mathrm{O}, \mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{6}, \mathrm{CH}_{4} \mathrm{O}$
8. Give four uses of ethyl alcohol.
9. What is a detergent ? Name one detergent.
10. What is meant by 'fermentation' ? Write chemical equations for the two steps involved in preparing ethanol by the fermentation of molasses.
11. Complete the following reaction equations:
(i) $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{OH} \xrightarrow[\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOH}]{\mathrm{CrO}_{3} \text { in }}$
(ii) $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOH}+\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{OH} \xrightarrow{\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}}$
12. (a) Name the organic acid present in vinegar. Write its chemical formula also.
(b) Name the products formed when
(i) ethanol burns in air.
(ii) sodium ethanoate is heated with soda lime.
13. What are esters? How are they formed? Where do they occur in nature ? Give one example.
14. Write two reactions to demonstrate that acetic acid is acidic in nature?
15. Give two differences between soap and synthetic detergents?
16. What are the harmful effects of drinking alcohol?
17. What are enzymes? Name the enzymes required for fermentation of sugar cane to ethanol.
18. What is meant by denatured alcohol? How is it prepared?
19. Suggest a solid dehydrating agent for ethanol. Give balanced equation also.
20. Give any three uses of ethanoic acid.
21. What is meant by isomers? Draw the structure of two isomers of butane, $\mathrm{C}_{4} \mathrm{H}_{10}$. Explain why we cannot have isomers of first three members of alkane series.
22. Write the molecular, electronic and structural formulae of ethyne.
23. The structure formula of an ester is :
<img class="imgSvg" id = "mpcazmeyar35ucnk70e" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,"/>

Write the structural formula of the acid and the alcohol from which it might be prepared. Name the process of formation of ester.
24. A compound ' X ' on heating with excess conc. sulphuric acid at 443 K gives an unsaturated compound ' Y '. ' X ' also reacts with sodium metal to evolve a colourless gas ' $Z$ '. Identify ' $X$ ', ' $Y$ ' and ' $Z$ '. Write the equation of the chemical reaction of formation of ' Y ' and also write the role of sulphuric acid in the reaction.
25. (a) Why are most carbon compounds poor conductors of electricity?
(b) Write the name and structure of a saturated compound in which the carbon atoms are arranged in a ring. Give the number of single bonds present in this compound.
26. In three test tubes $\mathrm{A}, \mathrm{B}$, and C , three different liquids namely, distilled water, underground water and distilled water in which a pinch of calcium sulphate is dissolved, respectively are taken. Equal amount of soap solution is added to each test tube and the contents are shaken. In which test tube will the length of the foam (lather) be longest? Justify your answer.

## Long Answer Questions

1. Explain the reason for difference in properties of diamond and graphite.
2. Give one example each of :
3. Cracking
4. Hydrogenation
5. Dehydration
6. Substitution reaction
7. Addition reaction
8. Differentiate between diamond and graphite.
9. What happens when :
(i) ethanoic acid reacts with sodium metal.
(ii) ethanoic acid reacts with sodium carbonate
(iii) ethanoic acid reacts with sodium hydroxide
(iv) ethanoic acid reacts with an alcohol in the presence of concentrated sulphuric acid.
10. Explain the following terms :
(i) Esterification,
(ii) Saponification,
(iii) Decarboxylation
(iv) Polymerisation.
11. (a) State any three physical properties of carbon compounds.
(b) Carbon is a versatile element. Justify this statement.
12. Explain why carbon forms compounds mainly by covalent bonds. Explain in brief two main reasons for carbon forming a large number of compounds. Why does carbon form strong bonds with most other elements?
13. Elements forming ionic compounds attain noble gas electronic configuration by either gaining or losing electrons from their valence shells. Explain giving reason why carbon cannot attain such a configuration in this manner to form its compounds. Name the type of bonds formed in ionic compounds and in the compounds formed by carbon. Also explain with reason why carbon compounds are generally poor conductors of electricity.
14. Describe the addition reaction of carbon compounds with its application. State the function of catalyst in this reaction. How this reaction is different from a substitution reaction?
15. Why are certain compounds called hydrocarbons? Write the general formula for homologous series of alkanes, alkenes and alkynes and also draw the structure of the first member of each series. Write the name of the reaction that converts alkenes into alkanes and also write a chemical equation to show the necessary conditions for the reaction to occur.
16. Write the chemical formula and name of the compound which is the active ingredient of all alcoholic drinks. List its two uses. Write chemical equation and name of the product formed when this compound reacts with-
(i) sodium metal
(ii) hot concentrated sulphuric acid
17. What is methane? Draw its electron dot structure. Name the type of bonds formed in this compound. Why are such compounds:
(i) poor conductors of electricity? and
(ii) have low melting and boiling points? What happens when this compound burns in oxygen?
18. (a) Compare soaps and detergents on the basic of their composition and cleansing action in hard water.
(b) What happens when ethanol is treated with sodium metal ? State the behaviour of ethanol in this reaction.
(c) Draw the structure of cyclobexane.
(d) Name the following compound.
<img class="imgSvg" id = "mpcazmf0qv1koxe0wr" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,"/>

## Reasoning Based Questions

1. Why some hydrocarbons are subjected to cracking ?
2. Why carbon atoms cannot form ionic bonds in its compounds ?
3. Why does carbon form compounds mainly by covalent bonding?
4. Why is diamond used for making cutting tools but graphite is not?
5. Why is graphite used for making dry cell electrodes but diamond is not?
6. Why does element carbon form a large number of carbon compounds?
7. Why diamond is hard while graphite is soft?
8. Why graphite can be used as lubricant but diamond cannot?
9. Why diamond has a high melting point?
10. Why common salt is added during preparation of soap?

## HOTS Questions

1. Why are unsaturated hydrocarbons more reactive than saturated hydrocarbons?
2. Why are carbon compounds not able to conduct electricity through them?
3. How many covalent bonds are there in a molecule of cyclohexane?
4. Define covalent bond? What do you mean by the term covalency. Give an example of each containing.
(i) single bond (ii) double bond (iii) triple bond
5. Why carbon shows the property of catenation? Can any other element shows the property of catenation.
6. Which of the following $\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{6}, \mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{OH}, \mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOH}$ is a hydrocarbon?
7. (i) What is the type of reaction taking place between ethane and chlorine to form monochloroethane?
(ii) The reaction between ethene and chlorine forms only one product. Name the type of this reaction.
(iii) (a) Draw the structural formula of ethene.
(b) What is the feature of the ethene structure which allows ethene to react with chlorine?
8. State the general formula of the homologous series to which each of the following belong :
(a) Methane
(b) Butane
(c) Butyne
(d) Ethyne
(e) Propyne
(f) Propylene
(g) Ethene
9. The molecules of alkene family are represented by a general formula $\mathrm{C}_{n} \mathrm{H}_{2 n}$. Now answer the following :
(i) What do $n$ and $2 n$ signify?
(ii) What is the name of alkene when $n=4$ ?
(iii) What is the molecular formula of alkene when $n=6$ ?
(iv) What is the molecular formula of the alkene if there are six H atoms in it?
(v) What is the molecular formula and structural formula of the first member of the alkene family?
(vi) Write the molecular formulae of lower and higher homologues of an alkene which contains four carbon atoms.
10. Write the molecular formulae of the third and fifth members of homologous series of carbon compounds represented by the general formula $\mathrm{C}_{n} \mathrm{H}_{2 n-2}$.
11. Give the molecular formula of one homologue of each of the following :
(i) $\mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{14}$
(ii) $\quad \mathrm{C}_{3} \mathrm{H}_{6}$
(iii) $\quad \mathrm{C}_{4} \mathrm{H}_{8}$
12. Give the general formula of paraffins, olefins and alkynes.
13. What happens when soap solution in a test tube is shaken with (i) soft water (ii) hard water ?
14. An organic compound ' A ' is a constituent of antifreeze. The compound on heating with oxygen forms another compound B which has a molecular formula $\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{4} \mathrm{O}_{2}$. Identify the compound ' A ' and ' B '. Write the chemical equation of the reaction that takes place to form the compound ' B '.
15. What are detergents chemically? Why are they more effective than soaps in cleansing action? How can detergent molecules be altered to make them biodegradable?
16. An organic compound A of molecular formula $\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{6} \mathrm{O}$ on oxidation gives an acid B with the same number of carbon atoms in the molecule as A . Compound A is often used for sterilization of skin by doctors. Name the compounds A and B . Write the chemical equation involved in the formation of B from A .

## Case Study Based MCQs

1. Read the following and answer any four questions from (i) to (v).

Food, clothes, medicines, books, or many of the things are all based on this versatile element carbon. In addition, all living structures are carbon based. The earth's crust has only $0.02 \%$ carbon in the form of minerals. The element carbon occurs in different forms in nature with widely varying physical properties. Both diamond and graphite are formed by carbon atoms, the difference lies in the manner in which the carbon atoms are bonded to one another. Carbon has the unique ability to form bonds with other atoms of carbon, giving rise to large molecules. This property is called catenation.
(i) From the given alternatives, whose chemical and physical properties are not same?
(a) Graphite and Diamond
(b) Phosphorous and Sulphur
(c) Carbon and Hydrogen
(d) Methyl alcohol and Acetic acid
(ii) Which of the following statements is not correct?
(a) Graphite is much less dense than diamond
(b) Graphite is black and soft
(c) Graphite has low melting point
(d) Graphite feels smooth and slippery
(iii) Which of the following are isomers?
(a) Butane and isobutene
(b) Ethane and ethene
(c) Propane and propyne
(d) Butane and isobutane
(iv) Which one of the following is not an allotrope of carbon?
(a) Soot
(b) Graphite
(c) Diamond
(d) Carborundum
(v) Pentane has the molecular formula $\mathrm{C}_{5} \mathrm{H}_{12}$. It has
(a) 5 covalent bonds
(b) 12 covalent bonds
(c) 16 covalent bonds
(d) 17 covalent bonds

## Exercise 21 Master NCERT (Textbook \& Exemplar)

## Text - Book Questions

1. What would be the electron dot structure of carbon dioxide which has the formula $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ ?
2. What would be the electron dot structure of a molecule of sulphur which is made up of eight atoms of sulphur? (Hint : The eight atoms are joined together in the form of a ring.)
3. How many structural isomers can you draw for pentane ?
4. What will be the formula and electron dot structure of cyclopentane?
5. Draw the structures of the following compounds :
(i) Ethanoic acid
(ii) Butanone
(iii) Bromopentane
(iv) Hexanal

Are structural isomers possible for bromopentane
6. How would you name the following compounds ?
(i) $\mathrm{CH}_{3}-\mathrm{CH}_{2}-\mathrm{Br}$
(ii)
<img class="imgSvg" id = "mpcazmf1f3ll43i9bnj" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,"/>
(iii)
<img class="imgSvg" id = "mpcazmf13ncg5hmk4q5" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,"/>
7. Why is the conversion of ethanol to ethanoic acid an oxidation reaction?
8. A mixture of oxygen and ethyne is burnt for welding. Can you tell why a mixture of ethyne and air is not used ?
9. How would you distinguish experimentally between an alcohol and a carboxylic acids?
10. What are oxidising agents?
11. Would you be able to check if water is hard using a detergent?
12. People use a variety of methods to wash clothes. Usually after adding the soap, they 'beat' the clothes on a stone, or beat it with a paddle, scrub with a brush or the mixture is agitated in a washing machine. Why is agitation necessary to get clean clothes?

## Text - Book Exercise

1. Ethane with the molecular formula $\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{6}$ has :
(a) 6 covalent bonds
(b) 7 covalent bonds
(c) 8 covalent bonds
(d) 9 covalent bonds
2. Butanone is four-carbon compound with the functional group :
(a) carboxylic acid
(b) aldehyde
(c) ketone
(d) alcohol
3. While cooking, if the bottom of the vessel is getting blackened on the outside, it means that :
(a) the food is not cooked completely.
(b) the fuel is not burning completely.
(c) the fuel is wet.
(d) the fuel is burning completely.
4. Explain the nature of the covalent bond using the bond formation in $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{Cl}$.
5. Draw the electron dot structure for :
(a) Ethanoic acid
(b) $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{~S}$
(c) Propanone
(d) $\mathrm{F}_{2}$
6. What is an homologous series? Explain with an example.
7. How can ethanol and ethanoic acid be differentiated on the basis of their physical and chemical properties?
8. Why does micelle formation take place when soap is added to water? Will a micelle be formed in other solvents such as ethanol also?
9. Why are carbon and its compounds used as fuels for most applications?
10. Explain the formation of scum when hard water is treated with soap.
11. What change will you observe if you test soap with litmus paper (red and blue)?
12. What is hydrogenation ? What is its industrial application?
13. Which of the following hydrocarbons undergo addition reaction : $\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{6}, \mathrm{C}_{3} \mathrm{H}_{8}, \mathrm{C}_{3} \mathrm{H}_{6}, \mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{2}$ and $\mathrm{CH}_{4}$ ?
14. Give a test that can be used to differentiate chemically between butter and cooking oil.
15. Explain the mechanism of the cleaning action of soap.

## Exemplar Questions

1. A compound X is formed by the reaction of a carboxylic acid $\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{4} \mathrm{O}_{2}$ and an alcohol in presence of a few drops of $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}$. The alcohol on oxidation with alkaline $\mathrm{KMnO}_{4}$ followed by acidification gives the same carboxylic acid as used in this reaction. Give the names and structures of (a) carboxylic acid, (b) alcohol and (c) the compound X . Also write the reaction.
2. Catenation is the ability of an atom to form bonds with other atoms of the same element. It is exhibited by both carbon and silicon. Compare the ability of catenation of the two elements. Give reasons.
3. (a) Write the formula and draw electron dot structure of carbon tetrachloride.
(b) What is saponification? Write the reaction involved in this process.

## Exercise 3

## Multiple Choice Questions

DIRECTIONS : This section contains multiple choice questions. Each question has four choices (a), (b), (c) and (d) out of which ONLY ONE is correct.

1. The number of $4^{\circ}$ carbon atoms in 2,2,4,4-tetramethyl pentane is -
(a) 1
(b) 2
(c) 3
(d) 4
2. Which is a general formula of alkenes?
(a) $\mathrm{C}_{n} \mathrm{H}_{2 n+2}$
(a) None of these
(b) $\mathrm{C}_{n} \mathrm{H}_{2 n}$
(c) $\mathrm{C}_{n} \mathrm{H}_{2 n-2}$
3. The functional group represent alcohol is -
(a) -OH
(b) -CHO
(c) -COOH
(d) $>\mathrm{C}=\mathrm{O}$
4. Which of the following is the purest form of carbon?
(a) charcoal
(b) coal
(c) diamond
(d) graphite
5. Organic compounds will always contain -
(a) carbon
(b) hydrogen
(c) nitrogen
(d) sulphur
6. Methane, ethane and propane are said to form a homologous series because all are -
(a) hydrocarbons
(b) saturated compounds
(c) aliphatic compounds
(d) differ from each other by a $\mathrm{CH}_{2}$ group
7. When methane is burnt in an excess of air, the products of combustion are -
(a) C and $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$
(b) CO and $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$
(c) $\quad \mathrm{CO}_{2}$ and $\mathrm{H}_{2}$
(d) $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ and $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$
8. Which of the following gases is called 'marsh gas'?
(a) $\mathrm{H}_{2}$
(b) $\mathrm{CH}_{4}$
(c) $\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{4}$
(d) $\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{2}$
9. The final product of chlorination of methane in the sun light is -
(a) $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{Cl}$
(b) $\mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{Cl}_{2}$
(c) $\mathrm{CHCl}_{3}$
(d) $\mathrm{CCl}_{4}$
10. The number of oxygen molecules used in the combustion of 1 molecule of ethanol is -
(a) 1
(b) 2
(c) 3
(d) 4
11. General formula of alkyne is -
(a) $\mathrm{C}_{n} \mathrm{H}_{2 n+2}$
(b) $\mathrm{C}_{n} \mathrm{H}_{2 n}$
(c) $\mathrm{C}_{n} \mathrm{H}_{2 n-2}$
(d) $\mathrm{C}_{n} \mathrm{H}_{n}$
12. When vanaspati oil reacts with hydrogen then it is converted into vanaspati ghee. In this process catalyst used is :
(a) Fe
(b) Mo
(c) V
(d) Ni
13. Observe the following pairs of organic compounds:
(I) $\mathrm{C}_{4} \mathrm{H}_{9} \mathrm{OH}$ and $\mathrm{C}_{5} \mathrm{H}_{11} \mathrm{OH}$
(II) $\mathrm{C}_{7} \mathrm{H}_{15} \mathrm{OH}$ and $\mathrm{C}_{5} \mathrm{H}_{11} \mathrm{OH}$
(III) $\mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{13} \mathrm{OH}$ and $\mathrm{C}_{3} \mathrm{H}_{7} \mathrm{OH}$

Which of these pair is a homologous series according to increasing order of carbon atom?
(a) (III) only
(b) (II) only
(c) (I) only
(d) All of these
15. Carbon exists in the atmosphere in the form of:
(a) carbon monoxide only.
(b) carbon monoxide in traces, and carbon dioxide.
(c) carbon dioxide only.
(d) coal
16. Oils on treating with hydrogen in the presence of palladium or nickel catalyst form fats. This is an example of :
(a) addition reaction
(b) substitution reaction
(c) displacement reaction
(d) oxidation reaction
17. Chlorine reacts with saturated hydrocarbons at room temperature in the
(a) absence of sunlight
(b) presence of sunlight
(c) presence of water
(d) presence of hydrochloric acid
18. Pentane has the molecular formula $\mathrm{C}_{5} \mathrm{H}_{12}$. It has
(a) 5 covalent bonds
(b) 12 covalent bonds
(c) 16 covalent bonds
(d) 17 covalent bonds
19. Structural formula of benzene is

20. Carbon forms four covalent bonds by sharing its four valence electrons with four univalent atoms, e.g. hydrogen. After the formation of four bonds, carbon attains the electronic configuration of:
(a) helium
(b) neon
(c) argon
(d) krypton
21. Which of the following does not belong to the same homologous series?
(a) $\mathrm{CH}_{4}$
(b) $\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{6}$
(c) $\mathrm{C}_{3} \mathrm{H}_{8}$
(d) $\mathrm{C}_{4} \mathrm{H}_{8}$
22. The enzyme involved in the oxidation of ethanol to form vinegar is -
(a) zymase
(b) oxidase
(c) acetobacter
(d) invertase
23. Glacial acetic acid is -
(a) $100 \%$ acetic acid free of water
(b) solidified acetic acid
(c) gaseous acetic acid
(d) frozen acetic acid
24. When ethanoic acid is heated with $\mathrm{NaHCO}_{3}$ the gas evolved is -
(a) $\mathrm{H}_{2}$
(b) $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$
(c) $\mathrm{CH}_{4}$
(d) CO
25. During decarboxylation of ethanoic acid with sodalime $(\mathrm{NaOH}+\mathrm{CaO}), \mathrm{CO}_{2}$ is removed as -
(a) $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$
(b) CO
(c) $\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{CO}_{3}$
(d) $\mathrm{CaCO}_{3}$
26. When ethanoic acid reacts with ethanol, a sweet smelling product is formed. The functional group in the product is
(a) aldehyde
(b) ketone
(c) alcohol
(d) ester
27. Detergents can lather well in -
(a) soft water
(b) hard water
(c) river water
(d) any one of the above
28. 'Drinking alcohol' is very harmful and it ruins the health. 'Drinking alcohol' stands for -
(a) drinking methyl alcohol
(b) drinking ethyl alcohol
(c) drinking propyl alcohol
(d) drinking isopropyl alcohol
29. The treatment of acetic acid with lithium aluminium hydride produces -
(a) methanol
(b) ethanol
(c) ethanal
(d) methanal
30. The fermentation reactions are carried out in temperature range of -
(a) $\quad 20-30^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$
(b) $\quad 30-40^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$
(c) $40-50^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$
(d) $50-60^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$
31. Soaps are sodium salts of fatty acids. Which of the following fatty acids does not form soap?
(a) butyric acid
(b) oleic acid
(c) palmitic acid
(d) stearic acid
32. The OH group of an alcohol or the -COOH group of a carboxylic acid can be replaced by - Cl using :-
(a) phosphorus pentachloride
(b) hypochlorous acid
(c) chlorine
(d) hydrochloric acid
33. Which compound represents the vinegar?
(a) HCOOH
(b) $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CHO}$
(c) HCHO
(d) $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOH}$
34. $\mathrm{A} \& \mathrm{~B}$ both compounds give $\mathrm{H}_{2}$ gas with sodium. If $\mathrm{A} \&$ B react in presence of acid catalyst then they form ethyl acetate. Thus, $\mathrm{A} \& \mathrm{~B}$ would be -
(a) $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOH}, \mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{OH}$
(b) $\mathrm{HCOOH}, \mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOH}$
(c) $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOH}, \mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{OH}$
(d) $\mathrm{C}_{3} \mathrm{H}_{7} \mathrm{COOH}, \mathrm{C}_{3} \mathrm{H}_{7} \mathrm{OH}$
35. During the cleansing action of soap dirt is surrounded by soap molecules. Soap molecule is like a tadpole which has a head and tail. These head and tail respectively are:
(a) hydrophobic and hydrophilic
(b) hydrophobic and hydrophobic
(c) hydrophilic and hydrophilic
(d) hydrophilic and hydrophobic
36. The total number of electrons and the number of electrons involved in the formation of various bonds present in one molecule of propanal $\left(\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{CHO}\right)$ are respectively.
[NTSE]
(a) 32 and 20
(b) 24 and 20
(c) 24 and 18
(d) 32 and 18

[NTSE]
(a) 3
(b) 5
(c) 4
(d) 2

(b) $\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{COOH}$
(c) $\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{COOCH}_{3}$
(d) $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{OH}$

[JSTSE]
(a) $23.3 \%$
(b) $46.7 \%$
(c) $69.9 \%$
(d) $11.66 \%$

[NTSE]
(a) $\mathrm{C}_{13} \mathrm{H}_{26} \mathrm{O}_{2}$
(b) $\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{4} \mathrm{O}_{2}$
(c) $\mathrm{C}_{9} \mathrm{H}_{18} \mathrm{O}_{2}$

[JSTSE]
(a) $\mathrm{CH}_{4}$ is lighter than Air
(b) $\mathrm{CH}_{4}$ is poisonous gas

[NTSE]

(b) Diamond is good conductor of heat.
(c) Graphite is a good conductor of electricity.

When ' X ' reacts with methanol in the presence of concentrated $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}$, a sweet smelling substance is produced. The molecular formula of the compound ' X ' is -
[NTSE]
(a) $\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{4} \mathrm{O}$
(b) $\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{4} \mathrm{O}_{2}$
(c) $\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{6} \mathrm{O}$
(d) $\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{6} \mathrm{O}_{2}$

<img class="imgSvg" id = "mpcazmfmwxe2oskywp" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,"/>
(a) alcohol, ketone and ester
(b) ester and carboxylic acid
(c) carboxylic acid and ketone
(d) ester and alcohol

[JSTSE]
(a) $\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{~N}$
(b) $\mathrm{C}_{3} \mathrm{H}_{4} \mathrm{~N}$
(c) $\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{HN}_{2}$
(d) $\mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{8} \mathrm{~N}_{2}$

[NTSE]
(a) 186 g
(b) 256 g
(c) 352 g
(d) 372 g
47. A hydrocarbon ' A ' $\left(\mathrm{C}_{3} \mathrm{H}_{8}\right)$ on treatment with chlorine in presence of sunlight yielded compound ' B ' as major product Reaction of 'B' with aqueous KOH gave 'C' which on treatment with concentrated $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}$ yielded 'D'. Hydrogenation of 'D' gave back 'A'. The sequence of reactions involved in above conversion is:
[NTSE]
(a) substitution, substitution, addition, dehydration
(b) substitution, substitution, dehydration, addition
(c) substitution, dehydration, addition, addition
(d) addition, substitution, dehydration, substitution.
48. An organic liquid ' $A$ ' with acidified potassium dichromate gave product ' B '. The compound ' B ' on heating with methanol in presence of concentrated sulphuric acid formed compound ' C ' which on subsequent treatment
with sodium hydroxide formed two product 'D' and ' E '. The product ' D ' is known to affect the optic nerve causing blindness. Intake of ' $D$ ' in very small quantities can cause death. What are compound 'A', 'B', 'C', 'D' and ' E '?
[NTSE]
(a) $\mathrm{A}=$ Ethanol, $\mathrm{B}=$ Ethanoic acid, $\mathrm{C}=$ Methanol D = Sodium acetate, $\mathrm{E}=$ Methyl ethanoate
(b) $\mathrm{A}=$ Ethanol, $\mathrm{B}=$ Ethanoic acid, $\mathrm{C}=$ Methyl ethanoate $\mathrm{D}=$ Methanol, $\mathrm{E}=$ Sodium acetate
(c) $\mathrm{A}=$ Sodium acetate, $\mathrm{B}=$ Ethanoic acid, $\mathrm{C}=$ Methyl ethanoate, $\mathrm{D}=$ Methanol, $\mathrm{E}=$ Ethanol
(d) $\mathrm{A}=$ Ethanol, $\mathrm{B}=$ Ethanoic acid, $\mathrm{C}=$ Methyl ethanoate, $\mathrm{D}=$ Sodium acetate, $\mathrm{E}=$ Methanol
49. In shaving creams is added to prevent rapid drying.
[JSTSE]
(a) Methanol
(b) Glycerol
(c) Ethanol
(d) Glycol
50. An organic compound A on heating with concentrated $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}$ gave product B and on warming with alkaline $\mathrm{KMnO}_{4}$ gave compound C . Compound A on heating with compound C in presence of concentrated $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}$ formed compound D , which has fruity smell. Identify the compounds $\mathrm{A}, \mathrm{B}, \mathrm{C}$ and D :
[NTSE]
(a) $\mathrm{A}=$ Alcohol, $\mathrm{B}=$ Carboxylic acid,
$\mathrm{C}=$ Alkene, $\mathrm{D}=$ Ester
(b) $\mathrm{A}=$ Carboxylic acid, $\mathrm{B}=$ Ester,
$\mathrm{C}=$ Alkene, $\mathrm{D}=$ Alcohol
(c) $\mathrm{A}=$ Alcohol, $\mathrm{B}=$ Alkene,
$\mathrm{C}=$ Carboxylic acid, $\mathrm{D}=$ Ester
(d) A=Alkene, $\mathrm{B}=$ Alcohol, $\mathrm{C}=$ Ester,

D = Carboxylic acid
51. Two organic compounds ' A ' and ' B ' react with sodium metal and both produce the same gas ' X ', but with sodium hydrogen carbonate, only compound B reacts to give a gas ' Y '. Identify ' A ', ' B ', ' X ' and ' Y ':
[NTSE]
(a) $\mathrm{A}=$ Ethylene, $\mathrm{B}=$ Ethyl alcohol,
$\mathrm{X}=$ Carbon dioxide, $\mathrm{Y}=$ Hydrogen
(b) $\mathrm{A}=$ Ethyl alcohol, $\mathrm{B}=$ Acetic acid,
$\mathrm{X}=$ Hydrogen, $\mathrm{Y}=$ Carbon dioxide
(c) $\mathrm{A}=$ Methyl alcohol, $\mathrm{B}=$ Ethyl alcohol,
$\mathrm{X}=$ Hydrogen, $\mathrm{Y}=$ Carbon dioxide
(d) $\mathrm{A}=$ Acetic acid, $\mathrm{B}=$ Formic acid,
$\mathrm{X}=$ Carbon dioxide, $\mathrm{Y}=$ Hydrogen

respectively, are
(a) $s p^{2}, s p, s p^{2}$

[JSTSE]
(a) Triethyl lead
(b) Trimethyl lead

54. Identify the correct order of boiling points of the following compounds-
[NTSE]
(A) $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{OH}$
(B) $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{CHO}$
(C) $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{COOH}$
(a) (A) $>$ (B) $>$ (C)

## More than One Option Correct

DIRECTIONS : This section contains multiple choice questions. Each question has four choices (a), (b), (c) and (d) out of which ONE OR MORE may be correct.

1. The acid in which - COOH group is (are) present
(a) ethanoic acid
(b) picric acid
(c) lactic acid
(d) palmitic acid
2. Which is not an organic compound?
(a) Wood
(b) Chalk
(c) Paper
(d) Cement
3. Which of the following is (are) property of diamond?
(a) It is the hardest substance.
(b) It has high refractive index.
(c) Like metals, it can conduct electricity.
(d) In diamond each C is at the centre of tetrahedron.
4. Which of the following is(are) property of graphite?
(a) It melts at $800^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$.
(b) It is smooth, crystalline form of carbon.
(c) It is good conductor of electricity.
(d) It forms a black sign on paper.
5. Which of the following statements is (are) correct?
(a) Graphite is more dense than diamond.
(b) Graphite is black and soft.
(c) Graphite has low melting point.
(d) Graphite feels smooth and slippery.
6. Which of the following statements regarding diamond is (are) correct ?
(a) Diamond does not refract light.
(b) When proper cut and polished, a diamond reflects light in an array of many colours.
(c) Diamond is less stable than graphite.
(d) Impure sample of diamond is often black and are used as abrasives.
7. Which of the following is (are) incorrect?
(a) Graphite is a bad conductor of electricity.
(b) Dry graphite in a vacuum is not slippery.
(c) The adsorption of substances increases the friction as layers slide pass each other.
(d) The graphite does not possess metallic properties.
8. Which of the following are combustion reactions?
(a) $\mathrm{CH}_{4}+2 \mathrm{O}_{2} \longrightarrow \mathrm{CO}_{2}+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$
(b) $\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{OH}+3 \mathrm{O}_{2} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{CO}_{2}+3 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$
(c) $\mathrm{CO}+\mathrm{O}_{2} \longrightarrow \mathrm{CO}_{2}$
(d) $2 \mathrm{CH}_{4}+\mathrm{O}_{2} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{OH}$
9. Soaps are:
(a) sodium salts of fatty acids containing 16 to 18 carbon atoms
(b) sodium salts of trihydroxy alcohols.
(c) week cleansing agent in hard water.
(d) biodegradable.
10. Which of the following is true for soaps?
(a) Soaps are bio-degradable.
(b) Soaps cannot be used in acidic solutions.
(c) Soaps form a white curdy precipitate with hard water.
(d) Soaps are relatively stronger in their cleansing action than synthetic detergents.
11. Detergents differ from soaps that
(a) their ionically charged heads are derivatives of sulfur.
(b) they can not be broken down by micro organism.
(c) detergents are human-made while soaps are produced by naturally occuring compounds.
(d) detergents have weak cleansing properties in hard water.
12. Which of the following is (are) detergents?
(a) Sodium alkyl sulphonate
(b) Sodium chloride
(c) Sodium sulphonate
(d) Sodium stearate
13. Which of the following enzymes is (are) involved in fermentation of sugar to manufacture ethanol?
(a) Invertase
(b) Zymase
(c) Amylase
(d) Proteinase

## Passage Based Questions

DIRECTIONS : Study the given paragraph(s) and answer the following questions.

## Passage - 1

A carbon atom attached to one, two, three and four other carbon atoms is called primary, secondary, tertiary and quaternary carbon respectively. Now consider following compound and answer the following questions.
<img class="imgSvg" id = "mpcazmfoqaen4n0m38f" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,"/>

1. In above compound how many carbon atom are primary?
(a) 7
(b) 5
(c) 6
(d) 4
2. In above compound how many carbon atoms are secondary?
(a) 2
(b) 1
(c) 3
(d) 0
3. In above compound which carbon atom is quaternary?
(a) $B$
(b) $D$
(c) $F$
(d) $C$

## Passage - 2

Reactions in which an atom or a group of atoms is replaced by some other atom or another group of atoms without causing any change in the structure of the remaining part of the molecule, are called substitution reactions.
All organic compounds containing double or triple bonds give addition reactions, i.e., alkenes, alkynes and aromatic hydrocarbons give addition reactions.
Reactions in which the compounds react with oxygen and form carbon dioxide and water is known as combustion reaction. This process occurs with release of great amount of heat.
4. The reaction
$\mathrm{CH}_{4}+\mathrm{Cl}_{2} \longrightarrow \mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{Cl}+\mathrm{HCl}$ is :
(a) substitution reaction
(b) addition reaction
(c) rearrangement reaction
(d) elimination reaction
5. The reaction $\mathrm{CH}_{2}=\mathrm{CH}_{2}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \longrightarrow \mathrm{CH}_{3}-\mathrm{CH}_{3}$ is :
(a) substitution reaction
(b) addition reaction
(c) rearrangement reaction
(d) elimination reaction
6. The reaction $\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{6}+\mathrm{O}_{2} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{CO}_{2}+3 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ is :
(a) substitution reaction
(b) rearrangement reaction
(c) addition reaction
(d) combustion reaction

## Assertion \& Reason

DIRECTIONS : Each of these questions contains an assertion followed by Reason. Read them carefully and answer the question on the basis of following options. You have to select the one that best describes the two statements.
(a) If both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of Assertion.
(b) If both Assertion and Reason are correct, but Reason is not the correct explanation of Assertion.
(c) If Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
(d) If Assertion is incorrect but Reason is correct.

1. Assertion : Diamond and graphite are allotropes of carbon.

Reason : Some elements can have several different structural forms in the same physical state. These differing forms are called allotropes.
2. Assertion : Carbon monoxide is extremely poisonous in nature.
Reason : Carbon monoxide is formed by complete combustion of carbon.

Column I
(A) $\mathrm{CH}_{2}=\mathrm{CH}_{2}$
(B)
<img class="imgSvg" id = "mpcazmfpthcspyjghjm" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,"/>
(C)
<img class="imgSvg" id = "mpcazmfpqdrmv5ms5go" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,"/>
(D)
<img class="imgSvg" id = "mpcazmfp96b31y25zhh" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,"/>

## Column II

(p) Saturated
(q) Unsaturated
(r) Acyclic
(s) Cyclic
3. Assertion : Carbon has ability to form long carbon chains.
Reason : Carbon has a unique property to form long straight and branched chains called catenation.
4. Assertion : All alcohols have similar chemical properties.
Reason : All alcohols contains similar hydroxy (- OH ) functional group.
5. Assertion : Hydrogenation converts an oil into a fat, called vegetable ghee.
Reason : Hydrogenation is carried out in presence of a catalyst, usually finely divided nickel.
