## More than One Correction Option

DIRECTIONS (Qs. 16-17) : This section contains multiple choice questions. Each question has four choices (a), (b), (c) and (d) out of which ONE OR MORE may be correct.
16. Which of the following is/are example(s) of substitution reactions?
(a) $\mathrm{CH}_{4}+\mathrm{I}_{2} \stackrel{\Delta}{\rightleftharpoons} \mathrm{CH}_{3}-\mathrm{I}+\mathrm{HI}$
(b) $\mathrm{CH}_{4}+\mathrm{HNO}_{2} \xrightarrow{\Delta} \mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{NO}_{2}+\mathrm{H}_{2}$
(c) $2 \mathrm{CH}_{4}+\mathrm{O}_{2} \xrightarrow{\Delta} 2 \mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{OH}$
(d) $\mathrm{CH}_{4}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4} \longrightarrow \mathrm{CH}_{3}-\mathrm{SO}_{3} \mathrm{H}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$
17. Which of the following statement(s) is/are true for following reaction?
$\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{CH}_{3}+$ fuming $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4} \longrightarrow \mathrm{X}$
(a) Given reaction is an example of sulphonation reaction.
(b) X is $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{3} \mathrm{H}$ in this reaction
(c) $\mathrm{H}_{2}$ is formed as a side product in this reaction.
(d) Mixture of $\mathrm{SO}_{3}+$ conc. $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}$ can also be used as reagent in this reaction.

## Passage Based Questions

DIRECTIONS (Qs. 18-20) : Study the given paragraph(s) and answer the following questions.

## Passage-1

## Markownikoff's rule

When an unsymmetrical alkene or alkyne reacts with unsymmetrical reagent, then negative part of reagent attach with that carbon atom which contains lesser number of hydrogen atom during the addition.
For example:
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-184.jpg?height=131&width=629&top_left_y=1599&top_left_x=251)
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-184.jpg?height=161&width=634&top_left_y=1736&top_left_x=251)
(Markownikoff's addition)
Addition of HBr (not $\mathrm{HCl}, \mathrm{HI}$ and HF ) on alkenes in presence of peroxides takes place in anti-Markownikoff's way (Peroxide effect).
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-184.jpg?height=56&width=671&top_left_y=2014&top_left_x=255)
18. Markownikoff's addition is applicable to:
(a) Symmetric alkenes
(b) Asymmetric alkenes
(c) Both (a) and (b)
(d) Neither (a) nor (b)
19. Which of the following reaction is correct as per Markownikoff's rule?
(a)
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-184.jpg?height=81&width=579&top_left_y=2270&top_left_x=264)
(b) $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CH}=\mathrm{CH}_{2}+\mathrm{HBr} \xrightarrow{\text { Peroxide }} \underset{\text { 1-Bromopropane }}{\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{Br}}$
(c) $\mathrm{CH}_{2}=\mathrm{CH}_{2}+\mathrm{HBr} \longrightarrow \mathrm{CH}_{3}-\mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{Br}$
(d) $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CH}=\mathrm{CH}_{2}+\mathrm{Br}_{2} \longrightarrow \mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CHBrCH}_{2} \mathrm{Br}$
20. Which of the following is a reagent for Markownikoff's addition?
(a) Peroxide
(b) $\mathrm{Cl}_{2}$
(c) HBr
(d) $\mathrm{Br}_{2}$

## Assertion \& Reason

DIRECTIONS (Qs. 21-22) : Each of these questions contains an assertion followed by reason. Read them carefully and answer the question on the basis of following options. You have to select the one that best describes the two statements.
(a) If both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of Assertion.
(b) If both Assertion and Reason are correct, but Reason is not the correct explanation of Assertion.
(c) If Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
(d) If Assertion is incorrect but Reason is correct.
21. Assertion : Cyclopropane is heterocyclic compound.

Reason : Cyclopropane comes into category of those compounds in which complete ring is formed by carbon atoms only.
22. Assertion : Propene reacts with HBr to give isopropyl bromide.
Reason : Addition of HBr to alkene takes place faster in presence of ionising substance.

## Matching Questions

DIRECTIONS (Q. 23) : Each question contains options given in two columns which have to be matched. Options ( $A, B, C, D$ ) in column I have to be matched with options ( $p, q, r, s$ ) in column II.
23.

| Column I |  |  | Column II |  |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| (A) | Halogenation |  | (p) | $\mathrm{SO}_{3}+$ conc. $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}$ |
| (B) | Nitration |  | (q) | $\mathrm{HI}+\mathrm{HIO}_{3}$ |
| (C) | Sulphonation |  | (r) | $\mathrm{Cl}_{2}$ + UV light |
| (D) | Iodination |  | (s) | Fuming nitric acid |
|  | A | B | C | D |
| (a) | q, r | s | p | q |
| (b) | $q, s$ | p,r | q | r |
| (c) | r | p,s | q | s |
| (d) | p,q | r, s | p | r |

(i) They have same general formula.
(ii) Each successive member differs by $\mathrm{CH}_{2}$.
(iii) They have similar chemical properties.
(iv) They have gradation in physical properties.
2. $\mathrm{H} \times \mathrm{C} \because \mathrm{C} \times \mathrm{H}\left(\right.$ Electron dot structure of ethyne $\left.-\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{2}\right) \mathrm{H}-\mathrm{C} \equiv \mathrm{C}-\mathrm{H}$ (Structural formula of ethyne)

With the result the oxygen transportation is disturbed and thus brain and body tissues do not get necessary oxygen and ultimately causes death.

$$

In the underground water and the water with pinch of calcium sulphate, the calcium ( $\mathrm{Ca}^{2+}$ ) and magnesium ( $\mathrm{Mg}^{2+}$ ) ions reacts with sodium salts present in soap, resulting in formation of a curdy precipitate or scum which in turn hinders the foam production.

## Long Answer Questions

1. Differences in the properties of diamond and graphite are due to the difference in their structures. In diamond, each C atom is linked to its neighbours by four single covalent bonds. This leads to a three-dimensional network of covalent bonds. In graphite, the carbon atoms are arranged in flat parallel layers as regular hexagons. Each carbon in these layers is bonded to three others by $s p^{2}$ hybrid orbitals. Graphite thus acquires some double bond character. Each layer is bonded to adjacent layers by weak van der Waal's forces. Moreover, one electron is free at each carbon atom. This allows each layer to slide over the other easily. Due to this type of structure graphite is soft and slippery, and can act as a lubricant. Graphite is also a good conductor of electricity due to presence of mobile electrons in it.
2. 3. Cracking

$$
\underset{\text { Octane }}{\mathrm{C}_{8} \mathrm{H}_{18}} \xrightarrow{\text { Heat }} \underset{\text { Hexane }}{\mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{14}}+\underset{\text { Ethene }}{\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{4}}
$$

2. Hydrogenation

$$
\underset{\text { Ethene }}{\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{4}}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \xrightarrow[\text { Heat }]{\mathrm{Ni}} \underset{\text { Ethane }}{\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{6}}
$$

4. (i) Ethanoic acid reacts with sodium metal to form sodium ethanoate and hydrogen gas.

$$
2 \mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOH}+2 \mathrm{Na} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COONa}+\mathrm{H}_{2}
$$

(ii) Ethanoic acid reacts with sodium carbonate to form sodium ethanoate and carbon dioxide gas.

$$
\begin{aligned}
& 2 \mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOH}+\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{CO}_{3} \longrightarrow \\
& 2 \mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COONa}+\mathrm{CO}_{2}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}
\end{aligned}
$$

(iii) Ethanoic acid reacts with sodium hydroxide to form a salt called sodium ethanoate and water.

$$
\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOH}+\mathrm{NaOH} \longrightarrow \mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COONa}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}
$$

This reaction shows that the hydrogen atom present in -COOH group is acidic in nature.
(iv) Ethanoic acid reacts with alcohols in the presence of concentrated sulphuric acid to form esters.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-188.jpg?height=110&width=663&top_left_y=1439&top_left_x=322)
5. (i) Esterification : Reaction between an alcohol and carboxylic acid in presence of $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}$ to form esters is known as esterification.
(ii) Saponification : When an oil or a fat (glyceride) is treated with sodium hydroxide solution, it gets converted to sodium salt of the acid (soap) and glycerol. The reaction is known as saponification.
(iii) Decarboxylation : When carboxylic acids are heated strongly with soda-lime, it losses $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ and the reaction is known as decarboxylation.
(iv) Polymerisation : The process by which simple molecules (called monomers) are converted into big molecules (called polymers) with or without elimination of simpler molecules like water, $\mathrm{NH}_{3}$, HCl etc., is called polymerisation.
6. (a) Three physical properties of carbon compounds are:
(i) Catenation property
(ii) Low melting and boiling point than ionic compound.
(iii) Poor conductors of electricity.
(b) Carbon is a versatile element because it has the ability to form covalent bonds with large number of elements and also catenation capacity to form compounds by chain of bonds with itself.
7. To attain the noble gas configuration, carbon can either lose or gain its valence shell electron (4-electron) and losing or gaining 4 electrons is not possible due to energy considerations to complete its octet hence it shares electrons to form covalent bonds.
Two reasons for large number of carbon compounds:
(i) Catenation : Carbon atom can combine to each other to form long straight chain, branched chain and closed rings, it shows the property of catenation to maximum extent.
(ii) Tetravalency: Carbon has 4-electron in its valence shell it is capable of bonding with four other atoms of carbon or atoms of elements like oxygen, hydrogen etc.
Carbonexhibits highercatenationproperties than any other element due to its smaller size which makes the $\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{C}$ bonds stronger as compared to other elements.
8. Carbon has 4 electrons in its outermost shell. It cannot lose 4 electrons to form $\mathrm{C}^{4+}$ because very high energy is required to remove 4 electrons.
In cannot gain 4 electrons to form $\mathrm{C}^{4-}$ ions because it is difficult for 6 protons to hold on to 10 electrons.
Ionic/electrovalent bonds and covalent bonds are formed in the ionic compounds and the compounds formed by carbon respectively
There are no charged particles in carbon compounds and hence these are poor conductors of electricity.
9. Addition reaction are those reaction in which unsaturated hydrocarbons combines with other molecule like $\mathrm{H}_{2}$ to give saturated hydrocarbons.

$$
\mathrm{CH}_{2}=\mathrm{CH}_{2}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \xrightarrow{\mathrm{Ni}} \mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{6}
$$

Application : Hydrogenation of vegetable oils.
Catalyst : It is used to increase the rate of reaction.
Substitution reaction : In this reaction, one atom is replaced by another atom

$$
\mathrm{CH}_{4}+\mathrm{Cl}_{2} \xrightarrow{\text { Sunlight }} \mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{Cl}+\mathrm{HCl}
$$

10. Hydrocarbons are those compounds which are formed mainly from carbon and hydrogen.
Formula for homologous series of alkanes, alkenes and alkynes:

|  |  | Series |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| (i) |  |  | Alkane $\quad$| $\mathrm{C}_{n} \mathrm{H}_{2 n+2}$ |
| :---: |
|  |$\quad$| H tructure |
| :---: |
| $\mathrm{H}-\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{H}$ |
| H |

(ii) Alkene $\underset{\mathrm{H}}{\mathrm{C}_{n} \mathrm{H}_{2 n}} \quad \mathrm{H}-\underset{\mathrm{H}}{\mathrm{C}}=\underset{\mathrm{H}}{\mathrm{C}}-\mathrm{H}$
(iii) Alkyne $\mathrm{C}_{n} \mathrm{H}_{2 n-2} \quad \mathrm{H}-\mathrm{C} \equiv \mathrm{C}-\mathrm{H}$

Hydrogenation converts alkenes into alkane.
$\mathrm{CH}_{2}=\mathrm{CH}_{2}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \xrightarrow{\mathrm{Pt} / \mathrm{Pd} / \mathrm{Ni}} \mathrm{CH}_{3}-\mathrm{CH}_{3}$
Alkene reacts with dihydrogen in the presence of catalysts like $\mathrm{Pt}, \mathrm{Pd}$ or Ni to given alkane.
11. Ethyl alcohol is a compound which is an active ingredient of all alcoholic drinks. Chemical formula of ethyl alcohol is $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{OH}$.
Uses of ethyl alcohol:
(a) It burns in air to give carbon dioxide and water and can be used in combination with gasoline (petrol).
(b) Ethyl alcohol is an important industrial solvent.
(i) If a small piece of sodium is dropped into ethanol, it reacts steadily to give off bubbles of hydrogen gas and leaves a colorless solution of sodium ethoxide.
$2 \mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{OH}+2 \mathrm{Na} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{ONa}+\mathrm{H}_{2}$
(ii) The original way to produce diethyl ether was by the dehydration of ethanol by conc $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}$.
$2 \mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{OH}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4} \longrightarrow \mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5}-\mathrm{O}-\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ (This occurs when the ethanol is in excess).
Ethanol with a large quantity of excess concentrated sulphuric acid on heating produces ethene.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-189.jpg?height=108&width=672&top_left_y=1201&top_left_x=386)

The concentrated acid acts only as a dehydrating agent and it is recovered unchanged , except diluted.
12. Methane is the simplest hydrocarbon, consisting of one carbon atom and four hydrogen atoms. It is the simplest member of paraffin series of hydrocarbons and is among the most potent of the green house gases. Its chemical formula is $\mathrm{CH}_{4}$.
Electron dot structure of $\mathrm{CH}_{4}$ is given below:
<img class="imgSvg" id = "mpcazmgm9l29y566dka" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,"/>

The molecule of methane has four carbon-hydrogen single covalent bonds. These covalent bonds are called nonpolar covalent bonds.
(i) These compounds are poor conductor of electricity because covalent bonds are formed by sharing of electrons. They don't have a free electron that is required for electricity transfer. Thus, they are bad conductors.
(ii) Covalent compounds generally have low melting points and boiling points because molecules are held together by weak van der Waal's forces.
Like all hydrocarbons, the combustion of methane forms water due to the presence of hydrogen but produces carbon dioxide as a result of complete combustion and carbon monoxide as a result of incomplete combustion.
$\mathrm{CH}_{4}(\mathrm{~g})+2 \mathrm{O}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \longrightarrow \mathrm{CO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{g})$
$2 \mathrm{CH}_{4}(\mathrm{~g})+3 \mathrm{O}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{CO}(\mathrm{g})+4 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{g})$
13. (a) The Difference between the soaps and detergents-

| Composition | Soaps are sodium (Na) salts or Potassium (K) salts of some long chain carboxylic acids. | Detergents are ammonium or sulphonated salts of long chains of carboxylic acids. |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Cleansing action | Produces scum in hard water which affects it's cleansing action. | Hard water does not affects it's cleansing action. |

(b) If a small piece of sodium is dropped into some ethanol, it reacts steadily to give off bubbles of hydrogen gas and leaves a colourless solution of sodium ethoxide $\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{ONa}\right)$.
$2 \mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{OH}+2 \mathrm{Na} \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{ONa}+\mathrm{H}_{2}$
Ethanol behaves as mild acid in the reaction and liberate Hydrogen gas.

## Reasoning Based Questions

1. On cracking, complex hydrocarbons decompose to give a mixture of simpler saturated and unsaturated hydrocarbons which are more useful.
2. Carbon atoms have 4 electrons in their outermost shell. So if it were to gain or lose electrons :
(i) it could gain four electrons forming $\mathrm{C}^{4-}$ anion. But it would be difficult for the nucleus with six protons to hold on to ten electrons.
(ii) it could lose four electrons forming $\mathrm{C}^{4+}$ cation. But it would require a large amount of energy to remove four electrons from its outermost shell.
Both the condition are not favourable therefore carbon cannot form ionic bonds in its compounds.
3. The atomic number of carbon is 6 , so the electronic configuration is 2,4 . Carbon atoms can achieve the inert gas configuration only by sharing of electrons, therefore carbon always form covalent bonds.
4. Diamond is extremely hard, therefore it is used for making cutting tools. Graphite is comparatively soft material.
5. Graphite is a good conductor of electricity due to the presence of free electrons therefore it is used for making dry cell electrodes. While diamond is not a good conductor of electricity.
6. Carbon form a large number of carbon compounds because:
(i) Carbon element exhibit catenation property due to which its atom can join with one another to form long carbon chain.
(ii) Carbon element is tetravalent. It can form bond with four other carbon atoms or with atoms of other monovalent elements such as $\mathrm{O}, \mathrm{S}, \mathrm{N}$.
7. In diamond, each carbon atom is bonded to four other carbon atoms forming a rigid three-dimensional structure. In graphite each carbon atom is bonded to three other carbon atoms in the same plane giving a hexagonal array. Due to the formation of three dimensional rigid structure diamond is hard.
8. Graphite has sheet like structure in which each carbon atom attached to three other carbon atoms making hexagonal arrangement. These layers of carbon atoms can slide over each other. This specific structure of graphite make it softer substance. It is the softness of graphite that makes it useful for lubricant purpose.
9. Diamond has rigid three dimensional structure. Large amount of heat is required to break the network of strong covalent bonds in the crystal. Therefore diamond has high melting point.
10. Common salt is added to precipitate out all the soap from the aqueous solution. Common salt decreases the solubility of soap in the solution due to which all the soap separates out from the solution in the form of a solid.

1. Unsaturated hydrocarbons are more reactive due to the presence of $\mathrm{C}=\mathrm{C}$ and $\mathrm{C} \equiv \mathrm{C}$ bonds. These are the reactive sites in the unsaturated hydrocarbons.
2. Carbon compounds have covalent bonds between them, these do not give ions in their solution form or in molten state. So carbon compounds do not conduct electricity through them.

<img class="imgSvg" id = "mpcazmgnwqv779wbp4r" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,"/>
i.e. it has 18 covalent bonds.
4. Covalent bond : The covalent bonds are formed by the mutual sharing of electrons between the participating atoms of same or different elements.
Covalency : The number of electrons contributed by an atom for sharing is known as its covalency.
Depending upon the number of electrons shared by each atom in bond formation, covalent bond may be single, double or triple.
A single bond is represented by a single line ( - ), a double bond by double line (=) and a triple bond by a triple line (三). Example :
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-190.jpg?height=266&width=424&top_left_y=370&top_left_x=1082)
5. Carbon has unique property of catenation as a result of which it is capable to form long straight and branched chains. Carbon forms strong bond with each other and atoms of other elements also because of its small size which enables the nucleus to hold on to the shared pairs of electrons strongly. Sulphur also shows property of catenation but only upto certain extent, sulphur forms $\mathrm{S}_{8}$ molecule.

Various methods (such as 'beating' the clothes on stones, beat it with a paddle, scrub with a brush, etc.) help to loosen the bonds between the dust or oil particles on fabrics of clothes. The process of agitating the mixture in a washing machine helps in washing the clothes.

Homologous series : A series of compounds having same functional group in which the same functional group substitutes hydrogen in a carbon chain is called a homologous series adjacent members differ by a $-\mathrm{CH}_{2}$ unit, e.g., $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{Cl}$ and $\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{Cl}$ similarly, $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{OH}, \mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{OH}$ and $\mathrm{C}_{3} \mathrm{H}_{7} \mathrm{OH}$ are also members of a homologous series.

8. The molecules of soap are sodium or potassium salts of long chain carboxylic acids. The ionic end of soap molecule dissolves in water while the carbon chain dissolves in oil.
Therefore soap molecules form structures called micelles where one end of soap molecule is towards the oil droplet while the ionic end faces towards water.
No, the micelle will not be formed when soap is added to other solvents such as ethanol. This is so because soap is soluble in ethanol (other similar solvents which are organic compounds).
9. Carbon and its compounds are used as fuels for most applications because they are inflammable and have high calorific value because they produce a large amount of heat and light. e.g.,
$\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+\frac{5}{2} \mathrm{O}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{CO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l})+$ Heat + Light
10. On adding soap to hard water, the soluble calcium and magnesium salts present in hard water react with soap to give insoluble calcium salt of soap which produces scum.
$\underset{\substack{\text { Sodium sterate } \\ \text { (Soap) }}}{2 \mathrm{C}_{17} \mathrm{H}_{35} \mathrm{COONa}}+\underset{\substack{\text { (Hard water) } \\ \text { due to } \mathrm{Ca}^{2+}}}{\mathrm{CaCl}_{2}} \longrightarrow$

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \left(\mathrm{C}_{17} \mathrm{H}_{35} \mathrm{COO}\right)_{2} \mathrm{Ca} \downarrow+2 \mathrm{NaCl} \\
& \text { Calcium sterate } \\
& \text { (Scum) }
\end{aligned}
$$

11. Soap solution will turn red litmus paper blue because soap is alkaline in nature. It has no action on blue litmus paper.

Out of $\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{6}, \mathrm{C}_{3} \mathrm{H}_{8}, \mathrm{C}_{3} \mathrm{H}_{6}, \mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{2}$ and $\mathrm{CH}_{4}$; only $\mathrm{C}_{3} \mathrm{H}_{6}$ and $\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{2}$ are unsaturated hydrocarbons and hence undergo addition reactions. Other compounds i.e., $\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{6}, \mathrm{C}_{3} \mathrm{H}_{8}$ and $\mathrm{CH}_{4}$ are saturated compounds.
14. Take butter and cooking oil sample in two separate test- tubes. Add bromine solution in both the test-tubes. Cooking oil sample decolourise bromine solution. Cooking oil consists of unsaturated hydrocarbon which decolourise bromine solution while butter consists of saturated hydrocarbons and it will not decolorise bromine solution.
15. Cleaning Action of Soap and Detergent : Both of them follow the same principle. They contain two parts, a large hydrocarbon which is hydrophobic (water repelling) and a negative charged head, which is hydrophillic (water attracting). In solution water molecules being polar in nature, surround the head and not the organic part of the molecule. When a soap or detergent is dissolved in water, the molecule gather together as clusters, called micelles. The tails stick inwards and the head outwards oily dirt will be collected in the centre of the micelle. The hydrocarbon tail attaches itself to oily dirt. When water is agitated, the oily dirt tends to lift off from the dirty surface and dissociates into fragments. The solution now contains small globules of oil surrounded by detergent molecules. The negatively charged heads present in water prevent the small globules from coming together and form aggregates. Thus the oily dirt is removed from the object.

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-194.jpg?height=323&width=709&top_left_y=1763&top_left_x=233)
Micelle formed by detergent molecules in water. The hydrocarbon tail sticks to oily dirt

(b) Alcohol is ethanol

2. Carbon exhibits catenation much more than silicon or any other element due to its smaller size which makes
the $\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{C}$ bonds strong, while the $\mathrm{Si}-\mathrm{Si}$ bonds are comparatively weaker due to large size of silicon.

## Think out of the box

## Case Study

1. Temporary hardness: It is a type of water hardness caused by the presence of dissolved bicarbonate salts of calcium and magnesium. Temporary hardness can be removed by boiling. When temporary hard water is boiled, the bicarbonates of $\mathrm{Ca} \& \mathrm{Mg}$ undergo decomposition to form insoluble carbonates.
The insoluble carbonates of $\mathrm{Ca} \& \mathrm{Mg}$ can be removed by filtration and the water thus obtained is free from $\mathrm{Ca}^{2+} \& \mathrm{Mg}^{2+}$ ions and becomes soft.
Permanent hardness: This type of water hardness is due to the presence of Ca or Mg sulphates or chlorides. Permanent hardness cannot be removed by boiling. It can be removed by using water softeners.
2. $\mathrm{C}_{17} \mathrm{H}_{35} \mathrm{COO}^{-} \mathrm{Na}^{+}(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{Ca}^{2+} \longrightarrow$

Soap

$$
\left(\mathrm{C}_{17} \mathrm{H}_{35} \mathrm{COO}\right)_{2} \mathrm{Ca}+2 \mathrm{Na}^{+}
$$

Calcium salt (scum)
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-197.jpg?height=165&width=734&top_left_y=2218&top_left_x=1151)
3. White material get deposited in the interior of boilers and electric kettles due to the precipitation of $\mathrm{CaCO}_{3}$ and $\mathrm{CaSO}_{4}$. The precipitation of $\mathrm{CaCO}_{3}$ is also caused due to thermal decomposition of bicarbonate ions.

## Periodic Classification of Elements

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-198.jpg?height=398&width=447&top_left_y=386&top_left_x=1370)
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-198.jpg?height=1740&width=1627&top_left_y=806&top_left_x=189)

Elements are the basic units of all types of matter. Earlier it was easy to study and remember properties of the elements individualy, as few elements were known, but later it become difficult due to the discovery of more number of elements. Thus, we needed a systematic way to organize our knowledge by classifying the elements. So that we can identify the physical and chemical properties of elements, and prediction of new elements can also be done. There were various attempts made to classify the elements, let's study them in detail.

## EARLY ATTEMPTS FOR THE CLASSIFICATION OF ELEMENTS

Elements like gold, tin, copper, lead, silver have been known since ancient times ; but the first element discovered through scientific inquiry by Hennig Brand was phosphorous (P) in 1649. By 1865, 63 elements had been discovered.

## Dobereiner's Triads

Between 1817-1829, Johann Dobereiner began to group elements with similar properties into groups of three elements called triads.
This began in 1817 when Johann Dobereiner noticed that the atomic weights of strontium, Sr , was halfway between the weights of calcium and barium. These elements possessed similar chemical properties. By 1829, he had discovered a halogen triad made up of chlorine, bromine and iodine and an alkali metal triad of lithium, sodium and potassium.
He postulated that nature contained triads of elements in which the middle element had properties that was an average of the other two elements.
In a triad, mean of the atomic masses of the first and the third elements was roughly equal to the atomic mass of the middle element. Observe the groups in the given table in which elements are arranged downwards in increasing order of atomic masses.

| A |  | B |  | C |  |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Element | Atomic mass | Element | Atomic mass | Element | Atomic mass |
| Lithium, Li | 7 | Calcium, Ca | 40 | Clorine, Cl | 35.5 |
| Sodium, Na | 23 | Strontium, Sr | 88 | Bromine, Br | 80 |
| Potassium, K | 39 | Barium, Ba | 137 | Iodine, I | 127 |

Mean of the atomic masses of the first and third element in triad $(\mathrm{A})=(7+39) / 2=23 \mathrm{u}$
Mean of the atomic masses of the first and third elements in triad $(\mathrm{B})=(40+137) / 2=88.5 \mathrm{u}$
Mean of the first atomic masses of the first and third elements in triad $(\mathrm{C})=(35.5+127) / 2=81.25 \mathrm{u}$
Actual atomic mass of the second element in triad $(\mathrm{A})=23 \mathrm{u}$
Actual atomic mass of the second element in triad (B) $=88 \mathrm{u}$
Actual atomic mass of the second element in triad (C) $=80 \mathrm{u}$
Dobereiner's idea of classification of elements into triads did not receive wide acceptance as he could arrange only a few elements in this manner.
Later, other scientists found other triads and recognized that elements could be grouped into set larger than three. The poor accuracy of measurements such as that of atomic weights hindered grouping of further elements.

## Learn More

In 1862, A.E. Beguyer de Chancourtois was the first person to make use of atomic weights to reveal that the elements were arranged according to their atomic weights with similar elements occurring at regular intervals. He drew the elements as a continuous spiral around a cylinder divided into 16 parts. A list of elements was wrapped around a cylinder so that several sets of similar elements lined up, creating the first geometric representation of the periodic law.

## - Who discovered first element and which element it was?

## SOLUTION

Phosphorus was the first element discovered by Hennig Brand.

## NEWLAND'S LAW OF OCTAVES

In 1865 John Alexander Newland, an English chemist noticed that when elements are arranged in the increasing order of their atomic masses, every eighth element had properties similar to the first element. Newland called it the Law of Octaves.

Look carefully at the Newland's arrangement of elements shown below:

| Element (At. wt) | Li (6.9) | Be (9) | B (10.8) | C (12) | N (14) | O (16) | F (19) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Element (At. wt) | Na (23) | Mg (24.3) | Al (27) | Si (28.1) | P (31) | S (32.1) | Cl (35.5) |
| Element (At. wt) | K (39.1) | Ca (40.1) |  |  |  |  |  |

With the help of the arrangement given above, you can see that starting from lithium, eighth element is the sodium and starting from sodium, it is potassium. Properties of all three ( $\mathrm{Li}, \mathrm{Na}$ and K) are similar. Similarly, aluminium is the eighth element from boron it shows properties similar to it.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-200.jpg?height=197&width=231&top_left_y=813&top_left_x=185)
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-200.jpg?height=232&width=1001&top_left_y=822&top_left_x=386)

However, Newland could arrange elements in this manner only up to calcium out of a total of over sixty elements known at that time. Because of this shortcoming, his work did not receive attention by the scientific community. The next breakthrough in classification of elements came in the form of Mendeleev's work.

## MENDELEEV'S LAW

Dmitri Ivanovich Mendeleev, a Russian chemist, arranged the elements on the basis of their fundamental property, the atomic mass, and gave a periodic law.
The chemical and physical properties of elements are periodic function of their atomic masses. A periodic function is the one which repeats itself after a certain interval. Thus, according to the periodic law (given by Mendeleev) the chemical and physical properties of elements repeat themselves after certain intervals when they are arranged in the increasing order of their atomic mass.
A tabular arrangement of the elements based on the periodic law is called periodic table. Mendeleev believed that atomic mass of elements was the most fundamental property and arranged elements in increasing order of atomic mass in horizontal rows till he encountered an element which had properties similar to the first element. He placed this element below the first element and thus started the second row of elements. Proceeding in this manner he could arrange all the known elements according to their properties and thus created the first periodic table.

## Dmitri Mendeleev (1834-1907) Periodic Table

Mendeleev predicted the existence and properties of unknown elements which he called eka? aluminum, eka-boron, and eka-silicon. The elements gallium, scandium and germanium were found later to fit his predictions quite well.

## Main Features of Mendeleev's Periodic Table

In Mendeleev's periodic table elements are arranged in tabular form in rows and columns. It has been modified from time to time. Now let us learn more about these rows and columns and the elements present in them.
a. The horizontal rows present in the periodic table are called periods. Originally, there were six periods and Mendeleev called them series.
b. Properties of elements in a particular period show regular gradation (i.e. increase or decrease) from left to right.
c. The vertical columns present in the periodic table are called groups. You must have noticed that these are nine in number and are numbered from I to VIII and Zero (Roman numerals).
d. Groups I to VII are subdivided into A and B subgroups. Groups zero and VIII don't have any subgroup.

Element placed on the right hand side in a column form sub group-B, while those placed on the left hand side form subgroup-A.
e. All the elements in a particular group are chemically similar in nature. They show regular gradation in their physical properties and chemical reactivities.
After learning about the main features we shall now learn about the main merits of Mendeleev's periodic table.

## Merits of Mendeleev's Periodic Classification

a. Classification of all elements : Mendeleev provide the first classification which successfully included all the elements.
b. Prediction of new elements: Mendeleev's periodic table had some blank spaces in it. These vacant spaces were for elements that were yet to be discovered. For example, he proposed the existence of an unknown element that he called eka aluminium. The element gallium was discovered four years later and its properties matched very closely with the predicted properties of eka aluminium.
c. Systematic study of all elements: Due to proper classification Mendeleev periodic table predicted properties of other elements, if one element is known. elements, if one element is known.

Time to
Check Your Knowledge

- Why Mendeleev left some places empty in his periodic table?


## SOLUTION

For the elements which were not discovered at that time.

## Defects in Mendeleev's Periodic Classification

In spite of being a historic achievement, Mendeleev's periodic table had some defects in it. The following were the main defects in it:
a. Position of hydrogen : Hydrogen resembles alkali metals (forms $\mathrm{H}^{+}$ion just like $\mathrm{Na}^{+}$ions) as well as halogens (forms $\mathrm{H}^{-}$ion similar to $\mathrm{Cl}^{-}$ion). Therefore, it could neither be placed comfortably with alkali metals (group I) nor with halogens (group VII).
b. Position of isotopes : Different isotopes of same elements have different atomic masses, therefore, each one of them should be given a different position in the periodic table. On the other hand, because they are chemically similar, they had to be given same position.
c. Anomalous pairs of elements : At certain places, an element of higher atomic mass has been placed before an element of lower atomic mass. For example, argon (39.91) is placed before potassium (39.1).
d. Position of element of group VIII: No proper place has been allotted to nine elements of group VIII which have been arranged in three triads without any justification.

## MODERN CLASSIFICATION

Henry Moseley, an English physicist discovered in the year 1913 that atomic number, is the most fundamental property of an element. Atomic number (Z), of an element is the number of protons in the nucleus of its atom. The number of electrons in the neutral atom is also equal to its atomic number. This discovery changed the whole perspective about elements and their properties to such an extent that a need was felt to change the periodic law also.

Moseley's Law describes the relationship between atomic number and wavelength of a spectral line : $\lambda=\frac{\mathrm{K}}{(\mathrm{Z}-\sigma)^{2}}$, where K and $\sigma$ are constants for a given spectral line. Moseley's determination of this relationship provided a simple test of the order of the elements according to Z . It showed where elements were missing from the periodic table and led to the discovery of some of these elements. For example, hafnium (Hf), which is chemically almost identical to zirconium ( Zr ), was identified by D.Coster and G. von Hevesy in 1923 from its X-ray spectrum.

## MODERN PERIODIC LAW

After discovery of atomic number the periodic law was modified and the new law was based upon atomic numbers in place of atomic masses of elements. After the change in the periodic law many changes were suggested in the periodic table. When the elements are arranged in the increasing order of their atomic number, most of the defects of Mendeleev classification get rectified.

Modern periodic law states that 'the properties of the elements are periodic functions of their atomic numbers.'
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-202.jpg?height=92&width=86&top_left_y=781&top_left_x=968)
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-202.jpg?height=56&width=483&top_left_y=847&top_left_x=196)
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-202.jpg?height=43&width=81&top_left_y=858&top_left_x=975)

## More


#### Abstract

Henry Moseley discovered that the wavelength (energy) of an X-ray depend upon the nuclear charge of an atom. In 1913, working at the University of Manchester, he photographed the X-ray spectrum of 10 elements that occupied consecutive places in the periodic table. He concluded that there was 'a fundamental quantity which increases by regular steps as we pass from one element to the next.' In 1920, Rutherford at Cambridge identified this quantity as the atomic number.

\section*{Moseley's Law}

Henry Moseley discovered that the wavelength (energy) of an X-ray depend upon the nuclear charge of an atom. In 1913, working at the University of Manchester, he photographed the X-ray spectrum of 10 elements that occupied consecutive places in the periodic table. He concluded that there was 'a fundamental quantity which increases by regular steps as quantity as the atomic number.


Moseley's Law describes the relationship between atomic number and wavelength of a spectral line : $\lambda=\frac{\mathrm{K}}{(\mathrm{Z}-\sigma)^{2}}$, where K and $\sigma$ are constants for a given spectral line. Moseley's determination of this relationship provided a simple test of the order of the elements according to Z . It showed where elements were missing from the periodic table and led to the discovery of some of these elements. For example, hafnium (Hf), which
is chemically almost identical to zirconium (Zr), was identified by D.Coster and G. von Hevesy in 1923 from its X-ray spectrum.

- Modern periodic law is based on which property of elements?


## SOLUTION

It is based on atomic number of elements.

The modern periodic table is also known as the long form of the periodic table or the extended form of the periodic table．

## MODERN PERIODIC TABLE

## Main Features of the Periodic Table

## Groups:

There are 18 vertical columns in the modern periodic table. Each column is called a group. These groups have been numbered from 1 to 18 (in Arabic numerals) from left to right. Group 1at extreme left position contains alkali metals (Li, Na, K, Rb, Cs and Fr) and group 18 at extreme right side position contains noble gases ( $\mathrm{He}, \mathrm{Ne}, \mathrm{Ar}, \mathrm{Kr}, \mathrm{Xe}$ and Rn ).
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-204.jpg?height=195&width=238&top_left_y=699&top_left_x=187)
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-204.jpg?height=192&width=119&top_left_y=715&top_left_x=402)

All elements present in a group have similar electronic configuration and have same number of valence electrons.
| Group 1 |  | Group 17 |  |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Element | Electronic configuration | Element | Electronic configuration |
| Li $(z=3)$ | 2, 1 | $F(z=9)$ | 2, 7 |
| Na ( $z=11$ ) | 2, 8, 1 | Cl ( $z=17$ ) | 2, 8, 7 |
| $K(z=19)$ | 2, 8, 8, 1 | Br $(z=35)$ | 2, 8, 18, 7 |
| $\boldsymbol{R} \boldsymbol{b} \boldsymbol{(} \boldsymbol{z}=\mathbf{3 7} \boldsymbol{)}$ | 2, 8, 18, 8, 1 | $I(z=53)$ | 2, 8, 18, 18, 7 |


Elements present in groups 1 and 2 on left side and groups 13 to 17 on the right side of the periodic table are called representative elements. Their outermost shells are incomplete. They are also called typical or main group elements. Elements present in groups 3 to 12 in the middle of the periodic table are called transition elements. However, it should be noted here that more number of electrons are added to valence shell only in case of main group elements. In transition elements, the electrons are added to incomplete inner shells.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-204.jpg?height=118&width=152&top_left_y=1356&top_left_x=187)

Group 12 elements ( $\mathbf{Z n}, \mathbf{C d}, \mathbf{H g}$ ) are not regarded as transition metals because according to modern IUPAC definition "an element whose atom has an incomplete $d$ sub-shell, or which can give rise to cations with an incomplete $d$ sub-shell, are considered as transition elements. Elements of group 12 do not satisfy this condition because their d sub-shells are complete to their capacity, however they resemble transition metals in their properties.

Group 18 on extreme right side of the periodic table contains noble gases. Their outermost shells are completely filled therefore they are inert.
Inner transition elements: 14 elements with atomic numbers 58 to $71(\mathrm{Ce}$ to Lu$)$ are called lanthanides and they are placed along with the element lanthanum (La), atomic number 57 in the same position (group 3 in period 6) because of very close resemblance between them. However, for, the sake of convenience they are shown separately below the main periodic table. 14 elements with atomic numbers 90 to103 (Th to Lr) are called actinides and they are placed along with the element actinium (Ac), atomic number 89 in the same position (group 3 in period 7) because of very close resemblance between them. They are shown separately below the main periodic table like lanthanides.

## Periods :

There are seven rows in the periodic table. Each row is called a period. The periods have been numbered from 1 to 7 (Arabic numerals). In each period, a new shell starts filling up. The period number is also the number of shell which starts filling up in it. For example, in elements of 3rd period, the third shell (M shell) starts filling up as we move from left to right. The first element of this period, sodium, $\mathrm{Na}(2,8,1)$ has only one electron in its valence shell (third shell), while the last element of this period, argon, $\operatorname{Ar}(2,8,8)$ has eight electrons in its valence shell. The gradual filling of the third shell can be seen below.

| Element | Na | Mg | Al | Si | P | S | Cl | Ar |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Electronic configuration | $2,8,1$ | $2,8,2$ | $2,8,3$ | $2,8,4$ | $2,8,5$ | $2,8,6$ | $2,8,7$ | $2,8,8$ |

The first period is the shortest period of all and contains only 2 elements, H and He .
The second and third periods are called short periods and contain 8 elements each.
Fourth and fifth periods are long periods and contain 18 elements each.
Sixth and seventh periods are very long periods containing 32 elements each, including elements up to atomic number 118. Elements of atomic numbers 114, 116 and 118 have been reported only recently.
The period of an element signifies the highest energy level of an electron in that element, occupies in an unexcited state.

## CONNECTING TOPIC

## CLASSIFICATION OF ELEMENTS

## Classification Based on Properties

(i) Elements classified as metalloids :

The 7 elements classified as metalloids are located in groups 13, 14, 15, 16 and 17 elements of the periodic table.
Elements classified as metalloids have properties of both metals and non-metals. Some are semi-conductors and can carry an electrical charge making them useful for electronic appliances like calculators and computers.
The metalloids in the periodic table are : Silicon, Germanium, Arsenic, Antimony, Tellurium, Polonium and Astatine.
(ii) Elements classified as alkali metals :

The 6 elements classified as alkali metals are located in group 1 of the periodic table. Elements classified as alkali metals are very reactive metals that do not occur freely in nature. Alkali metals are soft, malleable, ductile, and are good conductors of heat and electricity. The alkali metals are: Lithium, Sodium, Potassium, Rubidium, Cesium, Francium
(iii) Elements classified as alkaline earth metals :

The 6 elements classified as alkaline earth metals are located in group 2 of the periodic table. Elements classified as alkaline earth metals are all found in the Earth's crust, but not in the elemental form as they are very reactive (Inspite of being so reactive their reactivity is less than alkali metals). Instead, they are widely distributed in rock structures. The alkaline earth metals in the periodic table are : Beryllium, Magnesium, Calcium, Strontium, Barium and Radium.

Did You ${ }^{\text {® }}$ ?
Elements of group 1 and group 2 are called alkali and alkaline earth metals respectively. As they all are basic in nature and form alkaline oxides and hydroxides.
(iv) Elements classified as transition metals :

The elements classified as transition metals are located in groups 3-12 of the periodic table. Elements classified as transition metals are ductile, malleable, and conduct electricity and heat. The transition metals in the periodic table are:
Scandium, Titanium, Vanadium, Chromium, Manganese, Iron, Cobalt, Nickel, Copper, Zinc, Yttrium, Zirconium, Platinum, Gold, Mercury, Rutherfordium, Dubnium, Seaborgium, Bohrium, Hassium, Meitnerium, Roentgenium, Niobium, Iridium, Darmstadtium, Molybdenum, Technetium, Ruthenium, Rhodium, Palladium, Silver, Cadmium, Hafnium, Tantalum, Tungsten, Rhenium and Osmium copernicium.
They are called transition elements because their position in the periodic table is between the $s$-block and $p$-block elements. Their properties are transitional between highly reactive metallic elements of the $s$-block which typically form ionic compounds, and the elements of the $p$-block which mostly forms covalent compounds.
(v) Elements classified as other metals :

The elements classified as 'other metals' are located in groups 13, 14, and 15 of the periodic table. All of these elements are solid, have a relatively high density and are opaque. The other metals of the periodic table are: Aluminum, Gallium, Indium, Tin, Thallium, Lead and Bismuth
(vi) Elements classified as non-metals :

The elements classified as non-metals are located in groups 14,15 and 16, 17 of the periodic table. Non-metals do not easily conduct electricity or heat and do not reflect light. Non-metallic elements are very brittle, and cannot be rolled into wires or pounded into sheets. Non-metallic elements exist, at room temperature, in two of the three states of matter; gases (such as oxygen) and solids (such as carbon). The non-metal elements in the periodic table are : Carbon, Nitrogen, Oxygen, Phosphorus, Sulfur, Selenium etc.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-205.jpg?height=152&width=275&top_left_y=2103&top_left_x=404)

Phosphorus does not conduct electricity whereas lithium does. Why ?

## SOLUTION

Phosphorus is present in group 15. Thus, it is a non-metal which is a poor conductor of electricity. Lithium, on the other hand, is an alkali metal which is a good conductor of electricity.
(vii) Elements classified as halogens :

The elements classified as halogens are located in group 17 of the periodic table. Term halogen is derived from Greek words, Hals $=$ Sea salt, gen $=$ producer i.e. sea salt producer
The term 'halogen' means 'salt-former' and compounds containing halogens are called 'salts'. The halogen elements in the periodic table are: Fluorine, Chlorine, Bromine, Iodine and Astatine.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-206.jpg?height=196&width=1552&top_left_y=561&top_left_x=260)
(viii) Elements classified as noble gases :

The elements classified as noble gases are located in group 18 of the periodic table. Noble gases in the periodic table are: Helium, Neon, Argon, Krypton, Xenon and Radon.
These gases are said to be noble or inert because they have completely filled outermost shell. Thus they are chemically inert.

- Neon and fluorine both are gases in which fluorine is extremely reactive whereas neon is completely non reactive?


## SOLUTION

Fluorine gas belongs to halogen family which is one electron short in outermost shell. Thus it has tendency to attain completely filled shell. Thus it is highly reactive, whereas neon has completely filled outermost shell, thus it is inert in nature.
(ix) Elements classified as rare earth elements :

The elements classified as 'rare earth elements' are located in group 3 of the periodic table and in the 6th and 7th periods. The rare earth elements consists of the lanthanide and actinide series. Most of the elements in the actinide series are synthetic or man-made. The lanthanide and actinide series of rare earth elements in the periodic table are:
Lanthanide elements : Lanthanum, Cerium, Praseodymium Neodymium, Promethium, Samarium, Europium, Gadolinium, Terbium, Dysprosium, Holmium, Erbium, Thulium, Ytterbium, Lutetium
Actinide elements: Actinium, Thorium, Protactinium, Uranium, Neptunium, Plutonium, Americium, Curium, Berkelium, Californium, Einsteinium, Fermium, Mendelevium, Nobelium, Lawrencium

## Classification Based on Differentiating Electron

This classification divides the elements into four types. i.e., $s-, p-, d$ - and $f$-block elements depending on the type of the atomic shell into which the last electron enters.

The last electron is called differentiating electron.
(i) $\boldsymbol{s}$-Block elements : Those elements of the periodic table in which the last electron enters in $s$-orbital, are called $s$-block elements.
$s$-orbital can accommodate a maximum of two electrons. Their general formula is $n s^{1-2}$ where $n=(1$ to 7$)$.
I A Group elements are known as alkali metals because they react with water to form alkali. II A Group elements are known as alkaline earth metals because their oxides react with water to form alkali and these are found in the soil or earth. $s$-block element are soft and have low melting and boiling points. They mostly form ionic compounds. The total number of $s$-block elements are 14. ${ }^{87} \mathrm{Fr}$ and ${ }^{88} \mathrm{Ra}$ are radioactive elements, H and He are gaseous elements. Cs and Fr are liquid elements belonging to $s$-block.
(ii) $\boldsymbol{p}$-Block elements : Those elements of the periodic table in which the last electron gets filled up in the $\boldsymbol{p}$-orbital, are called $p$-block elements. A $p$-orbital can accommodate a maximum of six electrons. Therefore, $p$-block elements are divided into six groups which are III A, IV A, V A, VI A, VII A and VIII A groups. They include both metals and non-metals. They form mostly covalent compounds. The general formula of $p$-block elements is $n s^{2} n p^{1-6}$ (where $n=2$ to 6 ).
The VIII A group elements having general formula $n s^{2} n p^{6}$ are inert, because their energy levels are fully filled. The total number of $p$-block elements in the periodic table is 30 (excluding He). There are nine gaseous elements $(\mathrm{Ne}, \mathrm{Ar}, \mathrm{Kr}, \mathrm{Xe}, \mathrm{Rn}, \mathrm{F}, \mathrm{Cl}, \mathrm{O}$ and N$)$ belonging to $p$-block. Gallium ( Ga ) and bromine ( Br ) are liquids.
(iii) d-Block elements : Those elements of the periodic table in which the last electron gets filled up in the $d$-orbital, called $d$-block elements. The $d$-block elements are placed in groups named IIIB, IV B, V B, VI B, VII B, VIII, I B and II B. In $d$-block elements the electron gets filled up in the $d$ orbital of the penultimate shell. Though the total number of $d$-block elements is 39 in the periodic table but there are only 36 transition elements. Because only those elements are transition elements in which $d$ orbital is partly filled. The general formula of these elements is $(n-1) d^{1-10} n s^{1-2}$ where $n=4$ to 7 . They are metals having high melting and boiling points. Most of them form coloured compounds and exhibit several oxidation states
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-207.jpg?height=199&width=1553&top_left_y=644&top_left_x=328)
(iv) $\boldsymbol{f}$-Block elements : Those elements of the periodic table in which the last electron gets filled up in the $f$-orbital, called $f$-block elements. There are $28 f$-block elements in the periodic table. The elements from atomic number 58 to 71 are called lanthanides because they come after lanthanum (57). The elements from 90 to 103 are called actinides because they come after actinium (89). They are heavy metals with high melting and boiling points. They form coloured ions. All the actinide elements are radioactive. All the elements after atomic number 92 (i.e. ${ }^{92} \mathrm{U}$ ) are transuranic elements. The elements which do not occur in nature and are produced in the laboratories artificially are called transuranic or synthetic elements. The general formula of these elements is $(n-2) f^{(1-14)}(n-1) d^{0-1} n s^{2}$ where $n=6 \& 7$
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-207.jpg?height=195&width=1553&top_left_y=1119&top_left_x=328)
(Ev)
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-207.jpg?height=163&width=273&top_left_y=1311&top_left_x=406)

- Write general electronic configuration of $\boldsymbol{d}$-block elements.


## SOLUTION

$(n-1) d^{1-10} n \mathrm{~s}^{1-2}$, where $n=4$ to 7

## Merits of Modern Periodic Table Over Mendeleev's Periodic Table

The modern periodic table is based on atomic number which is more fundamental property of an atom than atomic mass. The long form of modern periodic table is therefore free of following defects of Mendeleev's periodic table.
a. Position of isotopes : All isotopes of an element have same atomic number but different atomic masses. Therefore, they occupy the same position in the modern periodic table which they should have because all of them are chemically similar.
b. Anomalous pairs of elements : When elements are arranged in the periodic table according to their atomic numbers, the anomaly regarding certain pairs of elements in Mendeleev's periodic table disappears. For example, atomic numbers of argon and potassium are 18 and 19 respectively. Therefore, argon with smaller atomic number comes before potassium although its atomic mass is greater.
c. It explains the periodicity of the properties of the elements and relates them to their electronic configurations.
d. The table is simple and easy way for remembering the properties of various elements and moreover lanthanides and actinides are placed separately.

## CONNECTING TOPIC

## NOMENCLATURE OF NEWLY DISCOVERED ELEMENTS IN THE MODERN PERIODIC TABLE

According to IUPAC, until the name of newly discovered element is officially recognized, a systematic nomenclature is derived directly from the atomic number of the element using the numerical roots for 0 and numbers 1-9.
To derive the name, roots are arranged in order of the digits of atomic number and 'ium' is added at the end. To derive the symbol, abbreviations are arranged in order of the digits of atomic number.

| Digit | Root | Abbreviation |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 0 | nil | n |
| 1 | un | u |
| 2 | bi | b |
| 3 | tri | t |
| 4 | quad | q |
| 5 | pent | p |
| 6 | hex | h |
| 7 | sept | s |
| 8 | oct | o |
| 9 | enn | e |


| Atomic Number | Name | Symbol |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 101 | Unnilunium | Unu |
| 103 | Unniltrium | Unt |
| 105 | Unnilpentium | Unp |
| 107 | Unnilseptium | Uns |
| 109 | Unnilennium | Une |
| 110 | Ununnillium | Uun |
| 112 | Ununbium | Uub |
| 114 | Ununquadium | Uuq |
| 116 | Ununhexium | Unh |
| 118 | Ununoctium | Uno |

These naming are used until the official name is decided by IUPAC

A new element is discovered with atomic number 125. What will be its nomenclature?

## SOLUTION

Unbipentiu

## TRENDS IN THE MODERN PERIODIC TABLE

In a period, the number of valence electrons and the nuclear charge increases from left to right. It increases the force of attraction between valence electrons and the nucleus of the atom. In a group, the number of filled shells increases and valence electrons are present in higher shells. This decreases the force of attraction between valence electrons and the nucleus of the atom. These changes affect various properties of elements, they also show gradual variation in a group and a period which repeat themselves after a certain interval of atomic number. Such properties are called periodic properties.

## 1. Valency

The combining capacity of an atom or radical is known as its valency. It is measured in terms of hydrogen, chlorine and oxygen atom. Valency of an element is defined as the number of hydrogen, chlorine and double the number of oxygen atom with which atom of an element can combine, e.g.

Valency of oxygen in water $\left(\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}\right)$ is 2 .
Valency of carbon in methane ( $\left.\mathrm{CH}_{4}\right)$ is 4 .
(a) Valency in a period : We have already learnt in the previous section that the number of valence electrons increases in a period. In normal elements, it increases from 1 to 8 in a period from left to right. It reaches 8 in group 18 elements (noble gases) which show practically no chemical activity under ordinary conditions and their valency is taken as zero. Carefully look at the table given below. Valency of normal elements with respect to oxygen increases from 1 to 7 as shown below
for elements of third period. This valency is equal to the number of valence electrons or group number for groups 1 and 2 , or (group number - 10 ) for groups 13 to 17.

| Group | 1 | 2 | 13 | 14 | 15 | 16 | 17 |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Elements in 3rd period | Na | Mg | Al | Si | P | S | Cl |
| No. of valence electrons | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 | 7 |
| Valency with respect to oxygen | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 | 7 |
| Formula of oxide | $\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ | MgO | $\mathrm{Al}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$ | $\mathrm{SiO}_{2}$ | $\mathrm{P}_{4} \mathrm{O}_{10}$ | $\mathrm{SO}_{3}$ | $\mathrm{Cl}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{7}$ |

In the following table for elements of second period you will observe that valency of elements with respect to hydrogen and chlorine increase from 1 to 4 and then decrease to 1 again.

| Group | 1 | 2 | 13 | 14 | 15 | 16 | 17 |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Element | Li | Be | B | C | N | O | F |
| No. of valence electrons | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 | 7 |
| Valency with respect to <br> hydrogen and chlorine | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 3 | 2 | 1 |
| Formula of hydride | LiH | $\mathrm{BeH}_{2}$ | $\mathrm{BH}_{3}$ | $\mathrm{CH}_{4}$ | $\mathrm{NH}_{3}$ | $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ | HF |
| Formula of chloride | LiCl | $\mathrm{BeCl}_{2}$ | $\mathrm{BCl}_{3}$ | $\mathrm{CCl}_{4}$ | $\mathrm{NCl}_{3}$ | $\mathrm{Cl}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ | ClF |

(b) Valency in a group : All the elements of a group have the same number of valence electrons. Therefore, they all have the same valency. Thus valency of all elements of group 1, alkali metals, is 1 . Similarly, valency of all elements of group 17, halogens, is 1 with respect to hydrogen and 7 with respect to oxygen.

| Group 1 elements | Formulae of oxides | Valency | Formulae of Hydrides | Valency |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Li | $\mathrm{Li}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ | 1 | LiH | 1 |
| Na | $\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ | 1 | NaH | 1 |
| K | $\mathrm{~K}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ | 1 | KH | 1 |
| Rb | $\mathrm{Rb}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ | 1 | RbH | 1 |
| Cs | $\mathrm{Cs}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ | 1 |  |  |

1. Valency remains constant for elements in group or in period ?
2. Observe the following periodic table

| H |  |  |  |  |  |  | He 2 |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Li | Be | B | C | Y | O | F | Ne |
| 2,1 | 2, 2 | 2,3 | 2, 4 | 2, 5 | 2, 6 | 2,7 | 2, 8 |
| Na | Mg | Al | Z | P | S | Cl | Ar |
| 2, 8, 1 | 2,8,2 | 2,8,3 | 2, 8, 4 | 2,8,5 | 2, 8, 6 | 2, 8, 7 | 2,8,8 |
| K | X |  |  |  |  |  |  |
| 2,8,8,1 | 2,8,8,2 |  |  |  |  |  |  |

Arrange the elements $\mathbf{X}, \mathbf{Y}, \mathbf{Z}$ in increasing order of their valencies :

## SOLUTION

1. Valency remains constant for elements in group.
2. $\mathbf{Z}($ valency 4$)>\mathbf{Y}($ valency 3$)>\mathbf{X}($ valency 2$)$

## 2. Atomic Radii

A number of physical properties like density, melting and boiling points are related to the size of atoms. Atomic radius determines the size of an atom. For an isolated atom atomic radius may be taken as the distance between the centre of nucleus of atom and the outermost shell of electrons. Practically, measurement of size of an isolated atom is difficult; therefore, it is measured when an atom is in company of another atom of same element. It is defined as one-half the distance between the nuclei of two atoms when they are linked to each other by a single covalent bond.

## Variation of atomic radii in a period :

Atomic radii (in picometer) of 2nd and 3rd period elements are given in the following table. In a period, atomic radius generally decreases from left to right with increase in atomic number.

| $\mathbf{2}^{\text {nd }}$ period | Li | Be | B | C | N | O | F |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Atomic Radius | 152 | 111 | 88 | 77 | 74 | 66 | 42 |
| $\mathbf{3}^{\text {rd }}$ period | Na | Mg | Al | Si | P | S | Cl |
| Atomic Radius | 190 | 145 | 118 | 111 | 98 | 88 | 79 |

In a period, there is a gradual increase in the nuclear charge with increase in atomic number. Since valence electrons are added in the same shell and the electrons in the same shell do not screen each other from the nucleus, the increase in nuclear charge is not neutralised by the extra valence electron. As a result, effective nuclear charge increases, therefore, valence electrons are more and more strongly attracted towards nucleus. This gradually decreases atomic radii.

## Variation of atomic radii in a group :

Atomic radii increases in a group from top to bottom. This can be seen from the following data for group 1 and 17 elements.

| Element (group 1) | Atomic radius (pm) | Element (group 17) | Atomic radius (pm) |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Li | 155 | F | 72 |
| Na | 190 | Cl | 99 |
| K | 235 | Br | 114 |
| Rb | 248 | I | 133 |

On moving down the group, the nuclear charge increases with increase in atomic number. However, while going down in a group from one atom to another the number of inner shells also increases, although the number of electrons in the outermost shell remains the same. The effect of increase in the size of the electron cloud (due to increase in number of shells) is more pronounced than the effect of increased nuclear charge. Thus, the distance of outermost electron from the nucleus increases as we move down a group. For example, in lithium the valence electron is present in 2nd shell while in sodium it is present in 3rd shell. Also, the number of filled shells between valence electrons and nucleus increases. Thus in group $1 \mathrm{Li}(2,1)$ has one filled shell between its nucleus and valence electron while $\mathrm{Na}(2,8,1)$ has two filled shells between them. Both the factors decrease the force of attraction between nucleus and valence electron. Therefore, atomic size increases on moving down a group

## Time to

## Check Your

Knowledge

1. Why iodine atom is larger in size in comparison to chlorine atom ?
2. Observe the following figure :
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-210.jpg?height=110&width=574&top_left_y=1841&top_left_x=671)

## In above series, why the size of atom decreases from Na to Cl

## SOLUTION

1. This is because iodine is below chlorine in group 17th, we know that atomic size increases as we move down the group.
2. The size of atom decreases from Na to Cl due to increase in nuclear charge as we know nuclear charge increases while going from left to right in a period

Ionic radius is the radius of an ion. On converting into an ion the size of a neutral atom changes. Anion (negative ion) is bigger than the neutral atom. This is because addition of one or more electrons increases repulsion among electrons and they move away from each other. On the other hand, a cation (positive ion) is smaller than the neutral atom. When one or more electrons are removed, the repulsive force between the remaining electrons decreases and they come a little closer.

## Variation of ionic radii in periods and groups :

Ionic radii show variations similar to those of atomic radii. Thus, ionic radii increases on moving down a group. You can see such increase in groups 1 and 16 elements from the data given below.

Group 16
| Element | Ionic radius |
| :---: | :---: |
| $\mathrm{O}^{2-}$ | 140 |
| $\mathrm{~S}^{2-}$ | 184 |
| $\mathrm{Se}^{2-}$ | 198 |
| $\mathrm{Te}^{2-}$ | 221 |


Ionic radii decreases in a period on moving from left to right. It can be seen from the data of ionic radii in picometer for 2nd period elements given below.

| Element | $\mathrm{Li}^{+}$ | $\mathrm{Be}^{2+}$ | B | C | $\mathrm{N}^{3-}$ | $\mathrm{O}^{2-}$ | $\mathrm{F}^{-}$ |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Ionic radii | 60 | 31 | - | - | 171 | 140 | 136 |

In the data given above, the positions of boron and carbon have been left vacant as they do not form ions. The trend in radii of cations is seen in $\mathrm{Li}^{+}$and $\mathrm{Be}^{2+}$ and in radii of anions is seen in $\mathrm{N}^{3-}, \mathrm{O}^{2-}$ and $\mathrm{F}^{-}$.
Some atoms and ion contains same number of electrons, hence they are called isoelectronic species. Their radii would be different as they have different nuclear charges. e.g: $\mathrm{O}^{2-}, \mathrm{F}^{-}, \mathrm{Na}^{+}$and $\mathrm{Mg}^{2+}$ have same number of valence electron (i.e. 10). Increasing order of their ionic radii is $\mathrm{Mg}^{2+}<\mathrm{Na}^{+}<\mathrm{F}^{-}<\mathrm{O}^{2-}$.

## 3. Metallic and Non-Metallic Character

Metals are electropositive in nature (the tendency to lose electrons), have lustre, ductility, malleability and electrical conductance.
