of ionic radii in picometer for 2nd
$\_\_\_\_$ different as they have different nuclear charges. e.g: $\mathrm{O}^{2-}, \mathrm{F}^{-}, \mathrm{Na}^{+}$and $\mathrm{Mg}^{2+}$ have same number of valence electron (i.e. 10).
Increasing order of their ionic radii is $\mathrm{Mg}^{2+}<\mathrm{Na}^{+}<\mathrm{F}^{-}<\mathrm{O}^{2-}$.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-211.jpg?height=155&width=1439&top_left_y=1674&top_left_x=440)

Variation of metallic character in a group :
Metallic character of elements in a group increases from top to bottom. As we move down in a group, atomic size increases, therefore, distance between valence electrons and nucleus also increases. Thus, electrostatic force of attraction on valence electrons decreases and they can be easily removed, i.e. electropositive or metallic character increases. This can be best seen in elements of group 14. Its first element, carbon is a typical non metal, next two elements Si and Ge are metalloids and the remaining elements Sn and Pb , are typical metals as shown below.

| Group 14 Elements : | C | Si | Ge | Sn | Pb |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Nature : | Non-metal | Metalloid | Metalloid | Metal | Metal |

## Variation of metallic character in a period :

Metallic character of elements decreases in a period from left to right as shown below for 3rd period elements. As we move from left to right in a period atomic size decreases. Thus, electrostatic force of attraction increases for valence electrons thereby decreasing electropositive character.

| Element: | Na | Mg | Al | Si | P | S |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | Cl

## CONNECTING TOPIC

## IONIZATION ENERGY

Negatively charged electrons in an atom are attracted by the positively charged nucleus. For removing an electron, this attractive force must be overcome by spending some energy. The minimum amount of energy required to remove an electron from a gaseous atom in its ground state to form a gaseous ion is called ionization energy. It is measured in unit of $\mathrm{kJ} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}$.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-212.jpg?height=217&width=341&top_left_y=1031&top_left_x=257)

Ionization energy is a measure of the force of attraction between the nucleus and the outermost electron. Stronger the force of attraction, greater is the value of ionization energy.

It corresponds to the following process : If only one electron is removed, the ionization energy is known as the first ionization energy. If second electron is removed the ionization energy is called the second ionization energy, same goes for third ionization energy and so on. Now we shall study the variation of ionization energy in the periodic table.

## Variation of Ionization Energy in a Group

We have already seen earlier, that the force of attraction between valence electrons and nucleus decreases in a group from top to bottom because of increase in atomic size due to addition of inner shells. Moreover, there is an increase in shielding effect on outermost (valence) electrons due to increase in the number of inner electrons. As a result, the electron becomes less and less firmly held to nucleus as we move down the group. Therefore, ionization energy decreases in a group from top to bottom. This can be seen from ionization energy values (in $\mathrm{kJ} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}$ ) of group 1 and 17 elements given below.

| Group 1 |  | Group 17 |  |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Element | Ionization Energy | Element | Ionization Energy |
| Li | 520 | F | 1680 |
| Na | 496 | Cl | 1251 |
| K | 419 | Br | 1143 |
| Rb | 403 | I | 1009 |

## Variation of Ionization Energy in a Period

We know that the force of attraction between valence electron and nucleus increases in a period from left to right due to increase in nuclear charge. As a consequence of this, the ionization energy generally increases in a period from left to right. This trend can be seen in ionization energies (in $\mathrm{kJ} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}$ ) of elements belonging to 2nd and 3rd periods.
$2^{\text {nd }}$ Period :

| Element | Li | Be | B | C | N | O | F | Ne |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Ionization Energy | 520 | 899 | 801 | 1086 | 1400 | 1314 | 1680 | 2080 |
| Element | Na | Mg | Al | Si | P | S | Cl | Ar |
| Ionization Energy | 496 | 738 | 578 | 786 | 1011 | 1000 | 1251 | 1521 |

However, some irregularities in the general trend have been noticed. These are due to extra stability of half filled and completely filled configurations. For example, as shown in above data nitrogen has more ionization energy in comparison to oxygen which is due to electronic configuration of nitrogen atom $\left(1 s^{2} 2 s^{2} 2 p^{3}\right)$. Here $2 p$-orbital is exactly half filled, hence it is more stable than that of oxygen ( $1 s^{2} 2 s^{2} 2 p^{4}$ ).
Second ionization energy of an element is greater than the first ionization energy, because energy required to remove second electron will be greater since attraction between electron and nucleus increases as the neutral atom changes to an ion.

1. Why ionisation energy of beryllium is more in comparison to boron?
2. Examine the following elements of the third period of modern periodic table :

Na, Mg, Al, Si, P, S, Cl
How will tendency to lose electrons vary in given series :

## SOLUTION

1. Beryllium has more ionisation energy in comparision to boron, because of the electronic configuration of Be atom ( $1 s^{2} 2 s^{2}$ ), where $2 s$ orbital is fully filled which is more stable than that of $\mathrm{B}\left(1 s^{2} 2 s^{2} 2 p^{1}\right)$
2. The given series of elements belongs to third period. Now since the atomic size and hence the tendency to lose electrons decreases along a period which leads to increase in ionisation energy along a period, i.e. from Na to Cl

## ELECTRON AFFINITY

Electron affinity is the energy released when an electron is accepted by an atom in the gaseous state. It corresponds to the process : $\mathrm{X}(\mathrm{g})+\mathrm{e}^{-} \longrightarrow \mathrm{X}^{-}(\mathrm{g})+\mathrm{E}$
Here, X is an atom of an element. The energy change is measured in the unit $\mathrm{kJ} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}$. By convention, electron affinity is assigned a positive value when energy is released during the process. Greater the value of electron affinity, more energy is released during the process and greater is the tendency of the atom to gain electron. Electron affinity depends on factors like nuclear charge, size of atom and electronic configuration.

## Variation of Electron Affinity in a Group

In a group, the electron affinity decreases on moving from top to bottom, that is, less and less amount of energy is released. On moving down a group, atomic size and nuclear charge increases. The effect of increase in atomic size is much more pronounced than the effect of nuclear charge, therefore, additional electron feels less attraction by nucleus. Hence, lower is electron affinity. Such trends in its values (in $\mathrm{kJ} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}$ ) for group 1 and group 17 elements are given below.

| Group 1 |  | Group 17 |  |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Element | Electron affinity | Element | Electron affinity |
| Li | 58 | F | 333 |
| Na | 53 | Cl | 348 |
| K | 48 | Br | 324 |
| Rb | 45 | I | 295 |

## Variation of Electron Affinity Along a Period

On moving across a period, the size of atom decreases and nuclear charge increases. Both of these factors result into greater attraction for incoming electron. Thus, electron affinity increases in a period from left to right. However, certain irregularities are due to stable electronic configurations of certain atoms.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-213.jpg?height=190&width=1534&top_left_y=2010&top_left_x=328)
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-213.jpg?height=152&width=350&top_left_y=2211&top_left_x=324)

- Why electron affinity value of bromine atom is lower than of chlorine atom ?


## SOLUTION

This is because the value of electron affinity decreases as we move down the group.

## ELECTRONEGATIVITY

We have learnt in the previous section that electron affinity of an element is a measure of an isolated atom to attract electrons towards itself. We normally do not deal with isolated atoms. Mostly we come across atoms which are bonded to other atoms. There is another property which deals with the power of bonded atoms to attract electrons. This property is known as electronegativity. Electronegativity is relative tendency of a bonded atom to attract the bond-electrons towards itself. Electronegativity is a dimensionless quantity and does not have any units. It just compares the tendency of various elements to attract the bond-electrons towards themselves. The most widely used scale of electronegativity was devised by Linus Pauling. Electronegativity generally decreases in a group from top to bottom. As we move in a group from top to bottom the atomic size increases. As a result, bonding electrons become away from the nucleus. Variation of electronegativity in groups 1 and 17 is given below.

| Group 1 |  | Group 17 |  |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Element | Electronegativity | Element | Electronegativity |
| Li | 1.0 | F | 4.0 |
| Na | 0.9 | C | 3.2 |
| K | 0.8 | Br | 3.0 |
| Rb | 0.8 | I | 2.7 |

Fluorine atom has the greatest power of attracting electrons and is the most electronegative element.
Now let us see its variation in 2nd and 3rd period elements. Electronegativity generally increases in a period from left to right. This is due to decrease in atomic size and increase in effective nuclear charge. Now as a result of increase in effective nuclear charge, the attraction for the outermost electrons increases.
$2^{\text {nd }}$ Period Elements :

| Element | Li | Be | B | C | N | O | F |
| :--- | :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Electronegativity | 1.0 | 1.5 | 2.0 | 2.5 | 3.0 | 3.5 | 4.0 |
| $\mathbf{3}^{\text {rd }}$ Period Elements : |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |
| Element | Na | Mg | Al | Si | P | S | C 1 |
| Electronegativity | 0.9 | 1.2 | 1.5 | 1.8 | 2.1 | 2.5 | 3.2 |

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-214.jpg?height=199&width=321&top_left_y=1503&top_left_x=187)

- Halogens have highest electronegativity.
- Non-metals have high electronegativity values in comparison to metals.

Learn More
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-214.jpg?height=657&width=1040&top_left_y=1827&top_left_x=470)

CASEI: The atomic size generally decreases from left to right in a period whereas it increases down the group. In the periodic table, noble gases comes after halogens, thus they should have smaller size than halogens. But this is not observed in reality, instead noble gases have exceptionally large size. Similarly, expected variation in the size of elements of $5^{\text {th }}$ and $6^{\text {th }}$ period is also not observed.

Why such anomalies are seen in periodic table? Inert gases have fully filled electronic configuration which results in greater inter electronic repulsion. Moreover in case of noble gases atomic radius is determined by van der Waal radius which is larger than both ionic as well as covalent radii. Consequently inert gases has larger atomic size than halogens in the corresponding period.

Atomic size of elements of $6{ }^{\text {th }}$ period does not show much increase as in the corresponding group. Effect of $4 f$ orbitals does not allow the size to increase when we move from $4 d$ to $5 d$ series. For example. Zr and Hf; Nb and Ta; Mo and W have approximately similar size. This resemblance in size is due to the presence of lanthanoids in between transition elements.

## CASE STUDY : Periodic Trends

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-215.jpg?height=744&width=413&top_left_y=694&top_left_x=359)

CASE II: Ionization energy is the minimum amount of energy required to remove the outermost electron from an atom or ion in the gas phase. Ionization energy generally increases from left to right along a period whereas it decreases top to bottom in a group. If you look at a chart of first ionization energies. Energy of boron is less than that of beryllium and the first ionization energy of oxygen is less than that of nitrogen. Why these variation are seen in ionisation energy trend?

Li $<B e<B<C<N<O<F$ Expected
$\boldsymbol{L} \boldsymbol{i}<\boldsymbol{B}<\boldsymbol{B} \boldsymbol{e}<\boldsymbol{C}<\boldsymbol{O}<\boldsymbol{N}<\boldsymbol{F} \quad$ Observed
The electronic configuration of all 4 elements which shows variation, can be written as follows. $4 \mathrm{Be} \Rightarrow 1 s^{2} 2 s^{2} \quad 5 \mathrm{~B} \Rightarrow 1 s^{2} 2 s^{2} 2 p^{1} \quad 7 \mathrm{~N} \Rightarrow 1 s^{2} 2 s^{2} 2 p^{3} \quad 8 \mathrm{O} \Rightarrow 1 s^{2} 2 s^{2} 2 p^{4}$

Generally, the fully filled or half filled orbitals are more stable as compared to other. That means more energy is required to remove electrons from these orbitals. Beryllium atom has fully filled 2s orbital hence more energy is required to remove an electron as compared to boron, where electron has to be removed from singly occupied $2 p$ orbital. Similarly, nitrogen atom has half filled $2 p$ orbital, therefore, more energy is required for electron removal than oxygen atom.
CASE III: Electron affinity is the amount of energy released when an electron is added to an atom in the gaseous phase to produce an anion. It follows similar trend as ionisation energy. Electron affinity increases across the period whereas it decreases down the group. Generally noble gases show zero electron affinity. Atoms of the element having low electron affinity has more tendency to lose electrons than gain electrons. Why? The noble gases show zero electron affinity because the addition of an electron to these atoms is not possible. Atoms with a low electron affinity readily loses valence electron because in these atom nucleus does not have strong hold on valence electrons.

## Multiple Choice Questions (MCQs)

DIRECTIONS : This section contains multiple choice questions. Each question has four choices (a), (b), (c) and (d) out of which ONLY ONE is correct.

1. The three elements calcium, strontium and barium form a triad. What is the basis of this grouping?
(i) Elements are in the increasing order of their atomic weights.
(ii) The atomic weight of the middle element is equal to the average of the atomic weight of extreme elements.
(iii) Elements in a triad have similar chemical properties.
(a) Only (i) and (ii)
(b) Only (ii) and (iii)
(c) Only (i) and (iii)
(d) (i), (ii) and (iii)

(c) An element with atomic number 12

## Assertion \& Reason

DIRECTIONS : Each of these questions contains an assertion followed by reason. Read them carefully and answer the question on the basis of following options. You have to select the one that best describes the two statements.
(a) If both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of Assertion.
(b) If both Assertion and Reason are correct, but Reason is not the correct explanation of Assertion.
(c) If Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
(d) If Assertion is incorrect but Reason is correct.

1. Assertion: Ionic size of $\mathrm{N}^{3-}$ is greater than $\mathrm{F}^{-}$.

Reason: $\mathrm{N}^{3-}$ and $\mathrm{F}^{-}$are isoelectronic anions.
2. Assertion: Atomic radius of aluminium atom is larger than magnesium atom.

Reason: Effective nuclear charge increase from magnesium to aluminium.
3. Assertion: Oxygen atom is divalent in most of its compounds.
Reason: Valency is the number of valence electrons present in the outermost shell of its atom.
4. Assertion: Silicon, germanium are the metalloids in the modern periodic table.
Reason: Silicon, germanium has properties of metal as well as non metals.
5. Assertion: Bulbs are usually filled with chemically active gases.
Reason: Nitrogen and argon gases are filled in order to prolong the life of the filament.

5. Write the names given to the vertical columns and horizontal rows in the modern periodic table. How does the metallic character of elements vary on moving down a vertical column? How does the size of atomic radius vary on moving left to right in a horizontal row? Give reason in support of your answer in the above two cases.
6. $\mathrm{Na}, \mathrm{Mg}$ and Al are the elements of the $3^{\text {rd }}$ period of the modern periodic table having group number 1, 2 and 13 respectively. Which one of these elements has the
(i) highest valency,
(ii) largest atomic radius, and
(iii) maximum chemical reactivity? Justify your answer stating the reason for each.
7. (a) The modern periodic table has been evolved through the early attempts of Dobereiner, Newland and Mendeleev. List one advantage and one limitation of all the three attempts.
(b) Name the scientists who first of all showed that atomic number of an element is a more fundamental property than its atomic mass.
(c) State modern periodic law.
8. Based on the group valency of elements write the molecular formula of the following compounds giving justification for each:
(i) Oxide of first group elements.
(ii) Halide of the elements of group thirteen, and
(iii) Compound formed when an element, A of group 2 combines with an element, B of group seventeen.
9. How it can be proved that the basic structure of the modern periodic table is based on the electronic configuration of atoms of different elements?

## Reasoning Based Questions

1. Why elements in any given group have similar properties?
2. Why some gaps were left in Mendeleev's periodic table?
3. Why are the group 2 elements called alkaline earth metals?
4. Why the size of the atom increases down the group?
5. Explain why the first ionization energy of lithium is less than that for beryllium, but the second ionization energy of beryllium is less than lithium.
6. Why does the metallic character increase down the group?
7. In the modern periodic table, why does cobalt with higher atomic mass of 58.93 appear before nickel having lower atomic mass of 58.71?
8. Why could no fixed position be given to hydrogen in Mendeleev's periodic table?
9. Why the properties of elements are repeated after 2,8 , 18 and 32 elements in the periodic table?

## HOTS Questions

1. Referring to a periodic table, arrange the following atoms in order of increasing first ionization energy : Ne, Na, $\mathrm{P}, \mathrm{Ar}, \mathrm{K}$.
2. For each of the following pairs, indicate which one of the two species is larger:
(a) $\mathrm{N}^{3-}$ or $\mathrm{F}^{-}$;
(b) $\mathrm{Mg}^{2+}$ or $\mathrm{Ca}^{2+}$;
(c) $\mathrm{Fe}^{2+}$ or $\mathrm{Fe}^{3+}$
3. Using only a periodic table as a guide, arrange each of the following series of species in order of increasing first ionization energy.
(a) O, C, F
(b) $\mathrm{Te}, \mathrm{Ru}, \mathrm{Sr}$
4. Predict which element of the following pairs will have the higher density and support your prediction using periodic trends :
(a) Cr or W ;
(b) W or Os;
(c) Pd or Ag ; and
(d) Y or Nb .
5. Why are the elements of group 18 called zero valent ?
6. List the following series of elements in order of increasing atomic radius : (a) $\mathrm{Be}, \mathrm{C}, \mathrm{Mg}$ (b) $\mathrm{Rb}, \mathrm{I}, \mathrm{Br}$.
7. Arrange the ions $\mathrm{S}^{2-}, \mathrm{Cl}^{-}, \mathrm{K}^{+}$and $\mathrm{Ca}^{2+}$ in order of decreasing ionic radii.
8. List the following ions in order of increasing ionic radius: $\mathrm{N}^{3-}, \mathrm{Na}^{+}, \mathrm{F}^{-}, \mathrm{Mg}^{2+}, \mathrm{O}^{2-}$.
9. Why are the electron affinities of the alkaline earth metals, either negative or small positive values?

## Case Study Based MCQs <br> (CBSE Sample Issued 2021)

1. Read the following and answer any four questions from (i) to (v).
Metallic Character The ability of an atom to donate electrons and form positive ion (cation) is known as electropositivity or metallic character. Down the group, metallic character increases due to increase in atomic size and across the period, from left to right electropositivity decreases due to decrease in atomic size. Non-Metallic Character The ability of an atom to
accept electrons to form a negative ion (anion) is called non-metallic character or electronegativity. The elements having high electro-negativity have a higher tendency to gain electrons and form anion. Down the group, electronegativity decreases due to increase in atomic size and across the period, from left to right electronegativity increases due to decrease in atomic size.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-220.jpg?height=401&width=570&top_left_y=653&top_left_x=301)
(i) Which of the following correctly represents the decreasing order of metallic character of Alkali metals plotted in the graph?
(a) $\mathrm{Cs}>\mathrm{Rb}>\mathrm{Li}>\mathrm{Na}>\mathrm{K}$
(b) $\mathrm{K}>\mathrm{Rb}>\mathrm{Li}>\mathrm{Na}>\mathrm{Cs}$
(c) $\mathrm{Cs}>\mathrm{Rb}>\mathrm{K}>\mathrm{Na}>\mathrm{Li}$
(d) $\mathrm{Cs}>\mathrm{K}>\mathrm{Rb}>\mathrm{Na}>\mathrm{Li}$
(ii) Hydrogen is placed along with Alkali metals in the modern periodic table though it shows non-metallic character
(a) as Hydrogen has one electron \& readily loses electron to form negative ion
(b) as Hydrogen can easily lose one electron like alkali metals to form positive ion
(c) as Hydrogen can gain one electron easily like Halogens to form negative ion
(d) as Hydrogen shows the properties of nonmetals
(iii) Which of the following has highest electronegativity?
(a) F
(b) Cl
(c) Br
(d) I
(iv) Identify the reason for the gradual change in electronegativity in halogens down the group.
(a) Electronegativity increases down the group due to decrease in atomic size
(b) Electronegativity decreases down the group due to decrease in tendency to lose electrons
(c) Electronegativity decreases down the group due to increase in atomic radius/ tendency to gain electron decreases
(d) Electronegativity increases down the group due to increase in forces of attractions between nucleus \& valence electrons
(v) Which of the following reason correctly justifies that "Fluorine (72pm) has smaller atomic radius than Lithium ( 152 pm )"?
(a) F and Li are in the same group. Atomic size increases down the group
(b) F and Li are in the same period. Atomic size increases across the period due to increase in number of shells
(c) F and Li are in the same group. Atomic size decreases down the group
(d) F and Li are in the same period and across the period atomic size/radius decreases from left to right.

## Exercise 2 Lil Master NCERT (Textbook \& Exemplar)

## Text - Book Questions

1. Did Dobereiner's triads also exist in the columns of Newland's octaves? Compare and find out.
2. What were the limitations of Dobereiner's classification?
3. Use Mendeleev's periodic table to predict the formulae for the oxides of following elements :
K, C, Al, Si, Ba
4. Besides galium, which other elements have since been discovered to fill the gaps left by Mendeleev in his periodic table? (any two)
5. What were the criteria used by Mendeleev in creating his periodic table?
6. Why do you think the noble gases are placed in a separate group?
7. How could the modern periodic table remove various anomalies of Mendeleev's periodic table?
8. Name two elements you would expect to show same kind of chemical reactivity as magnesium. What is the basis for your choice?
9. Name :
(a) Three elements that have only a single electron in their outermost shell.
(b) Two elements that have two electrons in their outermost shell.
(c) Three elements with completely filled outermost shell.
10. (a) Lithium, Sodium and Potassium are all metals that react with water to liberate hydrogen gas. Is there any similarity in the atoms of these elements?
(b) Helium is an unreactive gas and neon is a gas of extremely low reactivity. What, if anything, do their atoms have in common?
11. In the modern periodic table, among the first ten elements, which are metals?
12. By considering their position in the periodic table, which one of the following elements would you expect to have the most metallic characteristics?

Ga Ge As Se Be

## Text - Book Exercise

1. Which of the following statements is not a correct statement about the trends when going from left to right across the periods of periodic table.
(a) The elements become less metallic in nature.
(b) The number of valence electrons increases.
(c) The atoms lose their electrons more easily.
(d) The oxides become more acidic.
2. Element X forms a chloride with the formula $\mathrm{XCl}_{2}$, which is a solid with a high melting point? X would most likely be in the same group of the periodic table as :
(a) Na
(b) Mg
(c) Al
(d) Si
3. Which element has :
(a) two shells, both of which are completely filled with electrons?
(b) the electronic configuration 2, 8, 2?
(c) a total of three shells, with four electrons in its valence shell?
(d) a total of two shells, with three electrons in its valence shell?
(e) twice as many electrons in its second shell as in its first shell?
4. (a) What property do all elements in the same column of the periodic table as boron have in common?
(b) What property do all elements in the same column of the periodic table as fluorine have in common?
5. An atom has electronic configuration $2,8,7$.
(a) What is the atomic number of this element?
(b) To which of the following elements would it be chemically similar? (Atomic numbers are given in parentheses).
N (7)
F(9)
P (15)
Ar(18)
6. The position of three elements $\mathrm{A}, \mathrm{B}$ and C in the periodic table are shown below :

| Group 16 | Group 17 |
| :--- | :---: |
| - | - |
| - | A |
| - | - |
| B | C |

(a) State whether A is a metal or non-metal.
(b) State whether C is more reactive or less reactive than A .
(c) Will C be larger or smaller in size than B ?
(d) Which type of ion, cation or anion will be formed by an element A ?
7. Nitrogen (atomic number 7) and phosphorus (atomic number 15) belong to group 15 of the periodic table. Write the electronic configuration of these two elements. Which of these will be more electronegative? Why?
8. How does the electronic configuration of an atom relate to its position in the modern periodic table?
9. In the, modern periodic table, calcium (atomic number 20) is surrounded by elements with atomic numbers 12, 19, 21 and 38 . Which of these have physical and chemical properties resembling calcium?
10. Compare and contrast the arrangement of elements in Mendeleev's periodic table and the modern periodic table.

## Exemplar Questions

1. If an element X is placed in group 14, what will be the formula and the nature of bonding of its chloride?
2. Compare the radii of two species X and Y . Give reasons for your answer.
(a) X has 12 protons and 12 electrons
(b) Y has 12 protons and 10 electrons
3. An element is placed in 2nd group and 3rd Period of the periodic table, burns in presence of oxygen to form a basic oxide.
(a) Identify the element.
(b) Write the electronic configuration.
(c) Write the balanced equation when it burns in the presence of air.
(d) Write a balanced equation when this oxide is dissolved in water.
(e) Draw the electron dot structure for the formation of this oxide.
4. An element X of group 15 exists as diatomic molecule and combines with hydrogen at 773 K in presence of the catalyst to form a compound, ammonia which has a characteristic pungent smell.
(a) Identify the element X . How many valence electrons does it have?
(b) Draw the electron dot structure of the diatomic molecule of X . What type of bond is formed in it?
(c) Draw the electron dot structure for ammonia and what type of bond is formed in it?
5. Which group of elements could be placed in Mendeleev's periodic table without disturbing the original order? Give reason.

## Multiple Choice Questions

DIRECTIONS : This section contains multiple choice questions. Each question has four choices (a), (b), (c) and (d) out of which ONLY ONE is correct.

(a) 162.5
(b) 91.5
(c) 81.25
(d) 45.625

(a) copper
(b) chlorine
(c) calcium
(d) chromium

(a) increasing order of atomic groups

(c) seven periods and nine groups
(d) eight periods and seven groups

(a) 1st group
(b) 7th group
(c) 8th group
(d) none of these

(c) eight periods and eighteen groups

(b) Inert gases

(c) $\mathrm{M}^{-}$

(b) number of valence electrons

(a) s-block only
(b) p-block only
(c) $d$-block only

(b) hydrogen has been placed accurately.
(c) isotopes have been placed in same position.

(a) relative size
(b) configuration
(c) presence of charge
(d) mass of nucleus
8. Which of the following properties generally decrease along a period?
(a) atomic size
(b) non-metallic character
(c) metallic character

(a) Na
(b) Si
(c) Mg
(d) P

(a) increases, (i) from left to right across a period and (ii) on descending a group.
(b) decreases, (i) from left to right across a period and (ii) on moving up in a group from bottom to top.
(c) increases from left to right across a period and decreases on descending a group.
(d) increases from right to left across a period and increases on descending a group.
11. Which of the following statements is (are) correct statement about the trends when going from left to right across the periods of periodic table?
(a) The elements become less metallic in nature.
(b) The number of valence electrons increases.
(c) The atoms lose their electrons more easily.
(d) The oxides become more acidic.
12. Transition metals can -
(a) show variable oxidation states.
(b) form coloured compounds.
(c) float in air
(d) create oxygen
13. The statement that is true for the long form of the periodic table is -
(a) it reflects the sequence of filling the electrons in the order of sub-energy levels s, $p, d$ and $f$.
(b) it helps to predict the stable valency states of the elements.
(c) it reflects trends in physical and chemical properties of the elements.
(d) it helps to predict the relative atomicity of the bonds between any two elements.
14. The statement that is correct for the periodic classification of elements is -
(a) The properties of elements are the periodic functions of their atomic numbers.
(b) Non-metallic elements are lesser in number than metallic elements.
(c) The first ionisation energies along a period do not vary in a regular manner with increase in atomic number.
(d) For transition elements the $d$-sub-shells are filled with electrons monotonically with increase in atomic number.

PASSAGE - 2
Group VII A elements are strong non-metals because they can easily accept an electron to form an anion whereas group 1 A element are strong metals because they can very easily lose one electron to form cation.
Metals have the tendency to lose their valence electrons and form positive ions, so metallic character is related to the ionisation potential. Elements having low ionisation potential, lose electrons easily. Thus, metallic character generally decreases across a period and increases down a group.

## Assertion \& Reason

DIRECTIONS : Each of these questions contains an assertion followed by reason. Read them carefully and answer the question on the basis of following options. You have to select the one that best describes the two statements.
(a) If both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of Assertion.
(b) If both Assertion and Reason are correct, but Reason is not the correct explanation of Assertion.
(c) If Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
(d) If Assertion is incorrect but Reason is correct.

1. Assertion : Group 1 elements are known as the alkali elements.
Reason : $s$-orbital can accommodate only two electrons.
2. Assertion : Nitrogen has higher ionization energy than that of oxygen.
Reason : Nitrogen has smaller atomic size than that of oxygen.
3. Assertion : According to Mendeleev, periodic properties of elements are functions of their atomic number.
Reason : Atomic number is equal to the number of protons.
4. Assertion : Elements in the same vertical column have similar properties.
Reason : Elements have periodic dependence upon the atomic number.

## Passage Based Questions

DIRECTIONS (Qs. 44-49) : Study the given paragraph(s) and answer the following questions.

## PASSAGE-1

In the modern periodic table, elements are arranged in order of increasing atomic numbers which is related to the electronic configuration. Depending upon the type of orbitals receiving the last electron, the elements in the periodic table have been divided into four blocks, viz, $s, p, d$ and $f$. The modern periodic table consists of 7 periods and 18 groups. Each period begins with the filling of a new energy shell. In accordance with the Afbau principle, the seven periods ( 1 to 7 ) have $2,8,8,18$, 18, 32 and 32 elements respectively. The seventh period is still incomplete. To avoid the periodic table being too long, the two series of $f$-block elements, called lanthanoids and actinoids are placed at the bottom of the main body of the periodic table.
44. The element with atomic number 57 belongs to
(a) $s$-block
(b) $p$-block
(c) $d$-block
(d) $f$-block
45. The last element of the $p$-block in 6th period is
(a) Pb
(b) Bi
(c) At
(d) Rn
46. The elements with atomic numbers 35,53 and 85 are all $\_\_\_\_$ .
(a) noble gases
(b) halogens
(c) heavy metals
(d) light metals

## PASSAGE-2

The way atomic radius varies with increasing atomic number can be explained by the arrangement of electrons in shells of fixed capacity. Shells closer to the nucleus, those with a smaller radius are generally filled first, since the negatively charged electrons are attracted by the positively charged protons in the nucleus. As the atomic number increases along a row of the periodic table, additional electrons are added to the same, outermost shell. The radius of this shell gradually contracts as the attraction between the additional electrons and the nucleus increases. On moving down in group, atomic radius increases due to increase in the number of shells. As the number of shells increases, the force of attraction between the nucleus and the outermost electron decreases and hence the size increases.
47. In 3rd period of the periodic table the atom with the largest atomic radius is located in group -
(a) 1
(b) 3
(c) 13
(d) 17

(a) Sr
(b) Sn
(c) Rb
(d) In
49. Which of the following would have the smallest atomic radii?
(a) K
(b) Rb
(c) Li
(d) Fr

## Assertion \& Reason

DIRECTIONS (Qs. 50-52) : Each of these questions contains an assertion followed by reason. Read them carefully and answer the question on the basis of following options. You have to select the one that best describes the two statements.
(a) If both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of Assertion.
(b) If both Assertion and Reason are correct, but Reason is not the correct explanation of Assertion.
(c) If Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
(d) If Assertion is incorrect but Reason is correct.
50. Assertion : Ionization enthalpy is the energy released to remove an electron from an isolated gaseous atom in its ground state.
Reason : Elements have a tendency to lose or gain electrons to attain the stable configuration.
51. Assertion : The highest I.E. in a period is shown by noble gas.
Reason : Noble gases are at the extreme right of the period.
52. Assertion : Smaller the size of an atom greater is its electronegativity.
Reason : Electronegativity refers to the tendency of atom to share electrons with other atom.

Integer/Numerical Value Type Questions

DIRECTIONS (Qs. 55-58) : Following are integer/numerical value based questions. Each question, when worked out will result in an integer/numerical value.
55. The atomic number of an element is 17 . What will be its valency?
56. An element ' X ' belongs to group VA of the periodic table. What is its valency?
57. An element ' $X$ ' have atomic number 56. What will be its group number?
58. How many metalloids are there in periodic table?

3. Element ' V ' is the most electronegative element
4. (i) Dobereiner's Triads is $\mathbf{A}$.
(ii) Mendeleev placed elements of category $\mathrm{A}, \mathrm{B}$ and C in three different groups because they have different physical and chemical properties.
(iii) No, Newland's Law of octaves is not applicable for all three categories.
Because the law of octaves states that every eighth element has similar properties when the elements are arranged in the increasing order of their atomic masses.

## Very Short Answer Questions

3. According to Doberenier, elements having similar properties can be arranged in triads in which atomic mass of the middle element is the mean of the atomic mass of the other two elements.
4. Lithium forms the most basic oxide as it is a metal and nitrogen forms the most acidic oxide as it is a non-metal.
5. The vertical columns in Mendeleev's, as well as in modern periodic table, are called groups.
6. Henry Mosely, proposed the modern periodic table based on modern periodic law.

## Short Answer Questions

1. Cations are smaller than their parent atoms, so $\mathrm{Ca}^{2+}$ is smaller than the Ca atom. Because Ca is below Mg in group IIA of the periodic table, $\mathrm{Ca}^{2+}$ is larger than $\mathrm{Mg}^{2+}$. Consequently, $\mathrm{Ca}>\mathrm{Ca}^{2+}>\mathrm{Mg}^{2+}$.
2. When the elements are arranged in order of increasing atomic numbers, elements with similar chemical properties appear at definite intervals, this is known as periodicity. Yes, this periodicity is due to the periodicity in the number of electrons in the outermost shell of the atoms of the elements. Elements placed in the same group have same number of valance electrons and show similar properties.
3. Mendeleev's periodic law : It states that, the properties of elements are the periodic functions of their atomic masses. It means that the properties of the elements depend on their atomic masses and elements were placed in the periodic table on the basis of their increasing atomic masses.
4. (a) The $\mathrm{P}^{+}$cation has 14 electrons. Ar and $\mathrm{S}^{2-}$ have 18 electrons, $\mathrm{Si}^{-}$has 15 electrons, and $\mathrm{Cl}^{3+}$ has 14 electrons. Only $\mathrm{Cl}^{3+}$ is isoelectronic with $\mathrm{P}^{+}$.
(b) The $\mathrm{Mn}^{2+}$ cation has 23 electrons with electron configuration $[\mathrm{Ar}] 3 d^{5}$. The $\mathrm{Fe}^{3+}$ ion also has 23 electrons and the same electronic configuration as $\mathrm{Mn}^{2+}$; thus they are isoelectronic. The $\mathrm{Ni}^{3+}$ ion has 25 electrons $\left([\mathrm{Ar}] 3 d^{7}\right)$ and $\mathrm{Co}^{3+}$ has 24 electrons $\left([\mathrm{Ar}] 3 d^{6}\right)$. Neither of which is isoelectronic with $\mathrm{Mn}^{2+}$.
5. $\begin{array}{llll}\text { (a) } n s^{1} & \text { (b) } n s^{2} n p^{2} & \text { (c) } n s^{2} n p^{5}\end{array}$
6. (a) $\mathrm{Si}^{+}$(b) $\mathrm{Mg}^{+} \quad$ (c) $\mathrm{Fe}^{+}$
7. (a) Na, cation always has smaller size than neutral atom.
(b) $\mathrm{O}^{2-}$, the size of anion increase as the charge increases.
(c) $\mathrm{Ni}^{2+}$, the size of cation decreases as the charge increases.
8. (a) $\mathrm{O}<\mathrm{B}<\mathrm{Li} \quad$ (b) $\mathrm{N}<\mathrm{C}<\mathrm{Si} \quad$ (c) $\mathrm{S}<\mathrm{As}<\mathrm{Sn}$
9. (a) $\mathrm{Be}^{2+}<\mathrm{Be}<\mathrm{Li}$ (b) $\mathrm{Cl}<\mathrm{S}<\mathrm{S}^{2-}$
10. (a) In a group, the ionisation potential decreases from top to bottom, hence ionisation potential of Na will be higher than Rb .
$\mathrm{Na}>\mathrm{Rb}$.
(b) In a period ionisation potential increases from left to right, i.e., $\mathrm{F}>\mathrm{O}$
11. Boron. In both cases a $2 s$ valence electron is being removed, but $\mathrm{B}^{+}$has fully filled inert configuration.
12. Vertical neighbours are oxygen ( O ) and selenium ( Se ), horizontal neighbours are phosphorus $(\mathrm{P})$ and chlorine (Cl). Oxygen and selenium have similar chemical properties to that of sulphur.
13. As we move from left to right across a period there is a regular decrease in atomic radii of the representative elements. This is due to the fact that number of energy shells remain the same in a period but nuclear charge increases gradually as the atomic number increases, hence the correct order will be

$$
\mathrm{Na}>\mathrm{Mg}>\mathrm{Al}>\mathrm{P}>\mathrm{Cl} .
$$

14. N and P are in the same group and N is above P . Therefore, the radius of N is smaller than that of P (atomic radius increases as we go down a group). Both Si and P are in the third period, and Si is to the left of P. Therefore, the radius of P is smaller than that of Si (atomic radius decreases as we move from left to right across a period). Thus the order of radius is $\mathrm{N}<\mathrm{P}<\mathrm{Si}$.

Anions -Cl and O
(ii) He and Ne are inert elements

(ii) $\mathrm{K}(19)$ is placed before $\mathrm{Ca}(20)$ in the same period. Since, the atomic radius decrease across a period so, the atomic radius of calcium is smaller than that of potassium.
(iii) The formula of oxide of calcium is CaO , because the valency of both calcium and oxygen is 2 .
18. (i) Two elements of group 1 are sodium (Na) and potassium (K)

(ii) Similarity : Both the elements have one valence electron in outermost shell.
(iii) Oxide : $\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{O} / \mathrm{K}_{2} \mathrm{O}$.

'M' will form ionic compounds, because it has only two electrons in its outer most shell. Hence it can lose electrons easily.

Period = 3, Group = $17(2,8,7)$

## Long Answer Questions

1. (a) K has the electronic configuration $[\mathrm{Ar}] 4 s^{1}$ and $\mathrm{K}^{+}$has the electronic configuration of [Ar]. The electron in the $4 s$ orbital makes K much larger than $\mathrm{K}^{+}$.
(b) These two species have the same electronic configuration $[\mathrm{Ar}]$ as these two species are
isoelectronic. The size of $\mathrm{S}^{2-}$ is larger than $\mathrm{Cl}^{-}$ because generally anions with more negative charge are larger in size. Moreover, sulphur is at left to chlorine in periodic table. Therefore, periodic trend also favours larger size of $\mathrm{S}^{2-}$.
(c) Both $\mathrm{Co}^{2+}\left([\mathrm{Ar}] 3 d^{7}\right)$ and $\mathrm{Co}^{3+}\left([\mathrm{Ar}] 3 d^{6}\right)$ have valence electrons in the same sublevel, but the size $\mathrm{Co}^{2+}$ is larger because additional shielding caused by the extra electron will expand the electron cloud.
2. (a) Oxygen and sulfur are members of group VIA. They have the same valence electron configuration $\left(n s^{2} n p^{4}\right)$, but the $3 p$ electron in sulfur is farther away from the nucleus and experiences less nuclear attraction than the $2 p$ electron in oxygen. Thus, following the general rule that the ionization energy of elements decreases as we move down a periodic group, we predict that sulfur should have a smaller first ionization energy.
(b)
(I) Group IA
(II) Group VA
(III) Group VIIIA
3. (i) It was found that law of octaves was applicable only up to calcium because after Ca , every eighth element did not possess properties similar to first.
(ii) New elements discovered could not fit into the law of octaves on the basis of their properties.
(iii) Newland adjusted two elements in same slot e.g., Co and Ni were placed along with halogens which differ entirely from halogens. Iron resembles with Co and Ni , but placed far away from these elements.
4. The main aim of classifying elements is the systematic and simplified study of elements and their compounds. The basic property of elements used in development of modern periodic table was atomic number.
Modern periodic law : The properties of elements are a periodic function of their atomic number.
Metals are found on the left side and at the centre of the modern periodic table.
Metalloids are present in a zig-zag manner between the metals and the non-metals.
Non-metals are present on the right side of the modern periodic table.
5. 18 vertical columns are known as groups and 7 horizontal rows are known as periods. Metallic character increases on moving down a group in the modern periodic table. As we move down the group, the attraction between the nucleus and the outermost shell electron decreases due to the addition of new shell between them. Therefore, the valence electron can be easily lost by the element.
The size of atomic radius decreases on moving left to right in a horizontal row. When, we move across a period, the number of electrons in the same shell increases. This will experience greater attraction between the nucleus and the outermost electrons. This increased attraction bring the outermost electron closer to the nucleus, thereby decreases the atomic size.
6. (i) Na: Electronic configuration: 2, 8, 1
for $\mathrm{Na}:$ Valency = 1
for, $\mathrm{Mg}: 2,8,2 ; \quad$ Valency = 2
Al: 2, 8, 3; Valency = 3
Al has the highest valency beacause it contains the maximum number of electrons in the outermost orbital.
(ii) Na has the largest atomic radius in given elements because atomic radius decreases across the period due to increase in number of electrons and nuclear charge which tends to pull the electrons closer to the nucleus.
(iii) Na has the maximum chemical reactivity as it contains only one electron in its valence shell so it can lose electron easily to form cation in comparison to Mg and Al .
7. (a) Dobereiner:

Advantage:- He gave an idea of classification of elements in the form of triads.
Disadvantage:- He could arrange only a few elements in triads.
Newland:
Advantage:- He arranged known elements in order of increasing atomic mass and encouraged other chemist to correlate the properties of elements with their atomic masses.
Disadvantage:- Newland could arrange elements only upto calcium.
Mendeleev:
Advantage:- Mendeleev's periodic table had some blank spaces in it. These vacant spaces were for elements that were yet to be discovered.
Disadvantage: Argon (39.91) is placed before potassium (39.1)
(b) Henry Moseley
(c) The properties of the elements are periodic functions of their atomic numbers.
8. (i) Valency of group 1 elements: 1

Valency of oxygen:2

The formula for group 1 oxides is $\mathrm{M}_{2} \mathrm{O}$, where M is the group 1 element and O is oxygen.
(ii) Group 13 elements has valency 3 .

Valency of halogens: 1
The compound formed is:
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-233.jpg?height=118&width=389&top_left_y=2261&top_left_x=461)

Formula of the halides of group 13 is $\mathrm{MX}_{3}$, where M is the group 13 element and X is halogen.
(iii) Group 2 elements has valency 2 and valency of group 17 elements is 1 .

Element
Valency
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-233.jpg?height=115&width=236&top_left_y=372&top_left_x=1434)

Therefore, formula of the compound is $\mathrm{AB}_{2}$; where, A is the group 2 element and B is the group 17 element.
9. The basic structure of the modern periodic table has vertical columns known as groups and horizontal rows known as periods.
If we know the electronic configuration of an element, we can know its period from the number of shells present in its atom and we can know its group from number of electrons in the valence shell.
For example, Atomic number of chlorine $=17$
Its electronic configuration $=2,8,7$
KLM
Number of shells $=3$
∴ Chlorine belongs to $3^{\text {rd }}$ period
As Cl atom has seven electron in its valence shell
∴ Chlorine is present in seventeenth group.

## Reasoning Based Questions

1. The chemical properties of an atom are largely determined by its valance electrons. In a given group, the number of valance electrons in all elements is same, hence they have similar chemical properties.
2. Mendeleev left some gaps in his periodic table for the elements yet to be discovered. He even predicted the properties of these elements by studying the properties of the neighbouring elements.
3. These elements are called alkaline earth metals because their oxides and hydroxides are alkaline in nature and these metal oxides are found in the earth's crust.
4. On moving down a group, the charge on the nucleus increases with increase in atomic number, but at the same time, there is an increase in the energy levels. The number of electrons in the outermost shell, however, remains the same. Since the effect of additional energy levels outweigh the effect of increased atomic or nuclear charge, the distance of the outermost electron from the nucleus increase on going down the group. Hence size of the atom increases down the group.
5. ${ }_{3} \mathrm{Li}: 1 s^{2} 2 s^{1} ;{ }_{4} \mathrm{Be}: 1 s^{2} 2 s^{2}$

First ionization energy of lithium is less than beryllium because electron has been removed from $2 s$ subshell which is partially filled in Li and fully filled in Be. While second ionisation energy of Be is less than Li because second electron has been removed from partially filled $2 s$ subshell in Be and from fully filled subshell in Li. Half filled and completely filled subshell are stable configuration therefore require more energy for electron removal.
6. As we move down the group atomic size increases and effective nuclear charge experienced by the valence electrons decreases, consequently valence electrons can be removed easily. Therefore metallic character increase down the group.
7. Modern periodic table is based on modern periodic law and the elements are arranged in the order of increasing atomic number. Therefore, cobalt with atomic number 27 should come first followed by nickel with atomic number 28 .
8. Fixed position could not be given to hydrogen in Mendeleev's periodic table because hydrogen resembles both halogens and alkali metals in its properties.
Resemblence with halogen:
(i) Hydrogen exists as diatomic molecules $\left(\mathrm{H}_{2}\right)$
(ii) Hydrogen combines with certain metals to form ionic compounds.
(iii) It also react with non metals to form covalent compounds.
Resemblence with alkali metals:
(i) Hydrogen combines with halogens, oxygen and sulphur to form compounds having similar formulae as of alkali metals.
9. The electronic configuration of elements are repeated after 2, 8, 18 and 32 elements because 2, 8, 18 and 32 is the maximum number of electrons which can be accomodated in $\mathrm{K}, \mathrm{L}, \mathrm{M}$ and N shells of the atoms of the elements. The properties of elements are repeated after 2, 8, 18 and 32 elements because electronic configuration of the elements are repeated after 2, 8, 18 and 32 element.

## Hots Questions

1. The ionization energy increases as we move left to right across a period. It decreases as we move from the top of a group to the bottom. Because $\mathrm{Na}, \mathrm{P}$, and Ar are in the same period of the periodic table, therefore ionisation energy vary in the order : $\mathrm{Na}<\mathrm{P}<\mathrm{Ar}$.
Because Ne is above Ar in group 18, we expect Ne to exhibit the greater first ionization energy : $\mathrm{Ar}<\mathrm{Ne}$
Similarly, K is the alkali metal directly below Na in group IA, so we expect IE1 for K to be less than that of $\mathrm{Na}: \mathrm{K}<\mathrm{Na}$
From these observations we conclude that the first ionization energies follow the order : $\mathrm{K}<\mathrm{Na}<\mathrm{P}<\mathrm{Ar}<\mathrm{Ne}$.
2. In comparing ionic radii, it is useful to classify the ions into three categories: (1) isoelectronic ions, (2) ions carrying the same charge and are generated from atoms of the same periodic group, and (3) ions carrying different charges but are generated from the same atom. In case (1), ions carrying a greater negative charge are always larger; in case (2), ions from atoms having a greater atomic number are always larger; in case (3), ions having a greater positive charge are always smaller.
(a) $\mathrm{N}^{3-}$ and $\mathrm{F}^{-}$are isoelectronic anions. Because $\mathrm{N}^{3-}$ has only seven protons and $\mathrm{F}^{-}$has nine, $\mathrm{N}^{3-}$ is larger.
(b) Both Mg and Ca belong to Group 2A (the alkaline earth metals). The $\mathrm{Ca}^{2+}$ ion is larger than $\mathrm{Mg}^{2+}$ because valence electrons in Ca are in 4th shell while in Mg valence electrons are in 3rd shell.
(c) $\mathrm{Fe}^{2+}$ is larger
3. (a) $\mathrm{C}<\mathrm{O}<\mathrm{F}$
(b) $\mathrm{Sr}<\mathrm{Ru}<\mathrm{Te}$
4. (a) W , because density increases with atomic number
(b) Os, density increases with atomic number
(c) Pd, density decreases at the end of a row
(d) Nb , density increases with atomic number.
5. Group 18 elements have their outermost shell completely filled and the atoms of these elements have no tendency to gain or lose electrons. Thus, the elements of this group are zero valent and almost unreactive.
6. (a) Beryllium is in the same period with carbon and is to its left, so it is larger of the two. Magnesium is below beryllium in the same group and is larger. The order of increasing size is $\mathrm{C}<\mathrm{Be}<\mathrm{Mg}$.
(b) Rubidium is far to the left in the same period as iodine and is larger; bromine is above iodine and is the smallest among the three: $\mathrm{Br}<\mathrm{I}<\mathrm{Rb}$.
7. This is an isoelectronic series of ions, with all ions having 18 electrons. In such a series, size decreases as the charge of the cation increases and size increases as the charge of anion increases. The atomic numbers of the ions are S (16), Cl (17), K (19) and Ca (20). Thus, size of the ions decrease in the order :
$\mathrm{S}^{2-}>\mathrm{Cl}^{-}>\mathrm{K}^{+}>\mathrm{Ca}^{2+}$
8. $\mathrm{Mg}^{2+}<\mathrm{Na}^{+}<\mathrm{F}^{-}<\mathrm{O}^{2-}<\mathrm{N}^{3-}$

In a group ionic radii increases as the atomic number increases. In a period ionic radii decreases as we move from left to right
9. The outermost shell of alkaline earth metals has two electrons. For the process

$$
\mathrm{M}(\mathrm{~g})+\mathrm{e}^{-} \longrightarrow \mathrm{M}^{-}(\mathrm{g})
$$

where M denotes a member of the Group IIA, extra electron to be added must enter the outermost subshell. The outermost shell is completely filled means there is no vacancy for electron to be added. Therefore, electron affinity for IIA elements is negative value or small positive value.

## Text - Book Questions

1. Yes, Dobereiner's triads exists in the column of Newland's octaves e.g.
Li Be
Na Mg
K Ca
2. Dobereiner's triads were dismissed because all the known elements of that time could not be arranged in triads.
3. K is in 1st group and has valency one. Thus the formula of its oxide formed is $\mathrm{K}_{2} \mathrm{O}$. C and Si are in group 14, hence have valency 4. Thus the formulae of oxides formed are $\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ or $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ \& $\mathrm{SiO}_{2}$. Aluminium is in group 13 and has valency 3, thus the formula of its oxide formed is $\mathrm{Al}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3} . \mathrm{Ba}$ is in second group and has valency 2 , thus the formula of its oxide formed is BaO .
4. Germanium and scandium.
5. The criteria used by Mendeleev in creating his periodic table was classfication of elements on the basis of their atomic masses.
6. Noble gases are placed in a separate 18th group because all of them have completely filled outermost shell.
7. (i) Classification of elements in modern periodic table is based on the atomic number of the elements, which is their fundamental property.
(ii) Same position of the isotopes of an element in the periodic table can be justified as isotopes have same atomic number but different mass number .
(iii) The systematic grouping of elements into four blocks i.e. $s, p, d$ and $f$ has made the study of these elements quite simple.
8. Calcium and barium because they have same number of valence electrons as magnesium, i.e., they are in same group of periodic table.
9. (a) Lithium (Li), Sodium (Na) and Potassium (K).
(b) Magnesium (Mg) and Calcium (Ca).
(c) Neon (Ne), Argon (Ar) and Krypton (Kr)
10. (a) All these are alkali metals, placed in group 1 of periodic table and has one electron in their valence shell.
(b) Both are noble gases and have completely filled electrons valence shells.
11. Lithium and Berylium.
12. Be, the elements placed on the extreme left side of the periodic table have maximum metallic character. Among the given elements Be is placed in group 2, thus has maximum metallic character.

3.

6. (a) Element A belongs to group 17 which is on the extreme right side of the periodic table. Hence, A is a non-metal. It is a halogen.
(b) In group 17 (halogens) the reactivity decreases from fluorine to iodine.
$\mathrm{F}_{2}>\mathrm{Cl}_{2}>\mathrm{Br}_{2}>\mathrm{I}_{2}$

$A(17)=2,8,7$

7. $\mathrm{N}(7)=2,5$
$\mathrm{P}(15)=2,8,5$
Atomic size increases on moving down the group consequently effective nuclear charge decreases which results in decreases in electronegativity of an atom. Therefore N is more electronegative than P .
8. If we know the electronic configuration of an element, we can know its period from the number of shells present in its atom and from number of electrons in the valence shell of its atoms we can know its group e.g.
Atomic number of chlorine $=17$
Its electronic configuration $=2,8,7$
$\therefore \quad$ Number of shells $=3$
∴ $\quad$ Chlorine belongs to 3rd period.
As chlorine atom has seven electron in its valence shell.
$\therefore \quad$ It is present in group 17.
9. Element with atomic numbers 12 and 38 resembles calcium since they have two electrons in their valence shell like calcium.

Atomic number increase on moving down the group.
10.

|  | Mendeleev's Periodic Table |  | Modern Periodic Table |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1. | Elements are arranged in the order of increasing atomic mass. | 1. | Elements are arranged in the order of increasing atomic number. |
| 2. | There are nine vertical columns called groups. | 2. | There are 18 vertical column called groups. |
| 3. | Transition elements are arbitrarily placed together in group VIII. | 3. | Transition elements are placed in the middle of long periods. |
