## Think out of the box

## CASE STUDY

1. When an electron is present in the valence shell of an atom, it is attracted toward nucleus by nuclear force of attraction. In order to remove this electron, energy is required to overcome this force of attraction. During the addition of an electron to a neutral atom, the electron approaches neutral atom due to nuclear force of attraction. This process involves work done towards the force of attraction and hence energy is released. Thus, ionization potential is the energy absorbed and electron affinity is the energy released.
2. The given elements $\mathrm{x}, \mathrm{y}$ and z are Ti, Zr and Hf respectively. They belong to $4^{\text {th }}, 5^{\text {th }}$ and $6^{\text {th }}$ period of transitions series. Since they belong to same group and the value of ionization potential (I.P.) decreases down the group, hence the I.P. decrease from Ti to Zr. However, the I.P. value of Hf is double to the I.P. value of Zr due of the presence of lanthanide elements that causes lanthanide contraction.
3. (i) $\mathrm{F}<\mathrm{N}<\mathrm{Be}<\mathrm{Li}$
(ii) $\mathrm{Cu}<\mathrm{Ag}<\mathrm{Au}$
(iii) $\mathrm{B}<\mathrm{Na}<\mathrm{Ca}<\mathrm{Cs}$
(iv) $\mathrm{Yb}<\mathrm{Eu}<\mathrm{Pr}<\mathrm{Ce}<\mathrm{La}$

## 6 Chemical Kinetics

## Foundation

## (CONCEPT MAP )

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-240.jpg?height=1726&width=1466&top_left_y=824&top_left_x=315)

## CLASSIFICATION OF CHEMICAL REACTIONS BASED ON THEIR SPEED

In a chemical reaction, the bonds present in the reactants are broken and new bonds are formed to give the products. The speed of bond formation and bond breaking in different reactions is different. Chemical kinetics tells about the rate of a reaction. Depending upon the rate of reaction, by which the reactants are converted into products, reactions are classified in three types.

## (a) Fast or Instantaneous Reactions

Those reactions in which the reactants readily change into products within few seconds are fast reactions. Ionic reactions are fast reactions.

## Example :

Acid-base reactions complete in a fraction of second, e.g. Neutralisation of sodium hydroxide by hydrochloric acid.

$\underset{\substack{\text { Sodium } \\ \text { hydroxide }}}{\mathrm{NaOH}(\mathrm{aq})}+\underset{\substack{\text { Hydrochloric } \\ \text { acid }}}{\mathrm{HCl}(\mathrm{aq})} \longrightarrow \underset{\substack{\text { Sodium } \\ \text { chloride }}}{\mathrm{NaCl}(\mathrm{aq})}+\underset{\text { Water }}{\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l})}$

## (b) Moderate Reactions

Those chemical reactions which take few minutes or hours to complete are moderate reactions. (Neither very slow nor very fast)

## Example :

(i) $\mathrm{N}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{5} \xrightarrow{55^{\circ} \mathrm{C}} 2 \mathrm{NO}_{2}+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{O}_{2}$
(ii) $\underset{\text { Sucrose }}{\mathrm{C}_{12} \mathrm{H}_{22} \mathrm{O}_{11}}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \longrightarrow \underset{\text { Glucose }}{\mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{12} \mathrm{O}_{6}}+\underset{\text { Fructose }}{\mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{12} \mathrm{O}_{6}}$

## (c) Slow Reactions

Those reactions which take very long time to complete are called slow reactions.

## Example :

(i) Rusting of iron takes place in days or months.

$$
2 \mathrm{Fe}+\frac{3}{2} \mathrm{O}_{2}+x \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Fe}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3} \cdot x \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}
$$

(ii) Weathering of rocks and the reactions in earth's crust continue for several years.

Some reaction are slow. For example; reaction between potassium permaganate and oxalic acid or hydrogen peroxide solution proceed with slow rate. There are several reactions which are very slow and take very long time for completion.

## At what rate following reactions occur

(a) $\mathbf{A g N O}_{3}+\mathbf{K B r} \longrightarrow \mathbf{A g B r}+\mathbf{K N O}_{3}$
(b) $\mathbf{N H}_{\mathbf{4}} \mathbf{N O}_{\mathbf{2}} \longrightarrow \mathbf{N}_{\mathbf{2}}+\mathbf{2} \mathbf{H}_{\mathbf{2}} \mathbf{O}$

## SOLUTION

Reaction (a), being ionic reaction occur at faster rate. Reaction (b), it is a molecular reaction and will take time for the breaking of existing bonds and the formation of new bonds, hence it is a slow reaction.

## Learn More

An elementary reaction is that which occurs in a single step, while a reaction which involves more than one elementary reactions is known as complex or stepwise or composite reaction.

Example : Reaction between $\mathrm{NO}_{2}$ and CO

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \qquad \begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{NO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+\mathrm{NO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \longrightarrow \mathrm{NO}_{3}(\mathrm{~g})+\mathrm{NO}(\mathrm{~g}) \\
& \text { Overall reactions } \mathrm{NO}_{3}(\mathrm{~g})+\mathrm{CO}(\mathrm{~g}) \longrightarrow \mathrm{NO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+\mathrm{CO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \\
& \cline { 2 - 2 } 2 \mathrm{NO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+\mathrm{CO}(\mathrm{~g}) \longrightarrow \mathrm{NO}(\mathrm{~g})+\mathrm{NO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+\mathrm{CO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})
\end{aligned}
\end{aligned}
$$

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-242.jpg?height=159&width=421&top_left_y=890&top_left_x=196)

Take few mL of aqueous solutions of zinc sulphate and barium chloride in two separate test tubes and mix them immediately you will get white precipitate of insoluble solid. Now take an apple and cut it from side after some time you will notice that the exposed part of apple turns brown. Now take the help of resources like internet, science magazine etc. Discuss with your friends and teacher and try to find out exact reason for your observations.

## REACTION RATE AND ITS MEASUREMENT

Let us consider a simple hypothetical chemical reaction,

$$
\mathrm{X} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Y}
$$

The progress of the chemical reaction is followed by measuring the change in concentration of either reactant or product. Hence the rate of a reaction may be defined as the decrease in the concentration of reactant per unit time or increase in the concentration of product per unit time. It represents the average rate of reaction. So, mathematically the rate of reaction is expressed as given below.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text { Average rate of reaction } & =\frac{\text { Decrease in concentration of reactant }}{\text { Time interval }} \\
& =\frac{\text { Amount of reactant consumed }}{\text { Time interval }} \\
& =-\frac{\Delta[\mathrm{X}]}{\Delta t}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text { Similarly, Average rate of reaction } & =\frac{\text { Increase in concentration of product }}{\text { Time interval }} \\
& =\frac{\text { Amount of product formed }}{\text { Time interval }} \\
& =+\frac{\Delta[\mathrm{Y}]}{\Delta t}
\end{aligned}
$$

Reaction rates are always positive. The negative sign along with the change in concentration of the reactant shows that the concentration of the reactant decreases with time while the plus sign along with the concentration of the product denotes that the concentration of the product increases with time.
The decrease in concentration of the reactant and increase in concentration of the product can graphically be represented in the following fig.

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-243.jpg?height=492&width=480&top_left_y=370&top_left_x=829)
Fig. Variation of concentration with time.

Now let us extend this concept of expressing the rate of reaction to different kind of chemical reactions.
(a) Reactions Having Same Stoichiometric Coefficients of the Reactants and Products

For the reaction,

$$
\mathrm{PCl}_{5} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{PCl}_{3}+\mathrm{Cl}_{2}
$$

Suppose during a time interval, $\Delta t$,
Decrease in concentration of $\mathrm{PCl}_{5}=\Delta\left[\mathrm{PCl}_{5}\right]$
Increase in concentration of $\mathrm{PCl}_{3}=\Delta\left[\mathrm{PCl}_{3}\right]$
Increase in concentration of $\mathrm{Cl}_{2}=\Delta\left[\mathrm{Cl}_{2}\right]$
Where square brackets show molar concentration, i.e, moles litre ${ }^{-1}$ of the substance.
Thus, rate of reaction is given by
Rate of reaction $=-\frac{\Delta\left[\mathrm{PCl}_{5}\right]}{\Delta t}=+\frac{\Delta\left[\mathrm{PCl}_{3}\right]}{\Delta t}=+\frac{\Delta\left[\mathrm{Cl}_{2}\right]}{\Delta t}$
Likewise, for the reaction

$$
\mathrm{NO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+\mathrm{CO}(\mathrm{~g}) \longrightarrow \mathrm{CO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+\mathrm{NO}(\mathrm{~g})
$$

Rate of reaction $=-\frac{\Delta\left[\mathrm{NO}_{2}\right]}{\Delta t}=-\frac{\Delta[\mathrm{CO}]}{\Delta t}=+\frac{\Delta\left[\mathrm{CO}_{2}\right]}{\Delta t}=+\frac{\Delta[\mathrm{NO}]}{\Delta t}$
(b) Reactions Having Different Stoichiometric Coefficients of Reactants and Products

Let us consider the reaction involving the thermal decomposition of nitrogen pentoxide ( $\mathrm{N}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{5}$ ) as shown below,

$$
2 \mathrm{~N}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{5}(\mathrm{~g}) \rightleftharpoons 4 \mathrm{NO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+\mathrm{O}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})
$$

then,
The rate of disappearance of $\mathrm{N}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{5}=-\frac{\Delta\left[\mathrm{N}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{5}\right]}{\Delta t}$
The rate of appearance of $\mathrm{NO}_{2}=-\frac{\Delta\left[\mathrm{N}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{5}\right]}{\Delta t}$
The rate of appearance of $\mathrm{O}_{2}=+\frac{\Delta\left[\mathrm{O}_{2}\right]}{\Delta t}$
From the given reaction, it is evident that for each mole of $\mathrm{O}_{2}$ formed, four moles of $\mathrm{NO}_{2}$ are formed and two moles of $\mathrm{N}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{5}$ are disappearing. Therefore, the rate of appearance of $\mathrm{NO}_{2}$ is four times the rate of appearance of $\mathrm{O}_{2}$. The rate of disappearance of $\mathrm{N}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{5}$ is twice the rate of appearance of $\mathrm{O}_{2}$.

So, the rate of reaction irrespective of the species selected is expressed in the following form,
Rate of reaction $=-\frac{\Delta\left[\mathrm{N}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{5}\right]}{2 \Delta t}=+\frac{\Delta\left[\mathrm{NO}_{2}\right]}{4 \Delta t}=+\frac{\Delta\left[\mathrm{O}_{2}\right]}{\Delta t}$
In brief, the rate of reaction can be equated for any species (either reactant or product) by just dividing the rate of change of concentration by the stoichiometric coefficient of reactant or product expressed by the reaction.
Similarly, for the reaction

$$
\mathrm{N}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+3 \mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{NH}_{3}(\mathrm{~g})
$$

the rate of reaction can be represented by

$$
-\frac{\Delta\left[\mathrm{N}_{2}\right]}{\Delta t}=-\frac{\Delta\left[\mathrm{H}_{2}\right]}{3 \Delta t}=+\frac{\Delta\left[\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right]}{2 \Delta t}
$$

So, we can sum up the important points to express the rate of reaction as follows :
(i) The rate of a reaction is expressed by the change in concentration divided by the time taken for the change.
(ii) The rate of reaction can be expessed in terms of the reactant or the product both. Since molar concentration of reactant decreases with time and that of the product increases with time, the change in molar concentration of reactant is negative whereas that of product is positive.
(iii) The different expressions for the rate can be made equivalent by dividing the rate expression by the stoichiometric coefficient shown by the balanced chemical equation.

## Units of The Rate of Reaction

The units of the rate of reaction depend on the unit used to express the concentration of the reactant or product.
(i) Concentration expressed in moles litre ${ }^{-1}$

The concentration is usually expressed in moles litre ${ }^{-1}$ and the time in seconds or minutes, Therefore, the unit of the rate of reaction is moles litre ${ }^{-1} \mathrm{sec}^{-1}$ or moles litre ${ }^{-1} \mathrm{~min}^{-1}$
(ii) Concentration expressed in terms of partial pressure.

When the concentration of either gaseous reactant or product or both is expressed in atmosphere then the unit of the rate of reaction is given as atmosphere per second ( $\mathrm{atm} \mathrm{sec}^{-1}$ )
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-244.jpg?height=200&width=1620&top_left_y=1667&top_left_x=194)

## ILLUSTRATION : 1

For the reaction, $3 \mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+\mathrm{N}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{NH}_{3}(\mathrm{~g})$, how are the rate of reaction expressions, $-\frac{\mathrm{d}\left[\mathrm{H}_{2}\right]}{\mathrm{d} t}$ and $\frac{\mathrm{d}\left[\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right]}{\mathrm{d} t}$ interrelated?

## SOLUTION :

Rate of reaction can be expressed as: $-\frac{1}{3} \frac{\mathrm{~d}\left[\mathrm{H}_{2}\right]}{\mathrm{d} t}=-\frac{1}{3} \frac{\mathrm{~d}\left[\mathrm{H}_{2}\right]}{\mathrm{d} t}$

$$
+\frac{1}{2} \frac{\mathrm{~d}\left[\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right]}{\mathrm{d} t}=\frac{3}{2} \frac{\mathrm{~d}\left[\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right]}{\mathrm{d} t} .
$$

- Express the rate of following reactions in terms of concentrations of reactants and products
(i) $\mathrm{PCl}_{5} \longrightarrow \mathrm{PCl}_{3}+\mathrm{Cl}_{2}$
(ii) $\quad \mathbf{2} \mathrm{NO}_{2} \longrightarrow \mathbf{2 N O}+\mathrm{O}_{2}$


## SOLUTION

(i) Rate of reaction $=-\frac{\mathrm{d}\left[\mathrm{PCl}_{5}\right]}{\mathrm{d} t}=\frac{\mathrm{d}\left[\mathrm{PCl}_{3}\right]}{\mathrm{d} t}=\frac{\mathrm{d}\left[\mathrm{Cl}_{2}\right]}{\mathrm{d} t}$
(ii) Rate of reaction $=-\frac{1}{2} \frac{\mathrm{~d}\left[\mathrm{NO}_{2}\right]}{\mathrm{d} t}=\frac{1}{2} \frac{\mathrm{~d}[\mathrm{NO}]}{\mathrm{d} t}=\frac{\mathrm{d}\left[\mathrm{O}_{2}\right]}{\mathrm{d} t}$

## AVERAGE RATE AND INSTANTANEOUS RATE

Since the rate of reaction does not remain constant throughout the reaction time the average rate over the time, $\Delta t$ discussed in above expressions is used as the rate of reaction. However, remember that the average rate does not specify the true rate of reaction at a particular time. So, a new term instantaneous rate of reaction is used to specify the rate of reaction at a particular instant. Therefore, the instantaneous rate may be defined as the rate of change of concentration of any one of the reactants or products at a specific instant of time.
To get instantaneous rate, the time interval ( $\Delta t$ ) is made infinitesimally small ( $\Delta t \rightarrow 0$ ) so that the change in concentration during that time interval remains almost uniform, i.e., the concentration changes when $\Delta t$ approaches zero. Mathematically, instantaneous rate may be expressed by the expression $\frac{\mathrm{d} x}{\mathrm{~d} t}$ instead of $\frac{\Delta x}{\Delta t}$, where $\mathrm{d} t$ is infinitesimally small time change. For example, for a gaseous reaction

$$
2 \mathrm{SO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+\mathrm{O}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{SO}_{3}(\mathrm{~g})
$$

Average rate is given as,

$$
-\frac{1}{2} \frac{\Delta\left[\mathrm{SO}_{2}\right]}{\Delta t}=-\frac{\Delta\left[\mathrm{O}_{2}\right]}{\Delta t}=+\frac{1}{2} \frac{\Delta\left[\mathrm{SO}_{3}\right]}{\Delta t}
$$

But the instantaneous rate,

$$
\text { As } t \rightarrow 0 \frac{\Delta\left[\mathrm{O}_{2}\right]}{\Delta t}=\frac{\mathrm{d}\left[\mathrm{O}_{2}\right]}{\mathrm{d} t}
$$

Hence the instantaneous rate expression for the reaction will be,

$$
-\frac{1}{2} \frac{\Delta\left[\mathrm{SO}_{2}\right]}{\Delta t}=-\frac{\Delta\left[\mathrm{O}_{2}\right]}{\Delta t}=+\frac{1}{2} \frac{\Delta\left[\mathrm{SO}_{3}\right]}{\Delta t}
$$

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-245.jpg?height=99&width=394&top_left_y=1964&top_left_x=872)

A clear distinction between average rate and instantaneous rate can be made by following example. Suppose you travelled 10 km distance in 15 min , then your speed is $40 \mathrm{~km} / \mathrm{h}$. It is average speed and it is not necessary that you travelled with this speed all the time. Instantaneous speed is your speed at a particular moment and it can be calculated by dividing 15 minutes into infinitely small time intervals.

## For the given general reaction write average and instantaneous rate

$$
a \mathrm{~A}+b \mathrm{~B} \longrightarrow c \mathrm{C}+d \mathrm{D}
$$

## SOLUTION

Average rate $=-\frac{1}{a} \frac{\Delta[\mathrm{~A}]}{\Delta t}=-\frac{1}{b} \frac{\Delta[\mathrm{~B}]}{\Delta t}=\frac{1}{c} \frac{\Delta[\mathrm{C}]}{\Delta t}=\frac{1}{d} \frac{\Delta[\mathrm{D}]}{\Delta t}$
Instantaneous rate $=\frac{-1}{a} \frac{\mathrm{~d}[\mathrm{~A}]}{\mathrm{d} t}=-\frac{1}{b} \frac{\mathrm{~d}[\mathrm{~B}]}{\mathrm{d} t}=\frac{1}{c} \frac{\mathrm{~d}[\mathrm{C}]}{\mathrm{d} t}=\frac{1}{d} \frac{\mathrm{~d}[\mathrm{D}]}{\mathrm{d} t}$

## FACTORS AFFECTING RATE OF A REACTION

The rate of a chemical reaction is not a fixed quantity and varies with the experimental conditions. The rate of reaction depends on the following factors :
(a) Effect of Concentration
(b) Effect of Temperature
(c) Effect of Catalyst
(d) Effect of Light
(e) Effect of Surface Area
(a) Effect of Concentration on Reaction Rate

The rate of reaction increases as the concentration of reactant increases and vice versa.
Rate $\propto$ Concentration of reactant
Example: $\mathrm{Zn}(\mathrm{s})+\underset{\text { Zinc }}{\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}(\mathrm{aq})} \longrightarrow \underset{\text { Zulphuric acid }}{\mathrm{ZnSO}_{4}(\mathrm{aq})}+\mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})$
On increasing the conc. of $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}$ the rate of evolution of $\mathrm{H}_{2}$ will increase.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-246.jpg?height=400&width=433&top_left_y=1238&top_left_x=1384)

Different reactants can affect the rate of a particular reaction to different extents.

$$
\begin{gathered}
2 \mathrm{NO}(\mathrm{~g}) \\
\text { Nitric oxide }
\end{gathered}+\mathrm{O}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \longrightarrow \begin{gathered}
2 \mathrm{NO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \\
\text { Nitrogen dioxide }
\end{gathered}
$$

(i) The rate of this reaction becomes double when concentration of $\mathrm{O}_{2}$ is doubled.
(ii) The rate of this reaction becomes four times or quadruples when the concentration of NO is doubled.

Reason : For a reaction to occur, the reactant molecules must collide with each other. The probability of such collisions increases with increase in concentration of reactants.Thus, more is the number of collisions taking place per unit volume, more will be the rate of reaction.

$$
\text { i.e } \quad \text { Rate of reaction } \propto \text { [Reactants] }
$$

## Rate Law and Rate Constant

The mathematical expression which denotes the experimentally observed rate of a reaction in terms of the concentrations of the reacting species which influence the rate of reaction is termed as rate law.
Consider a general reaction

$$
a \mathrm{~A}+b \mathrm{~B}+c \mathrm{C} \longrightarrow \text { Products }
$$

Where $\mathrm{A}, \mathrm{B}$ and C are the reactants and $a, b$ and $c$ are stoichiometric coefficients of the balanced equation.
Rate of above reaction

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text { Rate } & =k[\mathrm{~A}]^{a}[\mathrm{~B}]^{b}[\mathrm{C}]^{c} \\
k & =\text { Rate constant }
\end{aligned}
$$

For example
Rate law expression :

$$
2 \mathrm{NO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+\mathrm{F}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{NO}_{2} \mathrm{~F}(\mathrm{~g})
$$

$$
\text { Rate }=k\left[\mathrm{NO}_{2}\right]\left[\mathrm{F}_{2}\right]
$$

Rate law expression according to law of mass action; Rate $=k\left[\mathrm{NO}_{2}\right]^{2}\left[\mathrm{~F}_{2}\right]$

Did You Know?
$\boldsymbol{a}, \boldsymbol{b}$ and $\boldsymbol{c}$ are coefficients of reactants in reaction. It is not necessary that they represent true
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-247.jpg?height=186&width=120&top_left_y=561&top_left_x=456)
powers of concentration of these reactants that should be determined experimentally.

If all the concentration of reactants are taken as unity i.e. $[\mathrm{A}]=[\mathrm{B}]=1$ mole/litre then Rate $=k$; where rate is termed as the specific reaction rate and $k$ is called as rate constant. Thus rate constant may be defined as the rate of reaction when the concentration of each reactant is taken as unity. Therefore, the rate constant is also referred as specific reaction rate.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-247.jpg?height=108&width=387&top_left_y=886&top_left_x=868)

## Order of a Reaction

Order of a reaction is an important parameter which is always determined experimentally. Let us consider a hypothetical reaction

$$
a \mathrm{X}+b \mathrm{Y}+c \mathrm{Z} \rightarrow \text { Product }
$$

Where $\mathrm{X}, \mathrm{Y}$ and Z are the reactants and $a, b$ and $c$ are the stoichiometric coefficients. Rate law expression for this reaction based on the experimental determination is

$$
\text { Rate }=k[\mathrm{X}]^{l}[\mathrm{Y}]^{m}[\mathrm{Z}]^{n}
$$

Where $k$ is the rate constant and $[\mathrm{X}],[\mathrm{Y}]$ and $[\mathrm{Z}]$ are molar concentrations of the reactants $\mathrm{X}, \mathrm{Y}$ and Z respectively. The exponents $l, m$ and $n$ are the order with respect to $\mathrm{X}, \mathrm{Y}$ and Z respectively.

The sum of exponents $l, m, n$ is known as the overall order of the reaction.

$$
\text { Order of reaction }=l+m+n
$$

These power or exponents ( $l, m$ or $n$ ) have no relation with the stoichiometric coefficients $a, b$ and $c$ of reactants. Following examples illsutrate the order of reaction.

|  | Reaction | Rate law | Reaction order |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1. | $2 \mathrm{~N}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{5} \longrightarrow 4 \mathrm{NO}_{2}+\mathrm{O}_{2}$ | Rate $=k\left[\mathrm{~N}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{5}\right]$ | 1 |
| 2. | $\mathrm{H}_{2}+\mathrm{I}_{2} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{HI}$ | Rate $=k\left[\mathrm{H}_{2}\right]\left[\mathrm{l}_{2}\right]$ | $1+1=2$ |
| 3. | $2 \mathrm{NO}_{2} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{NO}+\mathrm{O}_{2}$ | Rate $=k\left[\mathrm{NO}_{2}\right]_{2}$ | 2 |
| 4. | $2 \mathrm{NO}+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \longrightarrow \mathrm{~N}_{2}+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ | Rate $=k\left[\mathrm{H}_{2}\right][\mathrm{NO}]_{2}$ | $1+2=3$ |
| 5. | $2 \mathrm{NO}+\mathrm{O}_{2} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{NO}_{2}$ | Rate $=k[\mathrm{NO}]_{2}\left[\mathrm{O}_{2}\right]$ | $2+1=3$ |
| 6. | $\mathrm{CHCl}_{3}+\mathrm{Cl}_{2} \longrightarrow \mathrm{CCl}_{4}+\mathrm{HCl}$ | Rate $=k\left[\mathrm{CHCl}_{3}\right]\left[\mathrm{Cl}_{2}\right]^{1 / 2}$ | $1+1 / 2=1 \frac{1}{2}$ |
| 7. | $\mathrm{H}_{2}+\mathrm{Br}_{2} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{HBr}$ | Rate $=k\left[\mathrm{H}_{2}\right]\left[\mathrm{Br}_{2}\right]^{1 / 2}$ | $1+1 / 2=1 \frac{1}{2}$ |

Thus the order of a reaction can be defined as the sum of the powers of the concentration terms, as expressed in rate law.

## Molecularity of a Reaction

The molecularity of a reaction is defined as the total number of molecules of the reactants taking part in a reaction, i.e., the molecularity represents the number of reactant molecules undergoing collisions to bring about the chemical reaction. Thus in the reaction,

$$
\begin{gathered}
a \mathrm{~A}+b \mathrm{~B} \longrightarrow \text { Product. } \\
\text { Molecularity }=a+b
\end{gathered}
$$

The molecularity of the reaction may be $1,2,3$ etc. according to the participation of one, two or three reactant molecules in the reaction.

## ILLUSTRATION : 2

The rate of a reaction becomes eight times when the concentration of the single reactant $\mathbf{X}$ is made twice. Write rate law of reaction.

## SOLUTION :

The effect of concentration on the rate of reaction is of 3rd order w.r.t. X.
$\therefore \quad$ Rate $=k[\mathrm{X}]^{3}$

## ILLUSTRATIOM : 3

What is the effect on the rate of following reaction if concentration of nitrogen dioxide is doubled?

$$
2 \mathrm{NO}_{2} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{NO}+\mathrm{O}_{2}
$$

## SOLUTION :

Rate of above reaction $=k\left[\mathrm{NO}_{2}\right]^{2}$
Thus if we double the concentration of $\mathrm{NO}_{2}$
Rate of reaction $=k\left[2 \mathrm{NO}_{2}\right]^{2}=k\left[2 \mathrm{NO}_{2} \times 2 \mathrm{NO}_{2}\right]=4 k\left[\mathrm{NO}_{2}\right]^{2}$
∴ Rate of above reaction increases 4 times.

## (b) Effect of Temperature on Reaction Rate

Generally reactions take place much faster at a higher temperature. It means on increasing temperature rate of reaction increases.

$$
\text { Rate } \propto \text { Temperature }
$$

Reason : (i) As the temperature increases, the kinetic energy of the reactant molecules increases, hence, the number of collisions also increases.
(ii) At higher temperature the reactant molecules have greater chance of energy greater than the activation energy.
Note : Activation energy is the minimum amount of energy required to initiate the reaction.
Example : $\underset{\substack{\text { Calcium } \\ \text { carbonate }}}{\mathrm{CaCO}_{3}(\mathrm{~s})}+\underset{\substack{\text { Hydrochloric } \\ \text { acid }}}{2 \mathrm{HCl}(\mathrm{aq})} \longrightarrow \underset{\substack{\text { Calcium } \\ \text { chloride }}}{\mathrm{CaCl}_{2}(\mathrm{aq})}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l})+\mathrm{CO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})$
At 273 K the evolution of $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ is very slow, but at 323 K the evolution of $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ is rapid.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-248.jpg?height=402&width=492&top_left_y=1369&top_left_x=1335)

This graph shows that at higher temperature rate of evolution of $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ is also high.
The reactants do not change directly in the products, reactants first absorb a certain amount of energy above the average potential energy of the reactants so that they overcome energy barrier between reactants and products. Thus this minimum amount of energy required to overcome this energy barrier between reactant and product is called activation energy ( $E_{\mathrm{a}}$ ). For fast reactions, activation energies are low whereas for slow reactions, activation energies are high.

Activation energy $=$ [Threshold energy] - [Average energy of the reactants]

$$
E_{\mathrm{a}}=E_{\mathrm{t}}-E_{\mathrm{r}}
$$

$E_{\mathrm{a}}$-Activation energy
$E_{\mathrm{t}}$ - Threshold energy
$E_{\mathrm{r}}$ - Average energy of reactant
$E_{\mathrm{p}}$ - Energy of products
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-248.jpg?height=429&width=522&top_left_y=1982&top_left_x=1283)

Note : The minimum amount of energy which the colliding reactant molecules must possess to react is known as threshold energy.

## Learn

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-249.jpg?height=85&width=108&top_left_y=370&top_left_x=1014)

## More

- Effect of temperature on rate of reaction is observed for exothermic as well as for endothermic reactions. Reaction rate for most of the chemical reactions becomes almost double, for every $10^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ rise in temperature. This is expressed in terms of temperature coefficient.

$$
\text { Temperature coefficient }=\frac{\text { Rate constant at } 308 \mathrm{~K}}{\text { Rate constant at } 298 \mathrm{~K}}
$$

So temperature coefficient may be defined as the ratio of the constants of the reaction at two temperature differing by $10^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$.

- For some exceptional reactions, rate of reaction decreases with increase in temperature. For example rate of reaction between NO and $\mathrm{O}_{2}\left(2 \mathrm{NO}+\mathrm{O}_{2} \longrightarrow \mathrm{NO}_{2}\right)$, decreases with increase in temperature.
- Effect of temperature on reaction rate depends upon the nature of reaction.
(i) For Most Reactions : As temperature increases rate of reaction increases uniformly. (Fig. 1)
(ii) For Explosive Reactions : Initially as temperature increases reaction rate increases. As explosion occurs there is sudden rise in reaction rate. (Fig. 2)
(iii) For Enzyme Catalysed Reactions : Initially as temperature increases reaction rate increases, reaches a maximum value and then it drops. Thus a bell shaped curve is obtained. (Fig. 3)

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-249.jpg?height=268&width=314&top_left_y=1169&top_left_x=552)
Temperature

Fig. 1

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-249.jpg?height=286&width=307&top_left_y=1174&top_left_x=897)
Fig. 2

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-249.jpg?height=304&width=314&top_left_y=1165&top_left_x=1238)
Fig. 3

Time to Check Your Knowledge

- What kitchen device is used to lower the rate at which microorganisms grow on food ?


## SOLUTION

Microorganisms, such as bread mold, are everywhere and difficult to avoid. Lowering the temperatures of microorganism contaminated food by using the refrigerator, decreases the rate of the chemical reactions necessary for the growth of microorganisms. This ultimately prevents decaying of food.

## (c) Effect of Catalyst

A catalyst is a foreign substance which is added to reaction mixture to change the rate of chemical reaction (either increases or decreases) without itself undergoing any change. A catalyst is recovered unchanged in mass and composition at the end of the reaction.
e.g.

$$
2 \mathrm{KClO}_{3} \xrightarrow{\mathrm{MnO}_{2}} 2 \mathrm{KCl}+3 \mathrm{O}_{2}
$$

Here, $\mathrm{MnO}_{2}$ is acting as a catalyst to increase the rate of reaction.

## On the basis of physical state, catalyst are of two types -

(i) Homogeneous Catalysts : Those catalysts in which physical states of reactants \& catalyst are same.

$$
2 \mathrm{SO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+\mathrm{O}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \xrightarrow{\mathrm{NO}(\mathrm{~g})} \underset{\text { Sulphur trioxide }}{2 \mathrm{SO}_{3}(\mathrm{~g})}
$$

(ii) Heterogeneous Catalysts : Those catalysts in which physical states of reactants \& catalyst are different.

$$
\mathrm{N}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+3 \mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \xrightarrow{\mathrm{Fe}(\mathrm{~s})} \underset{\text { Ammonia }}{2 \mathrm{NH}_{3}(\mathrm{~g})}
$$

Enzymes are also called biocatalyst as they catalyse the biological reactions (reactions in living
Did You
Know!

On the basis of action of catalyst, catalyst are of two types -
(i) Positive Catalyst : Those catalysts which increase the rate of reaction are called positive catalyst.

$$
\underset{\text { Urea }}{\mathrm{NH}_{2} \mathrm{CONH}_{2}(\mathrm{aq})+\underset{\text { water }}{\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l})} \xrightarrow{\text { Urease }} \underset{\text { Ammonia }}{2 \mathrm{NH}_{3}(\mathrm{~g})}+\underset{\text { Carbondioxide }}{\mathrm{CO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})}}
$$

(ii) Negative Catalyst : Those catalysts which decrease the rate of reaction are called negative catalyst. They are also known as inhibitors.

$$
\underset{\text { Hydrogen peroxide }}{2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{2}(\mathrm{l})} \xrightarrow{\text { Glycerine }} 2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l})+\mathrm{O}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})
$$

Glycerine inhibits the decomposition of hydrogen peroxide.
Thus, a catalyst can have both positive and negative effects on the rate of a chemical reaction.
(d) Effect of Light on Reaction Rate

Those chemical reactions which are carried in the presence of light are known as photochemical reactions. Examples :
(i) Plants in presence of sunlight produces glucose. This is known as photosynthesis.

$$
6 \mathrm{CO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+12 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l}) \xrightarrow[\text { Sunlight }]{\text { Chlorophyll }} \underset{\text { Glucose }}{\mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{12} \mathrm{O}_{6}}(\mathrm{aq})+6 \mathrm{O}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+6 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}
$$

(ii) Oxygen in the upper atmosphere absorbs U. V. radiations \& gets converted into ozone.

$$
3 \mathrm{O}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \xrightarrow{h \nu} 2 \mathrm{O}_{3}(\mathrm{~g})
$$

(iii) When silver bromide is exposed to light it will break into silver \& bromine. This reaction occurs in photography.

$$
2 \mathrm{AgBr} \xrightarrow{h \nu} 2 \mathrm{Ag}(\mathrm{~s})+\mathrm{Br}_{2}
$$

Light supplies the necessary energy required for the reaction to take place.
Time to
Check Your Knowledge
(a) Hydrogen and oxygen gas as such does not react under normal condition to form water, which catalyst is required to carry out this reaction ?
(b) The reaction between $\mathbf{H}_{2}$ and $\mathbf{C l}_{2}$ is slow in dark but its rate increases when exposed to light. Explain.

## SOLUTION

(a) In the presence of a catalyst such as finely divided platinum, the reaction becomes quite vigorous and hydrogen and oxygen reacts in presence of finely divided platinum (catalyst) to form water.

$$
2 \mathrm{H}_{2}+\mathrm{O}_{2} \xrightarrow{\mathrm{Pt}} 2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}
$$

(b) The reaction between $\mathrm{H}_{2}$ and $\mathrm{Cl}_{2}$ is again a photochemical reaction. Photons present in incident light radiation having frequency $(v)$ which possess sufficient energy $(E=h v)$ to break bonds in reactants which then combines to form product molecules.

$$
\mathrm{H}_{2}+\mathrm{Cl}_{2} \xrightarrow{h \nu} 2 \mathrm{HCl}
$$

## (e) Effect of Surface Area

Rate of reaction is directly proportional to the surface area of the reactants. More will be the surface area, more will be the rate of reaction.

Rate of reaction $\propto$ Surface area of reactants
Example : $\underset{\text { Iron }}{\mathrm{Fe}(\mathrm{s})}+\underset{\text { Hydrochloric acid }}{2 \mathrm{HCl}(\mathrm{aq})} \longrightarrow \underset{\text { Ferrous chloride }}{\mathrm{FeCl}_{2}(\mathrm{aq})}+\mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})$
In this case, if metal is in powdered form then reaction would be more rapid than when taken in a ribbon form.
It is a common observation that small pieces of wood burn more rapidly than large logs of wood. This is due to the fact that the total surface area of the smaller particles is greater than that of large particles and this permits more molecules of the reactants to come in contact and form products.

Time to
Check Your
Knowledge

- Generally, if the solid is more finely divided the reaction will be faster but it does not happen in the case of gas reacting with powered solid.


## SOLUTION

If the solid powder was in one big heap, the gas may not be able to penetrate it. That means its effective surface area is much the same as (or even less than) it would be if it were present in a single lump.

## Learn More

Nature of reactants and the products : Rates of reactions are influenced by the nature of reactants and products. A chemical reaction involves the breaking of old bonds and formation of new bonds. The reactivity of a substance can, therefore, be related to the ease with which the specific bonds are broken or formed and the number of such bonds involved. For example, the oxidation of nitric oxide to nitrogen dioxide taken place fairly rapidly while oxidation of carbon monoxide to carbon dioxide takes place slowly:

$$
\begin{aligned}
& 2 \mathrm{NO}+\mathrm{O}_{2} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{NO}_{2} \\
& 2 \mathrm{CO}+\mathrm{O}_{2} \text { (fast) } \\
& 2 \mathrm{CO}_{2} \text { (slow) } \\
& \hline
\end{aligned}
$$

## ENERGY CHANGES IN CHEMICAL REACTIONS

It is common observation that when chemical reactions occur, they are accompanied by energy changes. Depending upon the evolution or absorption of energy, the chemical reactions can be classified into three types : exothermic, endothermic and photochemical reactions.

## (a) Exothermic Reactions

The chemical reactions which proceed with the evolution of heat energy are called exothermic reactions.
The heat energy produced during the reaction is indicated by writing $+q$ on the product side. In general, exothermic reactions may be represented as :

$$
\mathrm{A}+\mathrm{B} \longrightarrow \mathrm{C}+\mathrm{D}+q \text { (heat energy) }
$$

The heat evolved is expressed in the units of Joules (J) or kilo Joules (kJ).
Example : When one mole of carbon (coal) is burnt in oxygen, there is an evolution of 393.5 kJ of energy. It can be expressed as :

$$
\mathrm{C}(\mathrm{~s})+\mathrm{O}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \longrightarrow \mathrm{CO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+393.5 \mathrm{~kJ}
$$

(i) All combustion reactions are exothermic reactions.
(ii) The number of exothermic reactions are more than endothermic reactions.
(iii) During exothermic reaction, a part of the potential energy possessed by the reactants is released. Hence, products of an exothermic reaction have less potential energy than the reactants.

## (b) Endothermic Reactions

The chemical reactions which proceed with the absorption of heat energy are called endothermic reactions.
The heat energy absorbed during the reaction can be indicated by writing $+q$ with the reactants or it can be indicated by writing $-q$ (or the actual numerical value) with the products. In general, an endothermic reaction can be represented as,

$$
\mathrm{A}+\mathrm{B}+q(\text { heat }) \longrightarrow \mathrm{C}+\mathrm{D}
$$

or

$$
\mathrm{A}+\mathrm{B} \longrightarrow \mathrm{C}+\mathrm{D}-q \text { (heat), where } q \text { is the heat absorbed }
$$

Example : Formation of nitric oxide from nitrogen and oxygen proceeds with the absorption of 180.5 kJ of heat. It can be represented as :

$$
\mathrm{N}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+\mathrm{O}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{NO}(\mathrm{~g})-180.5 \mathrm{~kJ}
$$

(i) Decomposition reactions are generally endothermic.
(ii) The number of endothermic reactions is much less than the exothermic reactions.
(iii) During endothermic reactions, reactants gain energy. Hence, products of an endothermic reaction have more potential energy than the reactants.

Time to
Check Your Knowledge

## Dissolution of ammonium chloride in water is exothermic or endothermic reaction?

## SOLUTION

It is endothermic reaction.

$$
\mathrm{NH}_{4} \mathrm{Cl}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \longrightarrow \mathrm{NH}_{4} \mathrm{OH}+\mathrm{HCl}
$$

## Explanation for the energy changes during chemical reactions :

A chemical reaction involves the rearrangement of atoms. During the reaction certain bonds are broken while certain new bonds are formed between the atoms. Energy is absorbed for breaking the bonds and released during formation of bonds. If the energy required to break the bonds is more than the energy during formation of bonds then there is net absorption of energy and the reaction is endothermic. On the other hand, if the energy released during formation of bonds is more than the energy absorbed for breaking the bonds, then there is net release of energy and the reaction is exothermic.

## (c) Photochemical Reactions

The reactions which are either slow in dark or do not proceed, but takes place in the presence of radiations like sunlight or ultraviolet light. Such reactions are called photochemical reactions.

## Importance of photochemical reactions

(i) Photosynthesis is very common example of reaction occurring in plants by the light.
(ii) Light is used in the synthesis and manufacture of many organic compounds. This is called photochemical synthesis. For example, formaldehyde is synthesised from CO and $\mathrm{H}_{2}$ in the presence of Hg using light.
(iii) Polymerisation is generally initiated by light.
(iv) Photography is based upon photochemistry.
(v) Photo etching is used in electronic industry.
(vi) Printing technology also makes use of photochemical processes.
(vii) Sterilisation of water.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-253.jpg?height=191&width=333&top_left_y=374&top_left_x=255)

- Damaging effects of photochemistry-The most common damaging effect of photochemistry is the effect of sunlight on the dyes used for colouring of fabrics and other materials. Due to the effect of sunlight, the coloured fabrics or materials fade away.
- Some substances absorb radiation when exposed to it and immediately re-emit the absorbed energy. It is called fluorescence e.g., fluorite ( $\mathrm{CaF}_{2}$ ). Emission of light during a chemical reaction is known as chemiluminescence e.g., glowing of phosphorus in dark (due to oxidation).
- It may be noted that generally ultra-violet or visible radiations are used for carrying out photochemical reactions because their photons possess energies approximately of the order of 420 kJ per mol which is comparable to most of the bond energies. Thus, ultra-violet radiation energy is capable of breaking the bonds. On the other hand, infra-red radiations are generally not used because their photons possess energy of the order of 60 kJ per mol which is quite less for breaking of bonds.


## More

Photosensitization : Some substances do not react themselves even when exposed to sunlight. However, these can be made to react by the addition of a small quantity of a foreign substance. The role of the foreign substance is to absorb light energy and subsequently transfer it to one of the reactants. Such substance which can absorb light energy and thus induce a photochemical change in the reactants without itself taking part in the reaction is called a photosensitizer and the phenomenon is termed as photosensitization.

## ILLUSTRATIOM : 3

Distinguish between photochemical reactions and thermochemical reactions.

## SOLUTION :

|  | Photochemical reaction | Thermochemical reaction |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| (a) | These are initiated by absorption of radiations of appropriate energy. | These involve the absorption or evolution of heat. |
| (b) | These occur only in presence of light. | These can proceed even in the absence of light. |
| (c) | Variation of temperature has very little effect on the rate of a photochemical reaction. | Variation of temperature has significant effect on the rate of a thermochemical reaction. |
| (d) | Some of these reactions are initiated by the presence of photosensitizer whose role is different than of a catalyst. | The rate of such reactions can be altered by the use of a catalyst. |

Take 2 g of silver chloride in your school lab in two separate watch glasses A and B . Place watch glass A in the presence of sunlight whereas watch glass B in dark. Now after one hour weigh the amount of salt in both watch glasses. Record your observation and in case if you notice any change then discuss with your teacher to find exact reason for change.

(d) rate $=k[\mathrm{~A}]^{2} \cdot[\mathrm{~B}]^{0}$

(b) increases

(a) $\quad+\frac{\mathrm{d}\left[\mathrm{Br}_{2}\right]}{\mathrm{d} t}=-\frac{\mathrm{d}\left[\mathrm{Br}^{-}\right]}{\mathrm{d} t}$
(b) $+\frac{\mathrm{d}\left[\mathrm{Br}_{2}\right]}{\mathrm{d} t}=+\frac{3}{5} \frac{\mathrm{~d}\left[\mathrm{Br}^{-}\right]}{\mathrm{d} t}$
(c) $+\frac{\mathrm{d}\left[\mathrm{Br}_{2}\right]}{\mathrm{d} t}=-\frac{3}{5} \frac{\mathrm{~d}\left[\mathrm{Br}^{-}\right]}{\mathrm{d} t}$
(d) $+\frac{\mathrm{d}\left[\mathrm{Br}_{2}\right]}{\mathrm{d} t}=-\frac{5}{3} \frac{\mathrm{~d}\left[\mathrm{Br}^{-}\right]}{\mathrm{d} t}$

(a) exothermic reaction
(b) endothermic reaction

(d) catalytic reaction

$\mathrm{H}_{2}+\mathrm{I}_{2} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{HI}$ is

(b) $+\frac{\mathrm{d}\left[\mathrm{H}_{2}\right]}{\mathrm{d} t}=+\frac{\mathrm{d}\left[\mathrm{I}_{2}\right]}{\mathrm{d} t}=+\frac{1}{2} \frac{\mathrm{~d}[\mathrm{HI}]}{\mathrm{d} t}$
(c) $+\frac{1}{2} \frac{\mathrm{~d}\left[\mathrm{H}_{2}\right]}{\mathrm{d} t}=+\frac{1}{2} \frac{\mathrm{~d}\left[\mathrm{I}_{2}\right]}{\mathrm{d} t}=-\frac{\mathrm{d}[\mathrm{HI}]}{\mathrm{d} t}$
(d) $\quad-2 \frac{\mathrm{~d}\left[\mathrm{H}_{2}\right]}{\mathrm{d} t}=-2 \frac{\mathrm{~d}\left[\mathrm{I}_{2}\right]}{\mathrm{d} t}=+\frac{\mathrm{d}[\mathrm{HI}]}{\mathrm{d} t}$

## Passage Based Questions

DIRECTIONS: Study the given paragraph( $s$ ) and answer the following questions.

## PASSAGE

The minimum extra energy over and above the average potential energy of the reactants which must be supplied to the reactants to enable them to cross over the energy barrier between reactants and products is called activation energy. It depend on nature of reactant and is free from effect of temperature.

Activation $=$ [Threshold energy] - [average energy of energy the reactants]

$$
\Rightarrow \quad E_{\mathrm{a}}=E_{\mathrm{t}}-E_{\mathrm{R}}
$$

where, $E_{\mathrm{a}}=$ Activation energy
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-256.jpg?height=456&width=540&top_left_y=1233&top_left_x=315)

1. For an exothermic reaction, the energy of activation of the reactants is
(a) equal to the energy of activation of products.
(b) less than the energy of activation of products.
(c) greater than the energy of activation of products.
(d) sometimes greater and sometimes less than that of the products.
2. For an endothermic reaction the energy of activation of the reactant is
(a) equal to the energy of activation of products.
(b) less than the energy of activation of products.
(c) greater than the energy of activation of products.
(d) sometimes greater and sometimes less than that of the products.
3. Which of the following statement regarding activation energy is correct?
(a) It is the sum of threshold energy and average energy of reactants.
(b) For fast reactions activation energy is very high.
(c) For fast reactions activation energy is low.
(d) For slow reaction activation energy is low.

## Assertion \& Reason

DIRECTIONS : Each of these questions contains an assertion followed by reason. Read them carefully and answer the question on the basis of following options. You have to select the one that best describes the two statements.
(a) If both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of Assertion.
(b) If both Assertion and Reason are correct, but Reason is not the correct explanation of Assertion.
(c) If Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
(d) If Assertion is incorrect but Reason is correct.

1. Assertion : A catalyst remain unchanged in composition and quantity at the end of the reaction.
Reason : Catalysts are sometimes very specific in respect of reaction.
2. Assertion : The rate of reaction decreases with decrease of concentration and increases with increase of concentration of reactants.
Reason : The rate of reaction is directly proportional to the concentration of reactants.
3. Assertion : In the reaction,

$$
\mathrm{C}_{12} \mathrm{H}_{22} \mathrm{O}_{11}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \xrightarrow{\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}} \mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{12} \mathrm{O}_{6}+\mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{12} \mathrm{O}_{6}
$$

$\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}$ is called as a homogeneous catalyst.
Reason : $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}$ is in the same phase as the reactants.
4. Assertion : Emission of light during a chemical reaction is known as chemiluminescence.
Reason : Some substances absorb radiation when exposed to it and immediately re-emit the energy absorbed.

## Multiple Choice Questions (MCQs)

1. (b)
2. (b)
3. (a)
4. (c)
5. (c) $-\frac{1}{5} \frac{\mathrm{~d}\left[\mathrm{Br}^{-}\right]}{\mathrm{d} t}=+\frac{1}{3} \frac{\mathrm{~d}\left[\mathrm{Br}_{2}\right]}{\mathrm{d} t}$

$$
\therefore+\frac{\mathrm{d}\left[\mathrm{Br}_{2}\right]}{\mathrm{d} t}=-\frac{3}{5} \frac{\mathrm{~d}\left[\mathrm{Br}^{-}\right]}{\mathrm{d} t}
$$

6. (a) 7. (c)
7. (a) Increase in number of effective collisions.
8. (c) Activation energy is lowered in presence of positive catalyst.
9. (d)
10. (a) Enzymes are biological catalysts.
11. (d) In the given options $-\frac{1}{3} \frac{\mathrm{~d}[\mathrm{C}]}{\mathrm{d} t}$ will not represent the reaction rate. It should not have negative sign as it is product.
$+\frac{1}{3} \frac{\mathrm{dC}}{\mathrm{d} t}$ show the rate of formation of product C .
12. (b) The rate of a chemical reaction is directly proportional to the concentration of the reactants i.e. rate of reaction decreases with decrease of concentration of reactants and increase with increase in concentration of reactant. As the reaction progresses the reactant is converted into the product and thus the concentration of reactants decreases while that of the products increases.
13. (c) The catalytic action of enzymes is highly specific.
14. (c)
15. (b)
16. (b) Powdering the lumps of coal increases the surface area which increases the rate of reaction.
17. (b) $3 \mathrm{~A} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{~B}$

Rate of appearance of $B$ is equal to rate of disappearance of A.
$+\frac{1}{2} \frac{\mathrm{~d}[\mathrm{~B}]}{\mathrm{d} t}=-\frac{1}{3} \frac{\mathrm{~d}[\mathrm{~A}]}{\mathrm{d} t} \Rightarrow+\frac{\mathrm{d}[\mathrm{B}]}{\mathrm{d} t}=-\frac{2}{3} \frac{\mathrm{~d}[\mathrm{~A}]}{\mathrm{d} t}$
19. (c) Rate $=k[\mathrm{~A}][\mathrm{B}]=\mathrm{R}$
$\mathrm{R}^{\prime}=k[\mathrm{~A}][2 \mathrm{~B}]$
$\frac{\mathrm{R}}{\mathrm{R}^{\prime}}=\frac{k[\mathrm{~A}][\mathrm{B}]}{k[\mathrm{~A}][2 \mathrm{~B}]}=\frac{k[\mathrm{~A}][\mathrm{B}]}{2 k[\mathrm{~A}][\mathrm{B}]}$
$\Rightarrow 2 R=R^{\prime}$ i.e., rate become double.
20. (d) Rate constant is independent of concentration.
21. (c) $x \mathrm{~A} \rightarrow y \mathrm{~B}$
$\therefore \quad \frac{-\mathrm{dA}}{x \mathrm{~d} t}=\frac{1}{y} \frac{\mathrm{~dB}}{\mathrm{~d} t}$
$\frac{-\mathrm{dA}}{\mathrm{d} t}=\frac{\mathrm{dB}}{\mathrm{d} t} \times \frac{x}{y}$
$\log \left[\frac{-\mathrm{dA}}{\mathrm{d} t}\right]=\log \left[\frac{\mathrm{dB}}{\mathrm{d} t}\right]+\log \left(\frac{x}{y}\right)$
Comparing this equation with the equation given in question. We get,
$\log \frac{x}{y}=0.3010$ or $\log \frac{x}{y}=\log 2$
$\therefore \quad \frac{x}{y}=2$
$\therefore \quad$ The reaction is of type $2 \mathrm{~A} \longrightarrow \mathrm{~B}$.
Hence, option (c) is correct.
22. (b) $2 \mathrm{~N}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{5}(\mathrm{~g}) \rightarrow 4 \mathrm{NO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+\mathrm{O}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})$
rate of reaction $=\frac{-1}{2} \frac{\mathrm{~d}\left[\mathrm{~N}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{5}\right]}{\mathrm{d} t}=\frac{1}{4} \frac{\mathrm{~d}\left[\mathrm{NO}_{2}\right]}{\mathrm{d} t}=\frac{\mathrm{d}\left[\mathrm{O}_{2}\right]}{\mathrm{d} t}$
According to the question
$\frac{-\mathrm{d}\left[\mathrm{N}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{5}\right]}{\mathrm{d} t}=-\frac{(2.75-3)}{30}=\frac{0.25}{30} \mathrm{Mmin}^{-1}$
$\therefore \frac{\mathrm{d}\left[\mathrm{NO}_{2}\right]}{\mathrm{d} t}=2 \times \frac{-\mathrm{d}\left[\mathrm{N}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{5}\right]}{\mathrm{d} t}=2 \times \frac{0.25}{30}$
$=1.67 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{M} \mathrm{min}^{-1}$
23. (c) $\mathrm{N}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+3 \mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{NH}_{3}(\mathrm{~g})$
$\frac{-\mathrm{d}\left[\mathrm{N}_{2}\right]}{\mathrm{d} t}=\frac{-1}{3} \frac{\mathrm{~d}\left[\mathrm{H}_{2}\right]}{\mathrm{d} t}=+\frac{1}{2} \frac{\mathrm{~d}\left[\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right]}{\mathrm{d} t}$
24. (c) The number of collisions per second per unit volume of the reaction mixture is known as collision frequency (Z). collision frequency no. of reacting molecules or atoms. Higher the concentration of reactant molecules higher is the probability of collision and so the collision frequency.

if $n=0$

\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{N}_{2}+3 \mathrm{H}_{2} \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{NH}_{3} \\
& \frac{-\mathrm{d}\left[\mathrm{~N}_{2}\right]}{\mathrm{d} t}=-\frac{1}{3} \frac{\mathrm{~d}\left[\mathrm{H}_{2}\right]}{\mathrm{d} t}=\frac{1}{2} \frac{\mathrm{~d}\left[\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right]}{\mathrm{d} t} \\
& \begin{aligned}
\frac{-\mathrm{d}\left[\mathrm{H}_{2}\right]}{\mathrm{d} t}=\frac{3}{2} \frac{\mathrm{~d}\left[\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right]}{\mathrm{d} t} & =\frac{3}{2} \times 4.0 \times 10^{-3} \\

4. 8 Rate of reaction

$$
=-\frac{1}{2} \frac{\mathrm{~d}[\mathrm{X}]}{\mathrm{d} t}=-\frac{\mathrm{d}[\mathrm{Y}]}{\mathrm{d} t}=+\frac{\mathrm{d}[\mathrm{Z}]}{\mathrm{d} t}
$$

Rate of appearance of z

$$
=\frac{\mathrm{d}[\mathrm{Z}]}{\mathrm{d} t}=0.04 \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{~L}^{-1} \text { per min. }
$$

∴ Rate of disappearance of $\mathrm{X}=-\frac{\mathrm{d}[\mathrm{X}]}{\mathrm{d} t}$
From equation (i), we get

$$
\begin{aligned}
& -\frac{1}{2} \frac{\mathrm{~d}[\mathrm{X}]}{\mathrm{d} t}=\frac{\mathrm{d}[\mathrm{Z}]}{\mathrm{d} t} \quad \therefore \quad-\frac{\mathrm{d}[\mathrm{X}]}{\mathrm{d} t}=2 \times \frac{\mathrm{d}[\mathrm{Z}]}{\mathrm{d} t} \\
& =2 \times \frac{\mathrm{d}[\mathrm{Z}]}{\mathrm{d} t}=2 \times 0.04 \\
& =8 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{~L}^{-1} \mathrm{~min}^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

## (CONCEPT MAP

## Corrosion

In corrosion, a metal is oxidised by loss of electrons to oxygen and forms metal oxide. Corrosion of iron (which is commonly known as rusting) occurs in presence of water and oxygen (air).
Rusting of Iron : According to electrochemical theory, rusting can be represented as : etc.

$$
\mathrm{Fe}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}+\mathrm{xH}_{2} \mathrm{O} @ \mathrm{Fe}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3} \cdot x \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}
$$

## Prevention of Corrosion

- The metal surface is coated with paint which keeps it out of contact with air, moisture etc.
- By applying film of oil and grease on the surface of the iron tools and machinery.
- The iron surface is coated with non-corroding metals such as nickel, chromium, aluminium,

## CELLS

Electrolytic Cell : It is a device in which a non-spontaneous chemical reaction is carried out by electricity.

Galvanic Cell : It is a device in which electrical energy.

## Batteries

chemical energy converted into

## ELECTROCHEMISTRY

Electrochemistry deals with the study of electrical properties of solutions of electrolytes and with the interrelation of chemical phenomenon and electrical energy. It is the study of production of electricity from energy released during spontaneous chemical reactions and the use of electrical energy to bring about non-spontaneous chemical transformations.
Electricity has an important place in modern society. What constitutes an electric current? How does it flow? An electric current is a flow of particles (electrons) flowing through wires and components.
Substances which allow the electricity to pass through them are called conductors, whereas those which do not allow electricity to pass through them are called insulators.
Conductors are classified into two types based on the nature of decomposition: electronic and electrolytic.
Electronic conductors: The conductors which conduct electricity without causing decomposition of substance are electronic conductors. The flow of electricity is due to flow of electrons in the conductor.
Examples : Metals, graphite, and certain minerals.
Electrolytic conductors: The conductors which decompose when current is passed through them are electronic conductors. In these conductors flow of electricity is due to flow of ions.
Examples : Aqueous solutions of acids, bases, and salt or even fused salts.
Compounds which conduct electricity in solution or in the fused state are called electrolytes and those which do not conduct electricity are called non-electrolytes.

Aqueous solutions of electrolyte is a better conductor than fused electrolyte. Concentration of
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-261.jpg?height=56&width=77&top_left_y=1215&top_left_x=497)
free ions in aqueous solution is greater than in fused state, because an electrolyte dissociates to a greater extent in its aqueous solution than in fused state. Therefore, conductivity of aqueous solution of electrolyte is greater than that of fused electrolyte.

## ELECTROLYSIS

Electrolysis is the process of conduction of electricity through a chemical compound in an aqueous solutions or in the molten state accompanied by a chemical change. The process of electrolysis is carried out in an electrolytic cell.
An electrolytic cell has two electrodes immersed in a solution placed in a container. The electrode connected to the negative pole is called cathode and the one connected to the positive pole is called anode.
An aqueous solution of electrolyte contains both positive and negative ions. When electricity is passed, positive ions migrate towards the cathode and negative ions move towards the anode. A compound is converted into its respective elements at cathode by accepting electrons, and at anode by donating electrons.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-261.jpg?height=414&width=556&top_left_y=1768&top_left_x=790)

## Properties of Electrolysis

(i) The phenomenon of electrolysis appears only at the electrodes.
(ii) The electrolyte as a whole remains neutral during the process of electrolysis as equal number of charges are neutralised at the electrodes.
(iii) The deposition of different ions at the electrodes takes place only when electricity passes.
(iv) If two or more positive and negative ions are present in the solution, the least active ion is discharged first at the respective electrode.

## Applications of Electrolysis

The process of electrolysis has various applications.

## Electroplating

The process of plating a thin layer of one metal over the other with the help of electric current is called electroplating.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-262.jpg?height=471&width=622&top_left_y=587&top_left_x=687)

For example, for nickel plating of steel articles, take an aqueous solution of $\mathrm{NiCl}_{2}$ as an electrolyte in an electrolytic container. The steel article to be electroplated is made the cathode ( - ) and a pure nickel plate is made the anode ( + ). When electric current is passed through the electrolytic solution, the following reactions take place-

|  | $\mathrm{NiCl}_{2}$ | $\rightleftharpoons \mathrm{Ni}^{2+}+2 \mathrm{Cl}^{-}$ |
| :--- | ---: | :--- |
| At cathode: | $\mathrm{Ni}^{2+}+2 \mathrm{e}^{-}$ | $\longrightarrow \mathrm{Ni}$ |
| At anode : | $2 \mathrm{Cl}^{-}$ | $\longrightarrow \mathrm{Cl}_{2}+2 \mathrm{e}^{-}$ |

(Dissociation)
(Reduction)
(oxidation)
Since the object to be plated is made cathode, it attracts positively charged nickel cation and thus the layer of nickel is formed over the object.

## Eletro-Refining of Metals

The process of purifying metal by electrolysis is called electro refining. The impure metal is made the anode and a plate of the same pure metal is made the cathode. Aqueous solution of the metal salt is taken as an electrolyte.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-262.jpg?height=455&width=830&top_left_y=1585&top_left_x=587)

For example, in the refining of copper, impure copper plates are made anodes and suspended between pure copper sheets acting as the cathode. Acidified copper sulphate solution is taken as an electrolyte.
The reactions taking place can be shown as follows:

$$
\mathrm{CuSO}_{4} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{Cu}^{2+}(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{SO}_{4}^{2-}(\mathrm{aq})
$$

(Ionisation)
At cathode :

$$
\mathrm{Cu}^{2+}(\mathrm{aq})+2 \mathrm{e}^{-} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Cu}(\mathrm{~s})
$$

Copper gets deposited on the pure copper plate.
At anode :

$$
\mathrm{Cu}(\mathrm{~s}) \longrightarrow \mathrm{Cu}^{2+}+2 \mathrm{e}^{-}
$$

## CELLS

The process of eletrolysis requires chemical reaction to occur, which is carried out by using electrical energy. Similarly, electrical energy can also be generated by undergoing chemical reaction in an electrochemical cell.
Cell is a system or arrangement in which two electrodes are fitted in the same electrolyte or in two different electrolytes which are joined by a salt bridge. Cells are of two types :
(a) Electrolytic cell
(b) Galvanic or voltaic cell

## Electrolytic Cell

It is a device in which a non-spontaneous chemical reaction is carried out by using electricity or in which conversion of electrical energy into chemical energy is done.

## Galvanic or Voltaic Cell

It is a device in which chemical energy liberated during a chemical reaction is converted into electrical energy, i.e., electricity can be obtained with the help of oxidation and reduction reaction.
The chemical reaction responsible for production of electricity takes place in two separate compartments. Each compartment consists of a suitable electrolyte solution and a metallic conductor. The metallic conductor acts as an electrode. The compartments containing the electrode and the solution of the electrolyte are called half-cells. When the two compartments are connected by a salt bridge and electrodes are joined by a wire through galvanometer, the electricity begins to flow. This is the simple form of voltaic cell.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-263.jpg?height=472&width=805&top_left_y=1169&top_left_x=667)

Anode: $\mathrm{Zn} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Zn}^{2+}+2 \mathrm{e}^{-}$
( $\mathrm{I}^{\text {st }}$ half cell reaction)
( $\mathrm{I}^{\mathrm{st}}$ half cell reaction)
Total cell reaction :
Total cell reaction :
Total cell reaction :
-

$$
\mathrm{Zn}(\mathrm{~s})+\mathrm{Cu}^{2+}(\mathrm{aq}) \longrightarrow \mathrm{Zn}^{2+}(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{Cu}(\mathrm{~s})
$$

Cathode : $\mathrm{Cu}^{2+}+2 \mathrm{e}^{-} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Cu}$
(II ${ }^{\text {nd }}$ half cell reaction)

## Difference between Electrolytic Cell and Galvanic Cell

| Electrolytic cell | Galvanic cell |
| :--- | :--- |
| (i) Electrical energy is converted into chemical energy. | (i) Chemical energy is converted into electrical energy |
| (ii) Anode is positive electrode. Cathode is negative electrode. | (ii) Anode is negative electrode. Cathode is positive electrode. |
| (iii) Ions are discharged on both the electrodes. | (iii) Ions are discharged only on the cathode. |
| (iv) Both the electrodes can be fitted in the same compartment. | (iv) The electrodes are fitted in different compartments. |

## Cell Notation

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-263.jpg?height=186&width=396&top_left_y=2252&top_left_x=870)

By seeing the cell notation we can easily understand reactions undergoing in electrochemical cell. The first half of the notation represents anode reaction, whereas the second half represents cathode reaction.

In the notation, the anode or oxidation half cell is always written on the left. The cathode or reduction half cell is written on the right. The two electrodes are connected by means of a salt bridge, denoted by two vertical lines.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-264.jpg?height=150&width=424&top_left_y=438&top_left_x=191)

In a beaker take some salt water. Now place copper and zinc plates connected with wire to the positive and negative ends of LCD clock in the beaker. Note whether the clock functions or not. Repeat this process with distilled water and cold drink also. find which solution conducts electricity.

## ELECTRODE POTENTIAL

When a strip of metal is brought in contact with the solution containing its own ions, then the strip of metal gets positively charged or negatively charged and results into a potential difference developed between the metallic strip and its solution, which is known as electrode potential.

| At anode : | $\mathrm{M} \longrightarrow \mathrm{M}^{n+}+n e^{-}$(Oxidation potential) |
| :--- | :--- |
| At cathode : | $\mathrm{M}^{n+}+n e^{-} \longrightarrow \mathrm{M}$ (Reduction potential) |

- The value of electrode potential depends upon :
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-264.jpg?height=333&width=250&top_left_y=865&top_left_x=1562)
(a) the nature of electrode
(b) the concentration of solution
(c) the temperature


## Standard Electrode Potential ( $E^{\circ}$ )

If the concentration of ions is unity, temperature is $25{ }^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and pressure is 1 atm (standard conditions), the potential of the electrode is called standard electrode potential.
It is represented by $E^{\circ}$.

## Electromotive Force of Cell or Cell Voltage

The difference in the electrode potentials of the two electrodes of the cell is termed as electromotive force (EMF) or cell voltage.

$$
\begin{gathered}
\mathrm{E}_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}=\mathrm{E}_{\text {red }}^{\circ}(\text { cathode })-\mathrm{E}_{\text {red }}^{\circ}(\text { anode }) \\
\text { or } \\
\mathrm{E}_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}=\mathrm{E}_{\text {oxi. }}^{\circ}(\text { anode })-\mathrm{E}_{\text {oxi. }}^{\circ}(\text { cathode }) \\
\text { or } \\
\mathrm{E}_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}=\mathrm{E}_{\text {oxi. }}^{\circ}(\text { anode })+\mathrm{E}_{\text {red }}^{\circ}(\text { cathode })
\end{gathered}
$$

## ILLUSTRATION : 1

Standard reduction potentials for $\mathrm{Fe}^{2+} \mid \mathrm{Fe}$ and $\mathrm{Sn}^{2+} \mid \mathrm{Sn}$ are -0.44 V and -0.14 V , respectively. What should be the standard electromotive force for $\mathbf{F e}^{\mathbf{2 +}}+\mathbf{S n} \rightarrow \mathbf{F e}+\mathbf{S n}^{\mathbf{2 +}}$ cell reaction?

## SOLUTION :

Oxidation of Sn is taking place. Therefore, Sn acts as an anode and Fe as a cathode.
Thus, $\mathrm{EMF}=E_{(\text {Cathode })}^{\circ}-E_{(\text {Anode })}^{\circ}=-0.44-(-0.14)=-0.30 \mathrm{~V}$

## ILLUSTRATIOM : 2

Electromotive force of a cell formed by a standard copper electrode and a standard magnesium electrode couple is +2.7 V . If standard reduction potential of the Cu electrode is +0.34 V , then what should be the standard reduction potential of Mg?

## SOLUTION :

Here $\mathrm{Mg} \rightarrow \mathrm{Mg}^{2+}$ is an anode and $\mathrm{Cu}^{2+} \rightarrow \mathrm{Cu}$ is a cathode.

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{(\text {Anode })}^{\circ}= & E_{(\text {Cathode })}^{\circ}-\mathrm{EMF} \\
& =+0.34-(+2.7)=-2.36 \mathrm{~V}
\end{aligned}
$$

$\mathbf{Z n}(\mathbf{s})+\mathbf{C o}^{\mathbf{2 +}} \longrightarrow \mathbf{C o}(\mathbf{s})+\mathbf{Z n}^{\mathbf{2 +}}$. Find out the standard EMF of the above cell if,
$\boldsymbol{E}_{\mathbf{Z n | Z n}^{\mathbf{2 +}}}^{\circ}=\mathbf{0 . 7 6}$ volt; $\boldsymbol{E}_{\mathbf{C o} \mid \mathbf{C o}^{\mathbf{2 +}}}^{\circ}{ }^{\mathbf{2 +}}=\mathbf{0 . 2 8}$ volt
SOLUTION :
Reduction potential of $\mathrm{Zn}=-0.76$ volt
Reduction potential of $\mathrm{Co}=-0.28$ volt
$\mathrm{Co}^{2+}+2 \mathrm{e}^{-} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Co} \quad$ [Reduction]
$\mathrm{Zn} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Zn}^{2+}+2 \mathrm{e}^{-} \quad$ [Oxidation]
Standard EMF of cell $=E_{\text {(Cathode) }}^{\circ}-E_{\text {(Anode) }}^{\circ}=-0.28-(-0.76)$

$$
=+0.48 \text { volt }
$$

## ELECTROCHEMICAL SERIES

The arrangement of various elements in order of increasing values of standard reduction potential is called electro- chemical series.

Standard reduction potentials at $\mathbf{2 9 8 ~ K}$ (Electrochemical series)
| Element | Electrode Reduction Reaction |  | Standard Reduction Potential $\boldsymbol{E}$ (in Volt) |  |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Li |  | $\mathrm{Li}^{+}+\mathrm{e}^{-} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Li}$ |  | -3.05 |
| K |  | $\mathrm{K}^{+}+\mathrm{e}^{-} \longrightarrow \mathrm{K}$ |  | -2.93 |
| Ba |  | $\mathrm{Ba}^{2+}+2 \mathrm{e}^{-} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Ba}$ |  | -2.90 |
| Ca |  | $\mathrm{Ca}^{2+}+2 \mathrm{e}^{-} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Ca}$ |  | -2.87 |
| Na |  | $\mathrm{Na}^{+}+\mathrm{e}^{-} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Na}$ |  | -2.71 |
| Mg |  | $\mathrm{Mg}^{2+}+2 \mathrm{e}^{-} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Mg}$ |  | -2.37 |
| Al | 䓃箇 | $\mathrm{Al}^{3+}+3 \mathrm{e}^{-} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Al}$ |  | -1.66 |
| Mn |  | $\mathrm{Mn}^{2+}+2 \mathrm{e}^{-} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Mn}$ |  | -1.18 |
| Zn |  | $\mathrm{Zn}^{2+}+2 \mathrm{e}^{-} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Zn}$ |  | -0.76 |
| Cr |  | $\mathrm{Cr}^{3+}+3 \mathrm{e}^{-} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Cr}$ |  | -0.74 |
| Fe |  | $\mathrm{Fe}^{2+}+2 \mathrm{e}^{-} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Fe}$ |  | -0.44 |
| Cd |  | $\mathrm{Cd}^{2+}+2 \mathrm{e}^{-} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Cd}$ |  | -0.40 |
| Ni |  | $\mathrm{Ni}^{2+}+2 \mathrm{e}^{-} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Ni}$ |  | -0.25 |
| Sn |  | $\mathrm{Sn}^{2+}+2 \mathrm{e}^{-} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Sn}$ |  | -0.14 |
| Pb |  | $\mathrm{Pb}^{2+}+2 \mathrm{e}^{-} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Pb}$ |  | -0.13 |
| $\mathrm{H}_{2}$ |  | $2 \mathrm{H}^{+}+2 \mathrm{e}^{-} \longrightarrow \mathrm{H}_{2}$ |  | 0.00 |
| Cu |  | $\mathrm{Cu}^{2+}+2 \mathrm{e}^{-} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Cu}$ |  | 0.34 |
| $\mathrm{I}_{2}$ |  | $\mathrm{I}_{2}+2 \mathrm{e}^{-} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{I}^{-}$ |  | 0.53 |
| Hg |  | $\mathrm{Hg}_{2}^{2+}+2 \mathrm{e}^{-} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{Hg}$ |  | 0.79 |
| Ag |  | $\mathrm{Ag}^{+}+\mathrm{e}^{-} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Ag}$ |  | 0.80 |
| Hg |  | $\mathrm{Hg}^{2+}+2 \mathrm{e}^{-} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Hg}$ |  | 0.85 |
| $\mathrm{Br}_{2}$ |  | $\mathrm{Br}_{2}+2 \mathrm{e}^{-} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{Br}^{-}$ |  | 1.08 |
| $\mathrm{Cl}_{2}$ |  | $\mathrm{Cl}_{2}+2 \mathrm{e}^{-} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{Cl}^{-}$ |  | 1.36 |
| Pt |  | $\mathrm{Pt}^{2+}+2 \mathrm{e}^{-} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Pt}$ |  | 1.20 |
| Au |  | $\mathrm{Au}^{3+}+3 \mathrm{e}^{-} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Au}$ |  | 1.50 |
| $\mathrm{F}_{2}$ |  | $\mathrm{F}_{2}+2 \mathrm{e}^{-} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{~F}^{-}$ |  | 2.87 |


## Applications of Electrochemical Series

(a) Reactivity of metals :
(i) Alkali metals and alkaline earth metals react with cold water and evolve hydrogen and readily dissolve in acids.
(ii) Metals like $\mathrm{Fe}, \mathrm{Pb}, \mathrm{Sn}, \mathrm{Ni}, \mathrm{Co}$, etc. do not react with cold water but react with steam to evolve hydrogen.
(iii) Metals like $\mathrm{Cu}, \mathrm{Ag}$, and Au which lie below hydrogen are less reactive and do not evolve hydrogen from water.
(b) Electropositive character of metals : Electropositive character of metals decreases from top to bottom.
(c) Displacement reactions : The metal having low SRP will displace the metal from its salt solution which has higher value of SRP.
(d) Reducing power of metals : Reducing nature decreases from top to bottom in the electrochemical series.
(e) Oxidizing nature of non-metals : Oxidizing nature increases from top to bottom in the electrochemical series.
(f) Products of electrolysis : The ion which is a stronger oxidizing agent is discharged first at cathode.
(g) Corrosion of metals : The corrosion tendency increases from top to bottom.

Platinum and gold do not occur in combined state unlike other metals. They do not possess
Did You Know! tendency to form positive ions due to the high negative value of oxidation potential. Hence, they occur in free state in nature.

## Learn

## More

## Important Points about this Series

(a) Metals near the top of the series are strongly electropositive (or weakly electronegative). They lose electrons readily to give cations.
(b) Weakly electronegative metals in the upper part can displace more electronegative metals below them from their salts. For example, iron displaces copper from $\mathrm{CuSO}_{4}$ solution, Cu displaces silver from silver salt solution, silver displaces gold from gold salt solution as in photography during gold toning process.
(c) Hydroxides of metals in the upper part of the series are strongly basic and their salts do not undergo hydrolysis. On the other hand, hydroxides of the metals in the lower part of the series are weakly basic and their salts undergo hydrolysis.
(d) Metals lying above hydrogen are easily rusted. Those situated below are not rusted.
(e) Metals above hydrogen displace hydrogen from dilute acids and water.
(f) Iron and other metals above it decompose steam and liberate hydrogen.
(g) Oxides of iron and other metals below it can be reduced easily.
(h) Oxides of manganese and other metals above it are reduced when heated in a current of hydrogen.
(i) Oxides of mercury and other metals below it are decomposed on heating.

## SOME COMMERCIAL CELLS

## Primary Cell

The most familiar type of battery is the dry cell which is a compact form of Leclanche cell (fig.) known after its discoverer, Leclanche.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-267.jpg?height=729&width=760&top_left_y=370&top_left_x=687)

In this cell, the anode consists of a zinc container and the cathode is a graphite rod surrounded by powdered $\mathrm{MnO}_{2}$ and carbon. The space between the electrodes is filled with a moist paste of $\mathrm{NH}_{4} \mathrm{Cl}$ and $\mathrm{ZnCl}_{2}$. The electrode reactions are complex, but they can be written approximately as

## Anode :

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathrm{Zn}(\mathrm{~s}) & \longrightarrow \mathrm{Zn}^{2+}+2 \mathrm{e}^{-} \\
2 \mathrm{MnO}_{2}+2 \mathrm{NH}_{4}^{+}+2 \mathrm{e}^{-} & \longrightarrow \mathrm{Mn}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}+2 \mathrm{NH}_{3}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \\
\mathrm{Zn}(\mathrm{~s})+2 \mathrm{NH}_{4}^{+}+2 \mathrm{MnO}_{2} & \left.\longrightarrow \mathrm{Zn}\left(\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right)_{2}\right] \mathrm{Cl}_{2}+\mathrm{Mn}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}
\end{aligned}
$$

## Cathode :

Overall net reaction :

In the cathode reaction, manganese is reduced from the +4 oxidation state to the +3 state. Dry cells have a potential of

## Secondary Cell

In these cells, electrode reactions can be reversed by an external electric energy source, i.e., these cells can be recharged by passing electric current in opposite direction. Common examples are lead storage battery and nickel-cadmium storage cell.

## Lead Storage Battery

It consists of a lead anode and a grid of lead packed with lead dioxide as cathode. A solution of sulphuric acid is used as an electrolyte.
When the cell discharges, it operates as a voltaic cell.
Anode :

$$
\mathrm{Pb}(\mathrm{~s})+\mathrm{SO}_{4}^{2-}(\mathrm{aq}) \longrightarrow \mathrm{PbSO}_{4}(\mathrm{~s})+2 \mathrm{e}^{-}
$$

Cathode :

$$
\mathrm{PbO}_{2}(\mathrm{~s})+\mathrm{SO}_{4}^{2-}(\mathrm{aq})+4 \mathrm{H}^{+}+2 \mathrm{e}^{-} \longrightarrow \mathrm{PbSO}_{4}(\mathrm{~s})+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l})
$$

Overall cell reaction :

$$
\mathrm{Pb}(\mathrm{~s})+\mathrm{PbO}_{2}(\mathrm{~s})+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}(\mathrm{aq}) \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{PbSO}_{4}(\mathrm{~s})+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}
$$

When a potential slightly greater than the potential of battery is applied, the battery can be recharged. During recharging the cell is operated like an electrolytic cell i.e., now electrical energy is supplied to it from an external source. The electrode reactions are reverse of the reactions that occur during discharge :

$$
2 \mathrm{PbSO}_{4}+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \longrightarrow \mathrm{~Pb}+\mathrm{PbO}_{2}+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}
$$

It is clear that $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}$ is used up during the discharge \& produced during recharging reaction of cell. Another secondary cell is the nickel-cadmium storage cell which has a longer life than the lead storage cell.

## Nickel-Cadmium Storage Cell

It consists of cadmium anode and a metal grid containing $\mathrm{NiO}_{2}$ acting as cathode. The electrolyte in this cell is KOH . The reactions taking place during discharge and charge are:

## Anode:

$$
\mathrm{Cd}(\mathrm{~s})+2 \mathrm{OH}^{-}(\mathrm{aq}) \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{Cd}(\mathrm{OH})_{2}(\mathrm{~s})+2 \mathrm{e}^{-}
$$

## Cathode:

$$
\mathrm{NiO}_{2}(\mathrm{~s})+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l})+2 \mathrm{e}^{-} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Ni}(\mathrm{OH})_{2}(\mathrm{~s})+2 \mathrm{OH}^{-}(\mathrm{aq})
$$

$$
\mathrm{Cd}(\mathrm{~s})+\mathrm{NiO}_{2}(\mathrm{~s})+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l}) \underset{\text { recharging }}{\stackrel{\text { discharging }}{\rightleftharpoons}} \mathrm{Cd}(\mathrm{OH})_{2}(\mathrm{~s})+\mathrm{Ni}(\mathrm{OH})_{2}(\mathrm{~s})
$$

It produces a potential of 1.4 V . The cell has a longer life than lead storage cell and is used in cordless appliances (phones, pagers, mobile phones, electric shavers etc.)

## Fuel Cell

Galvanic cells that are designed to convert the energy of combustion of fuels like $\mathrm{H}_{2}, \mathrm{CH}_{4}, \mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{OH}$ etc., directly into electrical energy are called fuel cells. Such a cell converts $60-70 \%$ of chemical energy into electrical energy. The fuel used is in the gaseous state; $\mathrm{H}_{2}-\mathrm{O}_{2}$ fuel cell is a common example.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-268.jpg?height=496&width=794&top_left_y=900&top_left_x=607)

## $\mathrm{H}_{2}-\mathrm{O}_{2}$ fuel cell

## Learn More

In the cell, hydrogen and oxygen are bubbled through a porous carbon electrode into concentrated aqueous sodium hydroxide. Catalysts are incorporated in the electrodes. The electrode reactions are:

Anode :

$$
\left[\mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+2 \mathrm{OH}^{-}(\mathrm{aq}) \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l})+2 \mathrm{e}^{-}\right] \times 2
$$

## Cathode :

$$
\mathrm{O}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l})+4 \mathrm{e}^{-} \longrightarrow 4 \mathrm{OH}^{-}(\mathrm{aq})
$$

## Overall reaction :

$$
2 \mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+\mathrm{O}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l})
$$

## CORROSION

Corrosion is basically an electrochemical phenomenon. The rusting of iron, the tarnishing of silver, development of a green coating on copper and bronze are some of the examples of corrosion.
In corrosion, a metal is oxidised by loss of electrons to oxygen and forms metal oxide. Corrosion of iron (which is commonly known as rusting) occurs in presence of water and oxygen (air).
Chemically, rust is hydrated form of ferric oxide $\mathrm{Fe}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3} . x \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$.

## Electrochemical Theory of Rusting

According to this theory, flow of electric current between separate anode and cathode bars is the reason why corrosion occurs. The ferrous ions formed by action of moisture or water move through water and come at the surface of iron object where these are further oxidised to ferric state by atmospheric oxygen and constitute rust which is hydrated iron (III) oxide.

$$
\mathrm{Fe}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}+x \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \rightarrow \mathrm{Fe}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3} \cdot x \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}
$$

## Factors Which Promote Corrosion

(a) More active metals are readily corroded.
(b) Presence of impurities in metals enhances the chances of corrosion. Pure metals do not corrode e.g. pure iron does not rust.
(c) Air and moisture accelerate corrosion. Presence of gases like $\mathrm{SO}_{2}$ and $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ in air catalyses the process of corrosion. Iron when placed in vacuum does not rust.
(d) Corrosion (e.g. rusting of iron) takes place rapidly at bends, scratches, nicks and cuts in the metal.
(e) Electrolytes, if present, also increase the rate of corrosion.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-269.jpg?height=154&width=424&top_left_y=753&top_left_x=260)

Take one unprotected and one protected iron strips and place them in corrosive environment. The protected strip has zinc coating. After some days check both the strips. Do you find any change in them? If any, recall the reason for the change.

\end{array}
$$
