## Passage Based Questions

DIRECTIONS : Study the given paragraph (s) and answer the following questions.

## Passage

Billions of dollars are spent each year to replace or prevent the corrosion and subsequent destruction of government property such as ships, bridges, and metallic piping. Corrosion may result in decreased structural integrity and eventual ineffectuality. For example, a naval ship made from iron is highly susceptible to corrosion. One part of the hull acts as the anode, while another acts as the cathode, the iron hull itself connects the two parts, completing the circuit. Part of the metal hull begins oxidizing to $\mathrm{Fe}^{2+}$ in the presence of $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ and $\mathrm{O}_{2}$, the reduction reaction proceeds with an $E_{\text {red }}=1.229 \mathrm{~V}$. It is there that $\mathrm{Fe}^{2+}$ ions migrate from the anode and are further oxidized according to the following equation :

$$
\begin{aligned}
& 2 \mathrm{Fe}^{2+}(a q)+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{O}_{2}(g)+(6+x) \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(l) \longrightarrow \\
& \mathrm{Fe}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3} \cdot x \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}+4 \mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{O}^{+}(a q)
\end{aligned}
$$

The $\mathrm{Fe}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$, or rust, formed is only a small part of the problem. Pitting, or loss of solid metal, weaken the structure of the hull and may lead to serious damage and destruction of the hull. Using a sacrificial anode often successfully prevents corrosion. This metals is, more easily oxidized than iron. Although the sacrificial anode eventually corrodes as well, the cost of replacing it is far less than that of replacing the iron hull.

Table
| Half Reaction | Potential |
| :--- | :--- |
| $\mathrm{O}_{2}+4 \mathrm{H}^{+}+4 \mathrm{e}^{-} \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ | $E^{\circ}=1.229 \mathrm{~V}$ |
| $\mathrm{Fe}^{3+}+\mathrm{e}^{-} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{Fe}^{2+}$ | $E^{\circ}=0.771 \mathrm{~V}$ |
| $\mathrm{Cu}^{+}+\mathrm{e}^{-} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{Cu}$ | $E^{\circ}=0.521 \mathrm{~V}$ |
| $\mathrm{O}_{2}+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}+4 \mathrm{e}^{-} \rightleftharpoons 4 \mathrm{OH}^{-}$ | $E^{\circ}=0.401 \mathrm{~V}$ |
| $\mathrm{Cu}^{2+}+2 \mathrm{e}^{-} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{Cu}$ | $E^{\circ}=0.342 \mathrm{~V}$ |
| $\mathrm{Pb}^{2+}+2 \mathrm{e}^{-} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{Pb}$ | $E^{\circ}=-0.126 \mathrm{~V}$ |
| $\mathrm{Fe}^{2+}+2 \mathrm{e}^{-} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{Fe}$ | $E^{\circ}=-0.447 \mathrm{~V}$ |
| $\mathrm{Mn}^{2+}+2 \mathrm{e}^{-} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{Mn}$ | $E^{\circ}=-1.185 \mathrm{~V}$ |
| $\mathrm{Mg}^{2+}+2 \mathrm{e}^{-} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{Mg}$ | $E^{\circ}=-2.372 \mathrm{~V}$ |


1. Which of the following reactions may occur at the anode of the iron hull?
(a) $\mathrm{Fe}^{2+}(\mathrm{aq}) \longrightarrow \mathrm{Fe}^{3+}(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{e}^{-}$
(b) $\mathrm{Fe}(\mathrm{s}) \longrightarrow \mathrm{Fe}^{2+}(\mathrm{aq})+2 \mathrm{e}^{-}$
(c) $\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{O}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+2 \mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{O}^{+}(\mathrm{aq})+2 \mathrm{e}^{-} \longrightarrow 3 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l})$
(d) $2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l}) \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{OH}^{-}(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+2 \mathrm{e}^{-}$
2. What is the standard voltage generated by the corrosion reaction on the iron hull of a ship ?
(a) -1.676 V
(b) -0.782 V
(c) 1.782 V
(d) 1.676 V
3. Which of the following materials cannot serve as a sacrificial anode for lead?
(a) Copper
(b) Iron
(c) Magnesium
(d) Manganese

## Assertion \& Reason

DIRECTIONS : Each of these questions contains an assertion followed by reason. Read them carefully and answer the question on the basis of following options. You have to select the one that best describes the two statements.
(a) If both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of Assertion.
(b) If both Assertion and Reason are correct, but Reason is not the correct explanation of Assertion.
(c) If Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
(d) If Assertion is incorrect but Reason is correct.

1. Assertion : $\mathrm{KCl} / \mathrm{NaCl} / \mathrm{NH}_{4} \mathrm{Cl}$ etc. cannot be used in the salt bridge of a cell containing silver.
Reason : A salt bridge contains concentrated solution of an inert electrolyte like $\mathrm{KCl}, \mathrm{KNO}_{3}, \mathrm{NH}_{4} \mathrm{NO}_{3}$ etc. or solidified solution of such an electrolyte in agar-agar and gelatine.
2. Assertion : Identification of cathode and anode is done by the use of an ammeter.
Reason : Higher is the value of reduction potential, greater would be its reducing power.
3. Assertion : Zinc can be used while copper cannot be used in the recovery of Ag from the complex $\left[\mathrm{Ag}(\mathrm{CN})_{2}\right]^{-}$.
Reason : Zinc is a powerful reducing agent than copper.
4. Assertion : During electrolysis of $\mathrm{CuSO}_{4}$ (aq) using copper electrodes, copper is dissolved at anode and deposited at cathode.
Reason : Oxidation takes place at anode and reduction at cathode.

## Multiple Matching Questions

DIRECTIONS : Each question contains two columns. Column I have to be matched with Column II.

1. Match the items of Column I and Column II on the basis

## Column-I

(A) $\mathrm{F}_{2}$
(B) Li
(C) $\mathrm{Au}^{3+}$
(D) $\mathrm{Br}^{-}$ of data given below :
$E_{\mathrm{F}_{2} / \mathrm{F}^{-}}^{\circ}=2.87 \mathrm{~V}, \quad E_{\mathrm{Li}^{+} / \mathrm{Li}}^{\circ}=-3.5 \mathrm{~V}, \quad E_{\mathrm{Au}^{3+} / \mathrm{Au}}^{\circ}=1.4 \mathrm{~V}$, $E_{\mathrm{Br}_{2} / \mathrm{Br}^{-}}^{\circ}=1.09 \mathrm{~V}$

## Column-II

(p) Metal is the strongest reducing agent.
(q) Anion that can be oxidised by $\mathrm{Au}^{3+}$.
(r) Non metal which is the best oxidising agent.
(s) Metal ion which is an oxidising agent.
(a) (A) - (r), (B) - (p), (C) - (s), (D) - (q)
(b) (A) - (p), (B) - (r), (C) - (s), (D) - (q)
(c) $(\mathrm{A})-(\mathrm{q}),(\mathrm{B})-(\mathrm{p}),(\mathrm{C})-(\mathrm{s}),(\mathrm{D})-(\mathrm{r})$
(d) (A) - (r), (B) - (s), (C) - (p), (D) - (q)

## Integer Type Questions

DIRECTIONS : Following are integer based questions. Each question, when worked out will result in one integer from 0 to 9 (both inclusive).

1. For the cell reaction

$$
\mathrm{Al}+\mathrm{Cr}^{3+} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Al}^{3+}+\mathrm{Cr} ; E_{\mathrm{Al}^{3+} / \mathrm{Al}}^{\circ}=-1.66 \mathrm{~V}
$$

and $E_{\mathrm{Cr}^{+3} / \mathrm{Cr}}^{\circ}=-0.74 \mathrm{~V}$. What will be the approximate value of $E_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}$ ?
2. How many moles of electrons are required for the cathodic reaction of $\mathrm{H}_{2}-\mathrm{O}_{2}$ fuel cell for one mole of oxygen?
3. In the product formed at cathode of a Leclanche cell (Dry cell), what is the oxidation state of the metal?
4. How many of the following statements are correct for a lead storage battery?
(i) Perforated lead plates coated with pure lead act as positive electrode.
(ii) Perforated lead plates coated with $\mathrm{PbO}_{2}$ act as negative electrode.
(iii) During charging $\mathrm{PbSO}_{4}$ is converting to PbO at anode.
(iv) During discharging Pb is oxidized to $\mathrm{PbSO}_{4}$ at anode.
(v) About $38 \% \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}$ solution is used as an electrolyte.

## 8 GOC (Nomenclature and Isomerism)

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-276.jpg?height=417&width=368&top_left_y=383&top_left_x=1446)

## (CONCEPTMAP

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-276.jpg?height=1739&width=1500&top_left_y=811&top_left_x=311)

## IUPAC NOMENCLATURE

According to IUPAC system, the name of an organic compound consists of three parts :
(a) Root word
(b) Suffix
(c) Prefix

## Root word

Word root denotes the number of carbon atoms present in the principal chain which is the longest possible chain of carbon atoms. However, from $\mathrm{C}_{1}$ to $\mathrm{C}_{4}$ common names have been retained.

| Chain length | Word root | Chain length | Word root |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $\mathrm{C}_{1}$ | Meth- | $\mathrm{C}_{7}$ | Hept |
| $\mathrm{C}_{2}$ | Eth- | $\mathrm{C}_{8}$ | Oct- |
| $\mathrm{C}_{3}$ | Prop- | $\mathrm{C}_{9}$ | Non- |
| $\mathrm{C}_{4}$ | But- | $\mathrm{C}_{10}$ | Dec- |
| $\mathrm{C}_{5}$ | Pent- | $\mathrm{C}_{11}$ | Undec- |
| $\mathrm{C}_{6}$ | Hex- | $\mathrm{C}_{12}$ | Dodec |

## Suffix

The root word is linked to the suffix which can be primary or secondary or both.

## (a) Primary suffix

A primary suffix indicates the type of linkage in the carbon atoms. If the carbon atoms are linked by single covalent bonds, the primary suffix is 'ane'. If these are linked by double and triple bonds, the primary suffixes 'ene' and 'yne' are respectively used to represent them. Thus,

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \text { ane : primary suffix for } \mathrm{C}-\mathrm{C} \text { bond } \\
& \text { ene : primary suffix for } \mathrm{C}=\mathrm{C} \text { bond } \\
& \text { yne : primary suffix for } \mathrm{C} \equiv \mathrm{C} \text { bond }
\end{aligned}
$$

If the parent chain of carbon atoms contains more than one double or triple bonds, numerical prefixes like di (for two), tri (for three), tetra (for four) etc. are added to primary suffix.

## Example -

| Hydrocarbon | Word root | Primary suffix |
| :--- | :---: | :---: |
| $\mathrm{CH}_{3}-\mathrm{CH}_{2}-\mathrm{CH}_{2}-\mathrm{CH}_{3}$ | But | ane |
| $\mathrm{CH}_{2}-\mathrm{CH}-\mathrm{CH}-\mathrm{CH}_{2}$ | Buta | diene |
| $\mathrm{CH}_{3}-\mathrm{C} \equiv \mathrm{CH}$ | Prop | yne |

## (b) Secondary suffix

A secondary suffix is used to represent the functional group if present in the molecule and is attached to the primary suffix. Secondary suffixes of some of the functional groups are listed :-

| Functional group | Symbol | Suffix |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Sulphonic | $-\mathrm{SO}_{3} \mathrm{H}$ | sulphonic acid |
| Carboxylic | $-\mathrm{COOH}$ | - oic acid |
| Ester | <smiles>[R]OC([R])O</smiles> | - oate |
| Acid halide | <smiles>[Y][CH]O</smiles> | - oyl halide |

Acid amide
<img class="imgSvg" id = "mpcazmmnii3iyxyl42" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,"/>

| Aldehyde | <smiles>CO</smiles> | - al |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Cyanide | $-\mathrm{C} \equiv \mathrm{N}$ | - nitrile |
| Isocyanide | $\mathrm{N} \equiv \mathrm{C}$ | - isocyanide |
| Ketone | $>\mathrm{C}=\mathrm{O}$ | - one |
| Hydroxy | $-\mathrm{OH}$ | - ol |
| Thioalcohol | - SH | - thiol |
| Amine | $-\mathrm{NH}_{2}$ | - amine |
| Secondary amine | <smiles>[R]NC</smiles> | N -alkylamine |
| Tertiary amine | <smiles>[R]N([R])[2H]</smiles> | N -alkyl-N-alkylamine |

While adding a secondary suffix to the primary suffix, the terminal ' $e$ ' of the primary suffix (ane, ene or yne) is dropped if the secondary suffix begins with ' $a$ ', ' $i$ ' ' $o$ ' ' $u$ ' or ' $y$ '. In case, it begins with consonant, then the terminal ' $e$ ' of the primary suffix is retained.

$$
\begin{gathered}
- \text { ane }+ \text { ol }=- \text { anol } \\
- \text { ane }+ \text { thiol }=- \text { anethiol }
\end{gathered}
$$

For example: While adding a secon
(ane, ene or yne) is d
begins with consonan
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-278.jpg?height=287&width=351&top_left_y=1123&top_left_x=159)

| Organic compounds | Root word | Primary suffix | Secondary suffix | IUPAC name |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{OH}$ | Eth | an $(e)$ | ol | Ethanol |
| $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{CHO}$ | prop | ane $(e)$ | al | Propanal |
| $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{NH}_{2}$ | prop | ane $(e)$ | amine | Propanamine |
| $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{CN}$ | prop | ane $(e)$ | nitrile | Propanenitrile |

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-278.jpg?height=150&width=318&top_left_y=1717&top_left_x=187)

* What is the IUPAC name of the following compound ?

$$
\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{COOH}
$$

## SOLUTION

Root word: But; Primary suffix: ane; Secondary suffix: oic acid; IUPAC name: Butanoic acid

## Prefix

Prefix is also a part of IUPAC name which appears before the root word. Prefixes are of two types.
(a) Primary prefix

A primary prefix cyclo is used in order to differentiate a cyclic compound from an acyclic compound.
For example
<img class="imgSvg" id = "mpcazmmsozpz9hw1o7n" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHNwYW4gY2xhc3M9InNtaWxlcy1lcnJvciIgc3R5bGU9ImJhY2tncm91bmQtY29sb3I6IHllbGxvdzsgY29sb3I6IHJlZDsiPlN5bnRheEVycm9yOiBFeHBlY3RlZCAiKyIsICItIiwgIjoiLCAiQCIsICJIIiwgb3IgIl0iIGJ1dCAiMiIgZm91bmQuPC9zcGFuPg=="/>
<img class="imgSvg" id = "mpcazmmsps48cxgkh3c" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,"/>

Cyclopropane
<img class="imgSvg" id = "mpcazmmt4btcs16xfqu" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,"/>

Butane
<img class="imgSvg" id = "mpcazmmuf11elrpmfd" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,"/>

Time to Check Your
Knowledge

* What is the IUPAC name of the following compound ?
<img class="imgSvg" id = "mpcazmmvfd7wod0cqdv" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,"/>


## SOLUTION

| Primary prefix | Root word | Primary suffix | Secondary suffix |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Cyclo | hex | ane | - |

Thus, the IUPAC name of the compound is cyclohexane.
(b) Secondary prefix

In the IUPAC system of nomenclature, certain characteristic groups are not considered as a primary or secondary suffixes. These groups are considered as a substituents and are denoted by secondary prefixes. The secondary prefixes of a few substituents are given -

| Substituent group | Secondary prefix | Substituent group | Secondary prefix |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| - F | Fluoro | $-\mathrm{NO}_{2}$ | Nitro |
| - Cl | Chloro | $-\mathrm{CH}_{3}$ | Methyl |
| $-\mathrm{Br}$ | Bromo | $-\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5}$ | Ethyl |
| -I | Iodo | $-\mathrm{OCH}_{3}$ | Methoxy |
| -NO | Nitroso | $-\mathrm{OC}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5}$ | Ethoxy |

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-279.jpg?height=188&width=1622&top_left_y=1491&top_left_x=255&polygon=279,0,1615,7,1618,9,1620,16,1622,30,1622,156,1618,160,1503,165,78,188,55,188,44,183,3,156,0,55,0,34,3,12,39,7,224,0)

Groups given below have only prefix

| Functional group | Symbol | Prefix |
| :--- | :---: | :---: |
| Ether | -OR | alkoxy |
| Epoxide | $-\mathrm{O}-$ | epoxy |
| Azo | $-\mathrm{N}=\mathrm{N}-$ | azo |
| Nitro compound | $-\mathrm{NO}_{2}$ | nitro |
| Halogen | $-\mathrm{X}(\mathrm{F}, \mathrm{Cl}, \mathrm{Br}, \mathrm{I})$ | halo |

## Learn More

Besides these, some other functional groups are also treated as substituents in case of polyfunctional organic compounds. These are listed as :

| Functional group | Name | Secondary prefix |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $\mathrm{SO}_{3} \mathrm{H}$ | Sulphonyl | Sulpho |
| $-\mathrm{COOH}$ | Carboxylic acid | Carboxy |
| <smiles>[R]OOC([R])=O</smiles> | Ester | Alkylcarboxylate or alkoxycarboxy |
| -COX | Acyl halides | Halocarbonyl |
| $-\mathrm{CONH}_{2}$ | Amide | Carbamoyl |
| <smiles>[R]C(=O)OC([R])=O</smiles> | Acid anhydride | Oic anhydride |
| - CHO | Aldehyde | Formyl |
| <smiles>CC(C)=O</smiles> | Ketone | Oxo |
| -CN | Cyanide | Cyano |
| $-\mathrm{N} \equiv \mathrm{C}$ | Isocyanide | Isocyano |
| -OH | Alcohol | Hydroxy |
| -SH | Thioalcohol | Mercapto |
| $-\mathrm{NH}_{2}$ | Amine | Amino |
| - O - | Epoxy | Epoxide |

## Rules for IUPAC Nomenclature of Organic Compounds

There are certain (following) rules for naming a complex organic molecule according to IUPAC system. These rules are illustrated below.
(a) Longest chain rule : The first step for naming an organic compound is to select the longest continuous chain of carbon atoms which may or may not be horizontal (straight). This is called the parent chain or main chain, and other carbon chains attached to it are known as side chains. On the basis of the number of carbon atoms present in the parent chain, the parent hydrocarbon is ascertained. For example, if the parent chain contains six carbon atoms, the compound is considered to be a derivative of hexane.

For example : The structure I has the longest chain of six carbon atoms present in a straight line; therefore it is said to be a derivative of hexane. On the other hand, in structutre II the straight chain has only four or three carbon atoms, while the longest possible chain may have as many as six carbon atoms (zig-zag chain).
<img class="imgSvg" id = "mpcazmngrqx5rfza62n" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,"/>

I

Longest chain, straight

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-280.jpg?height=118&width=286&top_left_y=2320&top_left_x=1069)
II

Longest chain, zig-zag

Therefore, both the structures are derivatives of hexane.

In case a molecule contains two equally long carbon chains, the one carrying larger number of side chains is selected. For example, structures A and B have carbon chains of equal lengths $\left(\mathrm{C}_{7}\right)$, but the one carrying three substituents is selected.
<img class="imgSvg" id = "mpcazmnhk70b0fghz0l" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,"/>
(A)

Straight parent chain of C7 (correct) with three side chains
<img class="imgSvg" id = "mpcazmnkdpm0ykixvj" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,"/>
(B)

Zig-zag parent chain of C7 (incorrect) with only one side chain

## * Which of the following arrangement of selecting parent chain is correct?

<img class="imgSvg" id = "mpcazmnmialwe2kssjd" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,"/>

A
<img class="imgSvg" id = "mpcazmnnch4nng6oug7" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,"/>

B

## SOLUTION

Arrangement B containing 7 carbon atoms with more ( 2 ) number of side chain is correct, although arrangement A also contains 7 carbon atoms but it has only one side chain, hence incorrect.
(b) Lowest number or lowest sum rule : The carbon atom of longest carbon chain is numbered as $1,2,3,4, \ldots \ldots$ etc., starting from the end that gives the lowest possible number to the substituent. For example, in structures A and B numbering may be done in two ways;
<img class="imgSvg" id = "mpcazmnpgu9eiw154w5" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,"/>
(A)

Substituent at C3
(correct)
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-281.jpg?height=213&width=271&top_left_y=1617&top_left_x=1082)

In one case (A), the substituent is assigned position 3 while in other case (B), it is assigned position 4 . Hence the former, being smaller, is correct. The number that locates the position of a substituent is known as locant. Thus the locant for X in the above correct structure is 3 .

In case, the parent chain has two or more substituents, numbering must be done in such a way that the sum of the locants on the parent chain is the lowest possible. Thus in following structures A and B numbering may be done in two ways : in one (A), the sum of locants is 9 while in other (B), it is 12 , hence the former is correct while the latter is wrong.
<img class="imgSvg" id = "mpcazmnqsx2o57skue" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,"/>
(A)

Sum of locants : $2+3+4=9$ (correct)
<img class="imgSvg" id = "mpcazmntnpoy2y5k7xe" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,"/>
(B)

Sum of locants : $3+4+5=12$
(incorrect)

* Which of the following way of numbering of carbon atoms of parent chain is correct according to lowest sum rule?
<img class="imgSvg" id = "mpcazmnu9gog0d4ekrh" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,"/>

A
<img class="imgSvg" id = "mpcazmnw5rjiq0pij7b" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,"/>

B

## SOLUTION

A represents correct way of numbering as sum of locants is less in A than B.
Sum of locants in $\mathrm{A}=2+2+4=8$
Sum of locants in $\mathrm{B}=2+4+4=10$
(c) Naming of simple alkanes : The name of the substituent is prefixed to the name of the parent hydrocarbon and its position on the main chain is indicated by writing the locant before the prefix. A hyphen ( - ) is inserted between the locant and the substituent name. The final name is always written as one word. Thus the following compound is written as 2-Methylpentane.
<img class="imgSvg" id = "mpcazmnx75aajvdancu" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,"/>
(d) Alphabetical order of side chains : In case two or more alkyl groups (side chains) are attached to the parent chain, these are prefixed in alphabetic order, e.g.,
<img class="imgSvg" id = "mpcazmny2wp28yc28vh" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,"/>

3-Ethyl-2 methylpentane and not as
2-Methyl-3-ethylpentane

* Write IUPAC name of following compound :
<img class="imgSvg" id = "mpcazmnzhz9b6piub38" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,"/>


## SOLUTION

<img class="imgSvg" id = "mpcazmo17qnrsq76dcf" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,"/>

4-Ethyl-3-methylheptane
(e) If a substituent is present two or more times: This is indicated by the prefix di-, tri-, tetra-, etc. added to the substituent. The different locants of the substituents are separated by commas. For example,
<img class="imgSvg" id = "mpcazmo2oivnpkc41f" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,"/>

2, 3-Dimethylpentane
<img class="imgSvg" id = "mpcazmogznqtrkbqza" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,"/>

4-Ethyl-2,4-dimethylhexane

The spelling of the prefixes di-, tri-, and tetra- should not be considered while arranging the substituents alphabetically. In case of the same alkyl group, occurs twice on the same carbon atom, its locant is also repeated twice. For example,
<img class="imgSvg" id = "mpcazmoitrhqbxeobw" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,"/>

2, 2, 4-Trimethylpentane
<img class="imgSvg" id = "mpcazmojqggwysgk1m" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,"/>

4-Ethyl-3,3-dimethylheptane

When two different substituents are present at equivalent positions, the numbering of the parent chain is done in such a way that the substituent which comes first in the alphabetical order gets the lower number
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-283.jpg?height=173&width=712&top_left_y=849&top_left_x=858)

## * Write IUPAC name of following compound :

<img class="imgSvg" id = "mpcazmolqwl8dmc8rdc" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,"/>

## SOLUTION

<img class="imgSvg" id = "mpcazmomozp8np72i1" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,"/>

3,3-Diethylpentane

## NOMENCLATURE OF DIFFERENT CLASSES OF ORGANIC COMPOUNDS

The names of various homologous series and their simple members are given below.

## (a) Alkanes or Paraffins (Saturated Hydrocarbons):

These are such compounds containing only carbon and hydrogen atoms. The various carbon atoms present in the molecule are linked by single covalent bonds ( $\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{C}$ ). These are represented by the general formula $\mathrm{C}_{n} \mathrm{H}_{2 n+2}$. The IUPAC name for this homologous series is alkanes and the names of all alkanes will be ending with the suffix -ane. The common and IUPAC names for the first few members are identical.

| Formula | Common name | IUPAC name |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $\mathrm{CH}_{4}$ | Methane | Methane |
| $\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{6}$ | Ethane | Ethane |
| $\mathrm{C}_{3} \mathrm{H}_{8}$ | Propane | Propane |
| $\mathrm{C}_{4} \mathrm{H}_{10}$ | Butane | Butane |
| $\mathrm{C}_{5} \mathrm{H}_{12}$ | Pentane | Pentane |

* Write the structure of compound neononane?


## SOLUTION

Neo indicates $\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3}\right)_{3} \mathrm{C}$-group, and nonane indicates that total number of carbon atoms in the compound is nine.
<img class="imgSvg" id = "mpcazmoogdvp52cj7qh" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,"/>

Neononane
(b) Alkenes or Olefins (Unsaturated Hydrocarbons) :

These are characterised by the presence of a double bond between two carbon atoms, and contain two hydrogen atoms less than the corresponding alkanes. Their names are derived by substituting the suffix - ane of the corresponding alkanes by - ylene (common name) or -ene (IUPAC name). Thus IUPAC name for the series is alkenes.

General foumula : $\mathrm{C}_{n} \mathrm{H}_{2 n}$
Functional group :
<img class="imgSvg" id = "mpcazmop5nunremqaex" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,"/>

| Alkene | Common name | IUPAC name |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $\mathrm{CH}_{2}=\mathrm{CH}_{2}$ | Ethylene | Ethene |
| $\mathrm{CH}_{3}-\mathrm{CH}=\mathrm{CH}_{2}$ | Propylene | Propene |
| $\mathrm{CH}_{3}-\mathrm{CH}_{2}-\mathrm{CH}=\mathrm{CH}_{2}$ | Butylene | Butene |
| $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{CH}=\mathrm{CH}_{2}$ | Pentylene | Pentene |
| $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{CH}=\mathrm{CHCH}_{3}$ | $\beta$ - Hexylene | Hex-2- ene |
| $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{CH}=\mathrm{CHCH}_{2} \mathrm{CH}_{3}$ | $\gamma$-Hexylene | Hex-3-ene |
| $\mathrm{CH}_{2}=\mathrm{CH}-\mathrm{CH}=\mathrm{CH}_{2}$ | Butadiene | But-1, 3-diene |

* Write the IUPAC name of the following compound.
<img class="imgSvg" id = "mpcazmoq4zcy9wfrhkg" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,"/>


## SOLUTION

<img class="imgSvg" id = "mpcazmoscc57w3kszo6" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,"/>

## (c) Alkynes or Acetylenic Hydrocarbons

These are characterised by the presence of a triple bond between two carbon atoms, and contain four hydrogen atoms less than the corresponding alkanes. Their names are derived from the corresponding alkanes by substituting the suffix -ane by -yne.
General foumula : $\mathrm{C}_{n} \mathrm{H}_{2 n-2} \quad$ Functional group : $-\mathrm{C} \equiv \mathrm{C}-$

| Alkyne | Common name | IUPAC name |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $\mathrm{HC} \equiv \mathrm{CH}$ | Acetylene | Ethyne |
| $\mathrm{CH}_{3}-\mathrm{C} \equiv \mathrm{CH}$ | Methyl acetylene | Propyne |
| $\mathrm{CH}_{3}-\mathrm{C} \equiv \mathrm{C}-\mathrm{CH}_{3}$ | Dimethyl acetylene | But-2-yne |
| $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CH}_{2}-\mathrm{C} \equiv \mathrm{C}-\mathrm{CH}_{3}$ | Ethylmethyl acetylene | Pent-2-yne |
| $\mathrm{CH}_{3}-\mathrm{CH}_{2}-\mathrm{CH}_{2}-\mathrm{C} \equiv \mathrm{C}-\mathrm{CH}_{3}$ | Methylpropyl acetylene | Hex-2-yne |
| $\mathrm{HC} \equiv \mathrm{C}-\mathrm{CH}_{2}-\mathrm{CH}_{2}-\mathrm{C} \equiv \mathrm{CH}$ | Hexadiacetylene | Hex-1,5-diyne |

Did You
If both double and triple bonds are present at equal distance from end of the carbon chain, the Know?
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-285.jpg?height=172&width=134&top_left_y=1087&top_left_x=440)
double bond is always given lower number than the triple bond. For example,
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-285.jpg?height=103&width=730&top_left_y=1206&top_left_x=854)

* Write the IUPAC name of following compound
(i)
$\mathrm{CH}_{3}-\mathrm{CH}=\mathrm{CH}-\mathrm{C} \equiv \mathrm{CH}$
(ii) $\mathrm{CH}_{2}=\mathrm{CH}-\mathrm{CH}_{2}-\mathrm{CH}_{2}-\mathrm{C} \equiv \mathrm{CH}$


## SOLUTION

(i) ${ }^{5} \mathrm{CH}_{3}-{ }^{4} \mathrm{CH}={ }^{3} \mathrm{CH}-{ }^{2} \mathrm{C} \equiv{ }^{1} \mathrm{CH}$
(ii) ${ }^{1} \mathrm{CH}_{2}={ }^{2} \mathrm{CH}-\underset{\text { Hex-1-en-5yne }}{{ }^{3} \mathrm{CH}_{2}-{ }^{4} \mathrm{CH}_{2}-{ }^{5} \mathrm{C}} \equiv{ }^{6} \mathrm{CH}$

## (d) Halogen Derivatives

These are derived from alkanes by the replacement of one or more hydrogen atoms by halogen atoms $(\mathrm{Cl}, \mathrm{Br}$, or I). They are thus further classified as mono-, di-, tri-, or tetra-halogen derivatives when they contain 1,2 , 3 or 4 halogen atoms respectively in their molecules.
(i) Mono-halogen derivatives or alkyl halides : These are monohalogen derivatives of the alkanes.

General foumula : $\mathrm{C}_{n} \mathrm{H}_{2 n+1} \mathrm{X}$ or $\mathrm{R}-\mathrm{X}$, where R stands for an alkyl radical and X for a halogen atom.

Common names of alkyl halides are derived from the alkyl radical, while in IUPAC system these are named as substituted alkanes.

| Monohalogen derivative | Common name |
| :---: | :--- |
| $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{Cl}$ | Methyl chloride |
| $\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{Br}$ | Ethyl bromide |
| $\mathrm{C}_{3} \mathrm{H}_{7} \mathrm{I}$ | Propyl iodide |
| $\mathrm{CH}_{3}-\mathrm{CH}-\mathrm{CH}_{2}-\mathrm{CH}_{3}$ | Bromethane |
| Br | sechane |

* Give IUPAC name of following compound
<img class="imgSvg" id = "mpcazmot0qujqfc75iim" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,"/>


## SOLUTION

2-Iodopropane
(ii) Di-halogen derivatives : These are derived from alkanes by the replacement of two hydrogen atoms by two halogen atoms.
General foumula : $\mathrm{C}_{n} \mathrm{H}_{2 n} \mathrm{X}_{2}$

If the two halogen atoms are attached to the same carbon atom, the derivative is known as alkylidene halide, and when the two halogen atoms are attached to two different atoms, the compound is known as alkylene halide.
(a) Alkylidene halide :
<img class="imgSvg" id = "mpcazmouef25yrv27br" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,"/>
(b) Alkylene halide :
<img class="imgSvg" id = "mpcazmovhirpfs3kr2b" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,"/>

Di-halogen deriv

Common name
IUPAC name

Ethylene dibromide
1,2-Dibromoethane
<img class="imgSvg" id = "mpcazmox96lvbvbkkrn" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,"/>

Ethylidene dibromide
1,1-Dibromoethane
(iii) Tri-and tetra-halogen derivatives : These are obtained by the replacement of three or four hydrogen atoms from the corresponding alkanes by three or four halogen atoms respectively.
General foumula : $\mathrm{C}_{n} \mathrm{H}_{2 n-1} \mathrm{X}_{3}$ for trihalogen derivatives and $\mathrm{C}_{n} \mathrm{H}_{2 n-2} \mathrm{X}_{4}$ for tetrahalogen derivatives where X stands for a halogen atom.

| Formula | Common names | IUPAC name |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| $\mathrm{CHCl}_{3}$ | Chloroform | Trichloromethane |
| $\mathrm{CHI}_{3}$ | Iodoform | Triodomethane |
| $\mathrm{CCl}_{4}$ | Carbon tetrachloride | Tetrachloromethane |

(e) Alcohols

These are derived from alkanes by the replacement of one, two or three hydrogen atoms by hydroxy ( -OH ) groups, and thus these are correspondingly known as monohydric-, dihydric-, and trihydric alcohols.
(i) Monohydric alcohols : The IUPAC names of monohydric alcohols are derived from the corresponding alkane by replacing the $e$ of alkane by -ol. Thus, in general, these are also known as alkanols.
The monohydric alcohols may further be classified into primary, secondary and tertiary alcohols depending on the carbon to which the alcoholic $(-\mathrm{OH})$ group is attached.
General formula : $\mathrm{C}_{n} \mathrm{H}_{2 n+1} \mathrm{OH}$ or $\mathrm{R}-\mathrm{OH}$, where R stands for an alkyl radical and X for a halogen atom.
Characteristic groups:
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-286.jpg?height=93&width=104&top_left_y=2553&top_left_x=1818)
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-287.jpg?height=140&width=873&top_left_y=370&top_left_x=612)

| Formula | Common name | IUPAC name |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $\mathrm{R}-\mathrm{OH}$ | Alkyl alcohol | Alkanol |
| $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{OH}$ | Methyl alcohol | Methanol |
| $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{OH}$ | $n$-Propyl alcohol | 1-Propanol (pri.) |
| <smiles>CC(C)O</smiles> | iso-Propyl alcohol | 2-Propanol (sec.) |
| $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{OH}$ | $n$-Butyl alcohol | 1-Butanol (pri.) |
| <smiles>CCC(C)O</smiles> | sec-Butyl alcohol | 2-Butanol (sec.) |
| <smiles>CC(C)(C)O</smiles> | tert-Butyl alcohol | 2-Methyl-2-propanol (ter.) |

where $p r i=$ Primary; sec $=$ secondary; and ter $=$ tertiary.

## Draw the structure of 2-methyl-4-chloropentanol.

<img class="imgSvg" id = "mpcazmpi2o6urvij2mt" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,"/>

## * Give IUPAC name of the following compound.

<img class="imgSvg" id = "mpcazmpjpdsyve5ao1" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,"/>

## SOLUTION

<img class="imgSvg" id = "mpcazmpkib75zzb7su" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,"/>

2, 4, 4-Trimethyl pentanol
(ii) Dihydric alcohols : Compounds having two hydroxyl groups are known as dihydric alcohols, or glycols or alkanediols.

General foumula:
<img class="imgSvg" id = "mpcazmpm3o79vmf2iv" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,"/>

Characteristic group:
<img class="imgSvg" id = "mpcazmpnwm3aavvh84j" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,"/>
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-288.jpg?height=200&width=323&top_left_y=625&top_left_x=185)

Since compounds having two hydroxyl groups on the same carbon atom are usually unstable, the two hydroxyl groups of glycols must be attached to two different carbon atoms as glycols are quite stable compounds.

IUPAC names of the dihydric alcohols are derived by adding the suffix -diol to the name of the corresponding alkane and indicating the positions of the -OH groups by the numerals.

| Formula | Common name | IUPAC name |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| $\mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{OH} . \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{OH}$ | Ethylene glycol | 1, 2-Ethanediol |
| $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CHOH} . \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{OH}$ | Propylene glycol | 1, 2-Propanediol |
| $\mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{OH} . \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{OH}$ | Trimethylene glycol | 1, 3-Propanediol |

* Draw the structure of 1, 4-butanediol.


## SOLUTION

$$
\xrightarrow[\text { 1, 4-Butanediol }]{\mathrm{HOH}_{2} \mathrm{C}-\mathrm{CH}_{2}-\mathrm{CH}_{2}-\mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{OH}}
$$

(iii) Trihydric alcohols : These contain three hydroxyl groups attached to three different carbon atoms.

General foumula: $\mathrm{C}_{n} \mathrm{H}_{2 n-1}(\mathrm{OH})_{3}$
Characteristic group:
<img class="imgSvg" id = "mpcazmpohq13bvvtu3q" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,"/>

IUPAC names are derived by adding the suffix-triol to the name of the corresponding alkane and indicating the positions of the - OH groups by numerals. In case the compound contains more than three hydroxyl groups, it is known as polyhydric alcohol. In IUPAC system these are named by putting a suffix polyol after the name of the parent hydrocarbon. For example,

| Formula | Common name | IUPAC name |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| $\mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{OH} . \mathrm{CHOH} . \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{OH}$ | Glycerol | Propane-1, 2, 3-triol |
| $\mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{OH} .(\mathrm{CHOH})_{4} . \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{OH}$ | Sorbitol | Hexane-1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6-hexol |

## (f) Ethers

These are derived from alkanes by the replacement of one hydrogen atom by an alkoxy group (-OR).
When the two alkyl groups on either side of oxygen ( O ) are same, the ether is called as simple or symmetrical ether; and when they are different, it is called mixed or unsymmetrical ether. Thus

General formula :
$\mathrm{R}-\mathrm{O}-\mathrm{R}$
$\mathrm{R}-\mathrm{O}-\mathrm{R}^{\prime}$
Simple ether
Mixed ether

Functional group : -O-
The IUPAC (systematic) name for ethers is alkoxyalkanes.

| Formula | Common name | IUPAC name |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| $\mathrm{CH}_{3}-\mathrm{O}-\mathrm{CH}_{3}$ | Dimethyl ether | Methoxymethane |
| $\mathrm{CH}_{3}-\mathrm{O}-\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5}$ | Ethyl methyl ether | Methoxyethane |
| $\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5}-\mathrm{O}-\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5}$ | Diethyl ether | Ethoxyethane |

## (g) Thio-alcohols, Thiols, Mercaptans or Alkanethiols

Thioalcohols are derived by replacing O (oxygen) atom of alcohol by S (sulphur) atom.
General formula: R-SH
Characteristic group: -SH (mercapto or thiol group)
The secondary suffix used for thio alcohols or mercaptans in IUPAC system is thiol (in place of -ol used in alcohols), while the common names are obtained by adding thio alcohol to the name of the alkyl group.

| Formula | Common name | IUPAC name |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{SH}$ | Methyl mercaptan or <br> Methyl thioalcohol | Methanethiol |
| $\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{SH}$ | Ethyl thioalcohol | Ethanethiol |
| $\mathrm{C}_{4} \mathrm{H}_{9} \mathrm{SH}$ | Butyl thioalcohol | Butanethiol |
| $\mathrm{C}_{10} \mathrm{H}_{21} \mathrm{SH}$ | Decyl thioalcohol | Decanethiol |

## ILLUSTRATION : 3

Write the IUPAC name of the following compound.
<img class="imgSvg" id = "mpcazmprdfm1v5bd0g4" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,"/>

## SOLUTION :

2-Bromopropanethiol
(h) Thioethers or Alkyl Sulphides

These are obtained by replacing O atom of ethers by S atom.
General formula: R-S-R
Functional group: -S-
where the two R groups may be same (simple thioether) or different (mixed thioether). The common and IUPAC names for thioethers are as below.

| Formula | Common name | IUPAC name |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| $\mathrm{CH}_{3}-\mathrm{S}-\mathrm{CH}_{3}$ | Dimethyl thioether | Dimethyl sulphide |
| $\mathrm{CH}_{3}-\mathrm{S}-\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5}$ | Methyl ethyl thioether | Ethyl methyl sulphide |
| $\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5}-\mathrm{S}-\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5}$ | Diethyl thioether | Diethyl sulphide |

## (i) Aldehydes or Alkanals

These are derived from alkanes by the replacement of one hydrogen atom by aldehydic (- CHO ) group.
General foumula : $\mathrm{C}_{n} \mathrm{H}_{2 n+1} \mathrm{CHO}$ or $\mathrm{R}-\mathrm{CHO}$
Functional group : -CHO

| Formula | Length of carbon chain | Common name | IUPAC name |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| HCHO | $\mathrm{C}_{1}$ | Formaldehyde | Methanal |
| $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CHO}$ | $\mathrm{C}_{2}$ | Acetaldehyde | Ethanal |
| $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{CHO}$ | $\mathrm{C}_{3}$ | Propionaldehyde | Propanal |
| $\mathrm{C}_{3} \mathrm{H}_{7} \mathrm{CHO}$ | $\mathrm{C}_{4}$ | Butyraldehyde | Butanal |

## Did You Know

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-290.jpg?height=140&width=1424&top_left_y=694&top_left_x=388)

* Write IUPAC name of the following compound.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-290.jpg?height=179&width=419&top_left_y=1066&top_left_x=790)


## SOLUTION

<img class="imgSvg" id = "mpcazmps85n58x0z7dj" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,"/>

4-Methylhexanal

## (j) Ketones or Alkanones

These are derived by replacing two hydrogen atoms from two molecules of alkanes by a divalent ketonic ( $>\mathrm{C}=\mathrm{O}$ ) group.
General formula : $\mathrm{C}_{n} \mathrm{H}_{2 n+1}-\mathrm{CO}-\mathrm{C}_{n} \mathrm{H}_{2 n+1}$ or $\mathrm{R}-\mathrm{CO}-\mathrm{R}$
Characteristic group : $>\mathrm{C}=\mathrm{O}$
The two alkyl groups of a ketone may be similar (simple ketones) or different (mixed ketones).
The IUPAC names (alkanones) are derived by replacing terminal ' $e$ ' of the alkane by 'one'. The position of the CO group is indicated by placing the number of the carbon atom to which oxygen is attached at the end. For example,

| Formula | Common name | IUPAC name |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COCH}_{3}$ | Dimethyl ketone | Propanone-2 |
| $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COC}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5}$ | Ethylmethyl ketone | Butanone-2 |
| $\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{COC}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5}$ | Diethyl ketone | Pentanone-3 |

The prefix 2 or 3, indicates the position of the functional group carbon atom which must always get the lowest possible number.
(k) Carboxylic Acids

These are derived by replacing one or two hydrogen atoms of the alkanes by carboxylic (- COOH ) group.
(i) Monocarboxylic acids, fatty acids or alkanoic acids : These acids contain one-COOH group. Their IUPAC names are derived from the name of the corresponding alkane by replacing the final $-e$ with - oic acid.
General foumula : $\mathrm{C}_{n} \mathrm{H}_{2 n+1} \mathrm{COOH}$ or $\mathrm{R}-\mathrm{COOH}$
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-290.jpg?height=93&width=102&top_left_y=2553&top_left_x=1820)

Functional group : - COOH

| Formula | Length of carbon chain | Common name | IUPAC name |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| HCOOH | $\mathrm{C}_{1}$ | Formic acid | Methanoic acid |
| $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOH}$ | $\mathrm{C}_{2}$ | Acetic acid | Ethanoic acid |
| $\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{COOH}$ | $\mathrm{C}_{3}$ | Propionic acid | Propanoic acid |
| $\mathrm{C}_{3} \mathrm{H}_{7} \mathrm{COOH}$ | $\mathrm{C}_{4}$ | Butyric acid | Butanoic acid |

* Draw structure of the compound 2-chlorobutanoic acid.


## SOLUTION

<img class="imgSvg" id = "mpcazmpuykg1cqvp12m" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,"/>
(ii) Dicarboxylic acids or alkanedioic acids : These compounds contain two - COOH groups attached to the same or different carbon atoms.

| General foumula | $:$ | $\mathrm{C}_{n} \mathrm{H}_{2 n}(\mathrm{COOH})_{2}$ |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Functional group | $:$ | $-\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{COOH}$ |
|  |  | $-\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{COOH}$ |

or
<img class="imgSvg" id = "mpcazmpv48wev25dkod" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,"/>

Their IUPAC names are derived by adding the word-dioic acid to the name of the alkane. Thus

| Formula | Length of carbon <br> chain | Common name | IUPAC name |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| COOH | $\mathrm{C}_{2}$ | Oxalic acid | Ethanedioic acid or Dicarboxylic acid |
| COOH |  |  |  |

<img class="imgSvg" id = "mpcazmpxz2orpz0nlgj" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,"/>
$\mathrm{C}_{3}$
Malonic acid
Propanedioic acid or Methanedicarboxylic acid
<img class="imgSvg" id = "mpcazmpyo29qif6fbj" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,"/>
$\mathrm{C}_{4}$
Succinic acid
Butanedioic acid or 1, 2-Ethanedicarboxylic acid

The tri- or poly- carboxylic acids are known as alkanetrioic acid or alkane-polyoic acid. Know!
(I) Acids Derivatives

These are derived by the replacement of an atom or group of atoms of the carboxylic group by other atom or group of atoms. There are four important acid derivatives.
(i) Acid halides or acyl halides : These are obtained by the replacement of the -OH group of the -COOH group by a halogen atom. Their IUPAC (systematic) names are obtained by replacing the ' $e$ ' of alkane by 'oyl halide'. Alternatively, they can be named by replacing the suffix-oic acid of the carboxylic acids by-oyl halide.

| General foumula | $:$ | $\mathrm{C}_{n} \mathrm{H}_{2 n+1} \mathrm{COX}$ or $\mathrm{R}-\mathrm{COX}$ |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Functional group | $:$ | -COX |


| Formula | Length of carbon chain | Common name | IUPAC name |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| HCOCl | $\mathrm{C}_{1}$ | Formyl chloride | Methanoyl chloride |
| $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COBr}$ | $\mathrm{C}_{2}$ | Acetyl bromide | Ethanoyl bromide |
| $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{COCl}$ | $\mathrm{C}_{3}$ | Propionyl chloride | Propanoyl chloride |

(ii) Acid amides : These are derived by the replacement of -OH part of the -COOH group by $\left(-\mathrm{NH}_{2}\right)$. Their IUPAC names are derived by replacing the ' $e$ ' of alkane by 'amide' or by replacing the suffix-oic acid of the carboxylic acid by amide.
$\begin{array}{lll}\text { General formula } & : & \mathrm{C}_{n} \mathrm{H}_{2 n+1} \mathrm{CONH}_{2} \text { or } \mathrm{R}-\mathrm{CONH}_{2} \\ \text { Functional group } & : & -\mathrm{CONH}_{2}\end{array}$

| Formula | Corresponding acid | Common name | IUPAC name |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| $\mathrm{HCONH}_{2}$ | HCOOH (Formic acid or Methanoic acid) | Formamide | Methanamide |
| $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CONH}_{2}$ | $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOH}$ (Acetic acid or Ethanoic acid) | Acetamide | Ethanamide |
| $\mathrm{C}_{3} \mathrm{H}_{7} \mathrm{CONH}_{2}$ | $\mathrm{C}_{3} \mathrm{H}_{7} \mathrm{COOH}$ (Propionic acid or Propanoic acid) | Propionamide | Propanamide |

(iii) Acid anhydrides : These are considered to be derived from two (similar or different) carboxylic acid molecules by the elimination of a molecule of water.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-292.jpg?height=172&width=793&top_left_y=1183&top_left_x=603)

The common names of acid anhydrides are derived from the name of the parent acid or acids; while the systematic names are obtained by replacing 'acid' from the name of the acid by 'anhydride', e.g.,

| Formula | Parent acid | Common name | IUPAC name |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CO}$ | $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOH}$, Acetic acid | Acetic anhydride | Ethanoic anhydride |
| $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CO}$ | (Ethanoic acid) |  |  |

$$
\begin{array}{lc}
\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{CO} & \mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{COOH}, \text { Propionic acid Propionic anhydride Propanoic anhydride } \\
\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{CO} & \text { (Propanoic acid) }
\end{array}
$$

## (m) Amines

Amines can be regarded as derived from ammonia by the replacement of one, two or three hydrogen atoms by the corresponding number of alkyl groups. Amines are of three types depending upon the number of alkyl groups attached to nitrogen.
(i) Primary amines : These are monoalkyl derivatives of ammonia.
(ii) Secondary amines : These are dialkyl derivatives of ammonia.
(iii) Tertiary amines : These are trialkyl derivatives of ammonia.

| Types of amine | General formula | Functional group |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Primary amine | $\mathrm{R}-\mathrm{NH}_{2}$ | $-\mathrm{NH}_{2}$ |
| Secondary amine | $\mathrm{R}-\mathrm{NH}-\mathrm{R}$ | $>\mathrm{NH}$ |
| Tertiary amine | $\mathrm{R}_{3}-\mathrm{N}$ | $\frac{\searrow}{\nearrow} \mathrm{N}$ |

Common names of amines are derived according to the alkyl group (s) attached to the nitrogen
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-293.jpg?height=173&width=138&top_left_y=625&top_left_x=440)
atom and adding the suffix amine.

The systematic (IUPAC) names for primary, secondary and tertiary amines are alkanamine, alkylalkanamine, and dialkylalkanamine respectively.

| Formula | Type of amine | Common name | IUPAC name |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{NH}_{2}$ | Primary | Methyl amine | Methanamine |
| $\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{NH}_{2}$ | Primary | Ethyl amine | Ethanamine |
| $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{NHCH}_{3}$ | Secondary | Dimethyl amine (N-Methyl methyl amine) | Methylmethanamine |
| $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{NHC}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5}$ | Secondary | Ethyl methyl amine ( $\mathrm{N}-$ Methyl ethyl amine) | N -Methylethanamine |
| $\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3}\right)_{3} \mathrm{~N}$ | Tertiary | Trimethyl amine (N, N-Dimethyl methyl amine) | $\mathrm{N}, \mathrm{N}$-Dimethylmethanamine |
| $\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3}\right)_{2} \mathrm{NC}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5}$ | Tertiary | Ethyl dimethyl amine (N, N-Dimethyl ethyl amine) | $\mathrm{N}, \mathrm{N}$-Dimethylethanamine |

## (n) Alkyl Cyanides, or Carbonitriles

These are derived by replacing a hydrogen atom of the alkanes or $\mathrm{a}-\mathrm{COOH}$ group of the acid by nitrile (-CN) group.
General forumula : $\quad \mathrm{C}_{n} \mathrm{H}_{2 n+1} \mathrm{CN}$ or $\mathrm{R}-\mathrm{CN}$
Functional group : -CN or $-\mathrm{C} \equiv \mathrm{N}$
Their common names are obtained by adding suffix cyanide to the alkyl group or by replacing - ic acid from the name of the corresponding acid by the suffix onitrile, while systematic names are derived by adding suffix nitrile to the parent alkane, e.g.

| Formula | Length of carbon chain | Common name | IUPAC name |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| HCN | $\mathrm{C}_{1}$ | Hydrogen cyanide | Methanenitrile |
| $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CN}$ | $\mathrm{C}_{2}$ | Methyl cyanide or Acetonitrile | Ethanenitrile |
| $\mathrm{C}_{3} \mathrm{H}_{7} \mathrm{CN}$ | $\mathrm{C}_{4}$ | Propyl cyanide or Propiononitrile | Butanenitrile |

Did You Know!

While counting number of carbon atom in parent chain the carbon atom present in -CN group is also taken into consideration.

## (o) Alkyl Isocyanides, Isonitriles, Carbylamines

These may be derived by replacing a hydrogen atom of alkanes or a -COOH group of carboxylic acid by isonitrile (-NC) group. Thus

$$
\begin{array}{lll}
\text { General formula } & : & \mathrm{C}_{n} \mathrm{H}_{2 n+1} \mathrm{NC} \text { or } \mathrm{RNC} \\
\text { Functional group } & : & -\mathrm{N} \stackrel{\rightarrow}{=} \text { or }-\mathrm{NC}
\end{array}
$$

In the trivial (common) system these are named as alkyl isocyanides or alkyl isonitriles; while in the systematic names, the word carbylamine is added to the name of the alkyl radical.

| Formula | Common name | IUPAC name |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{NC}$ | Methyl isocyanide | Methyl carbylamine |
| $\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{NC}$ | Ethyl isocyanide | Ethyl carbylamine |
| $\mathrm{C}_{4} \mathrm{H}_{9} \mathrm{NC}$ | Butyl isocyanide | Butyl carbylamine |

## (p) Nitroalkanes or Nitroparaffins

These may be regarded as derived from alkanes by the replacement of a hydrogen atom by $-\mathrm{NO}_{2}$ (nitro) group.
General formula : $\quad \mathrm{R}-\mathrm{NO}_{2}$
Functional group : $\quad-\mathrm{NO}_{2}$ or $-\mathrm{N}_{\searrow_{\mathrm{O}}}$
The common and systematic names are generally the same.
They are named by adding the prefix nitro before the name of the corresponding alkane, e.g.

| $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{NO}_{2}$ | Nitromethane |
| :--- | :--- |
| $\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{NO}_{2}$ | Nitroethane |

## (q) Alkyl Nitrites

These are derived from alkanes by the replacement of a hydrogen atom by nitrite ( $-\mathrm{O}-\mathrm{N}=\mathrm{O}$ ) group.
General formula : $\quad \mathrm{R}-\mathrm{O}-\mathrm{N}=\mathrm{O}$
Functional group : $\quad-\mathrm{O}-\mathrm{N}=\mathrm{O}$
The common and IUPAC names are same. These are named by adding the suffix nitrite to the name of the alkyl group, e.g.

| $\mathrm{CH}_{3}-\mathrm{O}-\mathrm{N}=\mathrm{O}$ | Methyl nitrite |
| :--- | :--- |
| $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{CH}_{2}-\mathrm{O}-\mathrm{N}=\mathrm{O}$ | $n$-Propyl nitrite |

## Time to

## Check Your

Knowledge

* Which of the following compound is nitroethane?
(a) $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{NO}_{2}$
(b) $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{ONO}$


## SOLUTION

(a) represents nitroethane as it contains nitro group, whereas (b) is ethylnitrite since it has nitrite (- ONO) group.

## Learn More

Rules for nomenclature of poly functional organic compounds:
(i) If the organic compound contains two or more functional groups, one of the group is selected as principal functional group containing longest possible chain of carbon atoms and all the remaining functional groups are treated as substituents.
Order of preference used while selecting the principal functional group.
Carboxylic acids $>$ Sulphonic acids $>$ Anhydrides $>$ Esters $>$ Acid chlorides $>$ Acid amides $>$ Nitriles $>$ Aldehydes $>$ Cyanides $>$ Isocyanides $>$ Ketones $>$ Alcohols $>$ Phenols $>$ Thiols $>$ Amines $>$ Ethers $>$ Alkenes $>$ Alkynes.
(ii) Select the longest continuous chain of carbon atoms containing the principal functional group and maximum number of secondary functional groups and multiple bonds.
(iii) Number the principal chain in such a way that the principal functional group gets the lowest locant followed by double bond, triple bond and the substituents.

## ISOMERISM

In the study of organic chemistry we come across many cases when two or more compounds are made of equal number of like atoms. A molecular formula does not tell the nature of organic compound, sometimes several organic compounds may have same molecular formula. These compounds possess the same molecular formula but differ from each other in physical or chemical properties, and called as isomers and the phenomenon is termed isomerism proposed by Berzelius (Greek, iso $=$ equal ; meros $=$ parts). Since isomers have the same molecular formula, the difference in their properties must be due to different modes of the combination or arrangement of atoms within the molecule. Broadly speaking, isomerism is of two types.
(i) Structural isomerism
(ii) Stereoisomerism

The following figure shows the pictorial representation of different types of isomerism
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-295.jpg?height=465&width=1619&top_left_y=840&top_left_x=255)

## Structural Isomerism (Constitutional Isomerism)

Structural isomers possess the same molecular formula but different arrangement of atoms or groups within the molecules. The phenomenon is called structural isomerism.

The term constitutional isomerism is a more modern term of structural isomerism. It arises
Did You Know! because of the difference in the sequence of covalently bonded atoms in the molecule without reference to space.

## Types of Structural Isomerism :

(a) Chain / nuclear / skeleton isomerism :

This type of isomerism arises from the difference in the structure of carbon chain which forms the nucleus of the molecules. Here isomers have same molecular formula but different arrangement of carbon chain. e.g.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-295.jpg?height=284&width=366&top_left_y=1864&top_left_x=233)

Chain isomerism is observed when the number of carbon atoms is four or more than four in an organic compound. Chain isomer differ in the nature of carbon chain, i.e., in the length of the carbon chain.

- The isomer showing chain isomerism belong to the same homologous series, i.e., functional group, class of the compound (cyclic or open) remains unchanged.
(i) $\mathrm{C}_{4} \mathrm{H}_{10}$ :
<img class="imgSvg" id = "mpcazmqhai5byw2ygf7" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,"/>
<img class="imgSvg" id = "mpcazmqicvwi1mo17tm" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,"/>
or
<img class="imgSvg" id = "mpcazmqiocwur6kkd3m" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,"/>
or
<img class="imgSvg" id = "mpcazmqj8o51809ywxt" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,"/>
(ii)
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-296.jpg?height=999&width=1465&top_left_y=370&top_left_x=306)
(vi)
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-296.jpg?height=218&width=380&top_left_y=1155&top_left_x=459)

Chain isomers have almost similar chemical properties but different physical properties. For example the branched chain isomers have lower B.P. than that of their linear counter part.
(b) Position isomerism

It is the type of isomerism in which the compounds possessing same molecular formula differ in their properties due to the difference in the position of either the functional group or the multiple bond. For example :

| $\mathrm{C}_{4} \mathrm{H}_{8}$ | $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{CH}=\mathrm{CH}_{2}$ <br> 1-Butene | ; | $\underset{\text { 2-Butene }}{\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CH}}=\mathrm{CHCH}_{3}$ |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $\mathrm{C}_{4} \mathrm{H}_{6}$ | $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{C} \equiv \mathrm{CH}$ <br> 1-Butyne | ; | $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{C} \equiv \mathrm{CCH}_{3}$ <br> 2-Butyne |
| $\mathrm{C}_{3} \mathrm{H}_{7} \mathrm{Cl}$ | <smiles>CCCCl</smiles> <br> 1-Chloropropane | ; | <smiles>CC(C)Cl</smiles> <br> 2-Chloropropane |
| $\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{4} \mathrm{Cl}_{2}$ : | <smiles>CC(Cl)Cl</smiles> <br> 1,1-Dichloroethane | ; | <smiles>ClCCCl</smiles> <br> 1,2-Dichloroethane |
| $\mathrm{C}_{3} \mathrm{H}_{8} \mathrm{O}$ | <smiles>CCCO</smiles> |  | <smiles>CC(C)O</smiles> <br> 2-Propanol |
| $\mathrm{C}_{5} \mathrm{H}_{10} \mathrm{O}$ | <smiles>CCCC(C)=O</smiles> <br> 2-Pentanone | ; | <smiles>CCC(=O)CC</smiles> <br> 3-Pentanone |

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-297.jpg?height=232&width=1623&top_left_y=374&top_left_x=255)
(c) Functional isomerism : If the molecules have the same molecular formula but differ in the type of the functional group, then it is known as functional isomerism.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-297.jpg?height=270&width=1626&top_left_y=724&top_left_x=255)

Example :
(i) Molecular formula : $\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{6} \mathrm{O}$
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-297.jpg?height=120&width=803&top_left_y=1071&top_left_x=388)
(ii) Molecular formula : $\mathrm{C}_{3} \mathrm{H}_{6} \mathrm{O}$
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-297.jpg?height=182&width=798&top_left_y=1219&top_left_x=388)
(iii) Molecular formula : $\mathrm{C}_{3} \mathrm{H}_{6} \mathrm{O}_{2}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{CH}_{3}-\mathrm{CH}_{2}-\mathrm{COOH} \\
& \text { Propanoic acid } \\
& \text { (Acid) }
\end{aligned}
$$

and
<img class="imgSvg" id = "mpcazmqr22qk8l7n4s" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,"/>
(iv) Molecular formula : $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{NO}_{2}$
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-297.jpg?height=173&width=213&top_left_y=1644&top_left_x=388)

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{CH}_{3}-\mathrm{O}-\mathrm{N}=\mathrm{O} \\
& \text { Methyl nitrite } \\
& \text { (Nitrite) }
\end{aligned}
$$

(Nitro)

(d) Metamerism : Compounds having same molecular formula but different alkyl groups on either side of the functional group, are called metamers. This type of isomerism is shown by ethers and ketones.
<img class="imgSvg" id = "mpcazmqvsif3zoh8ewf" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,"/>

(e) Tautomerism : This isomerism is due to spontaneous intreconversion of two isomeric forms with different functional groups. The prerequisites for this is the presence of the $\mathrm{C}=\mathrm{O}, \mathrm{C}=\mathrm{N}$ or $\mathrm{N}=\mathrm{O}$ in the usual cases and an alpha H atom. The most usual is the 'keto-enol' tautomerism, but there can be other like nitro-aci and amine-imine forms. In general the keto form is more stable. Enols can be formed by acid or base catalysis from the ketone and are extensively used in making $\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{C}$ single bonds in organic synthesis.

## For example :

$$
\mathrm{C}_{3} \mathrm{H}_{6} \mathrm{O}:
$$

## Multiple Choice Questions

## DIRECTIONS : This section contains multiple choice questions. Each question has four choices (a), (b), (c) and (d) out of which ONLY ONE is correct.

1. The functional group present in $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOC}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5}$ is :
(a) ketonic
(b) aldehyde
(c) ester
(d) carboxylic
2. Butanone is a four carbon compound with the functional group -
(a) carboxylic acid
(b) aldehyde
(c) ketone
(d) alcohol
3. Which of the following is incorrectly matched?
(a) vinegar ⟶ carboxylic acid
(b) $\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{6} \longrightarrow$ alkane
(c) ethanol ⟶ alcohol
(d) methanol ⟶ ketone
4. If a hydrocarbon has any double bond, it is -
(a) alkyne
(b) alkane
(c) alkene
(d) all of these
5. The functional group present in an organic acid is -
(a) -OH
(b) -CHO
(c) -COOH
(d) $>\mathrm{C}=\mathrm{O}$
6. The IUPAC name of
<img class="imgSvg" id = "mpcazmrqbnomuv3oc4v" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,"/>
(a) 1,1-Methylethylpropane
(b) 2-Ethylbutane
(c) 1-Methyl-1-ethylpropane
(d) 3-Methylpentane
7. Alkynes are characterized by -
(a) $\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{C}$ bonds
(b) $\mathrm{C}=\mathrm{C}$ bonds

(d) cyclic structure

(a) al
(b) oxo
(c) amino
(d) amine

(b) carbamoyl
(c) cyano

## Passage Based Questions

DIRECTIONS : Study the given paragraph(s) and answer the following questions.

## Passage

Compounds having the same molecular formula but different properties are called as isomers. If compounds have same molecular formula but differ in the sequence of bonded atoms, they are called as structural isomers. Structural isomers may be of several types like chain isomers, ring chain isomers, positional isomers, functional isomers, metamers and tautomers.

1. How many structural isomers are possible for organic compound having formula $\mathrm{C}_{4} \mathrm{H}_{10}$ ?
(a) 4
(b) 5
(c) 6
(d) 2
2. How many chain isomers are possible for organic compound having molecular formula $\mathrm{C}_{4} \mathrm{H}_{8}$ ?
(a) 2
(b) 5
(c) 6
(d) 7
3. How many positional isomers are possible for organic compound having molecular formula $\mathrm{C}_{4} \mathrm{H}_{8} \mathrm{Cl}_{2}$ ?
(a) 7
(b) 8
(c) 6
(d) 5

## Assertion \& Reason

DIRECTIONS : Each of these questions contains an assertion followed by Reason. Read them carefully and answer the question on the basis of following options. You have to select the one that best describes the two statements.
(a) If both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of Assertion.
(b) If both Assertion and Reason are correct, but Reason is not the correct explanation of Assertion.
(c) If Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
(d) If Assertion is incorrect but Reason is correct.

1. Assertion : A primary suffix indicates the type of linkage in the carbon atom.

Reason : CN is a primary suffix.
2. Assertion : The general formula for a dihydric alcohol is $\mathrm{C}_{n} \mathrm{H}_{2 n}(\mathrm{OH})_{2}$.
Reason : Ethylene glycol is a dihydric alcohol.
3. Assertion : The correct IUPAC name for the compound
<img class="imgSvg" id = "mpcazmthecyvodjam6d" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,"/>
is 2, 3-Dimethylpentane not
3, 4-Dimethylpentane.
Reason : In case the parent chain has two or more substitutents, numbering must be done in such a way that the sum of the locants on the parent chain is the lowest possible.
4. Assertion : Chain isomerism is observed in compounds containining four or more than four carbon atoms.
Reason : Only alkanes show chain isomerism.
5. Assertion : But-1-ene and 2-methylprop-1-ene are position isomers.

Reason : Position isomers have same molecular formula but differ in position of functional group or $\mathrm{C}=\mathrm{C}$.
6. Assertion : Ethers are regarded as anhydrides of alcohols.

Reason : Ethers may be obtained by elimination of water molecule from two alcohol molecules.

## 9. Organic Compounds

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-306.jpg?height=397&width=437&top_left_y=365&top_left_x=1374)

Foundation

## (CONCEPTMAP)

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-306.jpg?height=1739&width=1497&top_left_y=809&top_left_x=312)

## ALKANES

Alkanes or saturated hydrocarbons are those in which all the four valencies of carbon atom(s) are satisfied by four atoms or groups i.e. the carbon valencies are fully satisfied. These compounds contain only $\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{C}$ and $\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{H}$ types of covalent bonds. Since they are relatively inert towards most of the chemical reagents under ordinary conditions, they are called paraffins (Latin-parum, little; affinis-affinity). The IUPAC name for these compounds is alkanes. Their general formula is $\mathrm{C}_{n} \mathrm{H}_{2 n+2}$. Alkanes are quite stable compounds and give substitution reactions while alkenes and alkynes are more reactive and generally give addition reactions.

## General Methods of Preparation of Alkanes

(a) From carboxylic acids
(i) By de-carboxylation of sodium salt of fatty acids :-

$$
\underset{\text { Sodium carboxylate }}{\mathrm{RCOONa}}+\underset{\text { Soda lime }}{\mathrm{NaOH} / \mathrm{CaO}} \xrightarrow{\Delta} \underset{\text { Alkane }}{\mathrm{R}-\mathrm{H}}+\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{CO}_{3}
$$

e.g. $\quad \mathrm{CH}_{3}[\mathrm{COONa}+\mathrm{NaO}] \mathrm{H}(\mathrm{CaO}) \longrightarrow \mathrm{CH}_{4}+\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{CO}_{3}$

$$
\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{COONa}+\mathrm{NaOH}(\mathrm{CaO}) \longrightarrow \mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{6}+\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{CO}_{3}
$$

Since -COOH present in the original acid is removed in the process (in the form of carbonate), the reaction is commonly known as decarboxylation.
The reaction is employed for stepping down a homologous series.
(ii) Kolbe's electrolytic synthesis :

$$
\begin{aligned}
& 2 \mathrm{RCOONa}+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \xrightarrow{\text { Electrolysis }} \mathrm{R}-\mathrm{R}+2 \mathrm{CO}_{2}+\mathrm{H}_{2}+2 \mathrm{NaOH} \\
& 2 \mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COONa}+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \longrightarrow \mathrm{CH}_{3}-\mathrm{CH}_{3}+2 \mathrm{CO}_{2}+\mathrm{H}_{2}+2 \mathrm{NaOH}
\end{aligned}
$$

At anode (oxidation) :
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-307.jpg?height=183&width=536&top_left_y=1375&top_left_x=833)

At cathode (reduction) :

$$
\begin{gathered}
2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \xrightarrow{+2 \mathrm{e}^{-}} 2 \mathrm{OH}^{-}+2 \mathrm{H}^{\bullet} \\
2 \mathrm{H}^{\bullet} \longrightarrow \mathrm{H}_{2}
\end{gathered}
$$

Methane cannot be prepared by this method.

Kolbe's electrolysis method can be used to prepare alkanes only with even number of carbon atoms (e.g. $\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{6}, \mathrm{C}_{4} \mathrm{H}_{10}, \mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{14}$ etc.) rather than odd number of carbon atoms (e.g. $\mathrm{C}_{3} \mathrm{H}_{7}, \mathrm{C}_{5} \mathrm{H}_{12}$, $\mathrm{C}_{7} \mathrm{H}_{16}$ etc.).
(b) Reduction of alkyl halides
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-307.jpg?height=108&width=529&top_left_y=2051&top_left_x=826)

Reductants: $\mathrm{Zn}-\mathrm{Cu}$ couple/EtOH, $\mathrm{Na}-\mathrm{EtOH}, \mathrm{Zn}-\mathrm{HCl}, \mathrm{Pt}$ or Pd or $\mathrm{Ni} / \mathrm{H}_{2}, \mathrm{Al}-\mathrm{Hg} / \mathrm{EtOH}, \mathrm{LiAlH}_{4}$ etc.
e.g. :

$$
\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{I}+\mathrm{Zn}-\mathrm{Cu} / \text { alcohol } \longrightarrow \mathrm{CH}_{4}+\mathrm{H}-\mathrm{I}
$$

The purpose of red phosphorus is to remove iodine during the reaction. It combines with iodine
Did You
Know! forming phosphorus triiodide $\left(\mathrm{PI}_{3}\right) .2 \mathrm{P}+\mathrm{I}_{2} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{PI}_{3}$. Therefore, it makes the reaction to proceed in the forward direction. If $I_{2}$ is not removed, it will convert back alkane into alkyl halide.

$$
\mathrm{R}-\mathrm{H}+\mathrm{I}_{2} \longrightarrow \mathrm{R}-\mathrm{I}
$$

## (c) Wurtz reaction

When an alkyl halide (usually bromide or iodide) is treated with sodium in dry ether, a symmetrical alkane containing twice the number of carbon atoms of alkyl halide is obtained.

## ILLUSTRATION : 1

What product will be obtained when propyl iodide is heated in presence of dry ether?

## SOLUTION :

n-Hexane is obtained as a major product in Wurtz reaction

$$
\underset{\text { Propyl iodide }}{2 \mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{I}}+2 \mathrm{Na} \xrightarrow[\text { Ether(dry) }]{\Delta} \mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \underset{n \text {-Hexane }}{\mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{CH}_{3}}+2 \mathrm{NaI}
$$

(d) From alkenes and alkynes (Sabatier - Senderen's reaction) :

When the catalyst is Pt or Pd , hydrogenation proceeds smoothly at ordinary temperature and pressure.
With nickel catalyst, higher temperature ( $250^{\circ} \mathrm{C}-300^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ ) and pressure are needed. The hydrogenation reaction of unsaturated hydrocarbons using nickel at a temperature of $523-573 \mathrm{~K}$ is commonly known as Sabatier and Senderen's reaction or reduction. With Raney nickel, the reaction takes place at room temperature.

$$
\mathrm{Ni} / \mathrm{Al}+\mathrm{NaOH} \longrightarrow \text { mixture of } \mathrm{Ni} \text { (Raney nickel) }
$$

Following alkanes can not be prepared from unsaturated hydrocarbons by this reaction.
Alkene / Alkyne ⟶ Methane
Alkene / Alkyne ⟶ Neopentane

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \qquad \mathrm{R}-\mathrm{Br}+2 \mathrm{Na}+\mathrm{Br}-\mathrm{R} \xrightarrow[\text { Ether (dry) }]{\Delta} \underset{\text { Alkane }}{\mathrm{R}}-\mathrm{R}+2 \mathrm{NaBr} \\
& \text { e.g. } \\
& \text { Methane cannot be prepared by this method. }
\end{aligned}
$$

## 1

1

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{R}-\mathrm{CH}=\mathrm{CH}-\mathrm{R}^{\prime}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \xrightarrow[\mathrm{Ni} / 300^{\circ} \mathrm{C}]{\mathrm{H}_{2}} \mathrm{R}-\mathrm{CH}_{2}-\mathrm{CH}_{2}-\mathrm{R}^{\prime} \\
& \text { Olefine (Alkene) } \\
& \mathrm{R}-\mathrm{C} \equiv \mathrm{C}-\mathrm{R}^{\prime}+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \xrightarrow[\mathrm{Alkyne}]{\mathrm{Ni} / 300^{\circ} \mathrm{C}} \mathrm{R}-\mathrm{CH}_{2}-\mathrm{CH}_{2}-\mathrm{R}^{\prime} \\
& \text { Alkane }
\end{aligned}
$$

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-308.jpg?height=204&width=332&top_left_y=1585&top_left_x=185)
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-308.jpg?height=40&width=122&top_left_y=1914&top_left_x=269)

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-308.jpg?height=77&width=108&top_left_y=1877&top_left_x=379)
Knowledge

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-308.jpg?height=50&width=201&top_left_y=1947&top_left_x=268)

## What is the product of following reaction?

$$
\mathrm{CH}_{3}-\mathrm{C} \equiv \mathrm{CH}+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \xrightarrow[\text { Room temperature }]{\text { Pd or } \mathrm{Pt}}
$$

## SOLUTION

$$
\mathrm{CH}_{3}-\mathrm{C} \equiv \mathrm{CH}+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \xrightarrow[\text { Room temperature }]{\mathrm{Pd} \text { or } \mathrm{Pt}} \underset{\text { Propane }}{\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{CH}_{3}}
$$

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-308.jpg?height=112&width=122&top_left_y=2579&top_left_x=1836)

## Method of Preparation of Methane, $\mathbf{C H}_{\mathbf{4}}$

Pure methane can be prepared by the following reactions
(i) From carbides :-
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-309.jpg?height=250&width=536&top_left_y=452&top_left_x=801)
(ii) From $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ / water gas :-

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{CO}_{2}+4 \mathrm{H}_{2} \longrightarrow \mathrm{CH}_{4}+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \\
& \underset{\text { (Water gas) }}{\mathrm{CO}+3 \mathrm{H}_{2}} \longrightarrow \mathrm{CH}_{4}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}
\end{aligned}
$$

## Physical Properties of Alkanes

(a) Alkanes from $\mathrm{C}_{1}-\mathrm{C}_{4}$ are colourless, odourless gases. $\mathrm{C}_{5}-\mathrm{C}_{17}$ are colourless liquids, $\mathrm{C}_{18}-$ onwards are waxy, white solids.

## (b) Boiling points

Boiling points of alkanes are lowest of all the groups of organic compounds. The forces of attraction among alkane molecules are weakest van der Waal's forces of attraction. With the increase in the number of carbon chain length (molecular size), the magnitude of van der Waal's forces also increases.
Among isomeric alkanes, boiling point decreases with increasing branching.
Example : n-Pentane > iso-Pentane > neo-Pentane

## (c) Melting point

The melting point of alkanes does not show regular variation with increase in molecular size. Unbranched alkanes containing 2, 4, 6, 8 etc. carbon atoms have higher melting points than the unbranched alkanes containing 3, 5, 7, 9 etc. carbon atoms respectively.

Example :
$n$-Pentane
(both methyl groups are directed on same side)
<img class="imgSvg" id = "mpcazmxpav0ym7ay2p" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,"/>

n-Hexane
(both methyl groups are adversely directed)
M.P. of even No. of carbon atoms > M.P. of odd No. of carbon atoms
M.P. $\propto$ mol.wt.

## Out of $\mathrm{C}_{3} \mathrm{H}_{8}$ and $\mathrm{C}_{4} \mathrm{H}_{10}$ which one has high melting point?

## SOLUTION

$\mathrm{C}_{4} \mathrm{H}_{10}$ because alkanes with even number of carbon atoms has higher melting point.

## (d) Fuel capacity

Decreasing order of fuel capacity is

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \text { More branched }>\text { Less branched }>\text { Unbranched } \\
& \text { Lower mol.wt. }>\text { Higher mol. wt. }
\end{aligned}
$$

## (e) Solubility

Alkanes are lighter than water. These are insoluble in water while soluble in organic solvents.

## Chemical Properties of Alkanes

(a) Thermal decomposition, cracking or pyrolysis

The decomposition of a compound by heat is known as pyrolysis. Thermal decomposition when applied to alkanes is known as cracking and leads to the formation of lower alkanes, alkenes and hydrogen, e.g.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-310.jpg?height=88&width=1127&top_left_y=584&top_left_x=434)

Note : Cracking has been used in the manufacture of petrol, petrol gas, oil gas, etc.

## (b) Isomerisation

The process of conversion of one isomer of a compound to another isomer is known as isomerisation. This is achieved by heating the normal alkane with anhydrous $\mathrm{AlCl}_{3}$ and HCl or anhydrous $\mathrm{AlBr}_{3}$ and HBr under pressure of 35 atmospheres.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-310.jpg?height=136&width=798&top_left_y=913&top_left_x=598)

Isomerisation of alkanes is of great importance in petroleum industry.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-310.jpg?height=144&width=270&top_left_y=1133&top_left_x=267)
