## SOLUTION

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-310.jpg?height=140&width=926&top_left_y=1498&top_left_x=534)

## (c) Catalytic oxidation

(i) $2 \mathrm{CH}_{4}+\mathrm{O}_{2} \xrightarrow{100 \mathrm{~atm}, 573 \mathrm{~K}} \underset{\text { Methanol }}{2 \mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{OH}}$
(ii) $2 \mathrm{CH}_{4}+\mathrm{O}_{2} \xrightarrow[\Delta \text {, pressure }]{\mathrm{Mo}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}} \underset{\text { Methanal }}{\mathrm{HCHO}}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$
(ii) $2 \mathrm{R}-\mathrm{CH}_{3}+3 \mathrm{O}_{2} \xrightarrow[\Delta]{\mathrm{Ag}_{2} \mathrm{O}} \underset{\text { Carboxylic acid }}{2 \mathrm{RCOOH}+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}}$

## ILLUSTRATION : 2

Why hydrocarbons are used as a fuel?

## SOLUTION :

It is because during their combustion, a large amount of heat is evolved; for example cooking gas, which is often called L.P.G. is a mixture of propane and butane which is used as a fuel.

## ALKENES

Alkenes are those unsaturated hydrocarbons which contain at least one carbon-carbon double bond in their molecules. Their general formula is $\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{n}} \mathrm{H}_{2 \mathrm{n}}$ and hence they contain two hydrogen atoms less than the corresponding alkane and hence designated as unsaturated hydrocarbon having general formula $\mathrm{C}_{n} \mathrm{H}_{2 n+2}$. These are also known as olefins or olifines (olifiant $=$ oil forming) and their IUPAC name is alkenes.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-310.jpg?height=108&width=122&top_left_y=2581&top_left_x=1836)

The carbon-carbon double bond ( $C=C$ ), commonly known as ethylenic or olifinic bond or linkage, is the characteristic group of alkenes.

## Methods of Preparation of Alkenes

(a) From alkanes
(i) Pyrolysis
<img class="imgSvg" id = "mpcazmxrppzbsesxsk" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,"/>
(ii) Dehydrogenation
e.g.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-311.jpg?height=97&width=780&top_left_y=1151&top_left_x=484)
(b) From alkynes (Partial hydrogenation)

By partial reduction of alkynes in the presence of $\mathrm{Pd} / \mathrm{CaCO}_{3}$.

$$
\mathrm{R}-\mathrm{C} \equiv \mathrm{CH}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \xrightarrow{\mathrm{Pd} / \mathrm{CaCO}_{3}} \mathrm{RCH}=\mathrm{CH}_{2}
$$

## (c) By dehydrohalogenation of alkyl halides

When monohalides react with alcoholic KOH or NaOH , then respective alkenes are formed along with the elimination of HX molecule; this reaction is called dehydrohalogenation.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-311.jpg?height=183&width=681&top_left_y=1576&top_left_x=724)

Time to
Check Your
Knowledge

- Complete the following reaction
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-311.jpg?height=177&width=495&top_left_y=1946&top_left_x=817)


## SOLUTION

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-311.jpg?height=150&width=926&top_left_y=2183&top_left_x=603)
(iv) From alcohols:

Alkenes are prepared from alcohols by heating with protonic acids such as sulphuric acid or phosphoric acid at about 443 K . This reaction is called dehydration of alcohols.

$$
\underset{\text { Ethyl alcohol }}{\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{OH}} \xrightarrow[443 \mathrm{~K}]{\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4} \text { or } \mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{PO}_{4}} \underset{\text { Ethene }}{\mathrm{CH}_{2}=\mathrm{CH}_{2}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}}
$$

## Physical Properties of Alkenes

(a) From $\mathrm{C}_{2}-\mathrm{C}_{4}$ they are colourless, odourless gases, from $\mathrm{C}_{5}-\mathrm{C}_{17}$ they are colourless liquids, $\mathrm{C}_{18}$ onwards alkenes are solids. Ethylene $\left(\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{4}\right)$ is a sweet smelling gas. It produces anaesthetic effect in large amounts.
(b) Alkenes are practically insoluble in water. They dissolve freely in organic solvents like benzene, chloroform, $\mathrm{CCl}_{4}$, petroleum ether, etc.
(c) The boiling and melting points of alkenes are slightly higher than the corresponding alkanes. Alkenes are therefore, lesser volatile than the corresponding alkanes. The branched chain alkenes, however, have lower boiling points than the corresponding straight chain alkenes.
Their boiling points, melting points and specific gravities rise with the increase of molecular weight.

The increase in branching in carbon chain decreases the boiling point among isomeric alkenes.

$$
\text { MP. and BP. } \propto \text { mol. wt. } \propto \frac{1}{\text { Branching in alkenes }}
$$

The boiling points of alkenes show a regular gradation with the increase in number of carbon atoms like alkanes. In general, for each added $-\mathrm{CH}_{2}$ group, the boiling point rises by $20-30{ }^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$.
The boiling points of alkenes show a regular gradation with the increase in number of carbon D
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-312.jpg?height=62&width=65&top_left_y=1158&top_left_x=443)

## Out of ethylene and butylene which has higher boiling point?

## SOLUTION

Butylene because in case of alkenes B.P. increases with increase in length of carbon chain.

## Chemical Properties of Alkene

This reaction is used industrially in preparation of vegetable ghee which contains mainly saturated fatty acids from vegetable oils which mainly contains unsaturated fatty acids.
(a) Polymerisation

The process in which simple molecules combine together to form large molecule is called polymerisation; the starting simple molecule is called a monomer while the larger molecule (final product) is called a polymer. Polymers are used as plastics, rubber and several other important products. Lower alkenes and alkynes undergo polymerisation to form products of great importance.
Lower alkenes (e.g. ethene, propene etc.) readily undergo polymerisation at high temperature and pressure and in the presence of a suitable catalyst like $\mathrm{AlCl}_{3}, \mathrm{BF}_{3}$, etc.

$$
n \underset{\text { Ethylene }}{n \mathrm{CH}_{2}=\mathrm{CH}_{2}} \xrightarrow[\text { high pressure, } \mathrm{O}_{2}]{473-673 \mathrm{~K}} \underset{\text { Polyethylene }}{\left(\mathrm{CH}_{2}-\mathrm{CH}_{2}\right)_{n}}
$$

## (b) Ozonolysis

Addition of ozone to an unsaturated hydrocarbon, forms an intermediate called ozonide, which on further hydrolysis in the presence of zinc dust gives corresponding carbonyl compounds (aldehydes and ketones). This reaction is used to identify and locate the position of multiple bonds in an unsaturated hydrocarbon.
For example, ethene on such reaction gives two molecules of formaldehyde.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-313.jpg?height=207&width=1093&top_left_y=593&top_left_x=523)

Time to
Check Your
Knowledge

## What are the products obtained by ozonolysis of 2-Methylpropene ?

## SOLUTION

Acetone and methanal are obtained
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-313.jpg?height=209&width=1287&top_left_y=1082&top_left_x=424)

## (c) Oxidation with oxidizing agents

(i) Oxidation with cold, neutral or alkaline $\mathrm{KMnO}_{4}$ :

Alkenes are readily oxidised by cold dilute ( $1 \%$ ) neutral or alkaline $\mathrm{KMnO}_{4}$ solution (Baeyer's reagent) to give glycols (1, 2-diols or vic-diols); $\mathrm{KMnO}_{4}$ itself is reduced to $\mathrm{MnO}_{2}$.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-313.jpg?height=175&width=591&top_left_y=1541&top_left_x=769)
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-313.jpg?height=136&width=719&top_left_y=1731&top_left_x=705)
(ii) Oxidation with hot alkaline $\mathrm{KMnO}_{4}$ solution :

Alkenes when treated with hot $\mathrm{KMnO}_{4}$ solution give carboxylic acids, ketones and carbon dioxide depending upon the nature of the alkene. During the reaction $=\mathrm{CH}_{2}$ (terminal alkene) group is oxidised to formic acid, which on further oxidation gives $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ and $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-313.jpg?height=147&width=810&top_left_y=2042&top_left_x=641)
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-313.jpg?height=102&width=859&top_left_y=2197&top_left_x=635)

## (d) Oxidation with oxygen

Alkenes, when heated with oxygen in presence of silver catalyst at 575 K , are oxidised to epoxides. For example,
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-313.jpg?height=141&width=636&top_left_y=2407&top_left_x=751)

## ALKYNES

Alkynes are characterised by the presence of a triple bond between two carbon atoms i.e. $-\mathrm{C} \equiv \mathrm{C}-$. They contain four hydrogen atoms less than the corresponding alkane and hence correspond to the general formula $\mathrm{C}_{n} \mathrm{H}_{2 n-2}$. The first and the most important member of this series is acetylene $(\mathrm{CH} \equiv \mathrm{CH})$ and hence these compounds are also called as acetylenes and the triple bond is usually referred to as the acetylenic linkage or acetylenic bond.
(a) Action of water on calcium carbide (only for acetylene)

Acetylene is prepared in the laboratory as well as on industrial scale by the action of water on calcium carbide.

$$
\underset{\text { Calcium carbide }}{\mathrm{CaC}_{2}}+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \longrightarrow \underset{\text { Acetylene }}{\mathrm{HC} \equiv \mathrm{CH}+\mathrm{Ca}(\mathrm{OH})_{2}}
$$

(b) By dehydrohalogenation of vicinal dihalides
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-314.jpg?height=135&width=565&top_left_y=1213&top_left_x=717)
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-314.jpg?height=102&width=931&top_left_y=1425&top_left_x=534)
(c) Higher alkyne can be prepared from lower alkynes by treating its sodium salt with alkyl halide
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-314.jpg?height=85&width=931&top_left_y=1617&top_left_x=534)
(d) From vinyl Halides
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-314.jpg?height=54&width=821&top_left_y=1758&top_left_x=589)

Bromine water test and Baeyer's test are generally used for detecting the presence of double bond, while ozonolysis helps in locating the position of double bond.

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \text { ed by the presence of a } \\
& \mathrm{n} \text { the corresponding alkan } \\
& \text { of this series is acetylene } \\
& \mathrm{y} \text { referred to as the acetyle } \\
& \text { aration of Alkynes } \\
& \text { n calcium carbide (only }
\end{aligned}
$$

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-314.jpg?height=20&width=22&top_left_y=529&top_left_x=1048)

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-314.jpg?height=244&width=346&top_left_y=344&top_left_x=182)

## Physical Properties of Alkynes

(a) Alkynes are colourless, odourless and tasteless.
(b) Lower alkynes are partially soluble in $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$.
(c) Higher alkynes are insoluble in water while completely soluble in organic solvents.
(d) Melting point and boiling point are directly proportional to molecular mass and inversely proportional to number of branches.
(e) Upto $\mathrm{C}_{4}$, alkynes are gaseous, $\mathrm{C}_{5}-\mathrm{C}_{11}$ are liquids, while $\mathrm{C}_{12}$ \& above are solids.
(f) Acetylene \& 1 - alkynes are acidic in nature. It is due to presence of active H .
(g) Oxyacetylene flame is used for cutting and welding metals.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-315.jpg?height=212&width=323&top_left_y=561&top_left_x=255)

Order of solubility, density, B.P., M.P. and acidic nature of above hydrocarbons is :
Alkyne > Alkene > Alkane
All terminal alkynes are acidic in nature.

## Chemical Properties of Alkynes

(a) Addition reactions
(i) Addition of hydrogen (Catalytic hydrogenation, hydrogenation in presence of catalyst) : Hydrogen adds to unsaturated hydrocarbons (alkenes and alkynes) in presence of metallic catalyst, such as finely divided platinum, palladium or nickel and forms the corresponding alkane. For example,
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-315.jpg?height=94&width=730&top_left_y=1110&top_left_x=703)

It may be noted that the hydrogenation can be controlled at the alkene stage only. This is possible by using a Lindlar's catalyst which is a mixture of palladium and barium sulphate poisoned by quinoline.

$$
\underset{\text { Acetylene }}{\mathrm{HC}} \equiv \underset{\text { (Lindlar's catalyst) }}{\mathrm{CH}} \underset{\text { Ethene }}{\mathrm{CH}_{2}}=\mathrm{CH}_{2}
$$

Alkynes add one or two molecules of halogens to form di- and tetra-halides respectively.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-315.jpg?height=175&width=965&top_left_y=1651&top_left_x=584)
(ii) Addition of hydrogen halides (hydrohalogenation) :

Alkynes add two molecules of hydrogen halides. For example,
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-315.jpg?height=150&width=755&top_left_y=1964&top_left_x=687)

In case of unsymmetrical alkynes addition of attacking reagent take place according to Markovnikov's rule.
(iii) Ozonolysis : Addition of a molecule of ozone to a molecule of ethyne at room temperature gives an addition compound, ethyne ozonide, which on further hydrolysis with zinc dust gives glyoxal.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-315.jpg?height=186&width=945&top_left_y=2298&top_left_x=593)

## ILLUSTRATION : 3

What product is obtained, when but-2-yne is reacted with $\mathrm{O}_{3}$ molecule followed by hydrolysis in the presence of zinc metal?

## SOLUTION :

The product obtained is Butane-2, 3-dione as per following reaction
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-316.jpg?height=213&width=1131&top_left_y=653&top_left_x=434)

## (b) Formation of sodium acetylides

Acetylene and monoalkylacetylenes react with sodium in liquid ammonia or sodamide to form sodium alkynides.

$$
\begin{gathered}
\mathrm{H}-\mathrm{C} \equiv \mathrm{C}-\mathrm{H}+\mathrm{Na} \xrightarrow[453 \mathrm{~K}]{\text { liq. } \mathrm{NH}_{3}} \underset{\text { Monosodium acetylide }}{\mathrm{H}-\mathrm{C} \equiv \mathrm{C}-\mathrm{Na}} \xrightarrow[483 \mathrm{~K}]{\mathrm{Na} / \text { liq. } \mathrm{NH}_{3}} \underset{\text { Disodium acetylide }}{\mathrm{NaC} \equiv \mathrm{CNa}} \\
\mathrm{R}-\mathrm{C} \equiv \mathrm{C}-\mathrm{H}+\mathrm{Na} \xrightarrow[453 \mathrm{~K}]{\text { liq. } \mathrm{NH}_{3}} \mathrm{R}-\mathrm{C} \equiv \mathrm{C}-\mathrm{Na}
\end{gathered}
$$

This reaction shows that terminal alkynes are acidic in nature.

## (c) Formation of copper and silver acetylides

Copper and silver alkynides are obtained by passing alkynes in the ammonical solution of cuprous chloride and silver nitrate respectively.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathrm{HC} \equiv \mathrm{CH}+\mathrm{Cu}_{2} \mathrm{Cl}_{2}+2 \mathrm{NH}_{4} \mathrm{OH} & \longrightarrow \underset{\substack{\text { Copper acetylide } \\
\text { (red ppt.) }}}{\mathrm{CuC} \equiv \mathrm{CCu} \downarrow}+2 \mathrm{NH}_{4} \mathrm{Cl}+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \\
\mathrm{HC} \equiv \mathrm{CH}+2 \mathrm{AgNO}_{3}+2 \mathrm{NH}_{4} \mathrm{OH} & \longrightarrow \underset{\substack{\text { Silver acetylide } \\
\text { (white ppt.) }}}{\mathrm{AgC} \equiv \mathrm{CAg} \downarrow}+2 \mathrm{NH}_{4} \mathrm{NO}_{3}+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}
\end{aligned}
$$

Time to
Check Your
Knowledge

## What will happen when 1-butyne and 2-butyne are reacted with ammonical solution of silver nitrate?

## SOLUTION

1-Butyne will give white precipitate but 2-butyne gives no reaction because this reaction is given by terminal alkynes (alkynes having hydrogen at first carbon only).

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \underset{\text { 1-Butyne }}{\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{C} \equiv \mathrm{CH}+2 \mathrm{AgNO}_{3}+2 \mathrm{NH}_{4} \mathrm{OH} \longrightarrow \mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{C} \equiv \mathrm{CAg}} \text { White ppt } \\
& \underset{\text { 2-Butyne }}{\mathrm{CH}_{3}-\mathrm{C} \equiv \mathrm{C}-\mathrm{CH}_{3}+2 \mathrm{AgNO}_{3}+2 \mathrm{NH}_{4} \mathrm{OH} \longrightarrow \text { No reaction }}
\end{aligned}
$$

## (d) Oxidation with alkaline $\mathrm{KMnO}_{4}$

On such oxidation, alkynes give diketones under normal conditions.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-317.jpg?height=145&width=990&top_left_y=461&top_left_x=571)

## ALKYL HALIDES

Alkyl halides (haloalkanes) are those compounds in which one or more hydrogen atoms in an alkane are replaced by halogen atoms.

Halogen derivatives of alkanes, alkenes and alkynes are known as alkyl halides (haloalkanes), alkenyl halides (haloalkenes) and alkynyl halides (haloalkynes) respectively.

On the basis of the number of halogen atom, alkyl halides are further classified as mono-, di-, tri-,
Did You Know? poly- and per-halohydrocarbons. The word perhalohydrocarbon means all the hydrogen atoms of the hydrocarbon are replaced by corresponding number of halogen atoms.

Besides the nature and number of halogen atoms, alkyl halides may be classified into primary $\left(1^{\circ}\right)$, secondary $\left(2^{\circ}\right)$ and tertiary $\left(3^{\circ}\right)$ according to the nature of the carbon atom bearing halogen.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-317.jpg?height=259&width=832&top_left_y=1562&top_left_x=651)

## Isomerism In Alkyl Halides

(a) Position isomerism:
<img class="imgSvg" id = "mpcazmyb4nck6trs92u" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,"/>
(b) Chain isomerism:
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-317.jpg?height=145&width=885&top_left_y=2133&top_left_x=591)

## Methods of Preparation of Alkyl Halides

(a) From alochols with $\mathbf{H X}, \mathbf{P X}_{3}(\mathbf{X}=\mathbf{I}, \mathbf{B r}, \mathbf{C l})$ or $\mathbf{S O C l}_{2}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathrm{R}-\mathrm{OH}+\mathrm{HX} & \longrightarrow \mathrm{RX}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \\
\mathrm{R}-\mathrm{OH}+\mathrm{PCl}_{5} & \longrightarrow \mathrm{R}-\mathrm{Cl}+\mathrm{POCl}_{3}+\mathrm{HCl}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{gathered}
\mathrm{R}-\mathrm{OH}+\mathrm{SOCl}_{2} \xrightarrow{\text { Pyridine }} \mathrm{R}-\mathrm{Cl}+\mathrm{SO}_{2}+\mathrm{HCl} \\
3 \mathrm{R}-\mathrm{OH}+\mathrm{PX}_{3} \longrightarrow 3 \mathrm{R}-\mathrm{X}+\mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{PO}_{3}
\end{gathered}
$$

(b) Addition of hydrogen halides to alkenes

$$
\mathrm{CH}_{2}=\mathrm{CH}_{2}+\mathrm{HBr} \longrightarrow \mathrm{CH}_{3}-\mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{Br}
$$

Time to
Check Your
Knowledge

## Complete the following reaction

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-318.jpg?height=76&width=608&top_left_y=900&top_left_x=701)

## SOLUTION

$$
\underset{\text { 1-Butylene }}{\mathrm{CH}_{3}-\mathrm{CH}_{2}-\mathrm{CH}}=\mathrm{CH}_{2}+\mathrm{HBr} \xrightarrow[\text { (anti-mark: addition) }]{\text { peroxides }} \mathrm{CH}_{3}-\underset{\text { 1-Bromobutane }}{\mathrm{CH}_{2}-\mathrm{CH}_{2}-\mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{Br}}
$$

(c) From alkanes

$$
\mathrm{R}-\mathrm{H}+\mathrm{X}_{2} \xrightarrow{\Delta \text { or } \mathrm{h} \nu} \mathrm{R}-\mathrm{X}+\mathrm{HX}(\mathrm{X}=\mathrm{Cl}, \mathrm{Br} \text { or I })
$$

(d) From ethers

$$
\mathrm{R}-\mathrm{O}-\mathrm{R}+2 \mathrm{HX} \xrightarrow{373 \mathrm{~K}} 2 \mathrm{R}-\mathrm{X}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{X}=\mathrm{Br} \text { or I })
$$

$$
\mathrm{R}-\mathrm{Cl}+\mathrm{NaI} \xrightarrow{\text { Acetone or Methanol }} \mathrm{R}-\mathrm{I}+\mathrm{NaCl}
$$

This reaction is called Finkelstein reaction. Only iodoalkanes can be obtained by this method by displacing Cl or Br.

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{COOAg}^{-} \mathrm{Br}_{2} \xrightarrow{\mathrm{CCl}_{4}} \mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{Br}+\mathrm{CO}_{2}+\mathrm{AgBr} . \text { This reaction is called Hunsdiecker } \\
& \text { reaction. Only bromoalkane is obtained by this method. }
\end{aligned}
$$

## Physical Properties of Alkyl Halides

(a) Alkyl halide is insoluble in water though the molecule is polar. This is due to the fact that haloalkenes can neither form hydrogen bonds with $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ nor break the hydrogen bonds, already existing.
(b) Boiling points

Greater the molecular mass, stronger the van der Waal's forces of attraction and hence higher is the boiling point.
(i) $\mathrm{RI}>\mathrm{RBr}>\mathrm{RCl}>\mathrm{RF}$ (For same alkyl group)
(ii) Boiling point decreases as the size of alkyl group decreases.

$$
\mathrm{CH}_{3}\left(\mathrm{CH}_{2}\right)_{2} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{X}>\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{X}>\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{X} \text { [For the same halogen atom] }
$$

(c) Stability order

Stability of alkyl halide decreases as the strength of the $\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{X}$ bond decreases.

$$
\mathrm{R}-\mathrm{F}>\mathrm{R}-\mathrm{Cl}>\mathrm{R}-\mathrm{Br}>\mathrm{R}-\mathrm{I}
$$

## Chemical Properties of Alkyl Halides

(a) Reaction with metals
(i) $\mathrm{R}-\mathrm{X}+2 \mathrm{Li} \xrightarrow{\text { dryether }} \mathrm{R}-\mathrm{Li}+\mathrm{LiX}$
(ii) $\mathrm{R}-\mathrm{X}+\mathrm{Mg} \xrightarrow{\text { dryether }} \mathrm{R}-\mathrm{MgX}$
(Grignard reagent)
(b) Reduction
(i) $\mathrm{R}-\mathrm{X}+\mathrm{Zn}+\mathrm{HX} \longrightarrow \mathrm{R}-\mathrm{H}+\mathrm{ZnX}_{2}$
(ii) $\mathrm{R}-\mathrm{X} \xrightarrow{\mathrm{LiAlH}_{4}} \mathrm{R}-\mathrm{H}$
(iii) $\mathrm{R}-\mathrm{X}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \xrightarrow{\mathrm{Ni}} \mathrm{R}-\mathrm{H}+\mathrm{HX}$
(iv) $\mathrm{R}-\mathrm{X} \xrightarrow{\mathrm{Mg}} \mathrm{R}-\mathrm{MgX} \xrightarrow[\text { or } \mathrm{D}_{2} \mathrm{O}]{\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}} \mathrm{RH}$ (or RD )
(v) $2 \mathrm{R}-\mathrm{I}+2 \mathrm{HI} \xrightarrow[423 \mathrm{~K}]{\text { red } \mathrm{P}} 2 \mathrm{R}-\mathrm{H}+\mathrm{I}_{2}$

Time to Check Your Knowledge

## How ethane can be prepared by reduction method?

## SOLUTION

It can be prepared by reduction of ethyl iodide with hydrogen iodide in presence of red phosphorus.

$$
\underset{\text { Ethyl iodide }}{\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{I}}+\mathrm{HI} \xrightarrow[423 \mathrm{~K}]{\text { red } \mathrm{P}} \underset{\text { Ethane }}{\mathrm{CH}_{3}-\mathrm{CH}_{3}+\mathrm{I}_{2}}
$$

(c) Halogenation

$$
\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{Cl} \xrightarrow{\mathrm{Cl}_{2}, h \nu} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{Cl}_{2} \xrightarrow{\mathrm{Cl}_{2}, h \nu} \mathrm{CHCl}_{3} \xrightarrow{\mathrm{Cl}_{2}, h \nu} \mathrm{CCl}_{4}
$$

(d) Isomerisation

$$
\underset{\text { 1-Bromopropane }}{\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{Br}} \xrightarrow[\text { anhy. } \mathrm{AlCl}_{3}]{573 \mathrm{~K}} \underset{\text { 2-Bromopropane }}{\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CHBrCH}_{3}}
$$

(e) Hydrolysis
(f) Williamson's synthesis

$$
\mathrm{R}-\mathrm{X}+\mathrm{KOH}(\mathrm{aq}) \longrightarrow \underset{\text { Alcohol }}{\mathrm{R}-\mathrm{OH}}+\mathrm{KX}
$$

$$
\mathrm{R}-\mathrm{X}+\mathrm{R}^{\prime} \mathrm{ONa} \xrightarrow{\Delta} \underset{\text { Ether }}{\mathrm{R}-\mathrm{O}-\mathrm{R}^{\prime}}+\mathrm{NaX}
$$

## (g) Elimination reaction

Alkyl halide when heated with alcoholic KOH solution, a molecule of hydrogen halide is eliminated and alkenes are formed. Generally $\beta$-carbon (the carbon atom next to the carbon carrying the halogen) is eliminated during the reaction. In case of unsymmetrical alkyl halide there is the possibility of formation of two products. The more substituted alkene will be formed as a major product. This phenomenon was explained by Saytzeff, so its called Saytzeff's rule.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-319.jpg?height=158&width=968&top_left_y=2193&top_left_x=584)
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-319.jpg?height=168&width=1104&top_left_y=2357&top_left_x=516)

## How you can prepare ethyl methyl ether ?

## SOLUTION

Ethyl methyl ether can be prepared by the reaction of methyl chloride with sodium ethoxide.

$$
\underset{\text { Methyl chloride }}{\mathrm{CH}_{3}-\mathrm{Cl}}+\underset{\text { Sodium ethoxide }}{\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{ONa}} \xrightarrow{\Delta} \underset{\substack{\text { Ethyl methyl ether } \\ \text { (methoxyethane) }}}{\mathrm{CH}_{3}-\mathrm{O}-\mathrm{CH}_{2}-\mathrm{CH}_{3}+\mathrm{NaCl}}
$$

## ALCOHOLS

Alcohols, generally indicated by $\mathrm{R}-\mathrm{OH}$, can be regarded as a derivative of alkanes where one hydrogen atom is replaced by a hydroxyl group (- OH ). The general molecular formula for alcohols is $\mathrm{C}_{n} \mathrm{H}_{2 n+1} \mathrm{OH}$. Alcohols are very important in organic chemistry because they can be converted to many other types of compounds.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-320.jpg?height=76&width=365&top_left_y=1087&top_left_x=817)

Examples :
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-320.jpg?height=99&width=492&top_left_y=1183&top_left_x=644)

## Classification of Alcohols

Alcohols are classified according to number of hydroxyl groups present in the molecule. Alcohols with one, two and three hydroxyl groups are called monohydric, dihydric \& trihydric alcohols respectively. Alcohols which contain four or more hydroxyl groups are called polyhydric alcohols.

$$
\mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{C}-\mathrm{OH}
$$

Methanol
<img class="imgSvg" id = "mpcazmyeq30kmexjtlo" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,"/>

Ethylene glycol
<img class="imgSvg" id = "mpcazmyemak7qd70rl8" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,"/>

Glycerol
<img class="imgSvg" id = "mpcazmyfnjove6ywpi" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,"/>

Butane-1,2,3,4-tetrol

When two or more hydroxyl groups attached to the same carbon atom of the compound is generally unstable. Such type of compound loses a water molecule easily and converted into a stable compound.

Monohydric alcohols are further divided into three classes:

## (a) Primary alcohols

The hydroxyl group is attached with primary carbon atom. They possess a characteristic group $-\mathrm{CH}_{2}-\mathrm{OH}$.
<img class="imgSvg" id = "mpcazmyhm32mice23ef" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,"/>
<img class="imgSvg" id = "mpcazmyh2apxqx6m8il" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,"/>
<img class="imgSvg" id = "mpcazmyi1kg57tqx398" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,"/>
(b) Secondary alcohols

The hydroxyl group is attached to secondary carbon atom. They possess $>\mathrm{CH}-\mathrm{OH}$ group.
<img class="imgSvg" id = "mpcazmyjktjmpnxnhci" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,"/>
<img class="imgSvg" id = "mpcazmykkb1sg5jmvw" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,"/>
<img class="imgSvg" id = "mpcazmyk4ht1qm5kxl9" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,"/>

## (c) Tertiary alcohols

The hydroxyl group is attached to tertiary carbon atom. They possess a characteristic group $-\underset{\mid}{\mid}-\mathrm{OH}$.
<img class="imgSvg" id = "mpcazmymiwaxfd59kf" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,"/>

2-Methylbutan-2-ol
<img class="imgSvg" id = "mpcazmynrwr6cqf81le" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,"/>

2-Methylpropan-2-ol

Check Your
Knowledge
□ Find the class of 3-Ethyl-2-methylbutan-2-ol.

## SOLUTION

It is a tertiary alcohol as -OH functional group is attached to tertiary carbon atom.

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-321.jpg?height=172&width=712&top_left_y=1023&top_left_x=712)
3-Ethyl-2-methylbutan-2-ol

## Isomerism in Alcohols

Alcohols exhibit following types of isomerism:
(a) Position isomerism
e.g. $\mathrm{CH}_{3}-\mathrm{CH}_{2}-\mathrm{CH}_{2}-\mathrm{OH}$
1-Propanol
<img class="imgSvg" id = "mpcazmyo5f16d515xn" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,"/>
2-Propanol
(b) Chain isomerism

$$
\begin{gathered}
\text { e.g. } \mathrm{CH}_{3}-\mathrm{CH}_{2}-\mathrm{CH}_{2}-\mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{OH} \\
\text { 1-Butanol(straight chain) }
\end{gathered}
$$

<img class="imgSvg" id = "mpcazmyp7d6qlioz3wh" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,"/>

2-Methyl-1-propanol (branched chain)

ETHYL ALCOHOL : Preparations and properties are already discussed in ch. 4

## ETHERS

Ethers are a class of compounds which contain an oxygen atom bonded to two alkyl groups (same or different). When both the alkyl groups are same they are designated as simple or symmetrical ethers and when both are different, they are called mixed or unsymmetrical ethers.

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \text { e.g. } \\
& \qquad \begin{array}{ll}
\mathrm{R}-\mathrm{O}-\mathrm{R} & \mathrm{R}-\mathrm{O}-\mathrm{R} \prime \\
\mathrm{CH}_{3}-\mathrm{O}-\mathrm{CH}_{3} & \mathrm{CH}_{3}-\mathrm{O}-\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5} \\
\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5}-\mathrm{O}-\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5} & \mathrm{CH}_{3}-\mathrm{O}-\mathrm{C}_{3} \mathrm{H}_{7} \\
\text { Simple ethers } & \text { Mixed ethers }
\end{array}
\end{aligned}
$$

Ethers form a homologous series with the general formula $\mathrm{C}_{n} \mathrm{H}_{2 n+2} \mathrm{O}$ where the value of ' $n$ ' is always more than 1 ; the functional group of ether is (- - - ). Ethers may be regarded as anhydrides of alcohol as they may be obtained by elimination of water molecule from two alcohol molecules.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-321.jpg?height=86&width=732&top_left_y=2462&top_left_x=715)

Just as alcohols are considered as monoalkyl derivatives of water, ethers may be considered as dialkyl derivatives of water.

## Isomerism in Ethers

Ethers exhibit two types of isomerism which are as follows.
(a) Functional isomerism

Ethers are isomeric with monohydric alcohols, e.g.
$\mathrm{CH}_{3}-\mathrm{O}-\mathrm{CH}_{3}$
Methoxymethane
$\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{OH}$
Ethyl alcohol
(b) Metamerism

It is a type of structural isomerism in which different alkyl groups are attached to the same functional group.
e.g.
$\underset{\text { Ethoxyethane }}{\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5}-\mathrm{O}-\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5}}$
Preparation of Ethers
<img class="imgSvg" id = "mpcazmyqwhilwoziic" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,"/>
2-Methoxypropane

## (a) By dehydrating excess of alcohols

Simple ethers can be prepared by heating an excess of primary alcohols with conc. $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}$ at 413 K . Alcohol should be taken in excess so as to avoid its dehydration to alkenes.

$$
\underset{\text { Ethanol (2 molecules) }}{\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5}-\mathrm{OH}+\mathrm{HO}-\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5}} \xrightarrow[413 \mathrm{~K}]{\text { Conc. } \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}} \underset{\text { Diethyl ether }}{\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5}-\mathrm{O}-\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5}}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}
$$

(b) By heating alkyl halide with dry silver oxide : (Only for simple ethers)

$$
\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{I}+\mathrm{Ag}_{2} \mathrm{O}+\mathrm{IC}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5} \longrightarrow \mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5}-\mathrm{O}-\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5}+2 \mathrm{AgI}
$$

(c) By heating alkyl halide with sod. or pot. alkoxides (Williamson synthesis)

This is the most important industrial and laboratory method for preparing simple as well as mixed ethers. For example,

$$
\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{ONa}+\mathrm{ICH}_{3} \longrightarrow \mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5}-\mathrm{O}-\mathrm{CH}_{3}+\mathrm{NaI}
$$

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-322.jpg?height=181&width=853&top_left_y=1859&top_left_x=575)

In this method generally primary alkyl halide is allowed to react with sodium ethoxide. If secondary or tertiary alkyl halide is taken for the synthesis of ether, along with ether alkene is also formed, that means elimination reaction competes over substitution. For example, the reaction of $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{ONa}$ with $\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3}\right)_{3} \mathrm{C}-\mathrm{Br}$ ( $3^{\circ}$ alkyl halide) gives only alkene instead of ether.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-322.jpg?height=174&width=995&top_left_y=2186&top_left_x=497)

It is because $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{ONa}$ are not only nucleophiles but strong bases as well, therefore, it leading to elimination reaction.

## How can you prepare methyl butyl ether by methyl iodide?

## SOLUTION

Sodium butoxide reacts with methyl iodide leading to methylbutyl ether (Williamson's synthesis).
$\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{I}+\mathrm{C}_{4} \mathrm{H}_{9} \mathrm{ONa} \longrightarrow \mathrm{CH}_{3}-\mathrm{CH}_{2}-\mathrm{CH}_{2}-\mathrm{CH}_{2}-\mathrm{O}-\mathrm{CH}_{3}+\mathrm{NaI}$

## Physical Properties of Ethers

(a) The first two members, i.e., dimethyl ether and methylethyl ether are gases; while all others are colourless, volatile pleasant smelling liquids. Among aromatic ethers anisole $\left(\mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{OCH}_{3}\right)$ and phenetole $\left(\mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{OC}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5}\right)$ are liquids; diphenyl ether is solid at room temperature.
(b) Lower ethers are highly volatile and inflammable and also act as anaesthetics.
(c) They are sparingly soluble in water, but readily soluble in organic solvents such as benzene, chloroform, etc.

## Chemical Properties of Ethers

(a) Properties due to alkyl groups
(i) Halogenation : When ethers are treated with chlorine or bromine in dark, substitution reaction occurs at the $\alpha$-carbon atom. The extent of substitution depends upon the reaction conditions.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-323.jpg?height=154&width=1481&top_left_y=1302&top_left_x=324)
(ii) Combustion : Since ethers are volatile and highly inflammable, they burn in air to form $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ and water.

$$
\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5}-\mathrm{O}-\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5}+6 \mathrm{O}_{2} \longrightarrow 4 \mathrm{CO}_{2}+5 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}
$$

## (b) Properties due to ethereal oxygen

(i) Chemical inertness : Since ethers do not have any active group in their molecules, so they do not react with active metals like Na , strong bases like NaOH , reducing or oxidising agents.
(ii) Basic nature : Owing to the presence of unshared electron pairs on oxygen, ethers behave as Lewis bases. Hence they dissolve in strong acids (e.g., conc. HCl , conc. $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}$ ) at low temperature to form oxonium salts.

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-323.jpg?height=175&width=723&top_left_y=1813&top_left_x=705)
Dialkyloxonium chloride

Being Lewis bases, ethers also form coordination complexes with Lewis acids like $\mathrm{BF}_{3}, \mathrm{AlCl}_{3}$,

## (c) Properties due to carbon-oxygen bond

(i) Hydrolysis : Ethers, when heated with dilute sulphuric acid under pressure are hydrolysed to the corresponding alcohols.

$$
\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5}-\mathrm{O}-\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \xrightarrow{\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}} \underset{\text { Ethyl alcohol }}{2 \mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{OH}}
$$

The hydrolysis may also be effected by boiling the ether with water or by treating it with steam.
(ii) Action of conc. sulphuric acid : On heating with concentrated sulphuric acid, ethers are converted to alkyl hydrogen sulphates.

$$
\begin{gathered}
\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5}-\mathrm{O}-\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4} \text { (conc.) } \longrightarrow \underset{\text { Ethyl alcohol }}{\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{OH}}+\underset{\text { Ethyl hydrogen sulphate }}{\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{HSO}_{4}} \\
\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{OH}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4} \longrightarrow \mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{HSO}_{4}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}
\end{gathered}
$$

(iii) Action of hydroiodic or hydrobromic acid : In this reaction, the ethereal linkage is broken; the product depends upon the temperature of the reaction.

- In cold, ethers react with HI or HBr to give the corresponding alkyl halide and alcohol.

$$
\underset{\text { Ethyl ether }}{\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5}-\mathrm{O}-\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5}+\mathrm{HI} \longrightarrow \underset{\text { Ethyl iodide }}{\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{I}}+\underset{\text { Ethyl alcohol }}{\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{OH}}}
$$

In case of mixed ethers, the nucleophile ( $\mathrm{I}_{-}$) attacks to the lower alkyl group side.

$$
\underset{\text { Ethylmethyl ether }}{\mathrm{CH}_{3}-\mathrm{O}-\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5}+\mathrm{HI} \longrightarrow \underset{\text { Mehyl iodide }}{\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{I}}+\underset{\text { Ethyl alcohol }}{\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{OH}}}
$$

However, one of the alkyl group is a tertiary group, the halide formed is a tertiary halide.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-324.jpg?height=174&width=788&top_left_y=1083&top_left_x=609)

- Ethers when heated with conc. halogen acids form two molecules of alkyl halides.

$$
\begin{gathered}
\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5}-\mathrm{O}-\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5}+2 \mathrm{HI} \xrightarrow{\Delta} 2 \mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{I}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \\
\mathrm{CH}_{3}-\mathrm{O}-\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5}+2 \mathrm{HI} \xrightarrow{\Delta} \mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{I}+\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{I}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}
\end{gathered}
$$

When HI is used in excess and the reaction is carried out at high temperature, ethanol further reacts with HI and is converted to $\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{I}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathrm{CH}_{3}-\mathrm{O}-\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5} & \xrightarrow{\mathrm{HI}} \mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{I}+\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{OH} \\
\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{OH} & \xrightarrow{\mathrm{HI}} \mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{I}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}
\end{aligned}
$$

(iv) Action of phosphorus pentachloride : Ethers react with phosphorus pentachloride on heating to form alkyl halides.

$$
\underset{\text { Diethyl ether }}{\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5}-\mathrm{O}-\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5}}+\mathrm{PCl}_{5} \longrightarrow \underset{\text { Ethyl chloride }}{2 \mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{Cl}+\mathrm{POCl}_{3}}
$$

Time to
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-324.jpg?height=86&width=108&top_left_y=1827&top_left_x=379)

Check Your
Knowledge

## Complete the following reaction,

$$
\underset{\text { Methylethyl ether }}{\mathrm{CH}_{3}-\mathrm{O}-\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5}}+\mathrm{PCl}_{5} \longrightarrow
$$

## SOLUTION

$$
\underset{\text { Methyl ethyl ether }}{\mathrm{CH}_{3}-\mathrm{O}-\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5}}+\mathrm{PCl}_{5} \longrightarrow \underset{\text { Methyl chloride }}{\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{Cl}}+\underset{\text { Ethyl chloride }}{\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{Cl}}+\mathrm{POCl}_{3}
$$

(v) Dehydration : Ether vapours when passed at over heated alumina undergo dehydration to form alkenes.

$$
\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5}-\mathrm{O}-\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5} \xrightarrow[633 \mathrm{~K}]{\mathrm{Al}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}} \underset{\text { Ethene }}{2 \mathrm{CH}_{2}}=\mathrm{CH}_{2}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}
$$

## Uses of Ethers

(a) The most important use of ethers is a laboratory and industrial solvent (for oils, resins, gums, etc.)
(b) Ether is used as an anaesthic in surgery.
(c) Ether may be used as a referigerant.

## ALDEHYDES AND KETONES

Aldehydes and ketones are characterised by the presence of carbonyl group $>\mathrm{C}=\mathrm{O}$ in their molecules. Aldehydes are written as
<img class="imgSvg" id = "mpcazmyr3mqonzvqmgv" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,"/>
and ketones as
<img class="imgSvg" id = "mpcazmys6rmhh300nk" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,"/>
O . If the two alkyl groups attached to carbonyl carbon are same, the ketone is symmetrical and if they are different the ketone is unsymmetrical.
(a) Isomerism in Aldehydes and Ketones
(i) Chain isomerism :

They exhibit three types of isomerism which as follows.
(i) Chain isomerism :

## 1. FORMALDEHYDE OR METHANAL (HCHO)

Formaldehyde is the first member of the aldehyde series. It is present in green leaves of plants where its presence is supposed to be due to the reaction of $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ with water in presence of sunlight and chlorophyll.

$$
\mathrm{CO}_{2}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \longrightarrow \mathrm{HCHO}+\mathrm{O}_{2}
$$

## Preparation of Methanal

It can be prepared by the following methods.

## (a) By oxidation of methyl alcohol

The oxidation is done either by oxygen in presence of a catalyst like platinised asbestos or copper at $300-400^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ or by acidified potassium dichromate.

$$
\begin{gathered}
2 \mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{OH}+\mathrm{O}_{2} \xrightarrow[300-400^{\circ} \mathrm{C}]{\text { Copper }} 2 \mathrm{HCHO}+\mathrm{O}_{2} \\
\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{OH}+[\mathrm{O}] \xrightarrow[\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}]{\mathrm{K}_{2} \mathrm{Cr}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{7}} \mathrm{HCHO}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}
\end{gathered}
$$

## (b) By dehydrogenation of methyl alcohol

Vapours of methyl alcohol are passed over copper or silver at $300-400^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ when hydrogen is eliminated with the formation of formaldehyde.

$$
\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{OH} \xrightarrow[300-400^{\circ} \mathrm{C}]{\mathrm{Cu} \text { or } \mathrm{Ag}} \mathrm{HCHO}+\mathrm{H}_{2}
$$

Time to
Check Your
Knowledge

- What are the products obtained when ethyl alcohol is dehydrogenated?


## SOLUTION

Acetaldehyde and hydrogen are obtained

$$
\underset{\text { Ethyl alcohol }}{\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{OH}} \xrightarrow[300-400^{\circ} \mathrm{C}]{\mathrm{Cu} \text { or } \mathrm{Ag}} \underset{\text { Acetaldehyde }}{\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CHO}}+\underset{\text { Hydrogen }}{\mathrm{H}_{2}}
$$

## (c) By heating calcium formate

Dry calcium formate is strongly heated to obtain formaldehyde.

$$
\underset{\text { Calcium formate }}{\mathrm{Ca}(\mathrm{HCOO})_{2}} \xrightarrow{\text { Heat }} \mathrm{CaCO}_{3}+\underset{\text { Formaldehyde }}{\mathrm{HCHO}}
$$

This method is used as a laboratory method for the synthesis of HCHO . However, other aldehyde is not prepared by this method due to very poor yield.
(d) By ozonolysis of ethylene

Ethylene is dissolved in an organic solvent such as chloroform or carbon tetrachloride and a stream of ozonised oxygen is passed. The ozonide formed is decomposed by passing hydrogen in presence of palladium as catalyst.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-326.jpg?height=179&width=884&top_left_y=2047&top_left_x=556)

In the above reaction methanal can also be obtained by hydrolysis of ozonide in presence of zinc.

## Manufacture of Methanal (Industrial Process)

Formaldehyde is manufactured by controlled oxidation of methane by air in presence of various metallic oxides as catalyst.

$$
\underset{\text { Methane }}{\mathrm{CH}_{4}}+\mathrm{O}_{2} \xrightarrow[\text { Catalyst }]{\text { Mo-oxide }} \underset{\text { Formaldehyde }}{\mathrm{HCHO}}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}
$$

It is also prepared by passing water gas at low pressure through an electric discharge of low intensity.

$$
\underbrace{\mathrm{CO}+\mathrm{H}_{2}}_{\text {Water gas }} \xrightarrow{\text { Elec. discharge }} \underset{\text { Formaldehyde }}{\mathrm{HCHO}}
$$

## Physical Properties of Methanal

(a) It is a colourless, pungent smelling gas.
(b) It is extremely soluble in water.
(c) It can easily be condensed into liquid. The liquid formaldehyde boils at $-21^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$.
(d) It causes irritation to skin, eyes, nose and throat.
(e) Its solution acts as antiseptic and disinfectant.

## Chemical Properties of Methanal

Formaldehyde is structurally different from other aldehydes as it contains hydrogen atom in place of alkyl group in other aldehyde. Though it shows general properties of aldehydes, it differs in certain respects. The abnormal properties of formaldehyde are given below :
(a) Reaction with ammonia

Like other aldehydes, formaldehyde does not form addition product rather a crystalline compound, hexamethylene tetramine is formed.

$$
\underset{\text { Formaldehyde }}{6 \mathrm{HCHO}}+4 \mathrm{NH}_{3} \longrightarrow \underset{\substack{\text { Urotropine } \\ \text { (Hexamethylene tetramine) }}}{\left(\mathrm{CH}_{2}\right)_{6} \mathrm{~N}_{4}}+6 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}
$$

Urotropine is used as a medicine to treat urinary infections.
Did You, Know!
<img class="imgSvg" id = "mpcazmzhj2i0xvx205a" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,"/>

Structure of Urotropine
(b) Reaction with sodium hydroxide (Cannizzaro's reaction)

It does not form resin with sodium hydroxide like acetaldehyde but when treated with a concentrated solution of sodium hydroxide, two molecules of formaldehyde undergo mutual oxidation and reduction forming formic acid salt and methyl alcohol (Disproportionation).

$$
\underset{\text { Formaldehyde }}{2 \mathrm{HCHO}}+\mathrm{NaOH} \text { (Conc.) } \longrightarrow \underset{\text { Sod. formate }}{\mathrm{HCOONa}}+\underset{\text { Methyl alcohol }}{\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{OH}}
$$

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-327.jpg?height=186&width=323&top_left_y=2046&top_left_x=255)

The above reaction is also called Cannizaro's reaction and is only given by those aldehydes which do not have $\alpha$-hydrogen atom (hydrogen atom present at carbon atom bonded to functional group of organic compounds).
(c) Aldol condensation

Formaldehyde in presence of a weak base undergoes repeated aldol condensation to give formose.

$$
\underset{\text { Formaldehyde }}{6 \mathrm{HCHO}} \xrightarrow{\mathrm{Ca}(\mathrm{OH})_{2}} \underset{\text { Formose (hexose) }}{\mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{12} \mathrm{O}_{6}}
$$

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-328.jpg?height=679&width=1458&top_left_y=370&top_left_x=363)

## Uses of Methanal

(a) The $40 \%$ solution of formaldehyde (formalin) is used as disinfectant, germicide and antiseptic. It is used for the preservation of biological specimens.
(b) It is used in the preparation of hexamethylene tetramine (urotropine) which is used as an antiseptic and germicide.
(c) It is used in silvering of mirror.
(d) It is employed in the manufacture of synthetic dyes such as para-rosaniline, indigo, etc.
(e) It is used in the manufacture of forma mint (by mixing formaldehyde with lactose) - a throat lozenges.
(f) It is used for making synthetic plastics like bakelite, urea-formaldehyde resin, etc.

## 2. ACETONE OR PROPANONE, OR DIMETHYL KETONE, $\mathbf{C H}_{3} \mathbf{C O C H}_{3}$

It is a symmetrical (simple) ketone and is the first member of the homologous series of ketones. In traces, it is present in blood and urine.

## Preparation of Acetone

Acetone is prepared in laboratory by heating anhydrous calcium acetate.

$$
\underset{\text { Calcium acetate }}{\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COO}\right)_{2} \mathrm{Ca}} \xrightarrow{\Delta} \mathrm{CaCO}_{3}+\underset{\text { Acetone }}{\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COCH}_{3}}
$$

## Manufacture of Acetone (Industrial Process)

Acetone is manufactured by following methods.
(a) By air oxidation of isopropyl alcohol

The air oxidation occurs at $500^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$.

$$
\underset{\text { Isopropyl alcohol }}{\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CHOHCH}_{3}}+\mathrm{O}_{2} \xrightarrow{500^{\circ} \mathrm{C}} \underset{\text { Acetone }}{\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COCH}_{3}}+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}
$$

(b) By dehydrogenation of isopropyl alcohol
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-328.jpg?height=129&width=659&top_left_y=2224&top_left_x=669)

## Physical Properties of Acetone

(a) It is a colourless liquid with characteristic pleasant odour.
(b) It is highly inflammable liquid. It boils at $56^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$.
(c) It is highly miscible with water, alcohol and ether.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-328.jpg?height=108&width=122&top_left_y=2581&top_left_x=1836)

## Chemical Properties of Acetone

It shows general properties of ketones. Some special properties of acetone are described below :

## (a) Haloform reaction

When heated with iodine and sodium hydroxide, it forms yellow crystals of iodoform.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COCH}_{3}+3 \mathrm{I}_{2} & \longrightarrow \underset{\text { Ariiodo acetone }}{\mathrm{CI}_{3} \mathrm{COCH}_{3}+3 \mathrm{HI}} \\
\mathrm{CI}_{3} \mathrm{COCH}_{3}+\mathrm{NaOH} & \longrightarrow \underset{\text { Iodoform }}{\mathrm{CHI}_{3}}+\underset{\text { Sodium acetate }}{\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COONa}}
\end{aligned}
$$

(b) Acetone forms chloroform when heated with bleaching powder in water.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathrm{CaOCl}_{2}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} & \longrightarrow \mathrm{Ca}(\mathrm{OH})_{2}+\mathrm{Cl}_{2} \\
\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COCH}_{3}+3 \mathrm{Cl}_{2} & \longrightarrow \mathrm{CCl}_{3} \mathrm{COCH}_{3}+3 \mathrm{HCl} \\
2 \mathrm{CCl}_{3} \mathrm{COCH}_{3}+\mathrm{Ca}(\mathrm{OH})_{2} & \longrightarrow \underset{\text { Chloroform }}{2 \mathrm{CHCl}_{3}}+\underset{\text { Calcium acetate }}{\mathrm{Ca}\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COO}\right)_{2}}
\end{aligned}
$$

## Uses of Acetone

(a) As a solvent for cellulose acetate, cellulose nitrate, celluloid, lacquers, resins, etc.
(b) For storing acetylene.
(c) In the manufacture of cordite - a smokeless powder explosive.
(d) In the preparation of chloroform, iodoform, sulphonal and chloretone.
(e) As a nailpolish remover.
(f) In the preparation of an artificial scent (ionone), plexiglass (unbreakable glass) and synthetic rubber.

Time to Check Your Knowledge

## For which alkyne, acetone is used as a storage material?

## SOLUTION

For acetylene, acetone is used as a storage material.

## CARBOXYLIC ACIDS

The compounds containing the carboxyl functional group $-\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{OH}$ are called carboxylic acids. The word carboxyl is a combination of two words carbonyl ( $>\mathrm{C}=\mathrm{O}$ ) and hydroxyl ( -OH ).

## Classification of Carboxylic Acids

Depending upon the number of -COOH groups, they are classified as (i) monocarboxylic acids: containing one -COOH group (ii) dicarboxylic acids-containing two -COOH groups and so on.

Fatty acids: Aliphatic monocarboxylic acids are commonly called fatty acids because higher members are obtained by the hydrolysis of oils and fats.

## Isomerism in Carboxylic Acids

They exhibit following type of isomerism.
(a) Position and chain isomerism
e.g. $\mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{12} \mathrm{O}_{2}$ represents
<img class="imgSvg" id = "mpcazmzo24v5s10ioie" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,"/>
<img class="imgSvg" id = "mpcazn03wpmuuvd31ef" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,"/>
<img class="imgSvg" id = "mpcazn04y9rhfxrk53" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,"/>
<img class="imgSvg" id = "mpcazn05bnbtsvq6wpn" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,"/>
<img class="imgSvg" id = "mpcazn068tgcygbpu" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,"/>
<img class="imgSvg" id = "mpcazn07nypeso3cet" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,"/>
<img class="imgSvg" id = "mpcazn081m3cfvamfkk" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,"/>
$\left.\begin{array}{l}\text { 1-Hexanoic acid } \\ \text { 2-Methylpentanoic acid } \\ \text { 2-Ethylbutanoic acid }\end{array}\right\}$ Position isomers

4-Methylpentanoic acid and 1-hexanoic acid are chain isomers
(b) Functional isomerism

Formula
Functional Isomers
$\mathrm{C}_{3} \mathrm{H}_{6} \mathrm{O}_{2}$ :
<img class="imgSvg" id = "mpcazn0aclnhidyq55a" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,"/>

Propanoic acid

2, 3 - Dimethylbutanoic acid

3, 3 - Dimethylbutanoic acid
All are chain isomers of 1-hexanoic acid.

## Multiple Choice Questions

DIRECTIONS : This section contains multiple choice questions. Each question has four choices (a), (b), (c) and (d) out of which ONLY ONE is correct.

1. Methane and ethane both can be obtained in single step from -
(a) $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{I}$
(b) $\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{I}$
(c) $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{OH}$
(d) $\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{OH}$
2. Petroleum consists mainly of -
(a) aliphatic hydrocarbons
(b) aromatic hydrocarbons
(c) aliphatic alcohols
(d) None of these
3. LPG is a mixture of -
(a) $\mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{12}+\mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{6}$
(b) $\mathrm{C}_{4} \mathrm{H}_{10}+\mathrm{C}_{3} \mathrm{H}_{8}$
(c) $\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{4}+\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{2}$
(d) $\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{4}+\mathrm{CH}_{4}$
4. Water gas is -
(a) $\mathrm{CO}+\mathrm{CO}_{2}$
(b) $\mathrm{CO}+\mathrm{N}_{2}$
(c) $\mathrm{CO}+\mathrm{H}_{2}$
(d) $\mathrm{CO}+\mathrm{N}_{2}+\mathrm{H}_{2}$
5. As the number of carbon atoms in a chain increases the boiling point of alkanes -
(a) increases
(b) decreases
(c) remains same
(d) may increase or decrease
6. A liquid hydrocarbon can be converted to gaseous hydrocarbon by -
(a) cracking
(b) hydrolysis
(c) oxidation
(d) distillation under reduced pressure
7. B.P. of branched chain alkanes as compared to straight chain alkanes are -
(a) lower
(b) equal
(c) higher
(d) independent of the chain length
8. The reaction $\mathrm{CH}_{4}+\mathrm{Cl}_{2} \xrightarrow{\text { uv light }} \mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{Cl}+\mathrm{HCl}$ is an example of -
(a) addition reaction
(b) substitution reaction
(c) elimination reaction
(d) rearrangement reaction
9. Aluminium carbide on reacting with water gives -
(a) methane
(b) ethane
(c) ethene
(d) ethyne
10. Maximum carbon-carbon bond distance is found in -
(a) ethyne
(b) ethene
(c) ethane
(d) benzene
11. Alkenes usually show which type of reaction?
(a) addition
(b) substitution
(c) elimination
(d) superposition
12. In a reaction, if half of the double bond is broken and two new bonds are formed, this is a case of -
(a) elimination
(b) addition
(c) displacement
(d) rearrangement
13. Ethyl bromide gives ethylene when reacted with -
(a) ethyl alcohol
(b) dilute $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}$
(c) aqueous KOH
(d) alcoholic KOH
14. Which is the most reactive hydrocarbon among the following?
(a) Ethane
(b) Ethyne
(c) Ethene
(d) Methane
15. Which of the following gas is used for welding ?
(a) Methane
(b) Ethane
(c) Acetylene
(d) Ethene
16. Power alcohol contains -
(a) $50 \%$ petrol and $50 \%$ ethanol
(b) $75 \%$ petrol and $25 \%$ ethanol
(c) $25 \%$ petrol and $75 \%$ ethanol
(d) $70 \%$ petrol and $30 \%$ ethanol
17. The highest boiling point is of -
(a) ethane
(b) ethanol
(c) methanol
(d) ethanoic acid
18. To prepare methane, we heat soda-lime with -
(a) calcium acetate
(b) sodium acetate
(c) sodium propionate
(d) sodium formate
19. Which of the following gas is produced when cold water is added on calcium carbide?
(a) $\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{2}$
(b) $\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{4}$
(c) $\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{6}$
(d) $\mathrm{C}_{3} \mathrm{H}_{8}$

(a) hydrogenation
(b) ozonolysis
(c) Baeyer's reagent
(d) dehydration
21. A reagent used to test unsaturation in alkenes is -
(a) ammonical $\mathrm{Cu}_{2} \mathrm{Cl}_{2}$
(b) ammonical $\mathrm{AgNO}_{3}$
(c) solution of $\mathrm{Br}_{2}$ in $\mathrm{CCl}_{4}$
(d) conc. $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}$
22. Acidic hydrogen is present in -
(a) ethyne
(b) ethene
(c) ethane
(d) benzene
23. By which reaction ethene is obtained from ethyne?

24. A Grignard reagent may be made by reacting magnesium with :
(a) Methyl amine
(b) Diethyl ether
(c) Ethyl iodide
(d) Ethyl alcohol

(a) $\mathrm{HCl}+\mathrm{ZnCl}_{2}$

(c) $\mathrm{PCl}_{5}$

the product Z is:

## Passage-1

The addition of ozone on the double bonds and subsequent hydrolysis of the ozonide formed is termed as ozonolysis.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-335.jpg?height=286&width=803&top_left_y=685&top_left_x=251)

The hydrogen peroxide formed in the reaction is removed by adding zinc dust because it, being an oxidising agent, oxidises aldehydes into carboxylic acids.
Ozonolysis of alkenes offers a valuable method of detection and location of double bonds in complex organic molecules. For knowing the ozonolysis products of an alkene, remove the $>\mathrm{C}=\mathrm{C}<$ bond and add oxygen atom ' O ' on both the doubly bonded carbon atoms,

Ex.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/3a0f04db-c54c-4692-a520-39c06e6b7649-335.jpg?height=150&width=534&top_left_y=1324&top_left_x=319)

2-Methyl-2-butene

$$
\underset{\text { Acetone }}{\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3}\right)_{2} \mathrm{C}}=\underset{\text { Ethanal }}{\mathrm{O}}+\underset{3}{\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CHO}}
$$

1. Which alkene on ozonolysis gives $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{CHO}$ and $\underset{\|}{\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CCH}_{3}}$ ?
(a)
<img class="imgSvg" id = "mpcazn11jkrqu8ayos" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,"/>

(a) 3-Methyl-1-butene
(b) Cyclopentane
(c) 2-Methyl-1-butene
(d) 2-Methyl-2-butene.
3. Which of the following compounds will produce two molecules of formaldehyde on ozonolysis?
(a) $\mathrm{CH}_{3}-\mathrm{CH}=\mathrm{CH}_{2}$
(b) $\mathrm{CH}_{2}=\mathrm{CH}_{2}$
(c) $\mathrm{CH}_{3}-\mathrm{CH}=\mathrm{CH}-\mathrm{CH}_{3}$

## Passage-2

Compounds derived from hydrocarbons by the replacement of one or more hydrogen atom(s) by the halogen atoms(s) are known as halogen derivatives. The halogen derivatives of alkanes, alkenes, alkynes and arenes are known as alkyl halides (haloalkanes), alkenyl halides (haloalkenes), alkynyl halides (haloalkynes), aryl halides (haloarenes) respectively. Halogen derivatives may be classified depending upon the number of the halogen atom. Thus halogen derivatives of alkanes may be monohalogen derivatives, dihalogen derivatives, tri- and tetra-halogen derivatives.
4. The general formula for the homologus series of alkyl halide is (where X is halogen) :
(a) $\mathrm{C}_{n} \mathrm{H}_{2 n} \mathrm{X}$
(b) $\mathrm{C}_{n} \mathrm{H}_{2 n-1} \mathrm{X}$
(c) $\mathrm{C}_{n} \mathrm{H}_{2 n+1} \mathrm{X}$
(d) $\mathrm{C}_{n} \mathrm{H}_{2 n} \mathrm{X}_{n}$
5. Which of the following compound cannot be considered as dihalogen derivative?
(a) $\mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{Cl}_{2}$
(b) $\mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{Cl} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{Cl}$
(c) $\mathrm{CHCl}_{2} \mathrm{CH}_{3}$
(d) $\mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{ClCH}_{2} \mathrm{ClCH}_{2} \mathrm{Cl}$
6. Which of the following is not a correct match?
(a) alkyl halide : $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{Cl}$
(b) alkenyl halide : $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{C}=\mathrm{CHCl}$
(c) alkynyl halides: $\mathrm{HC} \equiv \mathrm{CCl}$
(d) aryl halide : $\mathrm{CHCl}_{3}$

## Assertion \& Reason

DIRECTIONS : Each of these questions contains an Assertion followed by Reason. Read them carefully and answer the question on the basis of following options. You have to select the one that best describes the two statements.
(a) If both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of Assertion.
(b) If both Assertion and Reason are correct, but Reason is not the correct explanation of Assertion.
(c) If Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
(d) If Assertion is incorrect but Reason is correct.

1. Assertion : Branched alkanes have lower boiling point than their unbranched isomers.

Reason : Branched alkanes has relatively small surface area, so less van der Waal forces of attraction act among molecules.
2. Assertion : Alkanes float on the surface of water.

Reason : Density of alkanes is in the range of $0.6-0.9 \mathrm{g} / \mathrm{mL}$, which is lower than water.

$$

3. (b) Liquified petroleum gas is a mixture of ethane, propane and butane. The main component is butane.
4. (c)
5. (a) Boiling point of alkanes increases with the number of carbon atoms because surface area increases which increases the van der Waal forces of attraction.
6. (a) $\underset{\text { Hexane }}{\mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{14}(1)} \xrightarrow{\Delta} \underset{\text { Buane }}{\mathrm{C}_{4} \mathrm{H}_{10}}(\mathrm{~g})+\underset{\text { Ethene }}{\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{2}}(\mathrm{~g})$
7. (a) As the number of branches increases, surface area decreases, due to which van der Waal forces of attraction decreases. Hence, boiling point also decreases.

$\mathrm{Cl}_{2}+\mathrm{P}$ will give $\mathrm{PCl}_{3}$ in situ which reacts with $\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{OH}$ and forms $\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{Cl}$.

$14 n=70$
